SpaceX And The Historic IPO Wave

ARK Invest(637.0K)·36:14·13.0K views·2026-06-04
Cathie WoodCEO and CIO, ARK Invest Brett WintonChief Futurist, ARK Invest Charlie RobertsChief Investment Strategist, Private Equity, ARK Invest
TL;DRA highly anticipated wave of mega-IPOs led by SpaceX and Anthropic signals a massive structural shift in capital markets, driven by unprecedented private-market value creation and a rapid physical-digital technology convergence that could accelerate ...
real US GDP growth to...

TL;DR

A highly anticipated wave of mega-IPOs led by SpaceX and Anthropic signals a massive structural shift in capital markets, driven by unprecedented private-market value creation and a rapid physical-digital technology convergence that could accelerate real US GDP growth to 7–8% over the next five years.

The Impending IPO Wave and Macroeconomic Outlook

  • A Convergence of Giants: Companies like SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI, Databricks, Freenome, and Securitize are all actively lining up or preparing confidential filings to go public.
  • The Macro Driver:
      • Technology-Led Growth: Cathie Wood (CEO and CIO) attributes this sudden cluster of IPO filings to a massive technology revolution where the physical and digital worlds are merging. This capital-intensive transition supports ARK’s forecast of 7% to 8% real GDP growth over the next five years.
      • Infrastructure Incentives: Brett Winton (Chief Futurist) notes that disruptive technologies get "paid multiple times." The initial wave of capital motivates physical infrastructure buildouts (e.g., launch towers and data centers), which then yield exponentially higher returns on capital deployed.

The Evolution of Private Value Creation and "Private for Longer"

  • Unprecedented Private Scale:
      • Valuation Explosion: In the last five years, over $5 trillion in private company valuation has been created from scratch.
      • Extended Private Windows: Companies now routinely stay private for approximately 10 years, compared to the historical norm of 3 to 6 years. This shifts the majority of early-stage wealth generation away from public markets.
  • Rapid Scale and Lean Teams:
      • Anthropic’s Historic Velocity: Founded in January 2021, Anthropic is already tracking toward estimated run rates of well over $30 billion ARR, hitting scale thresholds in five years that took legacy giants like Microsoft 35 years to achieve.
      • The Entrepreneurial Explosion: Driven by the "ChatGPT moment," new business filings in the US have surged by 10% to 20% as AI agents allow single-person or ultra-small teams to scale into highly leveraged "unicorns."

SpaceX’s Expanding TAM and Generational Moat

  • The Starship Economics Paradigm:
      • Collapsing Launch Costs: If SpaceX successfully operationalizes the reusable Starship rocket, launch costs are modeled to fall from roughly $1,000/kg to under $100/kg.
      • Satellite Monetization: At these lower launch costs, a single Starship loaded with satellites is projected to generate roughly $1 billion in annual revenue over a five-year lifespan.
  • Trillion-Dollar TAM Expansion:
      • Starlink's Redefined Market: Originally sized by ARK as a $34 billion opportunity for rural/isolated areas, SpaceX now models Starlink's addressable market at $1.6 trillion as it positions itself to capture global telecom and internet traffic.
      • Invaluable Moats: While competitors struggle—demonstrated by the recent high-profile explosion of a competitor’s Blue Origin rocket—SpaceX's Falcon 9 has made high-frequency orbital launches routine.
      • Mars Incentives: Unprecedented multi-hundred-billion-dollar equity incentives tied directly to interplanetary colonization have been integrated into SpaceX’s S-1 filing.

The Enterprise AI S-Curve and Token Scrutiny

  • Early Adoption Framework: Chatbot-to-smartphone user penetration is currently in the low 20% range, mirroring internet adoption levels in early 1996. For enterprise AI agent penetration, the market resembles 1990 (pre-web browser).
  • The Market Opportunity: ARK projects that global AI enterprise software demand will reach $7 trillion by 2030, compared to the current $30 trillion spent globally on knowledge worker wages.
  • Transitioning from Hype to Efficiency:
      • CFOs Step In: Enterprises are transitioning from unconstrained experimentation to cost optimization. Organizations like Uber have begun capping employee token usage to curb inefficient "bad hygiene" spending.
      • Agentic Coding: Recursive self-improvement (RSI)—where AI-generated code is writing more code—is now active, with a vast majority of development work within frontier labs being built autonomously by their own systems.

Cross-Technology Convergence Beyond Core AI

  • Downstream Acceleration: Breakthroughs in core AI models are dramatically accelerating downstream technological platforms that rely on data processing and pattern recognition.
  • Notable Cross-Sector Platforms:
      • Freenome: Utilizing advanced AI to process complex molecular diagnostics from simple blood tests to identify early-stage cancers.
      • Securitize: Leading the digitization of traditional real-world financial assets onto public blockchains to drive friction out of the global economy.

Notable Quotes

"This technology revolution is so massive and happening so quickly and the digital and the physical world are coming together so profoundly that you almost have to use, you know, as many sources of financing as possible." — Cathie Wood
"In the last five years we've as far as we can tell over $5 trillion of private company valuation has been created essentially from nothing. So this this is unprecedented." — Charlie Roberts
"If you can equip enterprises with something that makes a knowledge worker, you know twice as productive, they'll happily pay 10% the value of that to equip that knowledge worker with that tool." — Brett Winton
Hello. I'm Brett Winton, and welcome to the SpaceX and IPO Wave livestream from ARK Invest. We have the three members of our venture investment committee here with us today. It's myself, it's Cathie Wood, our fearless leader, and Charlie Roberts. Hi Charlie, how are you? Hello. We're great. We're great. We're great, Brett. Thank you. Sorry. Yes, yes. Where is your enthusiasm? Didn't you hear the intro music? That was great. Yeah, yeah, Charlie, social media team. So, we are setting up for a SpaceX IPO that's coming in, you know, this forthcoming week likely, as well as Anthropic, another, you know, key holding in our venture strategies, confidently filing for an IPO, or filing a confidential filing for an IPO. And there are rumors that OpenAI and potentially Databricks, as well as Freenome and Securitize, are all lining up to list publicly. Cathie, you've been through a number of cycles. What do you think about kind of this wave of potential IPOs, why it's happening now, and what it means for capital markets generally? Yes, we've been noodling through this. And of course, the first thing that came to mind is, you know, could it be because of the midterm elections and everything that could change because of the midterm elections, because so many are considering IPOing at the same time? I also think that what is happening, and of course our research is showing it, is this technology revolution is so massive and happening so quickly, and the digital and the physical world are coming together so profoundly, that you almost have to use, you know, as many sources of financing as possible. And I think it says something about the magnitude of the investment that these companies see continuing as they scale into some of the biggest companies the world has ever known. Yeah, and with some of the fastest revenue growth that companies actually—probably the fastest companies have ever delivered at their revenue size, right? Yeah, and you know, I think just one last point on this, I also think this is feeding into our GDP growth scenario. I think growth is picking up generally, and that technology is leading the charge, so it supports our forecast of 7 to 8% real GDP growth in the next five years. Yeah, and I'll say a point on that because when you have disruptive technologies, you get paid multiple times. The first time you get paid is because you motivate capital to build the infrastructure for the technology. So rather than just sitting in government treasuries, the money gets pushed into actual, you know, physical built assets. And these IPOs are, in part, because SpaceX needs money to build, you know, multiple launch towers and to build data centers, and Anthropic needs the same to pay for the compute that maybe it's going to be built by somebody else, but it prompts kind of like the actual shovels in the ground which drive activity. And then if these are good things to build, then you have a higher return on capital deployed, and so that feeds through into the economy in a second layer. And we believe they are good things to build. Charlie, when we first began to look at venture, part of the reason we thought it was important to have a strategy that was available to everybody is because companies had been staying private for so long and seemingly maybe they'll never go public. Talk about unicorns and decacorns—does this mean that unicorns and decacorns are going to dry up and everything's going to go public? Or how do you think about the dynamic of private versus public here? Thank you, Brett, and it's a pleasure to be here talking about this stuff. I think as well as the private-for-longer move that's happening, what's probably more important is more value generation happening while private, faster. And this is, as you say, with companies like Anthropic. I mean, this is just a staggering—you know, all three of these frontier labs have built value in a staggering way, but Anthropic particularly because it's only five years old. It was founded literally in January 2021, and to get to estimates that put it well over 30 billion ARR already is just unbelievable. So while we're seeing, anyway, companies choosing to stay private for longer—so around 10 years is quite the norm now, whereas historically it was, you know, more in the three to five to six kind of range—it's more about the value generation. And what we're seeing with these unicorns, so there have been several peaks of actual newly minted unicorns. Around—probably unsurprisingly historically, you know, in some periods we saw many more unicorns. I think the peak period was in 2021 where we saw hundreds of unicorns minted, but the actual value of the unicorns has been steadily going up. So that in the last five years, we've, as far as we can tell, created over $5 trillion of private company valuation essentially from nothing. This is unprecedented. And with the venture strategy, we thought what really matters is to make sure that people, everyone, has access in a very democratized way to this value generation, which is historic and is occurring on the private side. And especially with AI, that's been the biggest catalyst of this because suddenly you can spin up a company easier than ever before. Apollo numbers suggest, or research suggests, that the new company formations in the US, the new filings, have gone up 10 to 20% potentially as a result of, or since, the ChatGPT moment. So it's never been a better time to start a company, it's never been a better time to scale a company, and it's never been a better time to build value. And guess what? So much of that value is built before you could even think about going public because it's happening so fast. And you know, there's even an interesting question about the role for venture capital at all as we go forward because companies can be built to so much value so quickly. You know, a lot of people have been talking about the single-person unicorn or the five-person decacorn or whatever. And you know, I think we are starting to see that kind of thing come true. Yeah, and I think that there's—it's kind of like the idea that, hey, because technology amplifies a human, then you can operate without humans. I think the reality is you actually will use humans for higher leverage. And same with capital. I think capital, you know, not only capital but also kind of capital curation, as in market-shifting capital to those ideas most promising, will still occur, and then there will be a lot of leverage on that capital, and I think a lot of value will be created. One of the ways I think about kind of the wave as a whole, which is clearly centered on AI, and we see that as the central dynamo that's accelerating all of these technologies, is that on a chatbot-to-smartphone user basis, we're low 20% penetrated. That's where we were in early 1996 for internet users to PC users. And so you could think of us as being in the early 1996 time frame of this cycle. In early 1996, Google was still two years away from being founded. So, I think that yes, you're going to see companies come public here that—and it's pretty clear SpaceX is going to be monumentally valuable. They're targeting the largest IPO of all time. And I think the conflagration of capability that AI provides is going to spark off to create all of these spotfires of other, like, giant pockets of value that are going to start out in the private market, and they could all begin to parade into the public market and be like, "Here we are, we're disrupting people and we're worth monumental sums of money." You know, I think that—so I think it would be a mistake to believe that, hey, there's these three big companies that are going to go public and then that's going to be it, this was a fun game, go home everybody. Actually, instead, I think them going public is going to create this entire bonfire of kind of activity in the private market that then, you know, proliferates into the public. Maybe rocket ships is a better word than bonfire, maybe, but yes. Yeah, I was just going to say, I think also, and in line with what you're saying, Brett, we are seeing an entrepreneurial explosion. I mean, I meet servers in restaurants who are, in their spare time, so excited about the project they're working on that they think could be worth millions because it's going to solve one of the world's problems. I'm seeing a lot of people worried about unemployment, you know, "we're all out of jobs." I'm seeing, for those who are harnessing and excited about these technologies, especially AI, incredible excitement about, you know, the possibility to create something out of nothing, as you said. Yeah, yeah, to go from zero to one. And part of going from zero, I think that this technology is so new. I said 1996 is maybe the proxy, but if you look at kind of more AI agents and AI engines penetration of enterprise, that penetration level is more like 1990, which is pre-web browser. This is, you know, the very, very... so, you know, I think that... So just since I was there, since I was there, Brett, at that time, people were scratching their heads, "Internet? What is this thing, the internet?" That is not happening this time around. People get what's going on, and they're experimenting and they're excited. Just to, yeah, to build on that quickly, Brett, the one thing I think we've been seeing—and I talked about previous unicorn cycles. Obviously, the DNA of the firm is to look at and model Nasdaq companies, even early Nasdaq companies in that same sort of unicorn valuation, and to do so with really tangible traction metrics and to get comfortable with the revenue, the MAUs, whatever is the metric that's relevant to that company. And I think the new thing we're seeing generally across the private companies is these are very real companies growing very fast with very real revenues, clear line of sight to profitability, real retention, and enterprises going back in for more. And then at the upper end, you know, maybe the three big labs that are coming close to IPO, those are... you know, we've never seen revenue generation and return, you know, enterprises and others building into it. If you look at even things like the entire product suite of Microsoft Office, these companies are headed for that and way beyond, and will probably blow right through those revenues very, very fast. And it took Microsoft 35 years to get there. We're talking about five years. So... Right, right. Five years and screaming past, like growing, or at least as we anticipate the market developing, still having plenty of headroom for growth as they eclipse literally the most important enterprise productivity suite in the world, including the operating system, Windows and Office. So speaking of screaming past and ascending rapidly, one of the companies, probably the most notable one here, SpaceX, is, you know, filed—we've all read its filing—it's preparing to list. Cathie, you've had a lot of experience investing alongside Elon in his various enterprises, and here we have another one about to go. How do you think about kind of this company that clearly has amazing ambitions and may be received into a public market that is looking at a revenue base versus a valuation base that is very different from what they're used to? How do you think it'll be received, and how do you think people should think about it? Well, judging from the kind of demand for SpaceX that we've seen in the private markets, I think it well exceeds the—what is it, 75, 85 billion offering? And so just like we've seen with other offerings, I think we will see a burst out of the gate, especially if anyone's tuning into our models and our thinking about the impact of Starlink and orbital data centers and the moon and then ultimately Mars and so forth. So we have that long-term time horizon. And then it's going to be very interesting. At some point it will reach a level—and we don't know what that is, and it might happen very quickly—where you will have more traditional players in the markets using valuation as a reason to sort of bash the stock. We've already seen it. Morningstar apparently came out and said it's worth half as much as this, and of course we've got, you know, a very different story. And the reason we do is because we have five analysts working on this. This is the convergence story of our lifetime. Even Elon nine months ago started using this concept of, "Wait a minute, my companies are converging more and more quickly than I thought they were," because obviously he had this orbital data center in mind. So I think you'll get that initial burst, and we'll certainly be out there educating, educating, educating, and we'll see what happens. The fact that it's going to get in the indexes as quickly as seems to be the case—the index providers are discarding their previous guidelines for SpaceX. It depends how big of the indexes SpaceX will represent. That will bring in natural institutional support, especially from those institutions who don't get as much as they want at the outset. So I do think there's a lot of support that way, but at some point, you know, we will have the bear stories out there just like we have with Tesla and all of the other stocks we own practically. Yeah, yeah, disruptive innovation is by its nature both volatile and easily misunderstood. That's why it's disruptive. And you can think of—you can imagine the way the market kind of does companies. It's like, "This is the company that we can see in the rearview mirror, this is the next year which the company is telling us what it's going to do, and then after that next year basically we just cause things to fall back down to like normal average." You know, and that approach to SpaceX, I'm sure people will take that approach and it will dramatically reduce your expectations for value in SpaceX versus kind of understanding that the company has, as far as we can understand and model, the opportunity for a very, very high return on invested capital. The reason they're coming to the capital market, it's like, they need to build rockets. If they build the Starship rocket, launch costs can fall from, you know, a thousand-ish per kilogram to less than 100 per kilogram. If they have $100 a kilogram rockets, then they can pack them full of satellites. One Starship full of satellites can generate them roughly a billion dollars of revenue per year for five years, you know. And so, kind of like, they can repeat that action until kind of they saturate the low Earth orbit constellation. And they own the market. They own that market, right? They have a 10-year lead on everyone. And that's—and that's the reason why it's rational that people say, "Well, there's growth this year, but then after that I'm just going to decay it," is because in most circumstances, if companies are printing very amazing gross profits and return on capital, it means a sea of competitors is going to come in and eat all that up. And so when that happens, then naturally your metrics decay because you are fighting off all the competitors. But SpaceX has... there's no other credible competitor for rocket launch services at the cost that they can do it. And the closest thing, they just exploded their rocket into a million billion smithereens just last week. I don't think that was actually good for SpaceX, but I do think it's just a sign of how hard rocket engineering is. And so it's a unique company at a unique time in the market. And, you know, we'll see, but I think it'll be a really exciting show. And I think another point, Brett, is moat is everything in a flattening world, right? In SpaceX, I don't think we've ever seen such a moat. And, you know, you just talked about an explosion, which is exciting to watch maybe, and something else that just happens within space. But what SpaceX has made boring, which is one of the most impressive feats—obviously the explosion was not SpaceX, it was a Blue Origin rocket—but the Falcon 9 is now somewhat boring to watch, right? Because it's so consistent. It's so good. They've been launching on average something like—not two a day, sorry—one every two days, or at least two or three a week for now 600 launches or so and almost no incident. Well, in the beginning, it was fail, fail, fail, and then takeoff. And that was really the last eight years. It's been almost, I won't say perfect, but... And I encourage, yeah, I encourage people to read *Liftoff*, which is a book that came across my radar because Elon reposted it, I think, on X, or it was probably Twitter at the time, as "mostly accurate," which was glowing praise indeed. It's a good read about the early days of SpaceX and quite how hard it was. If Starship can be made to be that consistent and frankly that boring—and I'm sure there'll be other great things to watch, including Mars and whatnot, and I'm sure Mars will now happen especially because of the multi-hundred billion dollar equity incentives that have been added on as part, which is, I think, the first time interplanetary incentives have been added to an S-1, which is interesting and fascinating to watch—but if Starship becomes that boring, as it were, that consistent, that is the next level of moat. And that will rapidly, as we know, bring down costs to orbit to a point at which orbital data centers get truly unlocked. And also, I'd encourage people to read the ARK analysis and forward model of SpaceX, which seemed outlandish at the time. Now, it didn't include orbital data centers, but with Starship—and Starship becoming consistent—that will become, I think it's under $300 per kilogram, begins to become viable. And under 100, it's actually way competitive with anything that will happen on Earth. And that looks like a "when" not "if" given the successes in Starship, given Elon's absolute passion and zeal and this incredible team he's assembled and continues to build and make that happen. Yeah, I mean, and there's a ton of engineering work to get there and to get Starship reusable. And I think one thing, just like a general statement about Elon's business strategy, is he is a master at, you know, investing in extending the exponential. And so, you know, in exponential growth, like, our human minds have a hard time really understanding—we think linearly, and so with exponential growth, something can look like it's moving very slowly and then suddenly it looks like it's all happening at once. And he anticipates across his businesses the future choke points that would cause growth to decay and then invests directly in the things to unlock, which makes investors nervous. You say, "Why are you building a Terafab? You have a great business here, why bother with all the execution risk and capital that is required there?" And it's because he's trying to actually extend the exponential beyond, you know, even the three, four, five-year forward view. So, Brett, one other example of that, and this has to do with our own research. When we started modeling out Starlink five years ago or whenever it was, we sized the opportunity originally, I think, at $34 billion, right? Because we were interpreting the market, okay, this will be for rural areas or over the water or what have you. Now, SpaceX thinks that this TAM is $1.6 trillion. And it's because it's not just that. It is really all of telecommunications and all of the internet. And it absolutely is possible, but we weren't thinking that way probably because we were constrained by what we thought was the possibility of hardware. We just weren't thinking in that way until Elon showed the way. Well, and it really did depend upon at that time a phased array antenna. Or the best in the world, a phased array antenna that could work cost like $27,000 or some—in that, I think it was $27,000. So, kind of, investing in all of the friction points that allow you to get to scale. So they built their own phased array antennas, and that allows for costs to decline, which then opens up both markets you can model and unexpected markets. So even Charlie referenced the research last year; kind of we underestimated the high-end purchaser of SpaceX bandwidth—the airlines, how quickly they would come on board, you know, government Starshield. And so actually, even in that work, leaving aside AI data centers, we were still under on their monetization rate per bandwidth in space. And, you know, we'll see how things proceed, but I think it's a, like I said, certainly a rocket ship company, literally. Okay, so now we're moving on to basically lightning round. So there's two other frontier labs, one that has confidently filed, at least according to public reporting, for its IPO: Anthropic. Charlie, are you using coworking code? How do you think about Anthropic? Yeah, used quite a lot of Claude, especially in—I mean, there was really a second call it a ChatGPT moment around AI-generated code, right? That occurred really December-ish with the launch, with the updated launch of the model at the time. And now what we're seeing is really a jockeying for position among the leading labs. So Codex and Claude, kind of maybe overtaking each other at least momentarily on different parts of it. Clearly, Grok from xAI or now SpaceX is doing great things as well, and a lot of people are using Gemini. So I think what we're seeing there is essentially an arms race between massively well-funded leading labs, which can only be good hopefully for society broadly, but certainly for growth and real GDP growth because we're seeing this massive, massive reinvestment into R&D of companies that could otherwise probably, potentially be profitable even. But certainly, you know... so I think the code generation, it is hitting a moment now where people talk about RSI now, recursive self-improvement, more even probably than AGI or ASI—artificial general intelligence, artificial superintelligence. But this whole concept of RSI where code is writing code is becoming very real, very quickly. And speaking to people—well, both public statements from companies like Anthropic and also speaking to folks building—it's just incredible how much, you know, up into the high 90s of percent of code is being built by the systems that the people are building or have been building. Yeah, and I think the key, like, threshold that was crossed is not just the code. The ability to do agentic coding also unlocked, or really unveiled, a set of index applications, the premier of which is Claude and Codex from OpenAI, that allow knowledge work to be done agentically. Because the ability to do code also allows the system to basically pick up its own tools and begin hammering for you. And so—and I don't know if that's a breakthrough that Anthropic or OpenAI anticipated, that being good at code would also become good at general knowledge work in quite as profound a way as it has turned out that it has—but it's clearly a threshold that's been crossed where, prior to the first quarter of this year, basically CEOs were pounding on everybody's heads like, "You have to use AI, you have to use AI," and people were kind of trying to but not necessarily figuring out how to. And now suddenly it's like people are like, "Oh, this tool actually really profoundly can help me do a lot of stuff." And so we're in—you can see it in Anthropic's reported revenue growth numbers—we're in takeoff mode on enterprise uptake of AI. People look at these numbers, they say, "Oh my gosh, you know, $30 billion ARR, crossing into..." there's reports of even higher numbers for them, and OpenAI the same at $30 billion ARR. And people think, "Wow, those are very big numbers." But you have to compare it to the $30 trillion in knowledge work wages that enterprises pay a year. And if you can equip enterprises with something that makes a knowledge worker, you know, twice as productive, they'll happily pay 10% of the value of that to equip that knowledge worker with that tool. And so, kind of, in our aggregate modeling, we think AI enterprise software demand by 2030 will get to $7 trillion, and so this kind of takeoff is exactly the trajectory, the thing that we thought would happen is you would cross a threshold and suddenly it would be like, "Hey, we need this stuff." And what's interesting about this one is, you think about having been through the internet revolution, tech and telecom bubble, we did not know who was going to win. Any company that slapped dot-com or internet in its description was getting funded. But think about how the market really has focused, and how the companies themselves have focused on enterprise. Remember, ChatGPT, we were looking at weekly average users, right, in the beginning? And that turned out not to be the relevant variable. Enterprise is taking off first. And I do think enterprise... well, not yet. Not yet, but it will. But that's an even more exciting, another big part of the story to happen. But the reason it's happening at enterprise is it's coming from the top down and the bottom up. You've got executives saying, "Oh my gosh, if I don't start harnessing these tools, I'm going to be uncompetitive out there." And from the bottom up—I don't know if I should use that word that way—but it is employees saying, "Oh my goodness..." At first they were saying, "I better try this stuff cause I might lose this job." Now they're saying it, "Oh look, I'm using this..." And we do our own show and tell in the mornings, and it's like, "Whoa, mind-blowing, mind-blowing ways to do work in some cases a tenth of the time." I think we're going to see... we just saw today the news that Uber—I think Uber AI had capped, or Uber generally, I think maybe had capped—employees' use of tokens for the first time. So I think we're going to see a wave of that kind of thing, right? Efficiency and turning token usage into outputs which become outcomes. I think that's going to be the next thing, probably for the coming few months, right? It kind of has to be, and it's like, how do you really maximize the human in the loop to drive something that's valuable for the org, and everyone becomes a 10x or 100x outputter of useful stuff? I think that, probably obviously, but it's going to become the next step. Well, I also think—I do think on the enterprise side—it's likely that this market resolves to, you know, three or four companies really jockeying for position in the space, but that can all occupy pretty good margin structure businesses, kind of like infrastructure as a service. And so to your... you know, I do think that the CEOs are like "Yay!" and then the CFOs are like "Wait a minute," because like, token spend is becoming a real thing. And even like it was probably a year ago we were talking about how $20 a month versus $200 a month, like, are people really going to pay $200 a month? And actually, you know, Jensen is saying, "Well, a good engineer should spend his own salary in tokens," you know, which is more like $200,000 a year or $20,000 a month or more. But then that created this bad hygiene problem. If that was the go... if that's what a good engineer... I mean, they were throwing projects and wasting a lot of money, so we're seeing some backing and filling on that. Yes, yes. And so I do think that the people looking at token spend and saying, "Hey, we need efficiencies here," will cause some jockeying in kind of market share amongst the labs. And ultimately, I think it's right to think of probably like market share is going to resolve to access to underlying compute infrastructure, which is why SpaceX AI is interesting because they're going to have orbital compute and should have an orthogonal way to get infrastructure. And why actually OpenAI, I think, is really competitively positioned here even relative to Anthropic because they bought and reserved a lot of future compute capacity, you know, before it became as dear as it is now, and so they have, you know, a better angle for both research and servicing the customers they have. And we'll see. It'll be a wild, you know, 6 and 12 months of kind of not just kind of market share analysis and revenue announcements, but also continued advances in the model. I think people are underestimating the degree to which we... this is amazing today, next year it's almost certainly going to be a whole lot better in terms of what these things can do. And you know what's interesting? I think many people began to understand—first it was 20, then 200, and even within our own organization, Brett being the power user that he is, I think you are approaching $2,000 a month, if I'm not mistaken, to the point where our CTO taps him on the shoulder and says, "What projects are you working on?" because he was looking at your consumption of tokens and saying... So but not to stop you, Brett, but just to alert you that, okay, you know, this is happening. So we don't want to stop you, Brett; we do not want to stop you. And as I said to him, I said the SpaceX model that we're building for this IPO is the best, the best we've ever built. And we didn't want to stop you. We didn't want to stop you, but it just tells you there's a little bit of the give and take even in our own organization like, "Okay," because we want to understand how other organizations—well, we want to be efficient, but we also want to understand how other organizations are going to be grappling with issues as well. Yes, and I'm a dedicated Codex user, by the way, not Claude, but I switch back and forth. And I think that again, you know, if you look at kind of per-task-done basis, Codex is actually maybe twice as efficient a model, maybe even more, and so it's possible that Anthropic, which has all this momentum, will fall under more scrutiny with kind of CFOs and CTOs being like, "You can get the same work done for half the cost if you just move over here to a very similar tool." So those kinds of dynamics will, you know, play out over time in the market. And it probably will result in, at some point within the next month, there's going to be a media story about, "Oh, the AI boom is over, nobody wants to spend money on tokens anymore, this whole thing is like a false dawn, this doesn't work..." Well, that's already starting. You mentioned Uber, and other organizations are taking a look at their expenses. And you know, especially venture capital firms, they're looking at their startups' use and efficiencies, and we're hearing about some surprises. "Oops, some mistake. I didn't realize it was costing this much" when venture capital teams scrutinize their expenses, right? So it's even at that level. Yeah, and it kind of reminds me even in the transition to the cloud, people were arguing, "Oh, this is actually much more expensive to do," but actually creating an agile, AI-first organization, I think, is going to be clearly the dominant strategy on a forward basis. And my gosh, we are already five minutes over on time. So I'm going to give you one lightning round. It's not just the core AI labs that are likely to kind of try to list over the next year. There's also platform as a service companies like Databricks which we find really fascinating and interesting. And even—I mentioned AI as the central dynamo, but, you know, Freenome, which is molecular diagnostics from blood tests for cancer... Using AI! Using AI. And Securitize, which is taking real-world traditional financial assets and actually truly digitizing them so that they can be interchangeably, like, moved in much more kind of efficient ways for the economy, are other examples of companies across technologies, across the technology areas, that are going to probably try to and have plans to, you know, list during this window. So I think that this business cycle, yes, it's AI-first, these big AI labs that are listing, but I think you're going to have a sea of startups behind them. And there's also, in embodied robotics, in multiomics, in the public blockchain space, waves that we think will follow. And that's what really makes this... the convergence of technologies also causes the technological waves to kind of overlap and stack because an acceleration in AI can accelerate things that are downstream of it. And so I do think this will be, you know, a historic cycle, a cycle without precedent. So thank you much for joining us. Yes, Cathie, Charlie, thanks for joining us, and yes, everybody go grab a surfboard and surf the coming wave.

What David Senra Learned Studying 400+ Founders

Sequoia Capital(211.0K)·56:52·7.3K views·2026-06-04
David SenraHost, Founders Podcast
TL;DRThe definitive trait shared by history’s greatest founders is an absolute, obsessive focus—the ability to "mute the world and build your own"—driven by a compulsive need for control and a deep founder-problem fit, rather than a desire for wealth or a well-balanced life.

TL;DR

The definitive trait shared by history’s greatest founders is an absolute, obsessive focus—the ability to "mute the world and build your own"—driven by a compulsive need for control and a deep founder-problem fit, rather than a desire for wealth or a well-balanced life.

1. The Power of Absolute Focus and Obsession

  • Muting the World: David Senra distills the single common trait of legendary founders into "focus," which he describes as an extreme, almost different-species-level of ignoring outside noise.
      • The Dana White Example: White purchased the UFC for $2 million, lost money for six years (putting in an additional $40 million of partner capital), and eventually built it into an organization with an $8 billion TV rights deal. He succeeded by operating purely as a passionate fan, never reading a business book or listening to a business podcast, effectively shutting out external conventional wisdom.
  • The Discipline of "No": Focus is not merely avoiding bad ideas, but actively saying no to good ideas that distract from the great ones.
      • Senra references Jony Ive’s recollection of Steve Jobs, who defined focus as the painful act of turning down highly desirable initiatives because they interfered with the core mission.

2. Founder-Problem Fit and Behavioral Archetypes

  • Founder-Problem Fit Over Product-Market Fit: Spotify founder Daniel Ek and Senra are developing a framework of founder archetypes to help leaders avoid the trap of destructive imitation (e.g., trying to copy Steve Jobs or Elon Musk).
      • Ek believes "founder-problem fit" is far more critical than product-market fit or founder-industry fit. A prime example is Demis Hassabis of DeepMind, who was uniquely built for that singular mission.
  • The Father-Son Dynamic: A recurring biographical pattern in historical founders is the profound psychological impact of the father-son relationship. Senra references the maxim, "You can always understand the son by the story of his father."
      • Francis Ford Coppola: Driven to an extreme work ethic by a brilliant but failed musician father who told him, "There can only be one genius in the family, and it's me."
      • John D. Rockefeller: Shaped by a father who was a bigamist and a scam artist who actively tried to cheat his own sons.
  • The "Imitation Deficit" Advantage: Tech founders often exhibit traits on the autism/Asperger’s spectrum, which Peter Thiel notes can be a massive advantage.
      • This condition often leaves individuals missing the "imitation socialization gene," preventing them from being talked out of highly unconventional, original ideas (like SpaceX) by social pressure.

3. The Myth of the Well-Balanced Life

  • A Brutal Statistical Reality: Out of more than 400 founders studied by Senra, only three maintained what could be considered a healthy, well-balanced life.
      • The three exceptions are mathematician and quantitative hedge fund pioneer Ed Thorp, retail giant Sol Price, and luxury fashion designer Brunello Cucinelli.
  • Compulsion Over Regret: Legendary founders are rarely motivated by money; instead, they are driven by a compulsive need for control and the desire to build.
      • Sam Walton: Wrote in his autobiography while dying of cancer that despite sacrificing immense family time, he would do it the exact same way if given another chance.
      • Phil Knight: Reflected in *Shoe Dog* on his grief after his son's tragic drowning, yet admitted his biggest regret was that he could not go back and build Nike all over again.
  • Shifting the Fuel Source: Many successful founders begin with a dark, negative inner drive (fear, anger, or a chip on the shoulder). Senra notes that to survive long-term, founders must transition their motivation from a punishing self-talk model to a generative model.
      • Billionaire entrepreneur Brad Jacobs (who built eight separate billion-dollar companies) realized that relentless self-punishment stops serving a founder once they discover their true life's work.

4. Organizational Design and the AI Platform Shift

  • The Age of Infinite Leverage: Michael Dell and Shopify's Tobi Lütke agree that the current AI-native shift is fundamentally different from previous platform changes (SaaS, mobile, internet) because of the extreme leverage it provides.
      • Organizations will require far fewer people, and planning cycles will dramatically compress.
      • In this era, top-tier talent will not be 2x or 10x more valuable, but up to 10,000x more valuable, deserving proportional compensation.
  • The Fallacy of Co-Founders: While partnerships exist (e.g., Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger, or Andrew Carnegie and operator Henry Clay Frick), historical data shows that companies are almost always powered by a single, dominant driving force. Co-founding teams rarely survive the test of time, and the secondary partner almost always exits.

Notable Quotes

"Mute the world and then build your own." — David Senra
"Focus is saying no to a good idea that you really want to do because it distracts you from a great idea." — Steve Jobs *(as quoted by David Senra)*
"If you pretend to be in control, everybody else will assume that you are." — Nolan Bushnell *(advice given to Steve Jobs, as quoted by David Senra)*
"If you give a mediocre idea to a great team, they’ll either fix it or throw it out and create something new." — Ed Catmull *(co-founder of Pixar, as quoted by David Senra)*
The best founders don't really rest on their laurels, and they don't sleep on wins. They essentially do something great, they'll celebrate for like a day, and then it's back to it. For example, I just did Tony Xu of DoorDash, whom I think you guys were investors in. He's like, "We got so much more to do." He's like, "Okay, we had something good happen, let's go out to dinner." And before the dinner is even over, he's already thinking of the 17 things that are not going right. That's why he's great. Today we have David Senra on the show. I've been a huge fan of his podcast for a long time. He basically goes way down the rabbit hole on great founders, from Jesus of Nazareth all the way up to Jensen Huang. He goes very, very deep down those rabbit holes. What I wanted to know is: what do all the great founders have in common? And how do we apply that to today's founders? How do we apply that to picking the best founders? And how do we apply that to coaching the best CEOs? I hope you like the episode. You have gone very deep on a lot of founders, a lot of CEOs, and a lot of successful people, from Jesus of Nazareth to Jensen Huang. What's the one thing they have in common? If I had to distill every single thing down to one word, I would just say focus. They are just unbelievably focused compared to not only the average person—where it's almost like they are a different species—but I would even say compared to today's founders. They are just much more focused and less distracted, and not really looking around at what other people are doing. They kind of don't really care how other people are doing what they're doing. The way I put this is: mute the world and then build your own. Yeah. And so, let me give you an example of this. This isn't out on my new show yet, but I just spent several hours with Dana White. I call him the founder of the UFC, even though people say, "Oh, they bought the company." Yeah. And Dana—first of all, it's one of my favorite conversations I've ever had, we had so much fun. But when he tells the story of the UFC, he started when he was 19 years old. He was a bellman in a hotel in Boston, and a self-described loser. He was just like, "I'm a loser. I have nothing going on." But he was obsessed with fighting. At that time, it was boxing; he was a huge boxing fan. And he had this idea: "I should just move to Vegas, the fight capital of the world, and try to just work in the industry." Now, he's trying to build the world's biggest combat organization, but back then, he was just like, "I just need to work in this industry." He was going back and forth, thinking, "This is stupid." He goes, "I'm a bellman. I could be a bellman at 35. If this doesn't work out, I could be a bellman at 35, or I could be a bellman at 50. So, I have nothing—no wife, no kids." And he just goes. He starts working in the industry, taking any job he can get. He starts promoting—or excuse me, managing—fighters, then starts managing this weird thing called MMA, and these fighters for this thing called UFC, which was nothing back then. They were performing in front of tiny crowds, and there was no money. And so, he's on the phone because he's repping two fighters, and one of the fighters is owed money by the UFC. He's like, "Dude, you need to pay him." The guy, the owner, screams at him, "There's no fucking money! There's no money!" So, Dana hangs up, calls the Fertitta brothers, whose father built the casino, and says, "I think we can buy this whole thing." What you realize is that he's a missionary founder. I think the best founders are missionary. If you go back and look at Jensen, or Steve Jobs, or Bezos, or Elon, or any of these people—they are missionary. He's like, "I think we can buy this thing." They buy it for $2 million. It takes them like six years, and he's making no money. They are just losing money; they have to dump another $40 million into it until it's profitable. And he tells this whole story, so I'll get to the end because he's still been running it for 26 years, and now they just did an almost $8 billion deal for their TV rights. During the first six years, he made no money. The first time he finally made money, they wrote the partner compensation on the board, and he couldn't believe it. He was like, "Oh my god, I'm going to make a million dollars." He thought he was rich. I can't even tell you what he told me he makes now off-camera; it would blow your mind. The reason I bring this up is because when I talked to him, he said, "I've never read a single book about business. I've never listened to a single podcast about business. All I did was act like the fan." When they bought the company, he had never sold tickets and didn't know anything about production. He just said, "I just made what I wanted to see." And so, that's "mute the world and build your own." His entire world is his business, and he doesn't care about anything going on outside of it. He's just unbelievably focused. I thought you were going to say the word obsession. Is it the same thing? I think they are closely related. If you are intensely focused on something, I don't know anybody who is intensely focused on something whom you wouldn't call obsessed. Yeah. But I think the difference between focus and obsession is that focus is, as Steve Jobs said, saying no. Jony Ive has this great interview that he did with Vanity Fair a few years ago. He said, "Steve was the most remarkably focused person I've ever come across." And one time, they were talking about this, and Steve asked, "Well, how many things have you said no to?" Jony was like, "I said no to this, and I said no to that." Steve replied, "No, no, no, you didn't want to do those things." Focus is saying no to a good idea that you really want to do because it distracts you from a great idea. Where does this come from? You've looked at their childhoods. Is there something that happened early to them? Do they have very normal upbringings? Are they normal kids, or are they weird? Where does this obsession and focus thing originate? The answer is that it's not just one thing. I came across a great line in a book I read back in 2022, which was the biography of Francis Ford Coppola. I am also obsessed with this. When I think of entrepreneurship, I'm pretty sure the word originated from French, meaning "has ideas and does them." Okay. And so, you built HubSpot, I built a podcast, and he's a filmmaker. I think it's all the same thing; it is legitimately the same thing. I had a two-and-a-half-hour conversation yesterday with Ed Catmull, the founder of Pixar. Yep. It's just the same kind of artist. These animators, storytellers, and directors have the same kind of spirit that Steve Jobs had. Ed worked with Steve Jobs for 26 consecutive years, which is longer consecutively than anybody else. But I would say, because most of them are men, especially when you're going back in history, if you were building a company 150 years ago, it was most likely a dude. Totally. The line I read in Francis Ford Coppola's biography put into words a recurring pattern that I had noticed over and over again. It says, "You can always understand the son by the story of his father. The story of the father is embedded in the son." And so, that's why when Charlie Munger says, "Hey, I'm a biography nut." I had met Charlie before he died. He had read hundreds of biographies by the time I met him. His whole thing is that to truly understand ideas, you have to tie them to the personality that developed them, which I thought was fascinating. And so, what was happening in Francis Ford Coppola's life? His dad was a brilliant but failed musician. What happens when you have aspirations, you think you're talented, but there's no evidence of that? He starts to resent this. He was one of the most cowardly people I've ever come across because he told his young son, "There can only be one genius in the family, and it's me." He would just put down his son. The world was not giving him what he wanted, so he took it out on his son like a coward, right? Which is the exact opposite of how we want to raise our kids. You have a son, and I do too. We tell them they can do whatever they want in the world. This guy's father was a coward and a terrible dad. And so, Francis Ford Coppola internalized that, and he developed a crazy work ethic. At the time when Francis Ford Coppola became a feature film director in Hollywood, there was no such thing as young directors. And so, he was a few years older than George Lucas and Steven Spielberg. They saw this guy doing it and realized, "Oh, I can do it." Fast forward: the guy would work 24/7, falling asleep editing the movie. He had an unbelievable work ethic. A decade later, he won an Academy Award, and he let his dad do the score of the movie. His dad actually won an Oscar for the score of the movie that his son made. Mm. It's almost like it came full circle. It's like, "Who's the genius now? You wouldn't have even had this opportunity if it wasn't for me." Are most of these people normal, well-adjusted people? Or are they kind of broken from an early age, and that brokenness causes this obsession? Do you consider yourself a normal, well-adjusted person? I wouldn't consider myself normal at all. Exactly, I don't either. I'm relatively well-adjusted. What does well-adjusted mean to you? I can function in society relatively well. I can have a conversation with you. I'm not so focused that I can't pick my head up and look around. Do you have the patience, like let's say, if your son was small, and you were put into a random collection of parents who aren't entrepreneurs and don't have that kind of personality? Can you sustain a conversation with them for a few hours? Or would you go crazy? I think I'm better at that than the average entrepreneur. Okay. My co-founder could not. Okay, exactly. So you know this personality type. No, they are obviously not normal. I would not consider them well-adjusted. But that doesn't mean you have to be a prick. This is a very important distinction. Okay, well, that's the thing. Because a lot of the best founders of my generation were described as assholes. Do you have to be an asshole to be a good CEO? No, definitely not. Absolutely not. I'm actually working on something with Daniel Ek, the founder of Spotify. We just hired an author who is going to write a book that may never be published, but we are building this framework because Daniel is very interested in mapping out all the different founder archetypes for this exact reason. Because when he started Spotify 20 years ago, everybody tried to imitate Steve Jobs. The founders today, who are they going to imitate? They imitate Elon or Jensen. Of course. And he said, "I wasted years of my life trying to imitate a person I am not." He is much more like a coach. He describes himself as a team player, which is a very different kind of archetype. He would try to wear somebody else's clothes or wear somebody else's personality, and he realized, "This isn't working." And what he realized now that he is in his 40s with two decades of experience, he knows himself way better, and he knows a ton of other founders. He's like, "Oh, there are all these different types, but we don't have words for them." And so, the project that we're working on right now... I love this. Yeah, and if you want to help, let me know, because I think we need some feedback and insights into it. Every founder I talk to says, "We need this." Because Daniel's point is that, more important than product-market fit or even founder-industry fit, he believes the most important thing is founder-problem fit. Think about Demis Hassabis from DeepMind. There was only one great company he had in him, and it was DeepMind. He was put on this planet to do what he is doing. That is the perfect example of founder-problem fit. Steve Jobs is another perfect example of founder-problem fit. And so, hopefully, what we are trying to do is map out these archetypes. There might be six, or there might be eight; we will figure this out. What we are doing is going through the back catalog of founders and using that to try to map all these different profiles out. And then we want a way for you to identify, "Oh, this is my archetype." And then, here are maybe 10 examples of historical founders who had that same archetype, so you can go read about how they ran their company and what they did. And then, what other archetypes of founders work well together? It's going to take a long time, but I reject the idea completely that you have to come from a broken home, or be an asshole, or be X, Y, and Z. I went up to Canada and recorded a podcast with Tobi Lütke, who is very fascinating. He said it the best way: "There's not one right way to do what you're trying to do. There's probably a hundred. You've got to figure out what's right for you." Let me ask you about a specific archetype, and this is a little awkward. If you think about autism spectrum disorder, it's a very wide spectrum. For some, it's debilitating, but for others, it's a superpower. I'm not as deep as you, but if I look at the modern trillion-dollar company CEOs—from Jensen to Jobs, Gates, Bezos, Zuckerberg, Elon, and Larry Ellison—six out of ten have publicly said they're on the spectrum. Maybe a couple of others are too, I don't know. What is your take on that? I just found a Peter Thiel quote about this that I think is interesting. I do think for some people, and for Elon in particular, it is definitely a superpower. Yeah. He has a line where he says, "I think it's a real weakness to want to be liked. A real weakness, and I do not have that." This is what Peter said about this, and let me read four sentences to you: "Many of the most successful entrepreneurs seem to be suffering from a mild form of Asperger's, where it's like you're missing the imitation-socialization gene." Now, I think Peter's take on this is really good: "We need to ask what it is about our society where those of us who do not suffer from Asperger's are at some massive disadvantage, because we will be talked out of our interesting, original, creative ideas before they're even fully formed." Think about trying to talk Elon out of building SpaceX. Good luck with that. The last thing Peter says is, "Oh, that's a bit too weird, that's a little too strange. Maybe I'll just go ahead and open that restaurant I've been talking about that everyone else can understand and agree with, or do something extremely safe and conventional." Yes. So, it's pretty common today, even among current founders. Definitely in tech. If you look back to Carnegie and Rockefeller, is there any evidence in what you've been reading that this was pretty prevalent back then, too? No, I don't think so. That is the interesting thing. Maybe there is something in particular about technology. We're in San Francisco today, and people tell me, "You should move here." It is very beautiful physically, but I have a very unique vantage point in the founder ecosystem because all kinds of founders listen to my show. What I would say about dropping into these different communities is that the problem with the Bay Area and San Francisco is that they're all memetic about being anti-memetic. Therefore, that makes them the most memetic of all. Mhm. And I think a lot of this is people pretending to have autism or pretending... You think? Oh, 100%. We naturally imitate. Yes. Even though Peter Thiel spread the issue of being memetic, I think that actually increased people being memetic. Yes. Okay. You know a lot of VCs, and there are some tropes about what VCs are looking for in founders. What do VCs have wrong? You've studied the best. I don't actually spend any time with VCs. Chip on your shoulder. Yeah. Carnegie, Ford, Jesus, Da Vinci, Rockefeller—did they all have a chip on their shoulder? I don't know about all of them, but we can take specific examples. If we go back to the impact the father has on the son, Rockefeller's father was a bigamist who had multiple families and was a scam artist. He would try to cheat his sons. This is in all his biographies. I collect obscure Rockefeller biographies and probably have about 15 of them. I even made my own biography out of a 1,700-page transcript of Rockefeller giving an interview later in life. You can't buy it; I got it from the Rockefeller archives, printed it, and bound it myself. That's how obsessed I am with Rockefeller. Was he on the spectrum? No. Okay. I don't think so. But he was obsessed. Focused. Okay. Did he have a chip on his shoulder? Yeah, for sure. Did they all? I'm trying to think of counterexamples that did not. I can't think of one right now, but I'm sure there are some. So, generally, yes. Generally, yes. I think when talking in absolutes, we're obviously going to find some exceptions, but generally, yes. Okay. What about the immigrant advantage? VCs like immigrants—people who landed in this country. I interviewed Kaz from OpenDoor, whom I love, by the way. There just weren't a lot of options for him, and the risk-adjusted option of starting a company was his best option. What is your take on that common trope among VCs regarding the immigrant advantage? I can only speak from my personal experience. My dad was born in Cuba, so I'm the son of a Cuban immigrant. I grew up meeting people who came to this country and literally risked their lives on rafts. Think about how much you love your son, and how bad Cuba and communism had to be to put your 14-year-old or 9-year-old son on a raft, hoping they would make that 90-mile journey to South Florida. Yep. What does that tell you? That had a profound impact on me. This is why I am the most pro-American, pro-capitalist person I know, because I know what we have here. I am unabashedly pro-American and pro-capitalist. I think a lot of these people, even if they don't become entrepreneurs, have fierce work ethics because they feel, "Oh my god, I didn't have any opportunity before, and now I come to this country and there is unlimited opportunity." It's weird that people whose families have been here for multiple generations think, "Oh, there's no opportunity, everything's gone, this sucks." One of the things that surprised me when I looked at modern tech CEOs was that only three of the ten were immigrants. Jensen was an immigrant, Elon was an immigrant from South Africa, and Sergey was an immigrant from Russia. Most of them were born here. But those three are responsible for what—like 80% of the market cap? Oh, yeah, yeah. But there are some other winners on the list, though. I was surprised; I thought it was going to be eight out of ten. Bezos's dad was a Cuban immigrant. Yep. A bunch of them had that. Bezos is a Cuban... Three out of ten were immigrants, and three out of ten had dads who were immigrants, but it certainly wasn't the single common factor. I would never be a VC, but if I were, I wouldn't waste time trying to run patterns through a rubric. I barely invest myself. For the investments I have done, I don't even want you to tell me what the company name is or what it does; I just bet on you. I knew for a fact that there was no way I wasn't going to succeed, and that I would kill myself before I failed. That is what I would look for. I wouldn't look for a chip on the shoulder, being an immigrant, or anything else. This person is just going to figure it out. Yesterday, Ed Catmull said one of my favorite things ever. Keep in mind, Steve Jobs said he learned more about managing highly talented people from Ed Catmull than from anybody else in his career. That's a hell of a compliment. That's a lot. And Ed said, "It's all about the people." He would give these talks and ask, "What's more important, ideas or people?" He said, "No one realizes it's a false dichotomy. Ideas come from people, therefore people are more important than ideas." His whole thing was that it is all about talented people. He said, "If you give a great idea to a mediocre team, they'll fuck it up. If you give a mediocre idea to a great team, they'll either fix it or throw it out and create something new." Yes. So, that's what I would look for. Look at Travis from Uber; if you look into his history, that dude was going to make Uber work, or he was going to die trying. Yep. You've studied all these folks. Some founders walk through the door at Sequoia as solo founders, some are co-founders, and some have big teams. For the most part, when I look back at history, some had co-founders at the beginning, but generally, it was one person who drove the company. It's always one. There could be some exceptions, but this is also true. Not many. I don't pay attention too much to the startup scene, but doesn't Y Combinator heavily recommend co-founders? I think, in general, the conventional wisdom is that co-founders are a very good idea. In fact, MIT did a big study and said the optimal number of co-founders is three, though I'm not sure I buy that. Anyway, you spent some time with Charlie Munger, who was not the co-founder of Berkshire Hathaway, but sort of was. What have you learned about the co-founder situation and partnerships from studying all these folks? We're at Sequoia, and I think people should read the foreword to the updated version of Michael Moritz's book, "Return to the Little Kingdom." He wrote that book before he was at Sequoia, focusing on the first six years of Apple. Then he did this updated version around 2009 or 2010. Read the foreword, because Moritz's writing is incredible. He is a great writer. I want to do a podcast so bad on the biography he did of Don Valentine. It's the perfect biography, and it's only about 60 pages long. Believe it or not, I've never read it. Oh my god, you need to read it today. Read it this weekend; it's phenomenal. Got it. Moritz was talking about how Steve is singular. He frames it in the foreword by saying, "Steve refounded Apple. He didn't just found Apple once; he refounded it twice. And the second time, he was alone, pulling off the craziest turnaround in technology history." So, when people say, "Oh, Buffett's not the founder of Berkshire Hathaway," I say, shut up. Munger is a co-founder. Among Carnegie, Ford, and all those folks, there weren't many partnerships. No, that's not true. Carnegie didn't even run his company; a guy named Henry Clay Frick ran it. There's a great biography on this called "Meet You in Hell" about the bitter partnership between Andrew Carnegie and Henry Clay Frick. You'll love it. Frick was the operator. Carnegie was off sailing around the world while Frick was the one building Carnegie Steel and forcing the sale to J.P. Morgan. It's a fascinating story. Anytime there is technological change and disruption, there is physical violence. We are seeing this now with the attacks against data centers and the founders of AI companies, which is unbelievably predictable and reoccurs throughout history. Let me tell you about Frick. During the Industrial Revolution, there was all this change, and when strikes happened, people literally died. An assassin went into Frick's office and shot him in the neck, grazing him. They arrested the guy, but Frick had the wound sewed up, insisted on finishing his day's work, and then went home. Okay, so Frick was a very badass dude. Is this an exception, though? Are lasting co-founders or partnerships the exception rather than the rule? I would take them case-by-case. Carnegie made all the money and was the largest shareholder, but Frick ran the company. Carnegie was smart enough to acquire Frick's company and leverage him. But what about Ford? Ford was an autocrat and wouldn't listen to anybody else. By 1919, he bought out all his shareholders and owned 100% of Ford Motor Company. What about Rockefeller? Rockefeller built a team of founders. If you look at his board, they weren't just board members; they were partners. Almost every single person taking dividends from Standard Oil was a previous founder. He essentially built a company of founders, and he happened to be the dominating personality. I talked to Munger about this, and he said, "Buffett is a once-in-a-century talent." Munger had a big ego, and when I met him, he told me the funniest thing. I'm actually surprised to hear that. I thought he subjugated his ego a lot. Well, he talks about this, and he used that exact word, subjugate. He said, "If I could do life again, I always thought I was smarter than everybody else. If I could do life again, I'd still think I was smarter than everybody else, but I would do a better job of hiding it." Okay. His point was, "I thought I was really smart, but I recognized the talent that Buffett had that I didn't. Buffett was more singularly focused, whereas I wanted to fish, boat, design architecture, do real estate, and do all this other stuff. How arrogant would I have to be to not subjugate myself to a once-in-a-century talent?" Got it. His perspective was that Buffett let him shape his mind and thinking, but he realized Buffett was the driving engine behind their success. One of my ahas, though, is that I don't think there are a lot of co-founding teams that lasted the test of time. I think it's pretty rare, and I see that today in Silicon Valley. It's usually one dominant person. Yeah, and then the others go away. The second person leaves—Wozniak went away, and the co-founders of Facebook went away. It's pretty common. That was my argument. Maybe that one person needed those other people at the beginning, but there's always just one singular driving force behind the company. Let's talk about these individuals. You get inside their heads. Some people have positive self-talk, while others, like me, have negative self-talk. Still? Yes. I just changed this about myself. It is the best ever. I've tried. What about these people you're researching? Do they have imposter syndrome? Do they have negative self-talk, or have they overcome it? Imposter syndrome is an interesting question. I can't think of anybody right now who has imposter syndrome. Do you still have it? Yeah, I feel kind of nervous right now. Why are you nervous? I just am. I was just in a board meeting for the last three hours, and I'm sitting there like, "What am I doing here?" Interesting. One of the greatest pieces of advice Steve Jobs ever got was from Nolan Bushnell, the founder of Atari, who was about nine or ten years older than him. He was his mentor and told Steve, "If you pretend to be in control, everybody else will assume that you are." Steve took that advice and ran with it. So, I don't know about imposter syndrome. What was the other part of your question? Just negative self-talk? Yeah. Jensen, for example. He wakes up every day, looks in the mirror, and asks, "Why do you suck so much?" Do you think that's common? Think about Elon. He says his mind is a storm. He seems to embrace chaos and need some kind of foil or problem, and he seems to get freaked out when things are going well. I definitely think he's got a dark mind. I'm kind of negative inside, but as a founder, I try to project positive energy outwardly. I think it's way more common. The best founders don't sleep on wins. They do something great, celebrate for a day, and then get back to it. I just did an episode on Tony Xu of DoorDash, whom I think you guys were investors in. His whole thing is, "We got so much more to do." He says, "Okay, we had something good happen, let's go out to dinner." But before the dinner is over, he is already thinking of the 17 things that are not going right. That's why he's great. That is different from being super critical and negative. For me, Brad Jacobs, who started eight separate billion-dollar companies, really shifted my perspective. Reading his book and building a relationship with him changed something in me. He has 45 years of experience as an entrepreneur, which is longer than I've been alive. He used to be ruthless with himself, but he realized, "It's not serving me." We were talking about this, and he said, "You love what you do. Why don't you stop?" He told me that negative source of drive wasn't serving me anymore. Now that you love what you do and have found your life's work, your inner drive should be generative. It should be, "Hey, I'm trying to make something that's good for the world, that I love to do, and that I'm proud of." Something clicked. I'm done with that negative self-talk. Now I wake up every day, and I can't believe it. I was texting my team last night when we finished with Ed Catmull, saying, "How is this my life?" But maybe that negative self-talk is what got you to where you are today. But then your fuel source has to change, because it can also destroy you. Fine. I don't want to do this for just 5, 10, or 15 years. I want to do it forever. The people I select to talk to have been doing it for 30 or 40 years. People say, "If you love what you do, you do it for free," but there's another level: "If you love what you do, they couldn't pay you to stop." Those are the people I love. Yes. You brought up something interesting about platform changes. The Industrial Revolution was a huge platform change, and Henry Ford's assembly line was another massive one. When those things happened, did the profile of the founder CEO change? And did the way they ran their companies change? We're going through something now that is probably even bigger. What do you mean by the profile changing? Paul Graham talked about "founder mode" versus "manager mode." Manager mode is like Jack Welch—military-style, top-down, organizational structure. Founder mode is like Brian Chesky—distrustful of conventional wisdom, thinner layers of management, getting right down to the details. Those are the two modes I see CEOs in today, with people tilting toward founder mode. In the Dorsey interview, Jack Dorsey talked about "Dorsey mode," where he gets rid of the org chart and titles. More decisions are made by an AI system in the middle of the company, and people are just in charge of giving context and judgment. Over time, the percentage of decisions the AI makes versus humans starts to flip. I call it Dorsey mode—a new way to build a company with far fewer people than previous generations needed. Did something similar happen to the way they built companies during previous platform shifts? That's a great question, and I actually just asked Michael Dell this. Think about Dell: he started his company when he was 19 with $1,000 in his University of Texas dorm room, deciding to take on IBM, the biggest company in the world. It was an absolute batshit crazy idea. Most people don't know that IBM was the first company in history to hit a $100 billion market cap, holding 80% market share of the entire tech industry. Marc Andreessen told me on my podcast that it was like having ten Googles. We haven't seen that level of domination since. Michael Dell succeeded, and Dell was actually profitable every single quarter for the first 20 years of its existence. I'm always skeptical when people say "this time is different," because everybody in history says that. Michael has navigated many platform shifts and technological revolutions, so I asked him because this current shift feels different to me. He said, "No, this is not like anything else I've been through. I actually think the way to run a company is completely different." Tobi Lütke believes this, too. The options available to you now are completely different because of the amount of leverage. I think the shape of the organization changes, the number of people you need changes, the way you compensate 100x people who are proficient with these tools changes, and the planning cycles change because things move so fast. I actually think it's different compared to when the internet, Google, SaaS, and mobile came along. This is a lot different. There is a great line in "The Almanac of Naval Ravikant" where he says, "In the age of infinite leverage, being at the extreme of your craft is very important." That was written before AI, and AI is just leverage. That's why the premium on focus is so high. Just trying to be the best in the world at whatever you do is going to reap so many more rewards. Being at the extreme edge of your craft is unbelievably valuable. We've already seen that talented people aren't just 100 times more valuable; they are like 1,000 times more valuable. I'm really close to John and Jordy of The Yard, and I'm obsessed with podcasters. When Jordy came into podcasting, he wasn't just twice as good a marketer as the next best podcaster; he was 100x better. If I were OpenAI, I would make him the CMO. A single individual can produce billions of dollars of value based on ideas from a very unique brain. Look at what he did for that show when it was only 17 months old. Everybody is trying to clone them, but they can't clone their minds. You are going to see people like that in marketing, research, and everywhere else whom you should pay 10,000x. Another thing that is different, and I want to press you on this: I was a preacher of focus inside of HubSpot. Every year, I'd say, "We can only do three things. What are the three things, and what are the 50 we are not going to do?" But I've noticed that modern, AI-native CEOs—like Winston from Harvey, Anton from Lovable, and Matti from ElevenLabs—aren't that focused. They are doing tons of stuff, and they are doing it fast. Bezos used to talk about one-way doors and two-way doors. Many things that used to be one-way doors, like going international or starting a big initiative, are now two-way doors. They can press hard for a month, see how far they get, and if they miss, just go back. What is your take on that? Maybe focus isn't as important in the age of AI. I don't know if I have a take on it yet, but I would be very wary. The people you mentioned have built great products, but they haven't built durable businesses yet. We have to see how this plays out. It's still early. Yeah, so that's the issue I have. Maybe those are good ideas, but I'm interested in people who build durable businesses. Personally, I don't want to be acquired; I want to be running my business forever. I've spent a bunch of time with older founders recently—people who are 70, 75, or 80 years old. I've realized that if you sell your main thing, you get out of the game completely. If you sell your best idea and then have two decades ahead of you, you end up just working on a bunch of bullshit. I don't know how many people can found multiple great companies. It's rare. The second-time founder thing is really overrated because most huge successes are first-time founders. What I've seen is that people who sold their generational company or had a falling out are now 75 years old, looking back on what happened 25 years ago. You don't want to be in that spot. My point is that you should be all-in or all-out. Why would you be all-in on your second, third, or fourth-best idea? Trying to recapture that magic is almost impossible, especially at 50 or 60 compared to what you did at 25 or 35. I had this weird sixth sense from having these conversations that their current work is so far below what they accomplished in the past, yet they feel like they have to keep playing the game. If you're out, just be done for the rest of your life. Go on your boat or do whatever. I don't think they're capable of being out. I don't think so either, which is why you don't sell. Be very hesitant of taking startup advice like, "Oh look, this company that raised a bunch of money is working on 15 different products." Let's give it time and see how it plays out.
