It's fashionable in the AI world to say the best founders have great taste. What's your take on that? Looking back through the 420 founders you've studied, is that a common thing, or is it new bullshit? I think taste is very real. I've been a big fan of Rick Rubin, who has built a wonderful career. He started at 18 in his dorm room and is still doing the same thing at 62. People want to work with him because of his taste. I realized he's scary good at making podcasts, too. Am I very, very bad? Is this your way of telling me? No, I wouldn't be here if you were. No, but I realized he took a skill from music and applied it to podcasts. You are a professional listener. When I said that, he lit up. I started explaining my theory on why he's so good at podcasting, and he said, "Stop, we have to record this. This is too good." He agreed that his skill set is not just his taste, but his ability to listen. Most people, when they are talking, are just waiting to respond. He doesn't form opinions or judgment; he is just really interested in people. That's why he is very selective about who he works with. He has very real taste. Is he an exception, though? Did Bezos have taste? Jobs obviously had taste. Did Jensen or Zuckerberg have taste? I think of the Broadcom guy, Hock Tan, who recently spoke about this. His whole thing was that he thinks founders who claim "I saw this strategy coming" are full of it. His company is worth like $1.5 trillion now, and he says there was no way to predict what was going to happen 20 years ago. The opportunity was in front of him, he made a good decision, and then he looked at what he could do next once he got to that next rung. He says that was the actual strategy. There are too many moving parts to predict the future. Be very wary of the echo chamber on X regarding "the current thing." I think that is almost useless to pay attention to, and I don't even think about it. That's what I wanted to ask you about. A lot of the people you see on X today, or whose biographies you read, are being studied after they are already incredibly successful. They've hired PR firms and get advice on how to post. Should founders just be themselves, or should they get help? It depends on who you are as a person. Some of these people are terrible people or terrible communicators, so I would not recommend being yourself. Exactly. Your raw self might suck, so don't be yourself. How much shaping should you take? As a CEO, I remember when we were going public, we hired people to try to shape me. We did a little of it, and I think it worked. Is that a good or bad idea? It obviously depends on the industry you're working in and who you are as a person. Everything comes down to who you are. We have one shot at life. How do you want to live it? That's why I like reading biographies. There are a lot of good business ideas in them, but essentially, every week, I am just looking at how a specific person did life. I want ideas on how I can do life myself. Personally, I am unmanageable. I ran a podcast for 10 years with no employees. What does that tell you? I have a very hard time working with other people because I am very disagreeable and micromanaging. On my new podcast, my team will tell you that I can be ruthless in a nice way. I'm not mean, but I will say, "No, we have to redo that." Are most of the great founders disagreeable? Yeah, for sure. But I don't want to censor myself. A good friend of mine was mentored by Sam Zell for 25 years. I was doing an episode on Sam Zell, and he invited me over for dinner to share some stories. Sam always ran his own money. His company was like his family office, and he made the investment decisions himself on a deal-by-deal basis, with no permanent capital structure or fund. My friend said, "Sam was constitutionally unsuited to manage institutional capital." He was a wild man who was uncontrollable, had no filter, and didn't want LPs calling him to say he wasn't being professional. Sam knew who he was. So it depends. Sam should be who he is, but maybe another founder shouldn't. It depends on the industry and who you are. I don't want to have multiple sides to myself; I'd rather just say, "This is what I believe, and this is what I think is interesting." When people ask how I pick what to talk about or what I am thinking when I read, the answer is absolutely nothing. I read, a line sticks out to me, I underline it, and I write down what comes to mind. Before I record, I go back through the highlights, and if it's still interesting the second time, I put it on the podcast. There's no overthinking; it's all instinct. You are an interesting case because you did 400 plus podcasts on mostly dead people, and now you are interviewing people who are very much alive. They say you should never meet your heroes. I met my hero, James Dyson. Okay, let's come back to that. But any surprises in meeting these people face-to-face versus reading about them in books? Is there whitewashing in the books, and are they different in person? I would imagine so, especially for autobiographies. If you or I wrote an autobiography, we would want to look our best. Even if we didn't lie, we would omit things that make us look bad. Of course. People always ask me, "How do you know the stuff in the books is true?" You don't. But this isn't a history test. If I read an idea in a book—like Sam Walton finding a way to beat back bureaucracy—and even if that never actually happened, it is still a good idea. If I apply that idea to my business, that's all I care about. I don't care if it's 100% true as long as it is a useful idea. It's not a history test. Mhm. What I would say about all the people I've met, they've been unbelievably, like, kind and generous and thoughtful. But I also don't work for them, so who knows? And I'm not in their family, I'm not in their house. It's like a different interaction. I don't think... Do you think people today, the great founders, are different than the ones of 100 years ago? No, no. Same, same personality type. Same phenotype. Yeah, same phenotype. What else is in common? Like, what kind of personality traits? Yeah. So, obsession, high level of disagreeableness... Talk about that. So, somebody comes in to pitch at Sequoia, let's say, and some people are very charming, and some people are a little disagreeable. I think it's just very natural to want to do business with the very charming person. Is that a bad instinct? I've done like 15 episodes on Steve Jobs and read almost everything I can find on him, and I learned something about him that I never knew yesterday from Ed Catmull. Ed said that Steve fired two people off the Pixar board, and he fired them because they never disagreed with him. He was like, "If they don't disagree with me, what value are they giving me?" He wanted you to fight back and to be... But he was disagreeable himself. Are they all disagreeable? Rockefeller had very advanced social skills, I would say. Okay. Did they all have advanced... No, no, no. But Rockefeller's an interesting thing because there are people that worked for him for like 40 years who said they never heard him raise his voice and he never said an unkind word. That's crazy. And then, even the way he would arbitrate disagreements between him and his partners was essentially like... there's this one thing where they wanted to invest like $3 million into some kind of new technology that I think would transport oil faster, or refine oil faster, something like that. And there was a huge disagreement on the board, and Rockefeller sat there quietly and let them fight. And then, he said, "I believe in this. If you guys don't want to do this, I'll put up the money myself. If it works, Standard Oil can pay me back. If it doesn't, I'll eat the $3 million." And his partners were like, "Well, if you can take the risk, we can too." And that's how they agreed to do it. That's not disagreeable, that's something else. Yeah. It just depends on the person. But yeah, there are these traits: that obsession, focus, cost control—obsessed with having the lowest costs. They see that advantage compounds over and over again. You don't see that with the new technology founders at all. I think it's a great disadvantage. Yeah. Micro-managers—what Paul Graham calls founder mode—that's not new at all. Mhm. I'm a huge fan of his essays. But when that essay went viral, and people were like, "Oh, we're bringing this back," I'm like, "This is a new thing?" This is not new at all. Interesting. At all. Nothing is new in this at all. What is new? I think what we talked about earlier, like what Jack Dorsey was saying, what Michael Dell was saying, that... Yep. ...I do think, I mean, Tobi Lütke, when I talked to him, you know, he said that, "We're going to look back on 2026, we're going to look back on this year in history as like the year that every single business was up for grabs. That you can rebuild almost every single business, AI-native, and have a massive advantage." And, I think that's an interesting thing. When you look back at all these folks, how many of them had, like, great, well-balanced personal lives, great families, had like a really deep life beyond, between the lines? I think three of them. Out of all of them, only three? Yeah, yeah. I think Ed Thorp, who is a very fascinating character, created the first quantitative hedge fund, invented the way to count cards for blackjack, phenomenal investor. But the fact there's only three... Yeah, three. Him, Sol Price. Do you know who Sol Price is? No. So, Sol Price is the most influential retailer of all time. Sam Walton said he took more ideas from Sol Price than anybody else. Jeff Bezos took a lot of ideas for Amazon from Sol Price, so he knows who he is. Bernie Marcus literally got the idea for Home Depot from Sol Price. Jim Sinegal of Costco literally said, "I didn't learn a lot from Sol Price, I learned everything I know from Sol Price." So, he created all these billionaire retailers. The other one I would say is Brunello Cucinelli. But, that was his autobiography, and I heard that may or may not be true. Advice to founders—so I spend all my days with founders trying to build trillion-dollar companies. Advice for them, on their lives? I don't ever give advice. Why not? I'm asking you for advice right now. For you? Let's use me as a proxy. I think people are going to do what they want to do. And so, they'll seek out useful information, but I think these people that go around—not you, but like, anybody that just goes around trying to identify what's going on in somebody else's life—I think it's almost like relatively useless. I think people literally do what they want to do. I talk to a lot of founders. Now, there is this thing where they find it very useful to have like a conversation. They kind of like dump what's going on in their company to me, and I kind of run it through everything I've read, and maybe I come up with something interesting. But I wouldn't consider it advice. It's just like, oh shit, Rockefeller ran into that situation, this is what he did, or Ford did this, or this guy did that. Is it possible to build a trillion-dollar company and have a very well-balanced life like Ed Thorp? I don't even pay attention to the public markets, there's 10. There's 10? There's 12 including like the Middle Eastern oil companies. Okay, let's just take the American companies, there's 10. Any of those guys well-balanced? There's one I will say about them that I thought was interesting. Only two of them are divorced. Most of them were married before they were famous, and stayed married. And most of them married a spouse who was kind of an intellectual equal. They met them and they were very accomplished on their own. And so they started out with an intellectually formidable partner very early and stuck with them. I thought that was interesting. Of course, like Larry Ellison's had four wives, like... I think six now. Okay, but... He's doing some heavy lifting for these other guys. But I thought it was interesting that they all got married very early to peers. And how about through history? In history, they're just like they're terrible husbands, like divorce, like just not good. I would not consider them, in many cases, bad fathers too. I see. It's a very difficult thing to do. I remember I just finished reading Phil Knight's autobiography, Shoe Dog, for the third time. I love it, I love that book. And think about what he says at the very end. You know, like, he had two sons, one of whom died tragically in a drowning accident, and he was like trying to reconcile this as a grown 75-year-old man at the time, and he's having a hard time understanding this. Like, this push and pull: "I wanted to spend more time with my sons, but if I'm being honest, my biggest regret is that I can't go back and do it all again." And in Sam Walton's autobiography, you know, he sacrificed a lot of time away from his family to build Walmart and he knows he's dying. He's writing that book when he's riddled with cancer and he's like... Did he regret that? No, that's what I was getting to. And he's like, "If I did it all again, I would do the exact same thing I did." I'm very skeptical of these people that get to the end of their lives and are like, "Oh, I have this regret." Because I think this personality type, it's almost like a compulsion. If they played out life again, they'd want to do it the same way. I talked to Jeffrey Katzenberg about this, who's very interesting, who swears he has no talent. He just throws hours at everything. I was like, "Jeffrey, you're obviously fucking talented." But he was telling me about this. He was like, "Listen, the biggest thing with your kids is like quality of time, not quantity." He's just like, "Don't do it where like you're on the phone, or you're like distracted. Like, if you only have 10 hours a week with them, make those the best 10 hours ever. Do a ton of planning with it, like make it so they'll... it's so memorable with them." I thought that was an interesting idea, like a recommendation. But look at the people like the AI war starting now, this is a game of kings. I met Demis Hassabis. He just did an interview on Fortune where he was just like his schedule is he has like his thinking time from like 10:00 PM to 4:00 AM in the morning, right? Then he goes to sleep, then he works a full day at the office the next day, then has dinner with his wife and kids, and then does his thinking time again. You think he's balanced? He's got two workdays. Yeah. Okay, you studied all these people, I spend all my time with Sequoia founders now, advice to them: how to become iconic, that someone that you would read their book and do an episode on them. I think the best founders, like, don't need advice. I'm very skeptical of this narrative that like we back and discover the greatest talent. The way I would put this is like Michael Jordan said something very interesting. He was at the end of his career and Kobe Bryant's coming in, they're like, "We discovered that, you know, Michael Jordan, we discovered the next or the next Michael Jordan through Kobe Bryant." And he's like, "This will happen again, there will be another me." And he goes, "First of all, you didn't discover me. I just happened to come around." And I think the best founders are like that. Like, they're not discovered, they make themselves known. And I'm highly skeptical. Like, these are forces of nature. Again, go back to Mike Moritz, the forward that he wrote in the Return to the Little Kingdom. In a paragraph, he can describe what a founder is. He's like, "These are irrepressible"—this is the line he says about Jobs and the best founders—"they're irrepressible forces of nature. They don't need your advice. They will find information, and if they want it from you, they will seek you out. They will find you. But going around like, 'Oh I would, you know, paint that a little different or I would do it a different way,' it's like they've got it." Yeah, I think you're mostly right about that. I don't proactively give advice. If they ask me, I give it to them. And I think they're good about sort of ranking it in their head and like, "I'll take that and I'm going to throw that away." And to the degree like I am somewhat helpful in this domain, and I'm trying to be, all I'm trying to do is like, "Hey, I'm going to go and read these obscure books, there's a gold mine here of all the past entrepreneur knowledge, no one's fucking doing anything with it." "I'm going to, like a goddamn demon, go and mine this for you. I'm going to make it into a podcast that you can listen to in 45 minutes or an hour while your eyes are busy doing something else, and hopefully push this information down to the next generation of entrepreneurs." I think that as a service is a life well-lived, when I get to the end of my life. It's like they'll figure it out, they'll know what to do with it. Like, I don't overexplain shit. You listen to Founders, like I don't overexplain shit. It's like, "This guy did that." And even when they do ask me, I'm like, "I don't know, man. Ford did this way, this is the way this guy did this. It's like, you pick what's right for you." One of the things that I think is interesting about you is there's actually not that many patterns. Like of the whole episode, kind of pushing on patterns. It's not... I would say it's not formulaic. There's patterns, there's no formula. Any other patterns we haven't hit? I think just a deep caring and love of what you're doing and the problem you're solving. In some cases, it's like... Let me give you an example. Ed Catmull talks about the very first time he met Steve Jobs. The very first time he met Steve Jobs was before Steve got kicked out of Apple the first time. You know what Steve would talk about for an hour? He would just use the word "insanely great products" over and over again. It was still... it was important to him when he was 19, when he was 25. It was still important to him when he was dying. He just was obsessed with insanely great products. No founders ever talk about, "I want to make a lot of money." How much do you think people being motivated by money matters, or do you think they whitewash that out with "We want an insanely great product"? I don't think small egos build big companies. And therefore, this is what I actually think. I don't think small egos build big companies. I think all of these people have giant egos, but I think some of them are just better at hiding it. And I think that what motivates most founders is not money, it's control. And if you maintain control, and you build a business or product that makes somebody else's life better—that's all business is, a business is just an idea that makes somebody else's life better—so if you maintain control and you build a product that makes somebody else's life better, you wind up with the money anyways. Yeah, it's a side effect. Yeah, they know that they're going to get the money. Yeah. I can't think of... I mean, Rockefeller... You don't think they're born very motivated by money, they're... they have large egos. By the way, I looked through those 10 CEOs, the founders of the American companies, in general, there's this narrative that they all grew up poor. In general, they were from upper-middle-class suburban families. That surprised me, especially in the tech industry. Yeah. But Bezos was a son of a single mom, like, and his dad dipped out, like... But mostly they were suburban upper-middle-class families. Yeah, like Gates, Zuckerberg... Gates was, Zuckerbergs, yeah, mostly. Yeah. All right, any advice for life advice for me? I'm on my second act. I think Dana White... there's like a simple genius to the way Dana White runs his company that I really admire. And he told me something funny, he's like, "I've never read a single book on business, I've never listened to a single podcast," which is hilarious. He said this because we talked a bunch after, and his thing was just like deeply know who you are. Like you've got to know for a fact, and he's like, "I've known who I am for exactly who I am for a long time," this is what Dana was telling me. He's like, "Understand deeply who you are, understand deeply what you want to do in this world, and then once you figure out those two things, you just wake up every day and get after it." And I think there's like this simple genius to that. Do you think when he started UFC when they bought it for $2 million—2 million with an M—that they were going to do... there was such a thing, streaming didn't even exist, right? Didn't even exist. You stay in the game long enough to get lucky, that's a maxim he repeats over and over again. And you think he could have predicted back then he was going to sign an $8 billion streaming deal? I like that maxim. Yeah, stay in the game long enough to get lucky. You guys are jumping around too much. This is what kills me about these founders: they have a million different ideas, they're like, "I'm going to start, scale, sell." Okay, then what? Everyone's like, "What's the best company that you can think of right now?" They're like, "Oh, SpaceX." Elon founded it when he was 30, 25 years ago, he didn't jump around. Like, he did other shit but he didn't let go of that thing. All the value is in the future. This is, I think, really important. But I think back to Dana's point, it's just like spend a lot of time understanding who you are as a person and what you want to do in the world, and then from there then you just wake up every day and opportunity handled well leads to more opportunity. And so Dana handled that opportunity well, couldn't have predicted he did an $8 billion streaming deal, right? And then he goes, "What do you think my next deal is going to look like?" Obviously, a lot bigger. Yeah, I think that's hugely important. Okay, I want to thank you not just for coming on the pod, but I've been listening to your content for a long time and as a CEO, it's super helpful. It's super helpful too, I'm sure, to tons of CEOs out here, so you're the man, I appreciate you, thanks for coming on the pod. Thanks for the invite. Thank you for being David Senra. Appreciate you. Hope you liked that episode with David. He's a very interesting cat, very knowledgeable. I had three takeaways from it. First is he talks a lot about focus. I think it's beyond that, I think it's obsession. And this may be me, but I think in general at Sequoia, we look for founders that are obsessed with the problem. We look for people who have gone very deep down a rabbit hole and been obsessed with it earlier in their careers, and then today, when they're building the company, to be very deep down that rabbit hole—a very deep founder-market fit. Like, that's a common trait of the best founders that he's interviewed, and the best founders that I've had on the show, and the best founders that I coach. It's just a deep, deep obsession, "the ability to mute the world and build your own." I really liked that quote. The second aha, this is kind of depressing: it is very, very difficult to have good work-life balance as a founder. He went very deep on 400 founders, only three had what he thought was a very well-balanced life. This isn't great news for me. I didn't have a great, balanced life. I didn't have a lot of free time when I was a founder, and I don't know a lot of successful founders that did. So, if you're thinking about starting a company and becoming a CEO, be careful. It's a lot of work if you really want to do it well. The last piece that I was curious about was this idea of imposter mode. I have imposter mode—I did when I was a CEO big time. I was always a little bit nervous. I liked what David had to say about it. I liked even more what Brian Chesky has to say about it. When he was early in Airbnb, he kind of led from a position of fear. He had deep, deep imposter syndrome, and Brian has done a lot of work on himself and now he leads from a position of love. I'm trying to be more like Brian. Like what I love is helping CEOs and founders like yourself go on that journey from early stage startup founder to big behemoth legendary scale-up CEO, and I'm trying to lead from a position of love versus a position of fear. I recommend you do the same. Hope you liked the episode, you can follow me @BHealy_ on X and we can chat about it more.

Palantir CEO Alex Karp on Tokenmaxxing & Taste

TBPN(117.0K)·23:32·12.6K views·2026-06-04
Alex KarpCEO, Palantir
TL;DREnterprise AI is currently trapped in an addictive, superficial cycle of "tokenmaxxing" that fails to deliver true return on investment (ROI).
). To survive and thrive, companies must move past generic LLMs, inject "taste" into their specialized software architectures, and...

TL;DR

Enterprise AI is currently trapped in an addictive, superficial cycle of "tokenmaxxing" that fails to deliver true return on investment (ROI). To survive and thrive, companies must move past generic LLMs, inject "taste" into their specialized software architectures, and actively engage with political realities to avoid a populist backlash and eventual nationalization.

The Illusion of "Tokenmaxxing" vs. True Enterprise Value

  • The Tokenmaxxing Trap: Much of current enterprise AI usage is characterized as "tokenmaxxing"—trivial, repetitive tasks like categorizing emails or generating infinite dashboards that feel productive but act as "tool-shaped objects."
      • Alex Karp describes this behavior as highly addictive yet fundamentally unproductive, comparing it to "rearranging deck chairs on a personal Titanic."
  • Limitations of Frontier Models: Standard Large Language Models (LLMs) are "magical" for generating probabilistic, free code or basic dashboards, but they are completely inadequate for managing complex, specialized business operations.
  • The Role of Knowledge Stores: To solve high-value business problems, LLMs must be anchored in structured knowledge stores and precise physical processes rather than general public clouds.

"Taste" as the Differentiator in AI Deployment

  • Taste Plus Money: Solving complex industrial and defense problems requires more than capital; it requires "taste"—the unique ability to identify which specific business workflows are valuable to optimize.
      • Specialized Processes: Examples of high-value domains requiring "taste" include specialized oil drilling methodologies, supply chain re-platforming, and combat intelligence.
  • The Security Dilemma: High-value enterprises, intelligence services, and agricultural operations cannot simply upload proprietary data to a public cloud.
      • Vulnerability Resolution: While LLMs can identify security vulnerabilities at 10x to 100x speed, patching these vulnerabilities on-premise requires specialized structures (like Palantir's "primitives" and "ontology") to keep proprietary data protected.

Competitors, Copycats, and Market Dynamics

  • Validating the Market: Karp welcomes the emergence of competitor companies (often referred to as "deploycos") that attempt to copy Palantir's codebase or ontology structures.
      • Off-Balance Sheet Sales: Competitors act as external sales resources by growing the overall size of the market, as institutional clients are hesitant to buy from a market with only one vendor.
      • Setting the Standard: The proliferation of inferior copies establishes a clear baseline of comparison, driving serious, high-value clients back to Palantir's tried-and-tested architecture.
  • Polarization vs. Dislike: Unlike frontier AI tech giants that are broadly disliked by the working-class public, Palantir embraces being polarizing. The company has tens of millions of global fans alongside vocal detractors, rather than facing unilateral public resentment.

The Impending Threat of AI Nationalization and Regulation

  • Sleepwalking into Nationalization: Karp warns that the domestic tech industry is severely underestimating the risk of being nationalized or aggressively regulated by political figures who do not understand basic economics.
      • The Myth of Tech Likability: Industry titans often ignore this warning because they believe their financial success and perceived "likability" shield them from government intervention.
  • The Anti-War Position of Citizen Involvement: Karp advocates for rebuilding cohesive national structures. He argues that involving all tiers of society (not just the working class) in national security decisions would naturally lead to fewer, more deliberate conflicts.
  • The Danger of "Chillaxing": Sensible leaders on both the left and right must stop "chillaxing" and actively participate in regulatory discussions before a populist political backlash strips tech companies of their autonomy.

Workforce Impact: Upskilling vs. Politically Dangerous Layoffs

  • Upskilling the Frontline: When implemented correctly, advanced software makes frontline employees significantly more valuable.
      • For example, vocationally trained, high-school-educated soldiers are successfully executing complex tactical operations using Palantir's software on the battlefield.
  • The Political Risk of Corporate Rhetoric: CEOs who publicly boast that AI allowed them to lay off two-thirds of their workforce are "playing with fire."
      • Karp warns that using AI as a scapegoat for layoffs is highly explosive and will inevitably trigger severe legislative consequences and support for populist manifestos.

Notable Quotes

"Business problems can never be solved... purely with money and just spending more. It is taste plus money. You can’t scale the taste of what is the business problem you want to solve, and need to solve, at the end of the day." — Alex Karp, CEO of Palantir
"Enterprise people—I have a secret way of selling. Don't even come talk to us. Go spend two days with a frontier company, and if you're lucky after you're done, I'll let you in my door. They're clamoring." — Alex Karp, CEO of Palantir
"If you run around saying AI allowed you to fire two-thirds of your workforce... you might as well just go sign up for Bernie Sanders' manifesto. These things are very, very explosive. The American people sense that there is something dangerous here, and when people are playing with that fire, that fire is going to consume us." — Alex Karp, CEO of Palantir
Yeah, get in here close. This is good. Feel good. Feel good. Okay, how's it going? How's AOC Con this time around? What's changed? Well, we're in a phase. Each one of these things marks a time. First of all, you guys are even more baller, more successful. Some tendies in your pocket? I think it might have been thanks to you. We've got to say thank you. You know, it's like I blew us up. You were one of our biggest guests. You're looking bigger and stronger somehow. Thank you. Hey, are you more attractive in your personal life now, randomly? Well, here's what we're actually focused on: dead hangs. And so you came on last time, you said your dead hangs are around, like, five? Oh no, it's—well, it's plateaued in the last couple months at... Okay, so we—the thing is, like, people are going to hear that, they're going to think, hanging on a bar, five minutes, hanging on—whatever. You've got to go and do it. The audience has to go and try to do it. We've started doing it. We're still in the—under two minutes, I think. Yeah, between one and two minutes. Somewhere around minute thirty, you feel like your tendons are going to rip. Well, dead hang is very respectable. Two minutes is super elite. It doesn't feel respectable when you have that five-minute number and you're looking at the timer. Strength matters. Now, the thing is—I don't want to go into a rabbit hole on training—the single biggest mistake people make is they try to hang every day. You need recovery. It's like anything else. So if you want to mimic and get progress, you just do what I do, which is once a week you hang as long as you can—doesn't even have to be super macho—and then that's your day. So like, let's say you can do... But multiple sets? No, no, you—one day a week you do your maximum. Max. Wow. So like, let's say you could do two minutes. You try to do at least... You fight to get to... but you don't fight to get to two minutes. Got it. That's your dead hang. And then you can basically fuck around the next day, do whatever you want. Don't overdo it, but you could do two times one minute with a long break. And then you just screw around, do less and less and less. Two days before, if you hit a two-minute dead hang, you do like four times fifteen seconds. The day before you take off. And you do that, just keep doing that, and your—the mistake people make is they hear my baller time and they're like, "Got to come for the crown." Fuck that guy, I'm going to—I'm going to—I mean, the mistake you're making is not doing a course. This could be a whole new revenue line for you guys. You could be like, "Hey, call in." The Karp Method. So, I mean, the dead hang is—it's like—and also some of it's just genetic. Like, my other metrics are elite, but this is somehow alien territory. God-given gift. What about—what about breath hold underwater? I don't do that, and I'm not sure I have a—like, I grew up swimming. I think I'm weaker at that. Like, I bet I would be in your guys's range. I'm a divemaster. I can hold my breath for three minutes. I'm just saying—I'm just saying—so I think you'd be—I think you'd be crushing me on that, honestly, very much so. But you have like the lung capacity of a whale. I mean, you've got—like, you guys are like—and if I'm not moving, I'm not using any oxygen. Oh my god. It's like you're like a—like a—like a whale floating out there under the ocean, waiting to surface. True. So, I'll say—I'll tell you the difference. And this is—oh god, they're always minding me out there. But like, okay, when we first met, it was like AI, maybe real. Okay. Then I would say somehow until about two weeks ago, there was like a "holy fuck, this is real," but somehow it's not working, but we're not allowed to say it publicly because we'll look stupid. And then there's a lot of investor hype. There still is, like investors printing tendies. So you have the investors on one side. I think people realize it's real, but, you know, it's like—you know, you have the whole tokenmaxxing. And people are onto that, and then so there's a whole value lecture there. You have a political situation where, you know, people who do not understand basic economics are winning the political argument. So you can—we could talk about where AI is going. Yeah, there's a lot here. Let's break it—let's break it up. Let's start with the tokenmaxxing thing. Let's start with what's—what's real. How are you actually thinking about deploying AI or ROI? Well, first of all, what's—what's Palantir's philosophy around token consumption? Well, like, we—okay, we have a product that will allow you to basically—I mean, internally it's called something, but externally—but really, we call it the demasturbatory—like, get-off-masturbation thing internally. Sure. It's like, people are just like—sitting there all day, kind of like a porn addiction. And enterprises are like, okay, we knew this—we believe this will create value, but we cannot have people just like some people checking the weather with it, just like—and just rearranging deck chairs on their personal Titanic. It is—it is literally like porn. Like, people are like—full-on—because it feels—it feels so—yeah, tool-shaped objects, right? Tool-shaped objects, you're looking at it more than you want. You hope no one notices. You're kind of before dinner, after dinner. It feels productive to have every email classified with tags. Here's—here's what it comes down to. Like, business problems can never be—I mean, sometimes they can be solved purely with money and just spending more, but very often— Actually, I think it's the opposite. So just to give you a weird—no, and I was going to say, very often it's more the opposite where it's about figuring out the right way to do something and then you can use capital to fuel that process. But— Well, let me give you a thing that's too generous for you guys. Okay. It—it—it's taste plus money. Okay. Yeah. And there is no—like AI, like if you look at—to pick any issue you want to talk about, token mechanism—mechanism maxxing, what's going on with deploycos, are other people going to build ontologies, why are—why—why does our political class not understand AI, especially in Europe? It's like, yes, because all these things can be scaled in a very valuable but largely going to commodified way, but you can't scale the taste of like, what is the business problem you want to have to solve and need to solve, at the end of the day, whether it's in the—whether it's the Ukrainians fighting, the Israelis, commercial entities? It's—there's somebody sitting there who's like, "Okay, but this problem is valuable, this problem isn't." And once—that value—that problem always, almost always but not always has attributes. So there are some problems you could solve with this, like "I want to write a report on GDP growth in China," right? Okay. But if it's a problem that requires a knowledge store—like, "I want to understand this specialized way I underwrite" (we're going to have a guest here), "I want to understand the specialized way I drill for oil and gas that's both legal, ethical, and reduces the cost of production," "I want to change the—the—the supply chain of my industry," whether that's military or whether that's building boxes or whether that's cars—these things require actual, precise, ongoing processes. They are enhanced by large language models. They are not replaced by large language models. And then you get to security issues. Like, for us, like the whole mythos things is just a boon, because like, yeah, we could take a—any model, their model, OpenAI model, open model, we can identify—we can now identify vulnerabilities at like 10, 100x. Yeah, but then who patches them? How do you patch them on-prem? How do you patch them on-prem so that your specialized knowledge stays on-prem? Like, if you're any business or Intel service, a lot of these things are very similar. Sure. Like, you're not putting your classified data in a public cloud. Same thing if you're like—you have a special way of farming soybeans. Well, you're not—so it's like, how do you have—how do you keep—so all these problems are exposed, identified, and then you always have a thing of "where's the charisma?", which people really underestimate. And it's—it's not global. There's no global charisma now. Like, so right now, the large language models are very—frontier companies are super charismatic with investors. I'll give you some news: they're super not uncharismatic with enterprise and the people. Like, in a way—even with enterprise? No, no, I mean, it's like—enterprise people—I have a secret—I have a secret—like, every company has a secret way of selling. You know what my secret way of selling is? Don't even call—don't—don't come talk to us. There's a frontier company. Go spend two days with them. And if you're lucky after you're done, I'll let you in my door. They're like clamoring. They're like—they're like, "Hey, I'll take your bad brand," which we have a great brand in enterprise. But like, it—it—it's like—it's like secret knowledge because the investors love this. They're like, "Hey, my stocks are all up, everything is up." I mean, Palantir's done very well where—like—but it's like—and you know, you guys are doing very well, I imagine, right? And it's okay. We're—you know, we can—but I'll tell you what. You go down the street, you talk to a Marine, you talk to a bus driver, you talk to the person who owns the bus driving company. They are not happy. Do not like these people. They're tired of people tokenmaxxing. It looks like masturbation at their—at their—that costs them money. They—they—they're like—and honestly, then you have something we're not allowed to talk about in this country: likeability. Like, at Palantir, we have—I think we have like 50, 100 million global fans. We have like five million people that wake up in the morning figu—literally calling me Satan. I didn't know I had that kind of warm hand. But, you know, it's like—that's what they believe. Yeah. And they—and they really believe it. Okay. What people are not allowed to really address is like, we have fans and enemies. Yeah, yeah. These people are polarizing. Yeah, we're polarizing, which means both sides. These people have one side. They're just—it is—so it's like—you—and then it's like—it's a really— Social media companies, too, have the same problem. Everyone uses them, but no one likes them. Yeah, the—yeah, but then they also live in the circle, and that circle is printing money. So it's like, you know, when you look in the mirror and you just printed a lot of money, you look pretty fresh. Is part of it—is part of it that some element of the technology—let's just say LLMs—is so magical that the companies involved, the companies that are making and selling frontier intelligence, can be bad at a bunch of other things and still grow? Well, no, no, no. They are magical at a certain kind of thing. Allowing you to write, for example, code. Now, that code doesn't—can't be used as a knowledge store. So if you look at code in like three different ways, like just using Palantir as a model, we have code that's basically infrastructure. So what are the Ukrainians using, what is the Department of War using, what do a lot of our enterprises—the—we call that primitives. It's basically hard-coded things that—that understand the world the way you do. It would take millions of technical hours and an understanding of all these enterprises to do it. So it's much more like, how do you build a steel beam? Then you have, like, code that is written by FDEs. Okay, so that's kind of managed. It's—the reason why FDEs work, the secret is, it's actually managed in something that we as a product—so you're writing to a codebase, we're managing that, we're increasing our product. It's not just random people writing. Then you have, let's call it, free code. That free code is—that's magical. Like, you can do it very quickly, it's almost right, it doesn't have to be exact. Dashboards. Dashboards, financial stuff, probabilistic stuff where you just have to get—it was magical. By the way, it's magical not only create—and it's magical in a way—and I know people don't like the porn thing, but it's also addictive. It's like, you know it's not good for you, but, you know, it made—it led to damage. One more dashboard. One more time, it can't hurt that much. I know my doctor says I shouldn't do it. But it's like—it's like that, right? And you just keep— And like—and if you're involved in the—that thing, you're also making money. And then, last but not least, in certain circles, like if you have—you want to be a researcher or you believe, essentially it's a religion. So, like, you know, and like one of the things is very charismatic especially to people who've never had a religion, because all of a sudden that hole in your heart that was yearning for—I don't know, I would say, you know, a—a fixed religion, Judaism, Christianity, Islam, is like being filled. Yeah. And then all the answers are there. But it—it—it's very, very successful at doing things that a company has to do. But it is not actually solving the problem that enterprises are—it is—now, it can solve them, that's the trick. It's not—it's not binary. It's not like you can't say they're not value—and they're totally putting our business on steroids. Like, without LLMs, nobody would be talking about our ontology, about Apollo managing security exploits, about our ability to manage an enterprise essentially as turning all these companies into FDEs. These deploycos, we love them, because now every company wants a deployco. You know how you do that? You replatform on Palantir. And like—and it actually works. It's not somebody with no taste who's never done enterprise, who has no earthly clue how these things work, who's done something else and is like just imagining they know how to do it, right? Yeah, is part of this moment quite entertaining for you because you guys have been working on understanding businesses at a deep fundamental level, creating—you guys have effectively been doing the work that people are promising AI could do for 20 years now, but actually doing it, finding all the really rough—finding all the really rough edges, and—and—not—and being at a point where you don't have to oversell the technology, you can sell both things? But now there's maybe—here we go, we got it together. Now there's maybe—oh, it's the wrong side, that's why. Flip it around. There you go, living the brand. Hopefully we got that, I don't know, the rest of all this stuff wasn't recording yet. I—I trailed off. But—no, I understand. But—but is part of it entertaining to you that it feels like, you know, Palantir has always been in some ways—not had competitors, because there's nobody with Alex Karp running a company that does what Palantir does besides Palantir. But at the same time, there's been tens of billions of dollars deployed now to effectively do what Palantir does, but just selling the intelligence part, not selling all the underlying kind of infrastructure that you guys built? Well, they're doing two things. They're selling—they're trying to sell the intelligence part and they're trying to pretend if you just hire a bunch of people and let them run around they're FDEs. Now, the—the very cool thing is when you've been in your basement doing your thing and everyone kind of used it as the freak show, it—it's really interesting and—and—and great to have adoption. The pretty ironic thing is half the people adopting now don't even know they're copying. But now, the copying thing helps and hurts. Where it hurts is in the beginning it puts clutter in the market. Yeah, and there's—there's no doubt about it. Where it helps—and we saw this with defense tech, honestly. So like, in defense tech, we were the only people, we were the first people despite what's—as I—I love these—partly in other podcasters, they're interviewing people who are parroting things I said 20 years ago, they don't know it. And it's like, "Oh, that's so insightful." It's like, "Yeah, of course it's insightful, Karp said it 25 years ago." And like—but it's a—but so that kind of—that part is super weird. But and like—but—but—but what really happens and we see is—is like, it expands the market. So like, in defense tech, we would not be doing this well in just purely in government unless there weren't 50 companies that were doing similar things, because then the people are like, "Okay, first of all—" You view it as like off-balance sheet sales resources. Where other people are basically making the same—well, it—well, it—well it's off—well now that's the large—it's—they do two things. They increase the size of the market because de facto nobody wants to finance underwrite a market where there's only one person. Sure. So like, if you're the one person, the percentage of the defense budget you can get is much smaller. And two, they set up a comparator. It's like, "You know, you may not like the freak show. Okay. If—like, but have you noticed the people who are serious buy it?" And then then it changed—and then three, it changes the standard. Now, what you're seeing now is like that times 100x. Yeah. And it does change like recruiting, retention, and like how you build a company, and we're—we're always think—you have to think about how to—being dyslexic's a huge advantage there because like you don't have a playbook and now things—you need things to shift, and we're doing that. The—the central thing though that is just cannot be developed even if you understood the playbook. A lot of these things are like appear like a—it's like, you know, LLM code appears like Palantir code but isn't for a deployed thing. Appears like Palantir, it isn't ontology. You could theoretically copy parts of it but there's essentially structures that are built deep into organizations that we own. And by the way, it would take you three years. And then three years, we're in a completely different world. But there is this magical thing called taste. Like, in the end of the day, the reason why you guys have done so well, it—of course there's aptitude and diligence and showing up and all those things. Yeah, but you have to be able to differentiate between two people who are in business, one of whom is saying something that sounds weird that is insightful, one of whom is parroting stuff that sounds weird and that's all they're doing. And a lot of people—very few people can do that. And it's give you the same thing, like the enterprises that succeed, there is a taste arbiter. And at Palantir, we have taste arbiters. We have taste in every product, taste in every deployment, taste in every casting. Who puts the people there? How do you put them there? How do you organize the thing? Our ontology then does that technically. How do you manage the whole org with taste? Who should be in charge? What data sets should come in? What—what are the ways in which you protect? What—what should you push into the public crowd? What should be on-prem? What—what should—I mean, leaving aside the law and like wars, war ethics, what do you want to protect? What should you protect? What should you not protect? Because quite frankly, you want that to be out there so you can get more data. All those things are orbited by taste. And then you have to have the credibility of having taste. That's a real problem for a lot of these places because they don't have—they're popular with their friends, they don't—they really don't understand how unpopular they are in enterprise. Like, they think it's like—"Oh yeah, it's—" like the way I think I have a problem with like professors at Columbia. It's like, no, it's a real problem. Like, they think I'm Satan. And, you know, it's like—I—I—I think, you know, we grew up in the same community, let's talk about Heidegger. They're like, they don't want to talk about Heidegger. So it's like—it's like—yeah, and so that's just a—it's a weird—it's going to be a super— The one thing I would say for anyone listening: if you're listening to this and you're chillaxing and not active—and I'm not saying you have to agree with me politically or anything—there—the—like, partly because of this dynamic and very self-inflicted because—I—I'll tell you—I—I can't name names. I called many of the titans of this world and like started this six months ago, like every couple days, we're going to be national— You called them every couple days? Like some of them I'm like, "Yeah, we're going to be—" I mean, you know, it's like, honestly, they're like the—they find me very entertaining. Like, I'm not sure—like, so they call—because yeah, it's like, yeah, this is going to be entertaining. You're going to pick up—all right, so anyway, so I've been telling them for six months, six months: "We're going to be nationalized. We're going to be nationalized." And they're like, "Why would anyone nationalize? Never happened in America. It's never—why would anyone nationalize us? We're so likable, we're creating so much value." I'm like, "Okay, I'm not going to debate that. I know how likable I am. I'm—I'm not going to tell you how likable you are. But I am telling you, and you know the momentum on this is on the side of people wanting to nationalize." Now, we don't get our act together and figure out ways we can say, "Hey look, there are problems here, we're going to deal with. These things are not going to—yes, they are going to create opportunities. You have to talk openly about how these things are valuable because we have adversaries. You can't just say these are—" all that stuff. So the primary risk, honestly, to Palantir and a lot of these other countries is, and then it's going to be nationalized. Before nationalized, it's going to be regulated by people who don't understand this. And now, they'll tell you in private, "I'm working on this, I'm d-d-d-d-d, and this lobbyist," it's like—not going to work. So like, that's something—like if you're listening to this and you're like, "Look, you know, you don't have to agree with me on all my proclamations," I got a lot of—by the way, there's some people who think I'm saying we should have a draft. Too lazy to read. I'm just saying we should—like, in a world where everything is changing, everything is changing, don't we have to find some communal structure to remember we're American? You know, like my—you don't like my idea of like, "We all do a week in the park"? Great. Come up with some other idea. Well, we can't have no idea. You know, and then they're like, "Well, I'm saying I do not want a draft, just to be explicit," they're like, "Oh, that's pro-war." No, honestly, you know what most of our wars are fought? Because no working-class person is making a decision. You start making sure everyone is involved in everything, I'll show you how few wars we fight. It's actually the anti-war position. But anyway, disagree with everything. If you're—we have on the right and on the left people, people who have no earthly clue what they're talking about, right and left. All they're talking about is how much they hate us. And those of us who are sensible in the middle, you know, too many of us are chillaxing. Like it—oh—like nationalization, it can't happen. America would never do that. Sleepwalking into—and you guys have tendies to protect now. You guys should be on the front line of this. Like, you've got full on—oh, I'm sorry, I have a—I have a full-on very impressive corporate leader coming on. So I've got to turn it down. Last question, last question if we have time. How are your conversations going with Fortune 500 CEOs around headcount planning? There's been so many layoffs this last year that people were saying, "Hey, we're getting so much out of AI, we're able to, you know, cut back here or there." People inside tech often know—like, these maybe there's just reduction because there needed to be a reduction, the org got bloated, maybe they do need to fund some AI initiatives. Or it's a declining business model, they're getting out-competed by someone or the business just doesn't have momentum. But how are those conversations going? What does it look like? Like the—like, I talk—by the way, I talk to Fortune 500 companies, I talk to unions, I talk to soldiers, I talk to fire—it if you upscale somebody, they're more valuable. Sure. And like, all these—whether it's people working on batteries, people driving trucks, people, corporate leaders—and again, this is where I think we have to be very careful to be more disciplined on the corporate side. Like, if you run around saying AI allowed you to fire two-thirds of your workforce and you did it because maybe your competitor is kicking your ass, that could—that is a real—like, you might as well just go sign up for Bernie—Bernie Sanders' manifesto. And part of the thing here: they really believe that can't happen, so they're free-riding on the fact that it could. Like, we have—and it just cannot work anymore. These things are very, very explosive. The American people sense that there is something dangerous here, and when people are playing with that fire, it's—it's—it's—they assume the fire won't burn their hands. We're—that's not the world we're in. That fire is going to consume us. And what we see, again, the warfighting example is just the most neutral—not for everybody—but like, the soldiers at the bottom have gotten much more valuable. Where—and I don't even just mean the special operators, which obviously they're in a different league, but like every—the people doing a lot of the operations now are doing our product, they're high school vocationally trained. You see this everywhere. The—the modern enterprise is going to have, like we have a—a true—like he—very, very smart, person coming on and it's like—you're going to have a very smart executive. He's much better at hiding it than I would be if I were him. But that's—you can talk to him about that. But and—and and then very talented, creative people with taste, all up and down the stack. Anyway, I think this is time for me to— I think this is time. Thank you so much. Great to catch up. Always fun.

How a reasoning model cracked an 80-year-old math problem — the OpenAI Podcast Ep. 20

OpenAI(2.0M)·41:18·9.5K views·2026-06-04
Alexander WeiReasoning Researcher, OpenAI Hongxun WuReasoning Researcher, OpenAI Lijie ChenReasoning Researcher, OpenAI
TL;DRAn OpenAI general-purpose reasoning model has disproved Paul Erdős’s 80-year-old unit distance conjecture.
This breakthrough, driven by "test-time compute" (allowing the model to think longer before responding), highlights how AI reasoning can solve major open scientific...

TL;DR

An OpenAI general-purpose reasoning model has disproved Paul Erdős’s 80-year-old unit distance conjecture. This breakthrough, driven by "test-time compute" (allowing the model to think longer before responding), highlights how AI reasoning can solve major open scientific problems and act as a powerful catalyst for human-AI collaborative research.

Cracking the Erdős Unit Distance Conjecture

  • The Conjecture: Formulated by legendary mathematician Paul Erdős, this 80-year-old problem in combinatorial geometry asks for the maximum number of point-pairs that can be exactly one inch apart in a set of $n$ points on a plane. Erdős conjectured that arranging the points in a square grid was asymptotically optimal.
  • The Disproof: The OpenAI model proved that the square grid is not optimal and that a far superior arrangement is possible.
      • Methodology: The model made a highly creative leap, bridging two historically distinct fields by applying *class field theory* (an advanced branch of number theory) to a problem in combinatorial geometry.
      • Significance: This was a central problem in discrete geometry, carrying a historic $500 bounty from Erdős. The resulting proof was described by researchers as "journal-publishable" and far beyond International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO) difficulty.
  • A Generalist Success: The breakthrough was achieved using a general-purpose reasoning model (the predecessor to OpenAI o1), not a specialized model trained solely on mathematics.

The Power of Test-Time Compute

  • Inference-Time Scaling: Unlike previous AI models that responded instantly, reasoning models utilize "test-time compute"—allowing them to think, execute Python code to test hypotheses, self-correct, and search through different reasoning pathways before outputting an answer.
  • A Direct Correlation to Accuracy: The researchers noted a distinct scaling curve. When given more time to think, the model's accuracy on the Erdős problem rose significantly, eventually reaching a ~50% success rate under high compute budgets.
  • Blistering Pace of Progress: Just 10 months prior to this breakthrough, researchers were debating whether AI could win a gold medal at the International Math Olympiad by 2026. The model achieved that milestone in June 2024, and Olympiad-level math is now considered "in the rearview mirror" of AI capability.

Verification and Immediate Community Impact

  • The Verification Process: Researchers Hongxun Wu and Lijie Chen first ran the problem on internal models. After the model self-verified, they handed the proof to math researchers within OpenAI (including contributors Matb and Maxelki).
      • Initially skeptical, the human mathematicians spent four to five days searching for bugs before concluding the proof was entirely correct.
      • The model's output included a highly complex, 125-page chain-of-thought process.
  • No Scaffolding Required: The model generated the proof entirely natively from a direct prompt, without the need for external logical scaffolding, steering, or specialized theorem-proving languages like Lean.
  • Immediate Scientific Acceleration: Within a week of OpenAI sharing the result, external mathematicians digested the model's novel methodology and successfully used its intuition to disprove the *sum-product conjecture for real numbers*, demonstrating an immediate multiplier effect on human mathematical output.

A New Paradigm for Human-AI Collaboration

  • Empowerment Over Displacement: The researchers emphasize that AI will not render mathematicians obsolete. While AI is highly effective at creative problem-solving and connecting distant mathematical ideas, humans remain essential for synthesizing these results, building new frameworks, and formulating high-level theories.
  • Methodology for Researchers: The team shared key strategies for scientists looking to integrate reasoning models into their workflows:
      • Trust Calibration: Lijie Chen recommends "exponentially doubling your trust" in the model month-by-month until it fails, then stepping back slightly, to discover the true limits of its capabilities.
      • Avoid Over-Decomposition: Rather than manually breaking down a problem into smaller steps, researchers should prompt the model with the end-goal directly. Human decomposition often introduces personal biases and blind spots, whereas the AI may find a completely novel shortcut.
  • Broad Scientific Horizon: The team expects this reasoning paradigm to rapidly accelerate other fields:
      • Cryptography: Stress-testing the mathematical foundations of security protocols to prove safety or find hidden vulnerabilities.
      • Quantum Computing: Using AI to discover more efficient quantum error-correcting codes, accelerating physical implementations of quantum hardware.

Notable Quotes

"Everyone had a hard time sleeping because it's so exciting... This is something that can be published in the best journal of math. We knew this day was going to come, but never knew that it was going to be reality so fast. It's like living in a dream." — Lijie Chen, Reasoning Researcher, OpenAI
"I think the reaction was very positive. I think people really get a sense that the frontier of AI today can really come up with research output that many human mathematicians would be proud to achieve." — Alexander Wei, Reasoning Researcher, OpenAI
"You could be an astronomer and not use a telescope, but you kind of have to ask why... We do math not just to enjoy the pleasure of problem-solving, but also to advance the field and to understand the truth we're looking for. Using AI is going to speed that up by a lot." — Hongxun Wu, Reasoning Researcher, OpenAI
Hello, I'm Andrew Mayne, and welcome to the OpenAI Podcast. On today's episode, we're speaking with Alexander Wei, Hongxun Wu, and Lijie Chen from the reasoning research team behind a recent math breakthrough from an OpenAI model. They'll tell us the story behind the discovery and what stood out to them about the reaction. "Everyone had a hard time sleeping because it's so... so exciting, yeah. Okay, this model is something that's really amazing. I mean, this is something that can be published in the best journal of math. Maybe this is the one in 100 times where it's too good to be true, but it's, it's actually true." Lijie, tell me what you work on. Oh, I work on reasoning with Alex. Okay. How did you find your way into reasoning? Last summer, Alex had this breakthrough in like IOI and IMO. You know, I used to be a participant in IOI, and then I was like, "Oh, that's crazy, you know, a model can already win medals, and gold medals." At that time, I was an assistant professor at UC Berkeley. But then, I was thinking like, maybe I should try to rethink my career. It seemed like making models smarter will have—maybe have—some bigger impact on the world. And then, I just kind of had a conversation with Alex back in last October, and then I got super excited about this thing, and eventually I just joined OpenAI. We hear IOI and IMO come up a lot. Alex, do you want to unpack those for everybody? So, IMO and IOI are these two competitions for high schoolers. These stand for the International Math Olympiad and the International Olympiad of Informatics, respectively, and these are just devilishly hard math problems. You get two sessions for each of these exams that are like four and a half to five hours, and you just have to do three problems. So for a long time, these were sort of an implicit, like, grand challenge in AI. Like, when will we be able to get models that could perform as well as the best humans on these exams? That was a pretty interesting starting point, I think, for measuring the success of the model, and we're here to talk about how far things have gone since then, which is pretty incredible. But how did you find your way into reasoning? So, I did my PhD in ML, and towards the end of my PhD, I got excited about this idea of spending more compute at inference time to solve, you know, harder and harder reasoning problems. At the time, I was playing with GPT-3.5 Turbo in the API, and I didn't really get any interesting results. But there was this team at OpenAI that seemed to be doing something pretty similar, and so I got super excited about it and was lucky enough to be able to join. So, probably the simplest way to describe that is like, inference time is basically letting the model think longer about it. Yes, that's right. So basically, before this era of test-time compute, models sort of answered immediately—like, right off the cuff, without thinking. What inference-time compute, test-time compute does is you now give the model a chance to think and improve its answer, and, like, try different things before having to finally output something. That obviously just helps make the model smarter; it lets them do things that they wouldn't otherwise be able to do instantly. When you started to work on reasoning, did you have any idea of where you wanted to see this go? Like, what your expectations were? Were you looking at it purely from, "Hey, this is very cool from an academic point of view," or did you have some sort of other vision? I think for me, the draw of reasoning, when I first got excited about it, was that this was something that models just obviously can't do right now. So, this was like end of 2023, start of 2024, when models were struggling with grade school math problems. At that time, it was just like, can we just get these models to do something reasonable on math at all, let alone have them be much, much better than I am at it? I remember, on my first day at OpenAI, Noam Brown asked me what I thought about when models would get IMO gold. That was just a benchmark we talked about, and I think at the time, a lot of people even within research thought that IMO gold was out of reach this year, but maybe, like, 2026. I felt like, you know, I had an idea that if we just pushed for it, maybe we could do it by April. It took until June to get a really good model, but then IMO rolled around, and, you know, we were able to get gold. I think zooming out, this happened a lot faster than I expected, and it's crazy to me that progress since then has kept up at this same sort of blistering pace. It was just 10 months ago, but it feels like the IMO level of problem is far in the rearview mirror of AI today. Oh, Noam Brown asked me the same question—I mean, not about IMO gold, but about whether and when a model can solve P versus NP. I think P versus NP might be something quite hard, because I think the reason is that for solving P versus NP, you would need to build a new theory. You would need—maybe you have to write many books of new ideas to get there. So currently, it seems we are still far from that. But you know, maybe who knows what will happen in the future, so yeah. Hongxun, what do you work on? Oh, I also work on theoretical computer science. I was also collaborating a lot with Lijie in my PhD. I was at Berkeley, and I remember when, like, o1 came out, I was talking to my advisor, saying, "Oh, there's no barrier in a model solving math problems anymore." I think he just smiled, and maybe he knew that he was going to lose a student. Oh, wow. Oh. Yeah. So, let's talk a bit about that because it's an interesting point. As you said, it went from the model just having a moment to try to figure out the answer, then all of a sudden, you've given it the ability to spend longer and to think about it—you know, reasoning. And the results have come pretty quickly and, I think, surprised a lot of people. You had a model that was able to basically disprove one of the Erdős conjectures. Can you explain that just a little bit? Yeah, so our models last week were able to produce a proof—or a disproof, rather—of the unit distance conjecture due to Erdős. This was an 80-year-old open problem in the field of combinatorial geometry, where basically, the question concerns if you have n points, let's say on a piece of paper, how many of them can be exactly one inch apart? How many pairs can be exactly one inch apart, and how does this number grow asymptotically with the number of points on the piece of paper? This wasn't a trivial problem. When Erdős put this together, the idea was just to say that it could, like, I think ideally it had to be only done on a plane or something like this. But there was, you know, the idea that maybe there was no better way, and this has been out there because it's a very interesting problem. The fact that a model solved this is pretty profound, and also, this model was a general-purpose model, correct? Yes, that's right. So, Erdős's original conjecture was essentially that the optimal solution to having as many distance-one points on the plane as possible was to arrange them in a square grid. What the model proved was that the square grid was not actually close to optimal at all, and that you can do much better with a different construction using a lot of high-powered number theory. Hongxun, how did you choose these problems? I guess we didn't really choose the problem. What happened was we wanted to test the upper bound of our model's capability, so we just used a select subset of Erdős problems to test the capability of the model. I would love to know, first of all, who was the one that hit enter and asked the model the question? I guess both of us—like, me and Hongxun. You guys at the same time, like, pressed? Yeah, maybe. I think what happened was actually we were testing, like, two slightly different internal models, and we both saw some correct solutions. It was really, really exciting for us. How did you know that it worked? Of course, you first ask the model to check it. Okay, but of course, you know, models sometimes are not reliable, so... Yeah, you got it, it's good, don't worry about it. Yeah, so then, after we checked with the model and it seemed plausible, we just asked a bunch of our mathematics friends in the company—you know, Matb and Maxelki. At first they were like, "Oh, there's no way this can be true, this is a major open problem." But after they thought about it for four or five days, they couldn't find any mistake. Then they became more convinced, and eventually they were like, "Actually, this may be correct." Yeah, then, like, everyone had a hard time sleeping because it was so exciting. What was the conversation like when you started getting people saying that this was accurate? For me, I was not that surprised because, when Matb first said—well, what happened first was Matb said this is definitely wrong. But I actually knew that he probably just spent, like, five or ten minutes looking at it, so in my heart, I didn't really believe that. But later, he told me it's 50%. I was thinking, "Okay, if we extrapolate the trend, then maybe next night, it will be 100%." So, yeah, I guess it was a little bit dreamlike, but it also felt a little bit natural that this model would do something amazing. Later, it just became more and more real that this might actually be correct, and that this might actually be a big deal. It's the first time the model can publish something that's getting into, like, top math journals. We knew this day was going to come, but we never knew that it was going to be reality so fast. It's like living in a dream. I mean, this is something that can be published in the best journal of mathematics. This is way beyond, like, the IMO level. So, I would expect something to happen at some point, but maybe not this May, yeah. One of the things I think we've seen emphasized at OpenAI is that OpenAI doesn't try to train to specific benchmarks. Instead, OpenAI tries to build really good, general overall models. Sometimes people say, "Well, we just try to build a generally smart model, and we find these things along the way." When it comes to reasoning, it's the same thing—something that's really good at reasoning overall, you find these capabilities. Does that ring true for you, or...? Yeah, I think for this model in particular, it's one that all of us have also just used, like in lieu of the current model in CodeX, and it works quite well as just a general-purpose model. Having the capabilities to do this Erdős unit distance result—I think people will be able to do this at home in the near future. It's been exciting to see people react to this and pay attention to it. We went from a very short period of time ago where people said models weren't good at math, and now models are doing this. What have been some of the more fun things you've seen online, or reactions from people? Ever since we announced the result, my friends in TCS started to ask me to try it on their open problems, including my advisor giving me like two or three open problems to try on. I think the reaction was very positive. I think people really get a sense that the frontier of AI today can really come up with research output that many human mathematicians would be proud to achieve. And I think it's really great that we're able to communicate this—that this is the frontier of progress—to the rest of the world. I've seen people make these designs trying to sketch out the model's construction. If you plot it on a grid, it's actually this very pretty, symmetric, geometric design. Yeah, and I was thinking maybe we could try to make one of the designs, put it in a frame, and put it on our desk to celebrate this moment. Yeah, I think it's going to be fun when we start seeing things like tiling problems and other stuff where we can actually just look at the artifact. It will be neat. So, we've been hearing more about Erdős problems lately, and some seem like they weren't as challenging to solve as perhaps people thought—they just needed some attention. Yet this one seems to be a little bit more complicated. Where would you rank this? I guess he proposed like 1,000 questions or more, right? Erdős's problems are just a collection of all the problems he asked. Some problems he offered some money for a solution, and some problems he just noted. For this problem, he offered, I think, $500, which is from last century, so it was a little bit... yeah. And also, this is one of the central questions in this field of discrete geometry. It has been heavily discussed by mathematicians in many discrete geometry papers, so it's one of the questions people have thought about a lot and really want to see the answer to. I would say this is more like a major open problem in a concrete field of mathematics, instead of just another Erdős question which may have been something he asked after a lunch. So, how do you collect that $500? Did it disappear when he passed away? I think there's a special agency for that, but usually people just frame the check. Yeah, so maybe we'll just frame the check in Sam's office, I don't know. Yeah, yeah. How do you feel this proves that reasoning is effective? Well, I think the biggest proof is that if you look at the plot in the official blog, if you give the model more time to think, the accuracy on this problem grows faster. If you give it a lot of time, it can get almost 50% correct. So, more thinking, more correctness. I think that's really a proof of reasoning being effective. But Alex, going back to this, this isn't a math model; this is a model that can do a lot of different things. Do you see a correlation where, as these get better at solving things like mathematics, they also get better with other general problems? The hypothesis, at least, is that this model was not trained specifically for math. We just had this new model and wanted to take it on a test drive, essentially. So we evaluated it on some very challenging math problems just to see what it can do. When you go through the proof and you look at what it came up with, what were the things that surprised you? What would you describe as creative? For some context, the proof is well above my own mathematical pay grade, but at a high level, my understanding was that this idea of taking class field theory and applying it to problems in combinatorial geometry hadn't really been done before. Though, some people knew there could be this bridge between these two fields. Being able to do that and execute it requires, first of all, to make the connection, which requires quite a bit of insight and creativity. And then to execute the proof is also a very delicate, careful affair that very few people would be able to do. I think the most surprising thing for me is that you tell it to do something, you go to have lunch, and when you come back, you see that it actually does much better than you thought. At that moment you feel like, "Okay, this model is something that's really amazing." So going back to GPT-3.5 Turbo and looking at a model that was doing automatic, instant inference, to now a model that's able to do incredible mathematical proofs: Is it using tools? Is it using Lean? Is it using other things like that, or is it doing it purely inside the model? For this particular case, the model is basically like CodeX. It can code, it can look at websites and find information, so it's basically a general ChatGPT setup. ChatGPT can also write Python and execute it, but I don't think the model wrote any Lean. I think Lijie has a story about the Cambridge dictionary. Oh, okay. So, the first thing the model did when it got to the website was check what "unit" means in the Cambridge dictionary. It's a little bit ridiculous, yeah. So it looked up the word "unit"? Yeah, it wanted to make sure it had the absolute correct understanding of what a unit is. Have you seen it do other things like that where you say, "Oh, it's trying to ground itself to make sure it understands the question?" Definitely. A lot of the time in the model's answers, it will actually explain the definition again to show that it actually grounded the definitions. As people who are very knowledgeable about computer science and mathematics, is it intimidating to all of a sudden see this happen? I think it should not be intimidating. I think it should be empowering instead. Okay. After the proof actually came out, mathematicians first improved the bound it proved, and second, they used the intuition and motivation of the construction to knock down other open problems as well. So, I think the trend is going to continue. A model can make a good breakthrough on some very hard questions we don't know how to solve, but then how to digest that idea and how to use that method for other good things—I think humans still have a role in this. So, what do you think the role of somebody working in mathematics is going to be like five years from now? I think there will be a lot of AI and human collaboration. AI knows a lot, right?
