They can connect distant ideas, but humans can also think for longer. Currently, it seems AI cannot build a new theory for math. But once humans have the help of AI, they can just grab all the ideas from different fields of math. I think it can empower humans way more, yeah. Do you see this working into other fields? Are we going to see discoveries in physics? I can't speak for physics, but we're all researchers in AI, and I think definitely for me, my day-to-day looks completely different from when I first started doing research in this field. I think so much of my work is now done by coding agents, so I can just do so much more. That's been a sort of magical feeling—that we're really starting now to feel like we can use AI to build AI faster. How much has AI changed the way you do these sorts of things? I think it has changed completely. Even when I joined half a year ago, I was hand-coding, looking up Slack channels for directions. But now, the default is just to ask CodeX. I ask CodeX to do a lot of things, and then I just go to lunch or go talk to people. The work has completely changed. Lijie, how do you explain this to your friends who are trying to understand what this means and how it's going to impact other fields? I have some mathematician friends and some friends in other fields. The way I want to tell them is that some people may be afraid AI will replace them, that AI will just replace mathematicians. But I think it's really about empowering every theoretical researcher, because AI has this advantage of knowing so much stuff and connecting things, connecting things. Currently, it seems like problems hard for humans may not be hard for AI, and that's a really great thing. We can use AI to solve those problems, get new ideas, and then we can digest them and make new discoveries, just like Hongxun said. So, I think some of them get very excited about this. Of course, currently it's only on math, but I believe because it's a general reasoning model, all theoretical researchers can benefit a lot from it. I think the dream world will be one where everyone has access to top-level reasoning ability, so all researchers can use it to discover whatever they want. Basically, OpenAI will accelerate science a lot because we are empowering every scientist to accelerate science worldwide. I think that's our mission. So, if I was a researcher, how would I get started? What advice would you have? Like, "Okay, try this first." We'll start with you, Hongxun. Get a ChatGPT Pro subscription, of course. It's really much better than thinking without Pro, because it thinks longer. And try to ask the boldest question you can ask. I had the experience that sometimes I tried to decompose the problem into smaller problems and ask the model, and it turned out that it was not as good as just directly asking the question, because my decomposition was not the best way. Why do you think that was? I think because, as humans, we have all kinds of priors on how problems should be solved. They are very helpful in reducing thinking time, but very often the priors are wrong and there are blind spots. AI models sometimes can just surprise us by discovering these hidden things. When I spoke to Alex, he talked about treating it like a graduate student. You know, not talking down too low, but not too high, but at the right level so you could just understand that it knew the terms and could work for you. Alex, how about for you? What advice would you give somebody who's a researcher who wants to try to figure out how to be more effective with this? Yeah, I think a lot of it is actually learning to trust the model, and figuring out how far you can go in trusting the model. You also have to learn what's beyond what the model can do, because if you don't have a sense of that, you don't maximally use the full capabilities of the model. I think Lijie has taught me a lot about how to use these tools better. I feel like I'm sort of a dinosaur in some respects in terms of adoption, because I started working at OpenAI well before these tools existed. I think I have a lot of old bad habits where I don't trust the models enough; I still think it's like the models of six months ago or something. That's an interesting paradigm. Okay, so Lijie, what advice would you give? Oh, I have this method where every time, you double your trust in the model and see when it fails. If it fails, you just go back, and if you do this every month, then you can quickly get to the point where you can maximally trust the model without breaking your stuff. And apparently, for the last five months, it's been going really, you know, exponentially. Back in the GPT-3 days, I had a list of tests and things like that I would do, and I'd watch them sort of incrementally get better. Then GPT-4 came, and then by the time o1 came out, I had to throw it out because those were just toy problems at that point. I feel like I have to continuously adjust and keep trying bigger and more complicated things. Do you think that for somebody who's in mathematics or in a related field right now, who's feeling a little bit concerned by this, they should be taking a more optimistic approach? I think it's legit to feel concerned, especially when a lot of the field is problem-solving oriented, because models are going to be really good at problem-solving. But mathematics is really much more than problem-solving. It's more about understanding the structure and building new theories, like Lijie said. I think we should try to figure out how to better use the model to help us in solving the problems that we meet, and then try to accelerate the speed at which we build new theories and come up with new understandings. I think that's the more optimistic view. When CodeX becomes much better, it can do so many more things for you. You'd expect to work less because CodeX is good, but somehow you actually work more because there are way more things you can do. So, I actually hope this can happen for math as well. The model becomes so good, and the mathematician has ten ideas, and you can ask the model to try them and see if one of them succeeds so they don't have to do tedious calculations by themselves, right? I would imagine that what happened with coding can happen to mathematicians. It's interesting too because, when we talk about the Erdős problems, Paul Erdős was a very interesting person who found a lot of things curious and said, "Oh, this is neat." We have this category of problems he put together, but there's not a lot of rhyme or reason to them. They were just things that he found curious or worked with other people on. I think that's a big thing that's neat about science in general. Often we think that there are these really specific hierarchies, but literally it can just be things we're curious about. That being said, how long before there are no more unsolved Erdős problems? Some of them are very, very hard, so yeah, I don't know. Do you foresee us, maybe Alex, needing to come up with a new category of problems? I think probably the hardest problems on that list—which includes things like the Collatz conjecture—are problems that feel very, very far out of reach of the mathematical technology of today, even though many of them are quite simple to state. So we'll still have more things to work on and continuously move things, which is good to know. It's exciting, though, to start to think about what happens when you start applying this to other areas like physics and astronomy, looking at datasets and seeing what kind of discoveries are going to be in store. Do you have any particular area that you're hoping to see? Oh, I hope it will solve P versus NP. Okay. How about you, Alex? I think the next milestone in my head is really AI that can do AI research. I think there are so many unsolved problems here where, in many ways, we are limited by all the limitations of our own intelligence. I'm optimistic about having AI broadly available as a technology, because there's just so much more demand for intelligence in the world than humans can supply. Lijie? Oh, I wanted to say P versus NP too, but Hongxun said it. So, I guess beyond that, one concrete thing I'm very interested in is: Currently, it seems AI is trying to combine ideas from different fields, and of course in a very novel and sophisticated way, but can AI actually generate completely new ideas from scratch? That's something we haven't really seen concretely in AI, and that's something I want to see happen next. That would be very cool. Have you seen traces of that yet? I think so. Even in this Erdős problem, if you look at the chain of thought, which is 125 pages, I think some of the thoughts are pretty creative, although they didn't work out. I mean, the final idea is more like combining old stuff, but some of it has creative thoughts. But it's interesting. You know, early on, arguments were like, "These models aren't creative." But you can give them two ideas that have never been connected before and say, "What's the relationship?" That would be something very new and feels like something different. I feel like we'll probably be seeing more of that. Do you see us coming up with new forms of mathematics? I think that actually will be further down the line, because... Next year? Maybe. I think because models now are very, very good at coming up with some idea to solve a problem, but they are not good at proposing a completely new, different kind of math, or proposing a new theory. How to get them to do that is still very, very open. How I would think about it is, we see this Moore's law for the time horizon at which these models are effective, and I think you sort of feel that in math. Every few months, the amount of time these models can work independently doubles—at least the amount of human-equivalent time. So, for solving problems, if you're really good at it, maybe some problems actually have pretty short paths to the solution and you don't need to take that long. But I think for inventing new ways of doing mathematics, that's much more like a years- or decades-long process, and so I think it'll still take a bit of time for that exponential to get there. This was done by an internal model that you guys worked on, and since then, o1 has been able to do the same thing, and we've seen other labs say they've been able to do this as well. But this was several weeks ago, which is now ancient history. What have we seen since then? I think one difference between the original result and the follow-up findings is that, for the original model, there was no scaffolding needed. You sort of just asked it to do the problem, and then it gave you the answer. You can read the original prompt and response in the note we uploaded on the blog, whereas I think the follow-up efforts have had a little bit more structure or steering of the models. But I think one interesting data point here is that it's really all about test-time compute scaling. After we initially solved the problem—and this is the plot Lijie brought up earlier—with enough test-time compute budget, the model is able to solve the problem around 50% of the time. So, it's not surprising that you can get there with other methods as well. But I think what's really important here is just that as you pour in more test-time compute, you get better results. It seems like it's a virtuous cycle, where you take today's model, give it more compute, let it solve, and understand how these points can be solved. Next-generation models can learn from that and just get more and more efficient. It basically seems like it just scales forever, right? What do you think we're going to see by the end of the year? What I want to see is people using our model to discover lots of new stuff, not only in math, but also in all of science. Of course, OpenAI wants to do some cool math stuff, but I think it would be better if everyone can use the model to discover their own science. And I would expect many mathematicians will use the model—not completely reliant on the model, but collaborating with the model—to discover a lot more math results. I think that would be really cool. Hongxun, I've talked to some mathematicians who are very reticent to even try using AI in mathematics. What is the best argument you can give? I think I'll just show them the proof—the disproof of the unit distance conjecture. I think it is just about productivity, right? We do math not just to enjoy the pleasure of problem-solving, but also to advance the field and to understand the truth that we're looking for. Using AI is going to speed that up by a lot. It's going to tell us what we are really struggling to find, and it will be hard to resist using AI at some point. You could be an astronomer and not use a telescope, but you kind of have to ask why. Yeah, exactly. I know one of the researchers here likes to watch computers play chess against each other, and it feels like he sometimes learns things from that. Do you think that we'll learn to be better mathematicians or researchers or scientists, or just thinkers in general, by watching the solutions the models come up with? Looking at the 125 pages of thinking is probably not very helpful for a mathematician. But just by looking at the answer, you actually do learn some idea that was not there before—that you didn't know before—and that inspires later mathematical works that knock down other problems. So, I definitely think people learn; mathematicians learn something from AI solutions. Some of the mathematicians we asked to review the proof, together with their collaborators, actually used the idea to disprove the sum-product conjecture, but for real numbers. I think that's one very good example: AI can crack down important questions and give us ideas that we can apply elsewhere. Yeah, and I think it's remarkable that this group of mathematicians has already, just in the span of a week, used it to disprove this result, which is of similar importance to the unit distance conjecture. This is a wonderful example of mathematicians seeing this, using it as inspiration, and bringing the ideas to bear on a different problem. What does this mean for the mathematical community? I think for us, when we do these experiments, we want to make sure we empower the academic communities we interact with. We don't want to just go to some community from the outside, try to solve a bunch of their problems, and give them a bunch of AI slop. What we really want to do is make these tools available to researchers and let them direct all this AI test-time compute at the problems they think are important. I don't think we should be viewed as running a race to solve as many Erdős problems as we can, but more like we want to make people aware that the technology is out there and this is what it can do. You're not trying to solve every Erdős problem. Yeah, I would not say that is our goal. This just happened to be a particularly significant result that we thought would be important to share with the world to show the capability level of models today. But it's really not the goal to just go through the list as if it were a race. Do you foresee things applying to cryptography? There's also some debate about whether these models get so good that we kind of surpass even where quantum computing goes, which sounds kind of crazy. Yeah, I think cryptography is really an important topic these days because the foundation of cryptography is really about some problems—like factoring—being hard to solve by computers, but basically, we only have conjectures. There's no mathematical proof of this fact. And suppose the model gets really good at algorithms. Maybe they will prove some of the cryptography conjectures and say, "Okay, those protocols are actually secure; we don't have to conjecture them to be secure." Or maybe they'll find some loophole, which is also very important, because we need to make sure the foundation of our security is good. The model can stress test the foundation of the cryptography to make sure we have better security. What about quantum computing? I think that's a very different territory. Actually, I used to study quantum computing—my first paper was on quantum advantage, which showed that for some tasks, quantum computers can do better than classical computers. But so far, the models are just classical computers. They do what humans can do, maybe a bit better. But quantum computers can sometimes do fancier stuff, like simulating quantum effects in chemistry, which we probably... okay, I'm not an expert on that, but it's unclear. They are just two different paradigms, so I'm not super sure how they compare to each other. But I think AI is going to greatly accelerate the pace at which we develop quantum computers. In recent years, there's been improvement in things like error-correcting—like quantum error-correcting codes that only use simpler types of operations—and that really speeds up the physical implementation. So, I expect more of this to come from collaboration with AI, where AI can propose new quantum error correction algorithms, and then we can develop quantum computers much faster. Once you ask a model to solve a question, you can, of course, follow up with, "How did you solve it?" or "Can you explain this part of the proof to me?" Then the model will patiently try to teach you how everything goes, line by line. It's actually not just one-shot problem-solving; you can ask it follow-up questions to learn how the proof works, and I really like that. One thing you learn very quickly as a researcher is that if your results are too good to be true, you probably have a bug somewhere. I think every researcher has had experiences where they see amazing numbers from their experiment, and it turns out the experiment was actually wrong and the numbers were wrong. When I first heard about this from Lijie and Hongxun, that was my prior. I was like, "Oh, I'll wait for them to find the bug." But then, as the days went on, you sort of have this growing optimism that, maybe, this is the one in a hundred times where it's too good to be true, but it is actually true. Well, gentlemen, thank you very much. Yeah, thanks so much. Thank you so much.

Fed's Daly Says Forward Guidance Could Be Misleading

Bloomberg Technology(727.0K)·14:45·228 views·2026-06-04
Mary DalyPresident, Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco
TL;DRFederal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly remains highly bullish on AI's long-term economic potential despite a current lack of macro productivity gains, while cautioning that elevated economic uncertainty makes near-term interest rate forward guidance potentially misleading.

TL;DR

Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco President Mary Daly remains highly bullish on AI's long-term economic potential despite a current lack of macro productivity gains, while cautioning that elevated economic uncertainty makes near-term interest rate forward guidance potentially misleading.

AI Adoption, Productivity, and Financial Stability

  • The Productivity Lag: Widespread, transformative productivity gains from AI have not yet materialized in macroeconomic data.
      • Litmus Test Timing: Businesses are actively transforming their internal processes, with many citing next year as the true "litmus test" for realized return on investment (ROI).
      • Beyond Tech: Daly is particularly bullish on non-tech sector investments. Examples include machining companies training AI on 50 years of technical blueprints and robotics firms optimizing shipping logistics.
  • No Financial Stability Risks: Despite high valuations and market enthusiasm, Daly does not see current AI investments posing systemic financial stability risks.
      • Capital Sources: Much of the current capital expenditure is funded directly by mega-cap technology companies (the "Magnificent Seven") rather than highly leveraged bank debt.
      • Systemic Spread: For a market dip to trigger a financial stability concern, it would need to spread directly to consumers, businesses, and the banking sector—which is not occurring.
  • Differences from the Dot-Com Era: While San Francisco is experiencing enthusiasm reminiscent of 1996, the current AI wave is far more pervasive and integrated into core business operations than the dot-com boom.

Monetary Policy and the Risks of Forward Guidance

  • Misleading Guidance: Daly warns that providing explicit forward guidance on interest rates right now is "misguided" due to an unusually high number of economic crosscurrents.
  • Geopolitical Disruptions: Geopolitical tensions (such as conflicts impacting oil and fertilizer prices) present ongoing inflation risks that could counter the disinflationary potential of technology.
      • Oil Projections: Despite current volatility, the futures market projects oil to stabilize around $80 a barrel by the end of the year.
  • Policy Readiness: Current monetary policy is well-positioned to react flexibly to economic data as it evolves in either direction. Daly’s top priority remains returning inflation to the Fed's target.

Labor Market Stabilization and Hiring Caution

  • Stabilized Conditions: The U.S. labor market has stabilized following supportive policy adjustments made late last year.
  • AI-Driven Hiring Hesitancy: Businesses are displaying extreme caution regarding adding headcount.
      • Agent Evaluation: Rather than aggressively hiring, firms are actively evaluating what tasks AI agents can perform first to avoid overhiring and subsequent painful layoffs.
      • Skill Alignment: Companies are pausing recruitment to better understand the exact skill sets they will require once AI tools are fully integrated.

Capex Bottlenecks and Fed Modernization

  • Infrastructure Capex: Massive capital commitments for data centers and electricity grid demands are causing short-term bottlenecks in sourcing generators and infrastructure equipment.
      • Disinflationary Timeline: While these projects create localized price competition today, they build the physical infrastructure that will ultimately lower operational costs in the long run.
  • San Francisco Housing Supply: Rising regional housing costs and local displacement are driven by a severe lack of housing supply, rather than excessive demand or speculative bubbles.
  • Fed Institutional Leadership: Commenting on the transition to new leadership under Chair Walsh, Daly emphasized the Fed's dual mandate as fiduciary stewards of public trust and public funds.
      • Safe Technology Adoption: The Fed is exploring safe, highly regulated methods to adopt AI in its own operations to increase efficiency without compromising security or human judgment in monetary policymaking.

***

Notable Quotes

"We haven't seen widespread productivity gains yet, the ROI is still to be developed, but I'm definitely seeing the enthusiasm, and it's picked up tremendously in the last year." — Mary Daly
"Right now, policy's in a good place. We are prepared to respond either way, whatever the economy brings. But I think giving more forward guidance about what's possible could be misguided in the end because we just have to wait for the economy to evolve." — Mary Daly
"They are interrogating how much AI can do for them before they hire... They want to be patient on their hiring and make sure we're not overhiring." — Mary Daly
Mary Daly, extraordinary the data that you now have to lean upon trying to understand what's fact, what's fiction, what's Utopia, what's... what's sensationalism. Who do you go to for your... for your data on whether productivity is working? I go to the businesses who are using the technology. Because you can always encounter enthusiasts or doomsayers, but it's really the people in the middle, as you said, who are using that technology, thinking about it. And I've seen tremendous interest in it last year, and now I am seeing tremendous investment in it. Thinking about how do they train their workforces to be AI-ready, how do they think about what AI can do, not just in the back office, but in the front of operations. And we're seeing this in small businesses, medium, and large, in global companies, and more regional ones. And importantly, in everything from agriculture to machining and building things to services. And I think that's really the place... we haven't seen widespread productivity gains yet, the ROI is still to be developed, but I'm definitely seeing the enthusiasm, and it's picked up tremendously in the last year. We haven't seen the productivity gains yet, you know, you're being very clear on that. As you remember, I asked you over and over again, for... for an hour, please show me the productivity gains. But in the economic data, whichever set of data you want to look at, do you see an impact from AI, negative or positive? It's really hard to take... we have had productivity growth that's been outside of the historical norm, and I think that's a positive for the US economy. Everyone wants to say that's AI. What I think of it as is as sure, it's possible that businesses are looking for cost savings, and they hire fewer workers and they do just as much because they're using, you know, an LLM assistant to help. But we just haven't heard from businesses that they're seeing transformative, ongoing productivity gains yet. And they want to always underscore 'yet'. And then I say, 'Well, what's the time frame?' And they said, 'Next year, year after.' Because what we know is it isn't just about getting a model and using it for things, or an agent. It's about transforming your business processes so that you really take advantage of things we don't even think about today. What can be done differently that will transform the economy? So, you can definitely find a single business or sectors who are using it and seeing the gains, but we haven't seen that across the economy going forward. But I'm pretty bullish. I see the possibilities, and I'm hearing more and more that people are seeing early rewards and really recognizing that next year is the litmus test. Bullish is an interesting turn of phrase. Today, putting aside the slight dip in the market, the exuberance that we have seen in financial markets to want to back these companies, and we're about to get more public companies coming, more liquidity, more... more money. Is that, in and of itself, a financial stability issue? Are you worried about the market riding so high? You remember, you know, who's... who's doing most of this investment, the Mag 7 who's who's really there. This is actually something they can do and fund, and their enthusiasm is real, they see what's possible. But I... I don't think that we should think, oh, there's financial stability concerns just because the market's gone up. It could go up or down as it has in the past, but a financial stability issue would mean it spread to the banks, it spread to consumers or businesses. Right now, I'm not seeing evidence of that. We keep our eye on it for absolute sure. But what I am seeing is that companies, other than the technology enthusiasts, companies outside of technology are using AI to think about how to do their business better in real ways. I mean, I was just meeting with some machine... you know, they make machines for a living. That's what they do. And they're thinking about how do I scan in 50 years of plans of these machines I built for companies, and then use those plans in a model to generate innovative new ideas of things I can sell that will be faster, better, cheaper than things I've sold before. We toured a robotics company that builds things that help manufacturers do better in terms of shipping and distribution. These are real things that do have a capacity to change the economy, so I'm not... that's why... that's the underlying part of my bullish. It has less to do about the investments that tech companies are making and more about the investments that everyday, regular companies that make things and provide services, the things they are doing. President Daly, inflation is still the biggest risk, and and you know we... those are not my words, we heard it, you know, all week long at the Bloomberg Credit Forum, for example. One of the things that you and I have discussed in the past is, is the massive, capex commitment build out of data center in conjunction with a bottleneck in some core areas like memory, inflationary? Or is it disinflationary? You know, that's the thesis that like a utility of PG&E would argue: disinflationary because the big guys are buying in aggregate. I still don't understand where we are with that. It's a timing issue, in my judgment, that, you know, in the beginning, of course, when companies want to invest in big construction projects, a lot of electricity demand, then the companies that are providing those things or areas that are providing those things are going to see competition for the limited amount of services they have. But what they're building creates the infrastructure, the data centers create the infrastructure, or if the big guys come in as you just said and help with electrical plants that help with electricity generation, that eventually can help with the prices of those things. But not here and now. But you just have to think about the timing. And one of the things is really important in policymaking is that we not assume we know, we actually look for the evidence of what are we seeing in prices today, what are we seeing in... what is the forecast of prices tomorrow, and then how do we think about policy. So, right now, I'm focused on, you know, other energy prices, oil prices, and food prices are driving up inflation. But we do know that down the road, these things could maybe compete for services and cost and raise cost, but we haven't seen real evidence that that's the limiting factor. The limiting factor seems it's hard to get generators, it's hard to get the infrastructure equipment you need, and so you see the big tech companies thinking about solutions they can provide for themselves. The inflationary data must be fascinating here in San Francisco. When I think about all these companies potentially going public, what that means for the employee base, what that means for your house price and the ability to be able to buy yet more real estate here. What it means for the cost of labor as well. Is there an inflationary pressure that you're seeing here in San Francisco? I've lived through... so I moved to San Francisco in 1996, and then that we had the dot-com, and I know what it feels like to not be able to rent a place that's affordable because we had people who are making many more orders of magnitude. And I was, you know, in a position where that's manageable, but it's... that's what's happening, is people feel like they're getting crowded out because other things are happening. But that's more about the supply of housing than it is about the demand for housing. We want people to come and invest in this community, we want the city to thrive, we want regional activity to thrive and employment to grow. But as you said, you know, the more interest people have in living in a place, if you have limited supply of housing, then you're going to have a... a run-up. So, those are the things that not the Fed, but other policymakers, other federal policymakers and local policymakers like the mayor are working on. But it looks very... you know, you can see the elements of 1996 already here where there's productivity growth, there's enthusiasm, etc. Is it a bubble? Is there a risk that we're in that same... You know, the dot-com was very different than the AI boom. And so I just want to... you know, there's a lot there that's already being put into businesses, and it's very pervasive. It's not just the dot-com. So, I... I don't jump to the conclusion that if it has similarities to the '90s, it's going to be the '90s. Well, if I may, President Daly, you you went out and did some of the most important work in that era of how the advent of the internet would change the economy. Look, so... so you've kind of established where we sit right now. Inflation continues to rise. How likely or unlikely would that make a rate cut in 2026? How do you tie the two together from this juncture onward? You know, I think one of the questions I get asked regularly, is what are the... what's the path for the rates going forward? Yeah. And the answer I give, because this is how I... I really think we have to think about it, is we don't know how the economy's going to play out. We have, as we've been talking about, this tremendous possibility with AI, but we at the same time, the war in Iran that is, with an uncertain end, which has pushed oil prices up and fertilizer prices, which have filtered into food prices. And right now, those are fairly contained. And if you look at the futures market for oil, it's $80 a barrel by the end of the year. But, you know, we have to think about that. And so, right now, policy's in a good place. We are prepared to respond either way, whatever the economy brings. But I think giving more forward guidance about what's possible could be misguided in the end because we just have to wait for the economy to evolve. Everybody wants to resolve the uncertainty today, but I think that's a mistake because it will... it will close off our mind about what we really have to look at: the inflation risk possibility, but also the possibility that the war ends, oil prices come back down, and we're back to the underlying dynamics with some of the positives of AI we've been talking about. Can I ask you about the labor market as well? Because you've tried to bring this transparency of how you're thinking about things with your blog, for example. And one of the really interesting ones that I was reading and caught my attention was the idea that we've got zero labor growth now. Immigration's changed, the way in which demographics change. Are you feeling like we've seen some improvements, some... some resilience in the labor market, or is it strengthening, and... and will that hold? Has it firmed? Yeah. You know, I think that's too early to say 'firmed.' I think we're, you know, there's always statistical error so you can bounce around from month to month. But if you, you know, I was one of the people who policymakers who were a little worried about the labor market at the end of last year, was very supportive of the cuts that we took to stabilize conditions there. So, relative to that point, I think this is we've really stabilized. And I'm starting to see businesses feel a little more cautiously optimistic, which will feed through to hiring. But they're not being, you know, they're not just running out to hire people. They are being cautious. And they can get an agent, right? Aren't they... They can get an agent. They're interrogating how much AI can do for them before they hire. And, you know, regularly we talk to our businesses and they say, 'We don't want to hire a bunch of people, find out AI can do certain things, and we need a different set of skills, so we want to wait. We want to be patient on our hiring and make sure we're not overhiring.' Because, you know, if you ever go through a period of time where a business has to lay workers off, it's a painful experience for the workers and for them. And so they just don't want to get overly confident only to find out they have to make adjustments. So, that caution will be with us for a bit. We are live on Bloomberg Television and Bloomberg Radio. We're in San Francisco and we're at the Bloomberg Tech Event, and we're speaking with San Francisco Fed President, Mary Daly. Reset a little bit but, if I may, it's the first opportunity we've had to ask you: have you spoken to Chairman Walsh about how he sees the Fed evolving? About changes to the institution? And if you may, fold in the context of your district, San Francisco Fed, yes, much more than that, and and your role going forward. Sure, absolutely. So, you know, I think what we really want when any new chair comes in, and what we want from all of our leaders of Federal Reserve Banks and all of our governors, is that you're thinking constantly about how can the Fed be better. How can it better serve the American people, how can we do our work more efficiently, more effectively, and more resiliently. You know, ultimately, everything we talk about is is put between two bookends. We are fiduciary stewards of public trust, which means we better have services that people can depend on, and we better work hard to achieve our goals. And we're fiduciary stewards of public funds, which is we are very careful about how we spend taxpayer dollars. So, with those two things in mind, you know, I mentioned I joined the Fed back in the '90s. So, I joined the Fed in the '90s and we had check processing. Remember checks? We had check processing. I still have to write them. Everywhere... everywhere in the everywhere we had a location, we had people who processed checks. But then check demand started to fall, and so we consolidated those activities into a few locations. And that level of modernizing, constantly thinking about how can you do better is what I see now. So, what is the chair's equivalent of that, President Daly? Well, he's just joined. So, I'm going to give him the right time he has to announce that. He's talked about making sure he's holding on to that tradition. And I... he comes in with a lot of ideas. But, you know, what I've heard him say again and again, which I really appreciate because all... he's the fifth chair I've worked with. Wow. But all of the chairs I've worked with have the same basic compass: it is to do our best work for the American people and work with all the individuals who are earnestly doing their work in the Fed to do it well. And I see that in Chair Walsh. That earnest work within the Fed, how much of that is being modernized to use, adopt AI? How hard or easy is it at this moment when you're such a regulated institution in and of yourself? Well, we're we're careful like all regulated institutions and importantly like all businesses. Businesses, you know, when I talk to businesses, the last thing they want to do is take huge risks that destroy their shareholders, the value of their company. The same is true for us. We're good fiduciary stewards of public trust and good fiduciary stewards of public funds, which means we're always striving to adopt new technology to do our work more efficiently, but we recognize we have to do that safely. You know, people want to know they can get their money when they need it, they want to know that the banks are well supervised, they want to know that monetary policy is not made by machines, it's made by people making judgments, not only about models and rules, but also about the lived experiences of people across our country. Very quickly before the show ends, what are you seeing in credit? That's a big story for us in how data centers are financed. For some, it's very worrying. Well, you know, we're we're watching that carefully. I watch that carefully. What we do see is that there's a lot of... there's those companies investing a lot of their own resources in those, so it's something to keep our eye on. But at this point, you know, again, if you stack-rank the things that are worrying today, I'd say getting inflation back to target, getting Americans the relief they deserve that we've been working on for quite a while, that's my number-one priority.

Ray Dalio Explains the 5 Forces Shaping the Future of the Global Economy

Forbes(2.1M)·17:20·2.8K views·2026-06-04
Ray DalioFounder, Bridgewater Associates Randall LaneChief Content Officer, Forbes
TL;DRBridgewater Associates Founder Ray Dalio warns of an impending sovereign debt crisis and a historic shift in global power, advising investors to combat extreme market concentration and geopolitical risk by building portfolios with at least 15 uncorrelated assets.
To guide...

TL;DR

Bridgewater Associates Founder Ray Dalio warns of an impending sovereign debt crisis and a historic shift in global power, advising investors to combat extreme market concentration and geopolitical risk by building portfolios with at least 15 uncorrelated assets.

The Five Forces Shaping the Future

To guide global macro investing, Dalio utilizes a historical framework based on five overlapping forces that have driven shifts in wealth and power over the last 500 years:

  • The Debt, Money, and Economic Dynamic: The cycle of credit expansion, asset appreciation, and eventual debt accumulation. When debt growth outpaces income growth, debt servicing costs inevitably squeeze out essential spending.
  • The Internal Political Cycle: The friction between the political left and right, which directly impacts taxation, wealth distribution, and policy stability.
  • Geopolitics: The external shifts in global power and international conflict.
  • Acts of Nature: Historically, pandemics, climate shifts, and natural disasters have killed more people and altered more world orders than wars or economic crises.
  • Technology: Human innovation and invention, spearheaded today by the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence (AI).

Geopolitics: China's Strategy and Changing Global Perceptions

Following a recent trip to China, Dalio highlighted major shifts in global power dynamics and economic strategy:

  • Declining US Deterrence: There is a growing global perception that domestic political divisions and cost-of-living concerns will prevent the US from entering a direct military conflict to defend allies or counter Chinese expansion.
      • Dalio compares this shift to the British Empire's loss of the Suez Canal, which permanently altered the global perception of British military dominance.
  • The Deployment of Chinese Capital: China earned approximately $1.2 trillion from exports last year. How Chinese entities reinvest this massive capital pool globally—particularly in regions like Africa—will heavily impact international capital markets.
  • The "Art of War" Strategy on Taiwan: Rather than a direct military invasion, China is expected to employ gray-zone warfare, leveraging economic pressure, political influence, and strategic deception. This strategy takes advantage of the belief that the US cannot comfortably fight a two-front war.
  • Divergent Views on AI:
      • The US View: Highly commercialized, focused on profitability, capital formation, and dominant market valuations.
      • The Chinese View: Viewed as a public utility like electricity or running water. The goal is to raise overall national productivity rather than secure high corporate profit margins.

The Sovereign Debt "Heart Attack"

Despite the markets ignoring rising debt levels, Dalio maintains that a systemic credit correction is inevitable.

  • The Cardiovascular Analogy: Dalio compares the current global economy to an unhealthy individual with plaque building up in their arteries. Just because they have not suffered a heart attack yet does not mean the underlying behavior is safe.
  • The Yield Curve and Central Banks: The demand for US dollar-denominated debt is weakening.
      • Global central banks are actively selling US dollar debt and purchasing gold.
      • Sustaining these debt levels requires offering higher real returns, which puts upward pressure on the long end of the yield curve.

The Investor Game Plan: The Power of 15 Uncorrelated Bets

In an environment dominated by FOMO (fear of missing out) and extreme market capitalization concentration in tech and AI, Dalio advises investors to prioritize portfolio construction over chasing momentum.

  • Uncorrelated Diversification: The holy grail of investing is holding 15 good, uncorrelated bets.
      • Mathematically, diversifying into 15 independent cash flows can reduce overall portfolio risk by up to 80% without reducing expected returns.
  • Targeting Real Assets: To hedge against monetary inflation and debt-market weakness, investors should look toward global opportunities and physical commodities, such as copper and other critical minerals.

Notable Quotes

"If I can have 15 good uncorrelated bets, what happens—just the math of it—is it means that you can eliminate your risk by up to 80% without reducing your return." — Ray Dalio
"Central banks are selling US dollar-denominated debt and buying gold, and there's a reluctance to accumulate that dollar-denominated debt. That's a reality." — Ray Dalio
"You're either going to deny reality or you can see reality that there is going to be this problem. It’s like telling somebody who has eaten a lot of fat, not exercised, and built a lot of plaque, 'I haven't had a heart attack yet.' ... This is a reality." — Ray Dalio
Please welcome Ray Dalio, founder, Bridgewater Associates, and interviewer, Randall Lane, Chief Content Officer, Forbes. Getting right into it, the big guns. I like it. I like it, I like it. Great to see you. Good to see you. Good to see you all. We're excited. Let's talk. You have, you know, a very strong point of view. You talk about, you know, again, you look at the world, you look at history. You have this theory of five forces. A lot of people here are familiar, a lot are not. Maybe, kind of, we could talk for two minutes about that and then delve into those forces and how they're driving everything right now. Okay, just so we put it in perspective: I'm a global macro investor, who has done that for about 60 years. They all know who you are, but yes. And then—no, but I'm bringing a perspective of, I need to have a game plan. All investors need to have a game plan. And I'm bringing a perspective which is: what is that game plan? How do you deal with that? Because over that period of time, we've seen a lot, and then you study history, you see a lot. And so we're in a moment in time, but it's important to take that moment in time and put it in perspective to know how to have a strategy. That's the background. So, okay, five big forces. I studied history going back 500 years in order to help me understand the dollar and markets and so on. And what it told me, and what my experience is, is that there are these five big things that interact. Okay, there is an economy, debt, and money dynamic that works, and, you know, we have money and we have credit. And when the money and credit grow, we buy assets—investment assets—and they go up in value, and you have a cycle. And then when debt increases faster than income, and then there are debt service payments, then there are issues. And so, just very quickly on that, there is an issue that the federal government has, and that a lot of governments have, which is about the amount of debt. And so when we start off looking at markets, the first market we look at is the risk-free interest rate. Okay, that's the backbone of all markets and the present value of cash flows. And there is an issue there. Bonds are not good, interest rates are there, and then we have premiums of markets over that. All right, so force number— Okay, I'll be quick. Two is the internal left and right political cycle. Okay? What taxes it affects, shifts in wealth it affects, it's very important. By the way, we're already—we're only two in, and I'm already very pessimistic based on what you're saying. Okay. Three is geopolitics. And now I'm even more pessimistic. Four is nature and five is technology. What's four again? What's four? Acts of nature have killed more people and changed more world orders. And number five is technology. Okay, the invention of new things, and now the greatest of all time, perhaps, artificial intelligence. And if you understand those and the interactions of those—and that's important because the capital markets are tied to the political markets and so on—those are the five. You see, what's interesting though about those five is right now, even if you look—you said you've been doing this 60 years—all five of those, or certainly four of the five, the needle is really running hot in your forces. So that makes this a very interesting time to talk about that. We appreciate you being here also because, you know, you're shaking off the jet lag, you're just back from a long trip to China. Maybe let's start with there, what you saw over there and how—what you see in terms of the forces in China and what you experienced there. Okay. I would say two big things in the world, very big, is the changing belief in the world now that the United States can and will fight a war in defense as a counter-influence of China in that region and in other regions. Taiwan— Taiwan, and also the regional expansion. In other words, there's been a containment policy and then our—and there's always been a belief of holding assets, holding bases in countries because that'll be for defense as a countervailing force of China. Okay. So what you're talking about is the perception in China that has changed— No, no, no, well, the perception everywhere. That the United States' politics, cost of living, not wanting to lose any people in a war, and so on, means that it won't fight a war. Okay? And the bases that existed all around the world. So for a lot of people they're comparing it around the world to when the British Empire had the Suez Canal taken away and that had—that changed the perception of everything. So anyway, there is in that world the sense that it will not be the counterbalancing influence. Okay, so that's number one and I think that that's well viewed around the world. The second is China is making a ton of money. In other words, the exports, if you look at the amount of money that they're earning from exports. Yep, you earn money from exports. That's like us earning income or any company earning income and those numbers are, you know, like, that was a trillion two last year or something. This is building up to huge amounts of money and what they do with that money is—what their businesses do, what everything—what they do with that money is a big influence on capital markets, right? Investing it around the world, Africa and other places, right? Everywhere. Where they put that money is going to matter a lot to the capital markets. So, those are the two big things in terms of this shift in, you know, in power. And then we have, of course, the competitions, right? And the competitions in AI are believed to be quite like the competitions that might be in electric vehicles, batteries, solar panels, all of that. And the question is: does the barrier stand? Like when we look at capital formation right now and how much money is going in and the IPOs and so on, well, how does that work in a competitive environment? Because it's very interesting. Their view of AI is very different from the American view in— How so? In that, what their view is, it's that it's like electricity or running water and that everybody should have it, and that the overall productivity of China will rise if everybody has it. And that it therefore doesn't have to be expensive or even profitable, but anyway, that's, you know, pervasive use of it. So their advances in that are significant, and then there are advances in some other areas like robotics and other things. So these are the only two competitive ones, and then there's a barrier and the question is: does the barrier hold? And, you know, so that's the picture. Did you—did you meet with President Xi while you were there? No. Have you met him? I have, yes. What's—what's your take? I think President Xi views himself and China in the context of what they would—the story which would be the long arc and where you are. Okay, so just to give a very, very quick sense of that. Their story maybe like our American 250th lasts 2,500 years, not 250 years, right? And so when they see the story—China 2,500 is going to be an awesome event. So what they see is that China was the leading empire in the world and self-sufficient, and that the British come and they say, 'We want to trade with you,' and they were weak, and then they had 100 years of humiliation, what they call the 100 years of humiliation. The 100 years of humiliation was that the foreign powers came in, they were weak, and for 100 years they deteriorated and they became independent in 1945. In 1945, the end of the war, while Japan had opened and controlled Taiwan, they threw the Japanese out and they said—they gave Taiwan back to China—and then they had the communist very isolated regime that comes up to where they are and the rising of power. When I started going there in 1984, since then per capita income has increased by 29 times, life expectancy has increased by 10 years, and so on. And so now they've become a great power with a different culture. Okay, so the way he sees it is that, okay, now—and there's been a containment policy and Taiwan is part of China. Okay, that's how he sees it, that's what he's living out. So the message that was given to President Trump at the time was largely about Taiwan, the importance of Taiwan. So we're now in a different spot, that's how they see it. He'll have one more term probably— But the way you see it having just been there, do you think that Taiwan situation is going to— You are going to see them play the Art of War. If you understand what the Chinese mean about the Art of War, you don't fight militarily, you operate with pressure and deceptions and so on. And so right now, because there's a belief that the United States cannot fight a war on two fronts, we will probably see greater and greater pressure. And so what does the United States or Donald Trump do in reaction to that? And so you'll start to see changes in China but not just related to Taiwan. You'll also see that the containment policies that affected the Philippines, that affected Vietnam, affected other countries in the region, affected Singapore, those things are all changing. All right, so that's a big shift in one of your forces. And money is a big part of it. Well, let's talk about money. Again, you and I have talked on and off over the last 10 years. You've been talking about debt for 10 years. It's worse than ever, but yet the markets don't seem to care. Well, let's—let's be clear what it's like and how does the system work. The credit market system is like the circulatory system. By the way, you'll be right at some point, so it's when. Well, it's like—it's having like a heart attack. Okay. I can't tell you the day that you'll have it, but I can watch it. I could take the MRIs. And the way it works, because it's shown in the charts and the numbers—and it's true of everybody—is when the debt and debt service payments accumulate and start to squeeze out other spending. So we can see that those debt service—let me just, let me get it out. Okay. Okay. And it squeezes out other spending, right? And so you could see that happening. And then there's a supply and a demand, meaning you produce a certain amount. One man's debts are another man's assets. And so to hold debt assets, you have to have an adequate real return. And so when you have a large supply that has to be sold and at the same time there's not the same demand for it for various reasons, you see the pressures in rates rising and we're seeing that dynamic exist throughout the country, throughout the world. And so it's like you, if I'm—if I'm saying, you could look at it, you could look at the numbers, you either going to deny reality or you can see reality that there is going to be this problem. So it's like telling somebody who is, I don't know, eating a lot of fat, not exercising, and built a lot of plaque in their arteries, 'I haven't had a heart attack yet, so what are you telling me?' I'm telling you that this is a reality. Right. So why do you think—because it's been right there in plain sight and yet there does seem to be this regarding debt, everyone just—there seems to be blinders on. How do you play that, actually? How do you play that? I think that the only thing that wakes people up to it is the heart attack. Okay. So look at the bond market. The bond market, is it attractive? Has it been a good attractive market? Okay. It's been—it's been a poor market, poor market, right? You're losing money in the bond market. And so we're talking about, and then credit, we're even dealing with the issue of private credit and credit spreads and so on. So how's that market been? Okay, despite a very easy Fed policy. So I would say that's not healthy, that doesn't look healthy to me. And when you're looking at it and seeing it rising by the long end of the yield curve, right? They're trying to hold rates down and you're seeing the long rate of the yield curve rise. Or if you look at who's doing the buying and the selling, okay? The demand for debt. Central banks are selling US dollar denominated debt and buying gold, and there's a reluctance to accumulate that dollar denominated debt. That's a reality. Yes. So how would this room, given—so there's a heart attack coming, we don't know when, so how does a room of Alpha Masters play that? I think that everybody has to have their investment principles and their game plan, and the key of everything is really to know how to diversify well. Okay. So here we are in a moment—and this is, I've got 16 seconds and I can't give the whole story—but what happens is, right now you are in a moment that only one thing matters in the markets, right? The concentration risk, that in terms of IPOs and we're talking about market capitalization and so on, and AI. Are you in on a market capitalization basis or even more, are you concentrated in that? That—the answer to that question will be either a yes or a no, okay? And if we study history and we look at that, you better have a game plan. And what is that game plan? And to know how to really diversify well in terms of being able to do that without reducing returns, because the thing about it is if you can have good uncorrelated bets, like my mantra is 15 good uncorrelated bets. If I can have 15 good uncorrelated bets, what happens just the math of it is it means that you can eliminate your risk by 80%, up to 80%, without reducing your return by knowing how to create balance. So there are things out there, okay? For example, there are some of the minerals out there, okay, copper and so on and minerals, or what if you look globally at who is making money and how things are changing, and you can create that kind of a diversified portfolio of good things that provide a way of operating? Because your choice, yes or no, is going to be, 'Do you then get concentrated because of FOMO and you're going to be in that one thing, or are you going to diversify?' And I've played the game—having played the game for 60 years and studied history and engineered a game plan—that's what I do. Good advice. I saw people writing it down. I want to thank you, Ray. I was a history major in college and people are like, 'Why were you a history major?' and you make me feel like that was a good choice. So, thank you. One could learn from history. That's right. Thank you, Ray Dalio.

Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon on Running a Bank in the Age of AI | Odd Lots

Bloomberg Podcasts(496.0K)·67:32·3.1K views·2026-06-04
David SolomonCEO, Goldman Sachs
TL;DRGoldman Sachs CEO David Solomon argues that while AI will dramatically boost productivity and reshape job structures, fears of a white-collar "job apocalypse" are overblown because emotional intelligence, trust, and human relationships remain irreplaceable business drivers.

TL;DR

Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon argues that while AI will dramatically boost productivity and reshape job structures, fears of a white-collar "job apocalypse" are overblown because emotional intelligence, trust, and human relationships remain irreplaceable business drivers. Furthermore, the massive capital demands of the AI transition are shifting market dynamics, forcing tech giants to execute historic equity raises and prompting large private firms to seek public listings.

AI, Productivity, and the Myth of the "Job Apocalypse"

  • The Historical Context of Disruption:
    • "Never Different This Time" Principle: Solomon rejects the narrative of a jobless future requiring universal basic income. Historically, technological accelerations shift the makeup of the labor force and create new opportunities rather than causing permanent mass unemployment.
    • Leveraging Human Capital: Rather than replacing workers, AI will serve as a tool of leverage. Young professionals will be freed from manual processing tasks and pushed into client-facing, relationship-building roles much earlier in their careers.
  • The Irreplaceable Value of EQ:
    • Interpersonal Domination: High-value corporate transactions and advisory services rely on trust, emotional intelligence (EQ), and creative problem-solving.
    • The Power of Direct Communication: Solomon stresses that human-to-human contact is a premium skill. He advises professionals to utilize the phone rather than relying entirely on digital messages, noting that personal calls carry exponentially more impact than texts or emails.

The Evolution of Junior Roles and the Apprenticeship Challenge

  • Historical vs. Modern Workflows:
    • Primitive Beginnings: Solomon contrasts modern banking with his early career in 1984, when performing a common stock comparison took six hours using microfiche, physical copies of the *Wall Street Journal*, and hand-drawn graph paper.
    • Immediate Answers: Today, technology delivers these outputs instantaneously, massively inflating the baseline productivity of employees.
  • The "Apprenticeship" Training Gap:
    • Loss of Foundational Absorption: Because AI and modern tools automate analytical tasks, junior employees no longer have to "work" to get basic answers. Solomon warns this makes it harder for their brains to absorb underlying financial mechanics.
    • Systemic Adaptation: The core challenge for financial institutions over the next decade is finding new ways to apprentice and educate junior staff when the traditional manual tasks used for training have been completely automated.
  • Quantifiable Productivity Gains:
    • Operational Overhauls: Under Goldman Sachs' "1GS 3.0" initiative, the firm is utilizing technology to remake core operations. For example, they aim to streamline client onboarding and KYC processes, reducing the number of employees touching the workflow from 3,800 to just a few hundred.

Data Integrity and Systemic Cybersecurity Vulnerabilities

  • The Imperative of Clean Data:
    • "Garbage In, Garbage Out": AI models perform exceptionally well on highly curated, clean proprietary databases, but they fail on public datasets. Solomon cites a personal example where an AI model incorrectly omitted Tiger Woods when asked about back-to-back Masters winners due to unverified online source material.
    • Goldman's Proprietary Database: Goldman Sachs is cleaning and leveraging its massive proprietary data repository, including its 40-year-old "SecDB" trading and transaction system, to train highly accurate internal models.
  • Cybersecurity Inequality in Banking:
    • Concentrated Resources: The top eight largest global banks have invested heavily in cybersecurity defense and are relatively well-protected.
    • The Mid-Tier Threat: Solomon expresses deep concern for mid-sized and regional banks (and local utility infrastructures) that lack the resources of major Wall Street firms. A successful cyberattack on a regional bank could spark systemic panic, similar to the run on Silicon Valley Bank (SVB).

Shifting Capital Markets: AI Capex, Equity Raises, and IPOs

  • Alphabet’s Unprecedented Capital Raise:
    • Goldman-Led Mega Deal: Goldman Sachs solely managed the initial stages of Alphabet’s historic secondary equity offering over five months, raising approximately $85 billion to $90 billion. This transaction stands as one of the largest follow-on equity offerings in history.
    • Strategic Shift to Equity: Large tech enterprises face voracious capital demands for AI infrastructure over the next five to ten years. To avoid over-leveraging with debt, tech giants are utilizing strong equity markets and high multiples to raise cash.
  • The Demise of the "Permanently Private" Model:
    • Voracious Capital Demands: Although abundant private capital allowed companies to delay public listings for years, the massive infrastructure costs of AI mean companies can no longer fund 100% of their growth privately.
    • Incentive to Transition: Solomon notes that companies must eventually go public to access public currency and massive capital pools, despite the regulatory frictions and the differing pressures of managing a public entity.
  • SpaceX Relationship Banking:
    • Decades of Groundwork: Commenting on Goldman Sachs securing the lead on the SpaceX IPO, Solomon emphasizes that the mandate was won over a 20-year period of relationship building with Elon Musk and CFO Brett Johnson, rather than a short-term pitch.

AI and Creative Expression

  • The Evolution of Music Production:
    • From Vinyl to Laptops: Solomon, a recreational musician and DJ, compares Bruce Springsteen recording *Darkness on the Edge of Town* (1978) using a full suite of physical studio musicians to modern digital production platforms (Logic Pro and Ableton) that allow a single user to simulate a full horn section in seconds.
  • The Limitations of AI in Art:
    • Absence of Personal Voice: AI-generated music and screenwriting lack a true human "voice" because the technology does not possess lived experiences, personal trauma, or emotions. Without human curation, AI outputs default to mediocrity.
    • Unresolved IP Conflicts: Solomon highlights the legal and ethical friction surrounding AI models training on existing artist IP without offering fair compensation or recognition to the creator community.

Notable Quotes

"It’s never different this time. Meaning—meaning I don’t think we’re going to wake up in a world where nobody works and there has to be universal basic income and we have massive unemployment. I just don’t think that’s the way our economy works." — David Solomon
"A telephone call to someone is 10 times more valuable than a text or email... If I call you personally on the phone, it’s going to have a completely different impact than if I sent you a text or an email." — David Solomon
"The reason these companies are going public now is because they have to. They have capital needs that are so voracious that it is not prudent for them to try to do 100 percent of it in the private market and not have a public currency." — David Solomon
I do think we've created a regulatory structure and a market structure that really makes it unattractive to go public until you have to. The reason these companies are going public now is because they have to. They have capital needs that are so voracious that it is not prudent for them to try to do 100% of it in the private market and not have a public currency. It's—it's just not prudent. And—and that's why I think you're going to see a bunch of these companies go public, because they actually need the capital. It's interesting, I've advised companies for years, and at one point 25 years ago, I actually ran the Equity Capital Markets business at Goldman Sachs. That was the job I had early in my career at Goldman Sachs. And—and I used to say to companies, "You know, there are a few reasons to go public. You need the capital. You need the currency. You have to create liquidity for, you know, early investors." But all the other stuff that people talk about is really not critical. And you have to recognize that if you're running a company successfully, and you don't need those things, when you take it public, you will run it differently. You will run it differently. It is different running a public company than a private company. There are different pressures, there's a different life cycle of how you have to respond. You will run it differently. And so my advice has always been: wait as long as you possibly can to go public. Hello and welcome to another episode of the Odd Lots podcast. I'm Tracy Alloway. And I'm Joe Weisenthal. So Joe, when we talk about AI and its impact on jobs and businesses, one of the things we like to do, or one of the prisms through which we like to explore AI, is through banks, right? Absolutely. So you think about banks, they've got big tech budgets, they're talking to AI companies all the time, they're their clients essentially. So they're deploying a lot of this technology, and then they have this whole array of jobs, right? You have the classic sort of process workers in the back office, you've got the junior analysts who are churning out PowerPoints, but you also have bankers in IB who are doing more, I guess, relationship-driven business. So you have this really good cross-section of the sort of white-collar jobs that people think about when we think about AI disruption. Do you remember in the 2010s when every bank CEO was like, "You know, we actually like to think of ourselves as a tech company instead of a bank"? I remember. Bring in the ping pong tables for the engineers, that was it, right? That was a fun little era. But no, you're absolutely right. And of course, you know, AI is largely expected, at least until we have much more advanced robotics, expected to affect white-collar labor first. You mentioned the whole range, but there are aspects of when I think about being a banker, working in a bank, that to your point really do feel like relationships. And like, there's the cliché of, you know, "Go out and golf," but I think they do actually golf a lot. I think that is part of the business. All right, well we'll find out. We have spoken to Marco Argenti at Goldman Sachs before—he's the CTO—about how the bank is actually deploying technology. But today, we have an even more, more perfect guest, if that's possible. We're going to be speaking, of course, to David Solomon. He's the CEO and Chairman of Goldman Sachs, and he just penned an op-ed in the New York Times with the headline, "I'm the CEO of Goldman Sachs. The AI job apocalypse is overblown." So truly the perfect guest to talk about all of this. David, thanks for coming on Odd Lots. Thank you for having me. I'm delighted to be with you guys. Congrats on the op-ed. You're in the media industry now. Well, I—I'm trying not to be in the media industry, but I'm—I'm glad—I'm glad I wrote the op-ed. By the way, not my title, the New York Times title, but—but I'm glad—I'm glad I wrote the op-ed. Why did you write it? Well, I—I wrote the op-ed—I haven't written a lot of op-eds. I think in my eight years I've written two, and the last one was back in 2019 before COVID. And it was around—it was around energy where—where I was trying to talk about the fact that I thought there was going to be real change in the context of the energy supply chain. It was very important that we made investments in things that were greener and we moved in that direction, but I also stated at the time, and took a little heat for this very emphatically, that oil and gas was going to be a significant part of the energy supply chain for decades to come, and Goldman Sachs would be financing, you know, fossil fuel companies for a long, long time. Good call. And, you know, in looking at the lens, you know, now, I—I think that—that it is very important that we have a thoughtful and detailed discussion about the change that's going on. And—and let's just start, if you don't—if you don't mind if I can step back. I am hugely, hugely optimistic about this technology and the impact that this technology can have on large-scale enterprises like Goldman Sachs and the productivity gains that it can create, which I do believe can increase economic growth, create more prosperity, and, you know, more impact, you know, on our economic system broadly in a very, very positive way. I am hugely optimistic. And I—I think about things in five-to-ten-year, you know, time bites—like, what—what should we expect to see over the next 10 years? That's the way I think about things when—when I make a statement like that. At the same point, I'm extraordinarily cognizant about the fact that it's not going to be a straight line. There's going to be disruption, there are going to be jobs displaced, as there have been with any other technology acceleration over the course of—of history. And, you know, we as an important company, and governments also do too, play a role in, you know, thinking about what's the best way to make this go as well as possible, but with the ultimate goal of having the technology make our society more productive and therefore allow for more economic growth and more participation by—by everyone. And I was listening to a variety of things that were being said and they didn't really make sense to me, and I thought it was important for Goldman Sachs to get into the discussion. And so, you know, my general—there are things that are always different, but I generally, you know, if you want to talk about kind of fundamental principles that I have after 40-some years of doing this, it's never different this time. Hm. Meaning—meaning I don't think we're going to wake up in a world where nobody works and there has to be universal basic income and we have massive unemployment. I just don't think that's the way our economy works. And—and when people start talking about those things or postulating about those things, I don't think they're being thoughtful about really looking in a granular way at the labor force and how job creation works in our economy, and—and how technology disruption in the past has—has changed and shifted, you know, the nature or the makeup of labor and the economy in the US. I think to even begin the discussion of like will AI cause a white-collar wipeout or, as you—as you did say in the op-ed, like some—some areas will really change and headcounts will change, but to begin this conversation, you have to know like well what is a job? What does someone actually do at their job? And so, I don't know, maybe this is a chance I'll let you like butter you up, let you brag about yourself for a little bit. I saw a quote, I was doing some prep and you're talking about earlier in your career and going from Bear Stearns to Goldman Sachs and you said, "I was a big producer at Bear Stearns. I built a lot of relationships there and brought a lot of relationships to the firm." This is the part where you can say: what made you good at your job? What was—like, there were a lot of people that were at Bear at the time. You eventually became the CEO of Goldman Sachs. What was it that like the skill that you had or that successful people in that role had that made them do their jobs well? Well, finance has evolved. You know, I started in 1984. I recently referred to that as the prehistoric days of finance, when by the way I had long flowing hair. And—you know, it was just a very different world. And so let's just start with the fact that finance was a relatively nascent industry and also had been relatively stagnant for 15 years because we really went through a period from the late '60s to the early 1980s where interest rates were going straight up. Yeah. September 15th, 1982, is a very, very important day in the history of financial markets because that is the day that the 10-year Treasury hit 15.9%. Phew. Put things in perspective. And it also happens to be, no, it also happens to be, you know, a little less than two years before, you know, I landed at the Irving Trust Company at One Wall Street to—to start my—career. And, you know, at the time you got trained to do analysis, but the business was very entrepreneurial. There was very little structure. And there was so much open running room, you know, to go out and participate that young people, especially when you got away from the most white-shoe firms, which at the time were Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, were really encouraged to go make their way. And so I was working at—at Irving Trust. And this was a time when almost everybody in finance went to business school. And I was applying to business school in—in 1985 and I got a job offer to work in the junk bond business at Drexel Burnham. And I decided to take it because it appeared lucrative and my dad gave— Junk bonds were like the only thing taking off basically in finance in the early '80s, right? They were—it was definitely an area of finance that was accelerating and it was very entrepreneurial. And—and I said to my dad, "You know, this is taking a risk because everybody else is going to business school." And my dad said, "Go try it for two years. If it doesn't work, you go to business school in two years." I mean, it was really—it was a pretty—it was a pretty simple, you know, piece of advice. Good advice. But I went to Drexel Burnham. It was very entrepreneurial and the culture—there were good things and bad things about the culture at Drexel, and some of the bad things are the reason why in 1990, on February 14th, 1990, Drexel Burnham went out of business, as many other Wall Street firms have in the history of Wall Street. But, you know, for the four and a half years that I was there, basically I was told: go find a way to build relationships and make money. Go find a way to build, you know, I was 24 years old, go bring business into the firm. Did you golf? We sat at a desk. I—I golfed a little bit, but you know, we sat at a desk and we called people and talked to people. And it was a time when—it was a time in the world when if you picked up the phone and called someone, you would get them on the phone and you would talk to them. And if you were an effective salesperson, you—you could do a lot of business. So to some extent you were like good at sales. You were good at making that call. One of the great experiences I had—this is an experience I've talked about a little bit but I haven't talked about a lot, but a—a seminal experience in my personal development was the summer after my freshman year in college. I got an internship in a Merrill Lynch office. It happened to be the office that was at—I think it was—I think it was like 10 Penn Plaza, it was by Madison Square Garden. And I—I got the internship because my—my high school girlfriend's father ran that Merrill Lynch office. Always helps. And—and he said, "Why don't you come and do an internship for the summer?" And I said, "What would you do?" And he said, "You know, you'd call on clients." I really had no idea what he was talking about. So I show up at this Merrill Lynch office and they plop me down and they give me a zip code list. Okay, basically, and the zip code was 10028 and 10128, basically the Upper East Side of Manhattan. This is 1981, okay? They give me a zip code list. And they say, "Make a hundred phone calls a day and ask people if they're interested in talking to a Merrill Lynch broker." And during the course of the summer, okay, for eight weeks, I made a hundred phone calls a day. I got five people to talk to a Merrill Lynch broker, and we went and had one meeting and opened one account. Oh my god. Okay? It was brutal. Brutal. And after—after the first week, I—I said to my dad, I was like, "I don't know that I can do this for the whole summer." And he said, "Yes you can. You took it on, you got to finish it." And I started thinking about—and this is the seminal thing that I'm—I'm trying to point to—I started thinking about: how am I going to make this more fun? Because it is very difficult to cold call. Now, this was a time when people would answer the telephone, and I just started making it a game. But what did it teach me? It taught me to pick up the phone and talk to anybody.
