Specifically, there are three types of workloads developing around LLMs: training, answer inference, and agentic inference. From the section making the case for agentic inference: "Critically, this articulation of an agentic-specific memory hierarchy implies a necessary trade-off of speed for capacity. Here's the thing, though. Lower speed isn't nearly as important a consideration if there isn't a human in the loop. If an agent is waiting around for a job that is being run overnight, the agent doesn't know or care about the user experience impact. What is most important is being able to accomplish a task, and if entirely new approaches to memory make that possible, then delays are fine. If delays are fine, then all of the focus on pure compute power and high-bandwidth memory seems out of place. If latency isn't the top priority, then slower and cheaper memory, like traditional DRAM, for example, makes a lot more sense. And if the entire system is mostly waiting on memory, then chips don't need to be as fast as the cutting edge either. This represents a profound shift in future architectures, but it also doesn't mean that current architectures are going away. Training will continue to matter, and Nvidia's current architecture, including high speed compute, large amounts of high bandwidth memory, and high speed networking, will likely continue to dominate. Answer inference will be a meaningful market, albeit a relatively small one, and speed from chips like Cerebras or Groq (I explained how Nvidia is deploying Groq's LPUs here) will be very useful. Agentic inference will gradually unbundle the GPU, which alternates between stranding high-bandwidth memory during the prefill process, and stranding compute during the decode process, in favor of increasingly sophisticated memory hierarchies, dominated by high capacity and relatively lower cost memory types, with good enough compute. Indeed, if anything, it will be the speed of CPUs for things like tool use that will matter more than the speed of GPUs. At the same time, these categories won't be equal in size or importance. Specifically, agentic inference will be the largest market by far, because that is the market that won't be limited by humans or time. Today's agents are fancy answer inference. In the future, true agentic inference will be work done by computers according to dictates given by other computers, and the market size scales not with humans, but with compute." End quote. It's agentic inference that makes the most sense for racks in space, and conveniently enough, that is also the market that is likely to be the largest in the long run. The third question about data centers in space is if there is a stick. Specifically, while I think that racks-in-space are both a lot more viable than people think, and a lot more relevant to agentic inference than current modes of compute, it is at the end of the day cheaper and easier to build on earth, all things being equal. All things are not equal, however. Right now we are at the very beginning of the AI buildout, and already one of the biggest constraints is not just power, expected, but zoning, unexpected. I wrote in an update last week: "That leads to an interesting contrast to globalization. When companies were closing down American factories and laying off workers and moving operations to China, none of the affected towns or workers had a say. They just suddenly no longer had a job, and a huge number of cities across the Rust Belt no longer had a reason to exist. People simply had to move, or worse, retreat to things like alcohol or drugs. AI, however, is the opposite. Building data centers requires permission, which is to say that people actually have a say. Again, I'm not at all saying that these people are well-informed about data centers, or about the economic impact on their communities, much less the economic impact of AI generally. What I am noting is that people who didn't have a say in globalization are suddenly finding they do have a say about AI, and it's not a surprise they are expressing their disapproval by blocking data centers." End quote. In that Update, I made the case that data center builders, and by extension the companies that use them, should straight up pay people for permission to build data centers in their communities. At a minimum, however, that increases the costs of terrestrial data centers. What seems very plausible in the long run is that the demand for compute ends up being so large that there eventually is nowhere left to build, making the vast expanses of space not just an alternative, but in fact the only choice. An IPO Worth Supporting. If all of this happens, and there are a lot of ifs here, then suddenly that $2 trillion valuation starts looking reasonable. SpaceX is already monetizing xAI's first data center, Colossus 1, to the tune of $15 billion a year for 300 megawatts of capacity. That's 3,000 racks-in-space. Anthropic, meanwhile, will probably make 3x the revenue on that capacity. It remains to be seen if xAI can get back in the state-of-the-art game, but if so, then the amount of revenue it can generate per rack-in-space will be commensurately higher. Even without xAI, however, SpaceX has the potential to be a monopoly provider of marginal compute capacity. There are, needless to say, a massive number of assumptions baked into this argument, including assuming a huge number of engineering challenges are solved, Starship actually works, SpaceX gets sufficient supply of the right kind of chips, compute demand is massively larger, agentic inference unbundles current architectures, and data center opponents are successful. The risk attached to all these assumptions should discount the valuation you put on this business, which is to say I still think this IPO is nuts. At the same time, I'm glad it exists for multiple reasons. The first one is the most obvious one. Musk, for all of his faults, has already pushed humanity forward on multiple vectors, including electric cars, self-driving, reusable rockets, satellite internet, etc., and I'm excited to see him try and do more. The second is that I am in fact concerned about our ability to muster enough compute to fully realize the gains from AI, and am very worried about a replay of nuclear power, where our failure to build denied us the opportunity to even imagine what could be invented in a world of unlimited energy. The fact Musk is proposing an alternative path to unlimited compute is a relief. The third is that I appreciate the extent to which this IPO is a return to what an IPO should be: the opportunity for people to contribute capital to actually build the business, and to benefit if it works out. As I noted, I can't make a financial model that necessarily justifies this valuation, particularly based on current financials, but neither can a VC investing in the Series A of a company. SpaceX has already invented a lot, and its early investors are going to make a lot of money with this IPO. At the same time, there is still so much more to invent that there remains a lot of upside, and to be very clear, a lot of risk. It's a testament to SpaceX's ambitions that retail investors get to play VC. And hey, you get Mars upside for free.

Verizon CEO on Using Tech to Transform Telecom

Bloomberg Live(185.0K)·21:26·128 views·2026-06-04
Dan SchulmanCEO, Verizon Tom GilesBloomberg
TL;DRVerizon CEO Dan Schulman is driving an aggressive cultural and technological turnaround to reverse five years of market share loss, prioritizing customer experience improvements and leveraging advanced AI agents that have already boosted customer satisfaction by 1,280 basis points.

TL;DR

Verizon CEO Dan Schulman is driving an aggressive cultural and technological turnaround to reverse five years of market share loss, prioritizing customer experience improvements and leveraging advanced AI agents that have already boosted customer satisfaction by 1,280 basis points.

Cultural Turnaround and Strategic Pivot

  • Reversing Market Decline: After taking over as CEO, Schulman addressed Verizon's five-year consecutive loss in market share, which had dragged the company from first to last in both market cap and forward P/E.
  • Increasing Risk Tolerance: Schulman is shifting the corporate culture from a bureaucratic "two or three" risk tolerance to an "eight or nine" to encourage rapid experimentation and failing fast.
      • *Exceptions:* Risk tolerance remains at a strict "one" for cybersecurity and financial reporting integrity.
  • Value Over Price Wars: Verizon is rejecting short-term promotional price wars, which Schulman notes can be matched by competitors within two hours, in favor of building unique, long-term value propositions.

Customer Experience and Churn Mitigation

  • Halting Arbitrary Price Hikes: To combat rising customer churn, Schulman instituted a rule to "stop digging," halting Verizon's previous practice of implementing price increases (30 over three years) without delivering incremental customer value.
  • Eliminating Friction Points: The company is focusing on solving retail and billing pain points, ensuring that in-store promises accurately reflect on a customer's first bill and that customer service agents are highly empathetic.
  • Continuous Incremental Improvement: Instead of seeking a singular "big bang" product launch, Verizon is focusing on daily, steady improvements in loyalty, plan simplicity, and tailored personalization.

AI-Led Transformation and Workforce Impact

  • Stellar AI Agent Performance: In pilot testing over a three-month period, Verizon's conversational AI customer service agents achieved customer satisfaction rates 1,280 basis points higher than previous baselines.
  • Job Displacement Realities: Schulman acknowledges that AI will likely displace a "large percentage" of rote customer service jobs (e.g., password resets and billing inquiries), though complex issues will shift to collaborative human-machine workflows.
      • This follows an earlier, difficult corporate restructuring that eliminated 13,000 jobs at Verizon.
  • Reskilling and Funding: Verizon has launched a $20 million training and reskilling initiative for employees and communities impacted by AI, with over 7,000 applicants already engaged. Schulman is advocating in Washington D.C. for a broader, coordinated Fortune 500 and government-funded reskilling coalition.
  • Rapid Tech Timelines: Schulman predicts the industry will achieve Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) within two to four years, humanoid robotics shortly thereafter, and is currently preparing Verizon for Quantum decryption risks ("Q-Day") expected as early as two years from now.

Advanced Cybersecurity and Network Autonomy

  • Deploying Mythos: Partnering with Glasswing, Verizon is utilizing "Mythos," an AI-driven cybersecurity tool that aggregates minor network vulnerabilities to identify and preemptively patch critical systemic risks.
  • Autonomous Network Healing: Within the next few months, Verizon aims to autonomously identify and patch 85% of network vulnerabilities within minutes, a process that previously took hours or days of manual labor.

Notable Quotes

"Anytime the pace of change outside is greater than the pace of change inside, we're falling behind, and that's a really high bar to get over." — Dan Schulman
"The first rule of getting out of a hole is stop digging." — Dan Schulman
"You should not be scared of AI. You need to learn AI. You need to be able to use it in your work. You need to be able to use it in your personal life because that's the age we live in." — Dan Schulman
It's always great to have the CEO of a company that you've been a customer of for, like, most of my adult life. So, all my grievances, I'm just going to bring them to the, to the, to the, to the table here. In all seriousness, you've been CEO of Verizon since October, something of a homecoming for you, having, having grown up at AT&T and getting Virgin Mobile off the ground. When it comes to running a wireless company—and you had that long interlude in the middle at PayPal, so we'll talk about that later—what are the old rules that no longer apply? Well, I think we live in a time right now of change I've really never seen before. The pace of change is dramatic. I say inside the company right now, anytime the pace of change outside is greater than the pace of change inside, we're falling behind, and that's a really high bar to get over. I think there's some similarities and some differences. I mean, way back when I was at AT&T, you know, we were a big bureaucratic company. Verizon is a big bureaucratic company. It loves its processes, it loves to show its work, but I'm about outcomes and how fast we can move the company forward. We're very risk-averse, and I get that. There should be places where we are risk-averse when it comes to cybersecurity, or when it comes to the integrity of our financial numbers. My tolerance for risk there is like a one. But for the most part, my tolerance for risk for trying things and failing fast, and then learning from it and moving forward, is like an eight or a nine. And I think, in general, the company's probably had a two to three. We're very hierarchical. I need to be very careful what I say because it's taken as gospel, and the truth of the matter is things are changing so fast. I'm always learning. I can't predict the future; I can try and do my best to ascertain the direction of it, but I'll frequently be wrong on that as well. It's still less than a year that you've been there, but how do you get from that—you said around two in those areas where you want to see people take more risk—how do you get closer to that eight-nine level of tolerance? Well, I think leadership is like three things in my view. The first is to clearly define reality, and nobody really wants to hear that, to tell you the truth. Certainly, my first time coming on stage in front of all the employees of Verizon, I had no slides and nothing on screen, no talk track, just kind of what I saw. And what I saw was a company that was losing in the market. We had lost market share for five straight years. We had gone from being number one in market cap to being last. Our forward PE was last in the market and terrible, and so I kind of laid that out for everybody. I think we had just gotten used to gently ceding market share as opposed to playing to win, and that just doesn't work for me. That's just not who I am. When I first came in, I think people were scared because I said that we wanted to play to win, we wanted to stop losing market share and start gaining market share. People were worried that was going to lead to a price war, but that's not the way that you compete. Anybody can compete on price. Price is easy. It's about competing on other parts of the value proposition where you can actually differentiate yourself and create lasting value. If somebody puts a promotion out there, I can match it now in two hours, so there's no competitive advantage to that. It's really about laying out the reality of where you are, and people don't want to be part of a losing team. Then the second thing a leader needs to do is inspire. Lay out a vision of what you want to do, how you are going to reclaim your market leadership, and then clearly lay out the path between reality and that vision. How are we going to get there, over what time frame, and what things are we going to chew off first and start to work on? It is going probably faster than I imagined. I'm proud of the team. I don't like giving out compliments that easily, but I am proud of where they are. There's a lot more work to do. So, you've talked about—you've given hints on some of that work that you want to do—moving away from low-margin, highly promotional activity, simplifying promotions, simplifying plans. Put some more flesh on those bones for us. What does that look like? Well, I think at the end of the day, the reason people leave Verizon is one, when we price up with no incremental value, and we had done that for three years in a row. We had done something like 30 price-ups with no incremental value, and that obviously distresses customers, so we had started to see our churn go up. I told the company, "The first rule of getting out of a hole is to stop digging." And so, we're just not going to raise prices without any corresponding value, and I'm beginning to see that as our churn starts to come down. The second is friction in the process. When you come into our store, how quickly can we help you? When we sell you something, is that reflected on your first bill? When you call into customer service, do you get somebody who is empathetic? Does what they see match with what you heard in the store and on your bill? And that's really, really difficult. That's the end-to-end customer experience, and that's hundreds, if not thousands, of things that you need to do better. We are focused on every single one of those incrementally getting better because I feel if we can have the best end-to-end customer experience, that's real differentiation. It's very hard to imitate and extremely hard to do, but extremely appreciated by customers. It's going to be hard for me as a customer who's been with Verizon for a very long time to notice even many incremental things. I might understand, okay, yeah, I do have a better customer experience. But is there something concrete that I should look forward to to keep me from, you know, taking that offer from T-Mobile or whatever? Yeah, there will be. Can you give us a little bit more of an indication? No. I, I want to be conscious... But to your point—I mean, the answer still is no, I won't—but I think that when we talk about kind of this end-to-end value proposition, there are little things, there are bigger things. There's how do you simplify things, how do you add elements of loyalty into what you're trying to go do. My view is there's never a big bang in what you're going to do in front of customers because if you can do a big bang, then your competitors can do a big bang. It's really, and unfortunately, it is like having a long-term plan and, as I say inside the company, every single day matters. I want to see incremental improvement every single day. So, learning to use the tools of our time, whether that be AI in how we serve customers much better, whether it be specific offers we put in front of you, or elements of the value proposition. When you see things, expect that there will be other things a month from now, the next month, the month after, because it's got to be a steady drumbeat of improvements. When it comes to using, encouraging your staff to use AI tools, what's your stance on that? And specifically, I think early on, a lot of businesses were saying, "Hey, get out there, use as much as you want, create things, create chatbots, build agents." Have you had to become more disciplined about that? And what are your marching orders when it comes to using AI tools to make either the workplace or the customer experience better? Well, I'm pretty outspoken on AI, as some of you may know. And I think as leaders of companies, we have a responsibility to talk about what could happen as a result of implementing AI. It's clear to me that whatever we have right now, these are the worst models that we will ever have in our lifetime because, in the next... Don't tell Daniela that. Yeah, she knows it. Like, she'd be the first to say it. I get your point. Yeah, OpenAI is going to release four new models between now and the end of the year. Each one of them will be a step function improvement from where they are today. And so, it's clear to me that AI will do at least three things inside the company. It will dramatically improve our productivity levels. It will dramatically improve our ability to satisfy customers. In the last three months, we've been experimenting with agents that are replacing some of our customer service reps. And those agents, their customer satisfaction rate is 1,280 basis points better than what we had before. Think about that. If it was 50 basis points, that'd be a big improvement. So, our ability to satisfy customers, and our ability to ingest all of the data around you, is dramatic. You may think of yourself as one customer, but we think of you as kind of like the sum total of what lines are under your account. Are your kids on your account? Where do they call? How do you use it? Do you have a Nest associated with your broadband service? We need to see all of that to be able to satisfy you. And then finally, AI is going to radically redefine our value proposition. My view of this is that we have an initiative inside the company called "Every customer has a name." That means every customer is individual. How we respond to you should be empathetic, but every part of our value proposition should be tailored to the 300-plus different ways that we can look at you and tailor a proposition to you. To what extent does AI just completely replace? If they're so much better, to what extent does AI replace human customer service agents completely? For sure, you're going to see disruption with AI in certain job functions. I don't see how that's not possible, and I don't see how anybody can look people in the eye and say that's not possible. What percentage of customer service gets displaced? It could be a large percentage of customer service. I think that function is a function that has a lot of rote parts of it. "I forgot my password" or "What's my billing amount?"—those are all simple and easy things for an agent to go do. More complex things will be a combination of human and machines working together to satisfy. What that doesn't mean—like, you will have functions that will be disrupted, for sure, and I don't see how anyone, again, could say that they won't be—but what we're seeing in our customer service right now is the rote stuff can be done by agents. More complex is a combination of an agent and a human working hand-in-hand to be able to satisfy that customer much better than either of them could do alone. And so, I think you'll see all of those things happen. But I'm going to lean heavy, as I think all of my colleagues will, and all of the Fortune 100 will lean heavy on AI. You cannot be a part of this age without understanding the technological revolution. It'll be AI. I think, personally, we get to AGI before this, like some are saying, but I think in the next two to four years we're in AGI. I think, in the next four years, we're at Quantum. We're already preparing for Q-Day as early as two years away because we need to look at all the encryption pieces of this. And then, you're at humanoid robotics a couple of years after that. That's all happening, and we need to be ready as a society, as leaders of corporations, and then we need to take the responsibility of that. So for instance, I think I was one of the first to pull together a pretty wide-scale... we put aside $20 million to train and reskill people that are impacted by AI. That's the tip of the iceberg for what we're going to go do. My view is we have a responsibility to put serious money, resource, and effort into training not just our employees, not just people that are impacted, but the communities we serve as well, in utilizing AI in a responsible way. But as I say to everybody inside the company, you should not be scared of AI. You need to learn AI. You need to be able to use it in your work. You need to be able to use it in your personal life because that's the age we live in. One of the first moves you made was a difficult move, admittedly, in eliminating a lot of jobs at Verizon to the tune of 13,000. You set up this fund, 20 million, and you said that's just kind of the tip of the iceberg. What are some of the early takeaways from that, and what do companies need to do to reskill workers who are affected by, you know, whether they're in customer service or some other area that's affected by AI? Yeah. Well, first of all, we're training everybody inside the corporation. We do week-long events now, and that can be from some people who don't even know the right prompts to go use and how to use basic chatbots, to being able to gin up agents. We have a whole AI tech stack that we are now implementing, most of which will be done next month in July, and all of it finished by November. But you just have to move quickly; you have to train. We have 7,000 people in this reskilling that have already applied for that, but as I mentioned, I think our responsibility is to work with government. I'm spending a lot of time down in DC to think about how we take our resources, maybe the resources of the Fortune 500 as well. Think about if we're putting 50 to 100 million into it, and you multiply that by, you know, my peer set and then what government can do, you could have a lot of money going into thinking about reskilling, retraining, and how to really utilize AI as hopefully a force that can unleash the best parts of humanity as opposed to a force that, you know, could add a lot of stress into our democracy. Speaking of powerful tools, briefly, in our last few seconds, I'd like your... We are out of time. They don't need to know that. 000. Recently joined Project Glasswing, given your background in cybersecurity, give us a sense of now that you've had a chance to get in there and test it, how powerful is it? What does it do? Should we be afraid of it? Well, you should always be concerned about cybersecurity. I mean, it is existential. Everything we do is controlled by software—the elevators you go in, the planes that you board, your financials, everything. I don't know how much money you have in your pocket right now, but, you know, it's probably not a lot. And all of your wealth is digital in some way. So, I think... and, you know, a consumer has their identity stolen every two seconds. So, I think cyber is existential for us. Mythos, which we've been working with as part of Glasswing, is a tool that can identify a lot of elements that might not have been seen as critical, but when you link them together, they become a critical infrastructure thing that needs to be repaired. Mythos is very powerful. It's enabling us to identify vulnerabilities but also fix them quite quickly, as well. But it's also forcing us to think about how we ingest third-party vendors into our network and how everything needs to be patchable remotely. Within the next couple of months, 85% of everything done in our network, instead of taking multiple hours or days to figure out what's going on, we will identify it in minutes, go right to the element, and fix it autonomously. If the government continues to designate Kaspersky a national security threat given its concerns about how its tools are used in defense, how problematic is that for you as a company? Well, we have access to it, so I would say Mythos had a head start, but all of these models catch up very quickly. I think OpenAI's model around cyber is extremely powerful, as well. And as I mentioned, every two to four months, you know, these models double in strength and capacity. So, I think Mythos was a real good look at what the capabilities of these models can do in specific functional areas. And, you know, I'm glad, because we do a lot for the critical infrastructure of the United States, and Mythos is going to help us protect it.

Conversations 2026: AI-Enabled Messaging Innovation for Developers

Meta Developers(308.0K)·81:46·41.6K views·2026-06-04
Mark ZuckerbergCEO, Meta
TL;DRAt Meta's fifth annual Conversations conference, Meta announced the global expansion of the Meta Business Agent and the Meta Business Agent Platform, allowing businesses of all sizes to automate end-to-end customer engagement, lead qualification,…
and transaction...

TL;DR

At Meta's fifth annual Conversations conference, Meta announced the global expansion of the Meta Business Agent and the Meta Business Agent Platform, allowing businesses of all sizes to automate end-to-end customer engagement, lead qualification, and transaction processing directly within WhatsApp, Messenger, and Instagram.

Meta's AI Agent Strategy & The Evolution of Business Messaging

  • The Messaging Paradigm Shift: Meta leadership highlighted that messaging has evolved from simple chat threads into the core "storefront, website, and customer relationship" platform for businesses, handling over one billion daily business-consumer conversations across WhatsApp, Messenger, and Instagram Direct.
  • Meta Business Agent:
      • Global Rollout: Now available worldwide on WhatsApp Business and Messenger, with an Instagram expansion currently underway.
      • Turnkey Setup: Small businesses can deploy the agent in minutes. It trains itself directly on pre-existing business assets, including Facebook pages, Instagram accounts, and product catalogs.
      • Monetization Model: Free to start, the agent will eventually be rolled out as part of Meta One paid subscription offerings.
  • Meta Business Agent Platform (Enterprise): Designed for larger enterprises requiring robust compliance and customization, allowing them to connect existing systems (such as Shopee and Zendesk) via APIs. It operates on four core layers:
      • Knowledge: Grounding AI responses in approved, company-specific sources of truth (websites, policies, catalogs) with visible source attribution.
      • Skills: Establishing behavioral guardrails, custom workflows, and smart handoff rules to seamlessly transfer complex queries to human agents with full context.
      • Tools: Enabling autonomous actions like scheduling appointments, querying stock levels, and completing sales in-thread.
      • Intelligence: Spotting patterns, generating automated customer re-engagement campaigns, and delivering real-time competitive business insights.

Enterprise-Scale Conversational Commerce

  • Movida (Brazilian Car Rental Franchise): CEO Gustavo Moscatelli presented a fully integrated, AI-powered WhatsApp booking flow for their fleet of 270,000+ vehicles.
      • End-to-End Transactions: Customers can search for cars, select insurance options, finalize bookings, and make payments entirely within WhatsApp.
      • Immediate Business Impact: The AI agent doubled Movida's sales conversion rate in its first week of deployment.
  • Air France KLM: General Manager Jérôme Salemi outlined a comprehensive, multilingual servicing and marketing lifecycle integrated into WhatsApp.
      • High Engagement Metrics: In one year, the airline exchanged 33 million messages with passengers. Marketing newsletters achieved a 72% open rate (2x email) and a 10% click-through rate (5x email).
      • Automated Servicing: Currently, one-third of customer service inquiries are completely handled by AI, with the remaining two-thirds operating on a hybrid AI-to-human model.
  • PLDT Home (Philippine Telco): VP Di Blanco detailed using Business AI on Messenger as an automated triage system.
      • Pre-Qualification: The AI handles high volumes of basic FAQs and filters prospects, delivering highly qualified, "ready-to-buy" leads directly to the internal sales team.

Unlocking Operational Efficiency and Sales for Small Businesses

  • Time Savings & Scale:
      • Albor Arte Mx: Jewelry designer Verónica Guevara saved 80% of her message-handling time, letting the Business AI automate repetitive catalog and delivery questions so she could focus on product creation.
      • Bordinhon Móveis: Furniture owners Bruno and Priscila Bordinhon grew their customer handling capacity from 5–10 contacts a day to 30–40. The AI was fully trained in one day and automatically handles complex voice-note inquiries from customers.
      • Tec Auto-trafo: Transformer manufacturer Luciano do Nascimento scaled his customer capacity to handle 30 inquiries simultaneously, boosting his sales closure rates from 10–15% to over 50%.
  • High-Yield "Click-to-Message" Campaigns:
      • Donna Capilli: Hair care brand owner Jennifer Martins scaled sales from 12–15 a day to 100–120 a day utilizing "Click to WhatsApp" ads, necessitating the opening of a new logistics hub in São Paulo.
      • Dining Couture: Creative Director Ami Kothari achieved a 16% customer conversion rate for her styling workshops, gaining 22 sales from a single ad spend of just 204 rupees.
      • Redpro USA: Pool safety business owner Andy Biet moved 95% of his marketing budget to "Click to Instagram Direct" ads, converting 60% of qualified leads into buyers.

Next-Generation WhatsApp Features and Partner Integrations

  • Frictionless Business Discovery:
      • Usernames: Users will soon be able to search for and message businesses directly by their company username, eliminating the need to input or remember phone numbers.
      • AI Searchability: AI-powered business agents will be discoverable directly within the WhatsApp search bar.
      • Rich Menus: Sharing a business contact card or link inside a WhatsApp group chat will display dynamic, interactive menus (e.g., "view menu" or "book reservation") directly in-thread.
  • In-Thread Shopping Demostration: WhatsApp leader Alice demonstrated a live Deliveroo integration. The conversational AI captured the user's location, recommended local bakeries (Crème), displayed popular cookie selections, and completed a 12-pack order and checkout flow without ever leaving the chat interface.
  • Meta Business Partners:
      • GUMAC & Haravan: Vietnamese fashion brand GUMAC partnered with Haravan to automate orders coming through Facebook Live streams, driving a 7-fold increase in Return on Ad Spend (ROAS) and reducing the cost per order by 5x.
      • Dalin Shabu & V Rich: Dalin Shabu utilized partner V Rich to automate backend product catalog management and reservation booking straight from Facebook Live Video Ads into Messenger.

Notable Quotes

"A couple of years ago, I said that every business would have an AI agent, just like you all have an email address, a website, and a social media account, and that's happening faster than I expected." — Mark Zuckerberg, CEO of Meta
"Messaging lets you build customer relationships at scale. But there's a ceiling on what any team can handle. AI breaks through that ceiling." — Naomi, Meta Presenter
"We consolidated the entire client journey into a single AI-powered conversation with no friction or any limitations that traditional apps have." — Gustavo Moscatelli, CEO of Movida
"WhatsApp has transformed how we serve our customers. It's not just a communication channel, it's a lifeline throughout the entire travel experience." — Jérôme Salemi, GM of Air France KLM (UK & Ireland)
Business AI on WhatsApp has taken away the most tedious part, which is answering repetitive questions, and it also helps filter so that I can follow up on the chats of the people who really want to buy. Hi, my name is Verónica Guevara, founder of Albor Arte Mx. At Albor Arte Mx, we promote the beauty and cultural richness of Mexican artisanal jewelry. The biggest challenge at Albor Arte Mx is time. I am the only one managing Albor Arte Mx, but I also have another job, so hours can go by without me checking WhatsApp. All of this caused me a lot of stress because I know that a good immediate response can make the difference between a purchase or losing a sale. The constant flow of repetitive questions took away time from more creative tasks, such as taking photos, uploading products to the online store, or even helping customers when they visit the showroom. Business AI on WhatsApp has helped me a lot. It takes away the stress of worrying about customers waiting on a response. Now I know they will receive an immediate response and also in a friendly way, with the personality and warmth of Albor. Business AI on WhatsApp answers the most common questions like sending catalogs, costs, delivery times, etc. It filters messages with higher purchase intent and helps me make better use of my time. I would dare to say that I've saved almost 80% of the time spent answering messages. Before, I didn't even dare to advertise on WhatsApp because there wouldn't be anyone to answer the messages, and now, messages are answered automatically. Business AI is a very easy tool to implement. You just go into WhatsApp, business tools, then Business AI, and it automatically shows you the questions so you can start using the tool. It also has a super cool feature, which is that you can manually intervene in any conversation in cases where the query got too complex, or because the customer is ready to close a sale. I would tell entrepreneurs to give it a try. It's very easy to implement, and honestly, it's free. It's a win-win, both for customers and for entrepreneurs. At Dalin Shabu, we wanted to bring the energy and flavours of our restaurant to a wider audience. Hello, I’m Chakorn Pornpattanapan, owner at Dalin Shabu restaurant. With live shopping on Facebook, we found a way to showcase our authentic mala shabu-shabu meals and connect directly with customers, no matter where they are. Using live video ads that click to Messenger, optimised for leads, was a turning point. We expanded the reach of our live stream, and by funnelling viewer outreach to Messenger, we can answer questions, take bookings, and process orders instantly during our live stream. The entire experience is seamless for our customers. Our goal was to make this powerful technology simple for Dalin Shabu. We automated the backend, from managing their product catalog in the live stream to capturing every order through Messenger. This ensures no opportunity is missed. The system manages reservations directly in chat, freeing the Dalin Shabu team to focus on creating great food and engaging their community. Partnering with V Rich gave us access to the latest Meta tools and the expertise to use them effectively. We’ve seen a significant increase in customer engagement and bookings. This isn’t just about sales; it’s about building lasting customer relationships. We are now expanding with new branches and product lines, all fueled by this partnership. Meta Business Partners can help you do more with conversations. When we see our guests, and when they come and tell me, "Oh, I have visited your cafe for the third time", I feel that... oh my God, I have achieved everything. Hi, I am Ashish. Hi, I’m Akshada, and we are the owners of Quattro Bistro Lonavala. Quattro Bistro Lonavala is an outdoor cafe that offers an open garden, cozy seating, as well as comfort food. And for all the couples, we do a lot of customizable setups like table decor and catering for villas. We started using ads that click to WhatsApp because six months into the business we thought we needed to reach out to more people. Ads that click to WhatsApp just make sense for us because the guests can directly make reservations or even customize their services by directly talking to us. If it is a catering service, we understand their food requirements and customize the menu accordingly. And if it is a table decoration, we understand the occasion they are decorating the table for and then we take it forward from there. I had a trick in detailed targeting: I actually selected the demographic services of people who just got married in the last 6 months. Especially, we targeted audiences from Pune and Mumbai. When we ran ads that click to WhatsApp on Valentine's Day, we saw that 10 couples had celebrated their anniversary with us. We did a lot of table decoration, and we did candlelight dinners. But at the end of the day, we realized we are also known to be the most romantic place in Lonavala. Like, now, our whole marketing has completely depended on click to WhatsApp for our campaign. As a young entrepreneur, I believe that you should take that leap of faith. Instagram ads that click to WhatsApp gave us that platform to reach millions of people with a very small amount that we had put in ads. It gave me the financial stability and freedom that I always wanted in my life. By embedding Business AI on Messenger into our sales process, we've created a more efficient and engaging pre-qualification funnel. Hi, I'm Di Blanco, and I'm the vice president for Online Sales at PLDT Home. PLDT is the largest telco in the Philippines. A major challenge was managing the immense volume of customer inquiries effectively, and scaling our team to meet that demand while managing quality was a significant hurdle. We wanted to grow our internal sales channel, but we recognized that our customers needed someone to talk to. Business AI on Messenger allowed us to meet this demand by providing instant guided interactions that feel personal and close. Business AI acts as a triage system. This filters out the basic inquiries and allows the AI agent to hand over primed, high-intent customers to our team—people who are essentially ready to buy. We teach the Business AI by feeding it more data, testing the unseen data, and adjusting the accuracy until it improves. There is an element of adjustment there, but eventually it becomes easier. Being the first in our industry to deploy Business AI is a point of pride. It empowers our team by freeing them up to focus on more complex, high-value interactions that require a human touch. This balance of efficiency and personalized service is key to our future growth and our vision of keeping Filipino families connected. Ads that click to Instagram Direct have helped me reach the length and breadth of the country and helped me fill up my workshop seats super fast. I'm Ami Kothari, and I'm a tablescape artist, and I'm the creative director and founder at Dining Couture. At Dining Couture, we specialize in table decor and table styling workshops, and we help curate tableware accessories for hotels, caterers, and families. So when I started getting queries for my table styling workshops from different parts of the country, I really didn't know how to fill up the seats. That's when a friend of mine told me about Instagram ads. Now, I'm somebody who's not tech-savvy, but I tried playing around with the Instagram ads. They are very easy to use and extremely user friendly. So I directed my ads to Instagram Direct, which encouraged people from that location to reach out directly in my Instagram inbox. Instagram allows you to target your ads to a particular city, to the kind of audience that you want to reach out to depending on what you are doing. For my workshops, they are more targeted to homemakers and women of a certain age bracket, of a certain interest. I would put table decor, homemakers. Those are my typical target audiences that I choose. For my last workshop, we had a 16% conversion rate. We had an ad of 204 rupees, and we had 22 sales out of that. Dining Couture has been a journey I never thought I would have. It helped me build a community across the country. And ads that click to Instagram Direct really help you cut through the clutter and get you genuine leads which actually convert. Business AI on WhatsApp completely transformed our business. Today we have more time to run promotions in the store, and we have more time with our family. So it didn't just change our business model, it also changed our lives. I am Bruno Bordinhon. I am Priscila Bordinhon. And we are the owners of Bordinhon Móveis. As husband and wife, our business story goes far beyond just selling furniture. Together, we make a team. I am responsible for marketing. I handle all the logistics, from the customer’s order until the delivery to their house. Before Business AI on WhatsApp, our phones never left our hands. It was an absurd frequency of messages outside of business hours. Today we have more time, so we can manage to do things that we didn't have time to do before. In the past, we were able to handle between 5 to 10 new contacts per day. And today, with Business AI, we can easily respond to 30 or 40. The more you customize your campaign, the more it will respond. To train Business AI, it was very smooth and very intuitive. We already have a website with all the products cataloged. It automatically learns everything that we have through our website. I did all the training in one day. Even our way of speaking, it has already adapted and adjusted to that. So it is really amazing; it's not a robotic thing. Once a customer came by the store, he sent a 19-second voice message. Business AI identified the item in question and sent the link directly to the customer. The customer saw it, liked it, and placed an order via WhatsApp right away. We know that it is responding as if it were us. It is an extension of our store, it's an extension of us, of me and Priscila. It revolutionized our company, our customer service, our time, our space. So there is really no explanation or words to express how much it has been super relevant in our lives. The way Fridum operates, especially with ads that click to Messenger, mirrors how I use Facebook. It's like saying, "I'm here, ask me anything," and I'm always ready to share everything. My name is Đan Anh, I'm the founder as well as the main manager of the healthy bakery Fridum. This bakery carries the mission of helping people fully enjoy the pleasure of eating cakes and drinking milk tea, while feeling completely at ease about the ingredients and nutrients they're putting into their bodies. When I started, I used to set the objective of my Facebook ads so the ads were mainly for people to see and react to. But I realized that the customers who were really interested in healthy food would always ask questions. They really want to know before making a decision. So after seeing this kind of engagement through the comments, I noticed the “click to Messenger” option and thought, “Yeah, let's just invite people to message us directly.” Customers can ask any questions they want, and the business stands there, listening and interacting with them. This builds trust and increases the business’s credibility and accountability. With ads that click to Messenger, you know exactly how many people have messaged you. You can also easily see which orders have been confirmed based on those messages. It's straightforward and easy to track. It helps increase the credibility of the business and helps create a bond and connection with the customers. For a typical campaign, if I spend around 1-2 million VND, I usually get around 20 to 30 messages. And those who click to Messenger, I feel super confident about them. I feel sure that person will turn into a customer. It's that message exchange that makes them feel we are approachable and creates a sense of connection with the bakery. The use of ads that click to Messenger, I think it shows my readiness to connect. It is a chance for us to establish a genuine human connection with customers. These days, I am chatting with Business AI every day. And it keeps evolving every day. Every day there’s progress; it never stops evolving. I am Luciano do Nascimento, and I am the owner of Tec Auto-trafo. The transformer is a device that is used to correct a voltage. These products of mine mainly serve machinery, solar energy, and electric car chargers. My passion for electricity started 30 years ago. When I started, I didn't have any sales experience, so I didn't have a client portfolio. So I had to figure out how to attract these clients. I started running ads that click to WhatsApp via Instagram. From those ads, I started to attract people who were interested. That's how, from there, I entered the market. When I started using Business AI on WhatsApp, it was an incredible innovation, because when I was assisting a customer who had a lot of questions, I had to spend a lot of time with them, answering their questions and providing technical information. Another client sometimes would call me or send me a message and I wouldn't be able to attend to them. Then, by the time I went to assist that customer, they would have already lost interest. After the arrival of Business AI, in two hours I can attend to around 30 customers at once. In the span of two hours, from one customer to 30, it’s a tremendous evolution, even more so for me, since I’m just getting started. As I kept feeding Business AI on WhatsApp, it was already acquiring more knowledge, and it was responding even more technically. Nowadays, it even responds to audio messages. The client asks me a question via audio, and it replies. In these six months, there’s been a big difference — very significant. For every 20 quotes, I would close two or three sales. Not anymore. Like, for every 30 quotes, I can close more than 50%. Since I no longer spend so much time stuck on the phone, I spend more time teaching my children the trade, teaching how to do a project, which takes a lot of time, both mine and theirs, to learn. An advice I would give for those who are just starting out, just like I did: use Business AI on WhatsApp. It will make a big difference in customer service and sales acquisition. At GUMAC, we create more than just clothing. Our vision of 'Happiness Wear' aims to inspire confidence in women, so they not only look beautiful on the outside but also feel happy on the inside. For over a decade, we brought this vision to life, pioneering with Facebook Live in Vietnam, turning livestreams into a cornerstone of our fashion brand. GUMAC persistently tested and improved through each live session. We connect with our audience through regular livestreams, broadcasting twice daily and generating up to 70% of our online leads through Facebook livestreams. But despite record viewership, our next challenge was to turn that massive engagement into actual business results. GUMAC had built a powerful community through Facebook Live. Haravan's goal was to provide the technology to unlock its full potential, converting views into orders by connecting customers with real shopping needs via Live and creating a seamless shopping journey from clicking on the ad to closing the sale. First, we supported GUMAC in reaching the right target audience by sending purchase events to Meta. Meta's algorithm then distributes ads to potential buyers through livestream ads that click to Messenger, guiding the right target to watch the live stream. From there, our Live Shopping tools take over, turning viewers into shoppers. Next, the live shopping tools will display product lists to help livestream viewers easily select and purchase. The entire journey, from viewing a pinned product to completing the payment, takes place directly within Messenger. And with automated ordering, GUMAC can close deals around the clock, even with minimal staff during night shifts, ensuring no opportunity is missed. Especially in the recent Mega Sales campaign, when implementing Live video ads that click to Messenger, we saw a 7-fold increase in Return on Ad Spend for Live. At the same time, the cost per order decreased significantly, more than 5 times lower than before. Haravan's automation gave GUMAC the confidence to handle peak demand and deliver the happiness their customers expect, more efficiently than ever. With this outstanding result as a foundation, GUMAC will continue to increase investment in livestream selling on Facebook in the near future. Meta Business Partners can help you do more with conversations. What inspired me to start Donna Capilli was a personal experience of mine. I wanted my hair to grow longer, and I used my knowledge of chemical techniques to start mixing a few ingredients to make a shampoo for myself. A friend saw it, liked it, and recommended it to another friend, who then recommended it to someone else. Before I knew it, we were selling it. I’m Jennifer Martins, and I'm the owner of Donna Capilli here in Uberlândia. Donna Capilli offers a complete line of hair care products, including shampoo, conditioner, tonics, and masks, all focused on hair care. When the business started, I relied heavily on word of mouth from my clients. I didn't know how advertising worked; I thought it was too complicated for me to replicate. Then I took a Meta Blueprint course, and I learned how ads work and how simple they are to use. I realized I could manage these ads myself for the business. Now, my clients see an ad on Instagram, they click on it, and are taken to WhatsApp. They chat with a hair care specialist and, in just a few minutes, discover what their hair needs and complete their purchase. All within the same app. With ads that click to WhatsApp, I was able to reach a much larger audience. We've seen at least a 50% increase in Uberlândia and a 40% increase across Brazil. Before using ads that click to WhatsApp, we averaged 12 to 15 sales per day. With ads that click to WhatsApp, we've grown to 100 to 120 sales in a single day. We even had to open a branch in São Paulo to meet the logistical demand and to serve all of Brazil much more efficiently and with greater quality. We have a resale program where I train women in my community so they can sell these products, earn their own income, and write their own stories. So today we sell to all the states in Brazil, thanks to ads that click to WhatsApp. Ads that click to Instagram Direct is a game changer. My focus now is on the strategy of the company and not on the sales of the company. So I have more time for thinking what’s coming for the future, and not spending time trying to find that client who is going to make us grow. Hi, my name is Andy Biet, and I'm the owner of Redpro USA. We've been in business in Miami, USA, for ten years now. Redpro is a safety business. We offer safety for householders like pool fences, balcony nets, and safety doors. In Redpro, we started using ads that click to Instagram Direct because I’ve been in marketing my whole life, and I saw that the way we were doing things in Redpro was not working. In my last campaign I received 400 messages, of which 60% were replied to by Business AI on WhatsApp. My name is David Alcántara and I am the owner of Guitarras David Alcántara. I'm a guitar teacher, a musician, and a guitar designer, and they all feed into one another. Business AI on WhatsApp keeps my business running 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, so my customers are always getting answers when they need them. It has had a huge impact on my life because it has allowed me to save time that I previously had to spend on answering repetitive questions. It's a way to scale your time and productivity. Hi, London, and welcome to everyone tuning in live around the world. This is our fifth annual Conversations conference, and our first time hosting in this incredible city. Thank you, thank you so much for having us. For businesses, there is a transformation happening in how we engage with our customers. It wasn't a long time ago that every business needed a physical store. And after the internet, every business needed a website. And today, every business needs messaging. This is true if you're a retailer with 10 employees or an airline with 100,000. Messaging has become the best way to engage customers where they already are. There are over one billion conversations between people and businesses every day on our messaging apps: WhatsApp, Messenger, and Instagram Direct. This includes your first-time shopper messaging a local boutique to a bank customer resolving a claim in minutes instead of days. And what's struck me is how a chat thread isn't just a series of messages anymore. For hundreds of millions of businesses around the world, messaging is the storefront, the website, the sales floor, the support desk, the entire customer relationship. But for every messaging success story, like all of the WhatsApp-first businesses, there are also real pain points. First, messages go unread or get delayed responses. And the bigger you grow, the harder it is to maintain quality and consistency. Third, bots feel like bots. Your customers are already messaging you on WhatsApp, billions of conversations every day. So the question isn't whether messaging matters, it's whether you're getting the most out of every interaction. That's why today we're launching the Meta Business Agent platform, a suite of new APIs and tools that help to transform your business with AI. Think of it in layers — layers that apply to any business of any size. First is knowledge. Your agent needs to know your business like your team does: your products, your policies, your brand voice, all grounded in your source of truth data. Next is skills. These are the rules of the road: what your agent should do, how far it can go, and when to hand off to a real person. In other words, skills structure your workflows and your desired outcomes. Then tools. This is where the agent can take action, like placing orders, making appointments, or taking payments, all connected to the systems you already run, working behind the scenes and autonomously taking action so you never miss a sale. And finally, intelligence. This is where it all clicks. Your agent doesn't just take action, it learns. It can spot patterns, surface insights, and get smarter over time. Combined with your own data, it can drive stronger results for your business and grow your bottom line. These layers are the foundation of our new agent platform, and here's how your business can leverage them. To speak for your business, an agent needs to know your business. That means every response is grounded in your approved sources: your catalog, your websites, your return policies. Connect your agent to your internal systems through our APIs or leverage any of the hundreds of out-of-the-box integrations like Shopee or Zendesk. And the agent shows its work. You can see exactly which source it pulled from to generate a response. And when the agent isn't confident, it says so: "I'm not certain about that. Let me connect you with someone who can confirm." Because when your customer asks "Where's my order?", they want a real answer, not a guess. And the best part, the agent keeps getting smarter. The more you connect, the better it gets. No coding required. The agent learns from your ongoing conversations and improves itself over time. Once the agent knows your business, it needs instructions and guardrails. That's where skills come in. They tell the agent what to do and how to do it, and importantly, what not to do. This can be as simple as answering common customer questions or as sophisticated as looking up a specific order, checking a customer's loyalty status, and then offering a refund. And one of the most important skills is knowing when to pause. When the conversation needs a human representative, the agent can hand off seamlessly with full context so your team can pick up where it left off. And if you're in a regulated industry like banking or healthcare, we're building for that. Every conversation is properly attributed, every interaction verified with the compliance guardrails that your legal team needs. And with stacked skills, you can create full end-to-end customer journeys for your agent: qualify a lead, book an appointment, and then update your CRM every time. Let's bring up Brazil's top car rental franchise, Movida, to show you how they doubled their conversion rate in the first week with a Meta Business Agent. Thank you, James. Thank you, everyone. Together they help turn conversations into business outcomes. And here's Air France on how they're transforming their business and the way they engage with customers. Thank you. Thank you. Good afternoon everyone. I'm Jérôme Salemi, General Manager of Air France KLM for the UK and for Ireland. It's an honor to represent Air France, a leading airline connecting Europe and the rest of the world here on Meta's largest business stage. When you travel, every moment matters — from booking to boarding, from check-in to your bags' delivery. You need the right information at the right time in the right place. For years, we relied on legacy channels to communicate with our customers. But emails go unread, customers are hard to reach, and even with a severe disruption, our call center may be overwhelmed. Therefore, in 2022 we made a strategic move. We needed to meet our customers where they already are. So we brought the entire Air France experience into WhatsApp. We started with service messages using intelligent chatbots that can answer questions about your trip instantly. We have a seamless hand over to a human agent when needed, available 7 days a week in five languages across 22 countries. The results were immediate. WhatsApp became our number-two digital contact channel. From there, we built across the full customer lifecycle. Utility messages: 30 hours before departure you receive your check-in reminder on WhatsApp. You receive relevant updates: boarding gate, gate changes, and the belt number to collect your luggage on arrival, all inside the same conversation. Then we launched the ancillary messages, or revenue-generating messages, offering personalized offers for upgrades in our premium cabins, a better seat, extra luggage, and lounge access right there in the chat. And we extended WhatsApp to become a marketing channel, sharing special deals, tactical campaigns, or inspirational messages on destinations and loyalty rewards, all sent to customers who explicitly opted in. Finally, we also handle authentication, two-factor verification, and Flying Blue loyalty program check-ins, all within WhatsApp. Let me share what this means for our business. In just one year, Air France exchanged 33 million messages with our passengers on WhatsApp. For marketing messages, we achieved a 72% open rate, twice as high as for emails, and a 10% click-through rate, which is five times higher than emails. Today we have one-third of the servicing messages handled with AI only, while two-thirds are hybrid: AI plus a human agent. And in terms of customer satisfaction, it's very high, with 96% of our opted-in customers reporting being happy with our marketing newsletter and 60% scoring our service messages four or five out of five. Fantastic. Looking ahead, we're expanding to more markets, more languages, and deeper personalization. And of course, it wouldn't be a look at the future without AI. We are experimenting with how AI can make these conversations even more intuitive, more fluid, anticipating what a customer needs before they ask. Our vision is simple: Air France wants to be in contact with each customer in one conversation on the platform of their choice. WhatsApp has transformed how we serve our customers. For large businesses like Air France who are handling many conversations every day, our agent pricing will be volume-based, scaling with how much you use. We're building towards a world where every business has a smart, always-on business agent that knows their business, serves their customers, and drives growth, all in one place, accessible in the apps where your customers already are. This is where we're going and we want to build with you. Okay, at this point you've listened to enough American accents. Let's bring out our resident Brit, Alice. For businesses, that means building ways for you and your customers to get more done right in a chat. Here's what that looks like when we bring AI into the fabric of WhatsApp and how it connects your business to more than 3 billion potential customers. I have a few new features to share, some starting today and others coming soon. We're going to make it easier for more people to discover your business in the first place. We've already made this possible in a couple of ways: with ads on Facebook and Instagram that click straight into a WhatsApp chat and ads in Status, which we introduced last year so your business can get discovered directly within the app. But there's more we can do to make WhatsApp work harder for you. Here's an example. A few months ago, I saw a clever ad from Oatly, the oat milk company, on the side of a bus. It had their phone number and a prompt to reach out on WhatsApp. I grabbed my phone, but before I could type in the number, the bus had moved on. It would have been much more useful if I could have just typed "Oatly" into WhatsApp rather than trying to remember a phone number I'd seen for approximately three seconds. This is what usernames can unlock: a simpler way for people to find your business and start a conversation without the friction. So, if someone in the school group chat mentions a kids' clothing shop that they love, I can type the first few letters of their name into WhatsApp search, see them appear, and start a conversation right there, knowing I'll get a quick, helpful response because they're a business agent user. Now, when a friend in a WhatsApp group shares a link to a new restaurant, I'll see options to make a reservation, see the menu, and more. You'll see a rich menu under the contact details showing how you can interact with that business via their agent. There's no need to switch over to a separate app; the business agent is right here where you're already chatting. Of course, bringing customers to your chat is only the first step. What matters next is the experience and whether it's good enough that they want to come back. Whether it's booking a holiday, making an online supermarket order, or ordering a takeaway, we want people to be able to do all of this within the chat in a few clicks, with AI adding speed, personalization, and convenience. So, we're making it possible for customers to book an appointment directly in chat, to fill out a structured form so your business gets the details you need without the back and forth, and to complete an entire checkout flow from finding something they like through to paying for it, all in one place. Let's go back to the example earlier about sharing a restaurant link in a group chat. Let me get my phone. Okay, say I'm chatting with a friend about a talk I'm giving in front of, oh, about 1,500 people, as it happens, and I'd like to know how to make it even better than last year. My friend helpfully suggests bribing the audience with cookies and shares Deliveroo's contact info. And I agree, so let's do it. I'm going to click on the link to start a chat. "Hi there, I want to order some cookies. Please can you recommend some local bakeries?" Waiting... let's see what it has to say. Okay, food discovery assistant: "Where would you like to order to?" Send location. Okay, send my current location. That's pretty straightforward. What happens next? "Found some brilliant spots nearby." Okay, oh man, these all look amazing. Some favorites in here as well. I think I'm going to go with the one it recommended, which is Crème. Okay, I'll go with Crème, please, and can you tell me what are some of their most popular chocolate cookies? That would definitely be my favorite. I hope you all feel the same. Here we go: "Classic milk, vegan double chocolate, triple chocolate and sea salt." Oh man, these look amazing. Okay, I think I'm going to have to go for the sampler. I think I'll get the box of 12, please, with a selection of all of the other ones that you recommended. Does this work? Let's see what happens next. "Added the box of 12." That's great. Delivery fee... yeah, this all looks right. So, I'm going to go to checkout and then I'm done. Okay, well, if that worked, the cookies should be here in a few minutes. And don't worry, even though I only ordered 12, the team is going to bring along enough for everyone. So, that's just one example of what the future of AI-enabled chat experiences can look like. But watch this space, because there's a lot more to come. Now here's Naomi again. Thanks very much. Earlier today, I told you how the chat thread is the new storefront or website. But after everything you've seen from Fred, James, Alice, and businesses on the stage, I actually think it's bigger than that. It is the heartbeat of the customer relationship. Every message a customer sends you is an opportunity. And until now, most businesses couldn't capitalize on that opportunity at scale. You couldn't be in every conversation. You couldn't remember every customer. You couldn't convert every chat into a sale. Now you can. That's what the Meta Business Agent unlocks. Speaking of unlock, your cookies have arrived! Okay, let's recap. Today we've announced: one, the expansion of our Meta Business Agent to businesses of all sizes globally; two, how Meta Business Agent can help you run your business behind the scenes; three, our new Meta Business Agent platform; and four, new features that help people discover and engage with your businesses on WhatsApp, Messenger, and Instagram. We started this journey because billions of conversations were beginning on our messaging apps, but not all of them were ending in results. Today, we showed you how that changes. The businesses that move quickly—your businesses—will build those relationships and close those sales. So here's my ask: don't wait. Start building with us. You won't just adapt to this transformation, you will define it. Thank you again for joining us, and enjoy the rest of Conversations. Goodbye.

Murray Asks Rubio Point Blank: 'Did You Advise The President Against The War' In Iran?

Forbes Breaking News(5.6M)·12:07·60.4K views·2026-06-04
Patty MurrayUS Senator (D-WA) Marco RubioUS Secretary of State
TL;DRSen.
Patty Murray (D-WA) sharply criticized Secretary of State and National Security Advisor Marco Rubio over the ongoing war in Iran, a proposed $1.5 trillion defense budget, and deep cuts to diplomacy and global health. Rubio defended the administration's military...

TL;DR

Sen. Patty Murray (D-WA) sharply criticized Secretary of State and National Security Advisor Marco Rubio over the ongoing war in Iran, a proposed $1.5 trillion defense budget, and deep cuts to diplomacy and global health. Rubio defended the administration's military strategy as a necessary strike against a nuclear threat and argued that State Department structural reforms have made foreign aid delivery faster and more efficient.

The War in Iran and Executive Accountability

  • Debate Over Private Counsel: Sen. Murray pressed Secretary Rubio on whether he advised the President against launching the war in Iran.
      • Rubio refused to disclose his private advice, stating it would be "unwise" and "unfair" to break confidentiality, but affirmed his strong support for the President’s decision.
  • Strategic Threat Justification:
      • Preventing Nuclear Breakout: Rubio argued the military action was necessary because Iran was on the verge of developing a nuclear weapon behind a conventional shield that would have become "impenetrable" within a year.
  • Critique of Campaign Promises: Murray pointed out that the conflict has lasted four months despite Rubio’s previous claims that it would take "weeks, not months."
      • She noted the war has resulted in hundreds of American casualties and billions of dollars spent, contradicting administration promises of lower prices and "no new wars."

State Department Budget and Structural Reforms

  • The $1.5 Trillion War Budget: Murray criticized the administration's budget priorities, noting it allocates $1.5 trillion to war while proposing a 40% cut to global health programs and offering zero funding to lower domestic childcare or healthcare costs.
  • Reorganizing the State Department: Rubio disputed the characterization that the State Department was "gutted," claiming it was one of the least impacted government agencies.
      • Personnel Reductions: Rubio stated that no overseas foreign service employees were laid off; staff reductions were concentrated in the career civil service.
      • Empowering Regional Bureaus: The administration eliminated functional bureaus to centralize power under regional bureaus, a structural change Rubio claims is already improving operational efficiency.
  • Alleged Mismanagement: Murray highlighted severe operational and morale issues within the department, including:
      • Leaving 500 tons of food aid to rot and eventually be incinerated in ports.
      • The abrupt firing of thousands of dedicated public servants.
      • Prioritizing vanity projects, such as a plan to print the President's face on U.S. passports.

Global Health Funding and Disaster Response

  • Impact on Life-Saving Programs: Murray raised concerns over a massive decline in foreign aid efficacy, citing specific disruptions to critical health initiatives:
      • PEPFAR Decline: HIV testing supported by PEPFAR reached nearly 5 million fewer people than the previous year.
      • Delayed Testing in Zambia: Infant HIV testing, which previously occurred within hours of birth, has been delayed to six weeks.
      • Withheld Emergency Funds: Murray accused the administration of withholding nearly $2 billion in FY25 global health funding during active Ebola and Hantavirus outbreaks.
  • Transition to Bilateral Compacts: Rubio defended the funding cuts by explaining a transition away from broad global funding "buckets" toward "global health compacts."
      • Targeted Agreements: The U.S. is entering direct agreements with 32 countries (including 27 in Africa) to build self-sustaining domestic healthcare infrastructure via local embassies.
  • Consolidated Disaster Aid: Rubio claimed that combining emergency disaster accounts allowed the U.S. to deliver aid at a record pace following recent hurricanes in Cuba and Jamaica ($3 million in aid) and typhoons in the Indo-Pacific.

Notable Quotes

"The President of the United States saw a threat of Iran developing a nuclear weapon behind a conventional shield that in about a year would have been impenetrable and we could not allow them to develop that immunity..." — Secretary Marco Rubio
"Trump promised the American people this war would be fast and decisive. It has been slow and secret and endless... We are cutting diplomacy and paying defense contractors." — Sen. Patty Murray
"This is not about denying or being punitive towards the world. This is about delivering aid but delivering it in a more effective and concise and consolidated way that actually gets more aid to more people faster." — Secretary Marco Rubio
"Budget is a statement of values, I've said it many many times and I think it is in big question where the values are in this budget." — Sen. Patty Murray
Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, you—you are not only the Secretary of State, you're also the President's National Security Advisor. Those are both full-time jobs when we're at peace, let alone as we have troops deployed in multiple conflicts around the world and the President is threatening to invade Cuba. So I want to just ask you specifically about Iran. You were one of just a handful of top aides with a seat at the table when the President ultimately did decide to launch the Iran war. Did you advise the President against the war? I'll never tell anybody what I advised the President privately, but I will tell you that the President had before him all the information that he needed. I agree with the decision that he made, if that's what you're asking, because the President of the United States saw a threat of Iran developing a nuclear weapon behind a conventional shield that in about a year would have been impenetrable. We could not allow them to develop that immunity and then they could break out to a weapon. So you—you won't tell us. You know, this is a question that millions of Americans are asking: how on earth did we get here? So I wanted to know what you did advise the President. Were you for or against this war? Or did you, the Secretary of State and National Security Advisor, have no opinion? No, I just told you, I support the President's decision. I think he made the right decision. I heard you say that. What I cannot tell you and will never do—and you have to understand, no—nobody in my role's ever done—is to go to you and say, "Oh, I was in a meeting and I told the President this," or I just can't do that. I won't do that. It's unwise to do that and it's unfair. But I am telling you the President made the right decision. That's my view, and I believe in it strongly. You—you do now, okay. Let me just... I did, I always have. I mean in terms of my view of the—the challenge that it poses. Okay. Well, back in March you said this war would end in, quote, "weeks not months," and here we are four months, hundreds of troops injured or killed, and billion—billions of dollars later. Trump promised everybody he was going to lower prices and no new wars. Now we have higher prices, new war. Trump promised the American people this war would be fast and decisive. It has been slow and secret and endless, and the majority of Americans do oppose this conflict. What my constituents are telling me is they want childcare, they want healthcare to be more affordable. They don't want Trump to have one and a half trillion dollars more for a defense budget to start wars around the globe. I hear that constantly from so many people. You know, Secretary Rubio, let me just say this. Diplomacy and development tools help keep us safe here at home by mitigating conflict, by di—mitigating disease, and other global threats. But the budget that you are here to defend, which would slash this work to the bone while sending war spending through the roof, makes clear that diplomacy is Trump's last priority. And by the way, it's not just the budget, or the unhinged rants attacking allies like Greenland and Canada, or threatening to end civilizations, or Trump treating war like a game—the White House posting literal video game edits as if he weren't getting American soldiers injured. It's also the actions that you've taken over the past year to gut the State Department, which is deeply concerning, dismantle foreign aid, and abdicate American leadership. Under your leadership, it is deeply concerning that things like the State Department leaving five hundred tons of food to rot in ports, and it had to be incinerated. Or pushing out thousands upon thousands of dedicated public servants, including families who put country first here and left their home to serve around the world, only to be sacked with no rhyme, no reason—I've heard from them. Meanwhile, you are planning to put Trump's face on US passports as if that's going to help our image when all that's happening. And the hack-and-slash job that you have done to foreign assistance, and you're asking for in this budget, has not only shattered America's global leadership, it has led to millions of preventable deaths. Programs have been frozen, grants have been cancelled, and life-saving work has been utterly turned upside down. I want to talk about global health. The stakes of life and death are here under global health. PEPFAR-supported testing is reaching nearly five million fewer people than the year before. In Zambia, babies born to HIV-positive moms used to be tested within hours of birth and treatment started immediately for positive cases. Now babies are not being even tested until they're six weeks old. So you're not just cutting resources that I just referenced, you are actually cutting the United States out of the conversation on global health threats and leaving all of us less prepared. We're in the middle of a deadly Ebola epidemic. We're seeing a worrisome Hantavirus outbreak. This administration has halted funding to the World Health Organization, and you are currently withholding nearly two billion dollars in FY 25 global health funding that was appropriated with bipartisan support here, signed into law by President Trump, and expires in less than four months. Now, I know that Ebola funding somehow miraculously started moving when we were seeing bad headlines, but what is moving right now, Mr. Secretary, is less than two percent of what is available.
