So my point is that the delay in mobilizing those resources has cost us valuable time and let this disease kill more people. And the fact is we already had these support systems in place—they were in place until this administration destroyed them. And even as we stare down a crisis caused by this administration's incompetence, in my opinion, you are here today to defend a budget that doubles down on that. That is what is really disturbing to me, with a forty percent cut to global health programs in this budget. So, to my point of view, this budget doesn't make America great again; it makes the world sicker and less safe. And—and that, I'm just talking about the cuts that you're proposing with this budget, because we can't ignore the biggest line item in the President's overall budget that's in front of us, which is war. One and a half trillion dollars for war. Not a cent for childcare, not a cent to—to make healthcare more affordable. That's the budget that you are here today to defend, and it spends one and a half trillion dollars on war and slashes your department to ribbons. So that is what is concerning to me as you come before our committee today to—to back this request up. It just seems to me we are cutting diplomacy and paying defense contractors. And I just believe, from my point of view, and I know you disagree with me, but I just think this is the wrong way for our country to go. Budget is a statement of values, I've said it many, many times, and I think it is in big question where the values are in this budget. So that's where I am. Who's the chair right now, Mr.—Mr. Senator Moran? Can I respond? Because she touched on a lot of topics. I don't know if I can get to all of them, you know, but—but I'm going to get to most of them. Yeah, sure. Because I strongly disagree with almost everything that was said. I thought you would. I know. Well, a couple points. First, let's talk about the State Department. The State Department was actually one of the least impacted of all the agencies in government. I'm not talking about least impacted, you heard... No, no, no, but let's be frank. We didn't—not a single, for example, overseas employee was riffed from the State Department. The—the vast majority of the reduction in forces came from the career civil service, not the foreign service. And that's because we got rid of the functional bureaus and put all the power under the regional bureaus, which is one of the best things we've ever done and I think is going to prove to be very wise, and we already see the impacts of it. Let's walk through some of the programs you've pointed to. So, for example, our disaster response today around the world, because we combined those accounts, is faster than it's ever been and more effective than it's ever been. These are not theories; this is a reality. We responded to hurricanes in the Caribbean—Jamaica and Cuba, by the way, three million dollars in aid to Cuba—faster, at a record pace, than ever before. We've responded to two typhoons in the Indo-Pacific faster than we've ever responded, because we combined and consolidated those accounts and were able to move very, very quickly in that regard. Beyond that, you mentioned PEPFAR. The reality of it is, first of all, you have to combine it with all these other programs that we're involved in. But if you look at the numbers for the third quarter of 2025, the exact same number of people were receiving medications during that period of time. The exact number. And it's going to even improve because we're adding innovation to it. There have been recent innovations in AIDS treatment and HIV treatments that are even more effective than some of the legacy programs that are available. Mr. Secretary, it is very clear why you are the Secretary, because you're very good at words and... No, but I'm giving you—I don't know how—how else can I answer you other than with words. I will stand by my statement against yours. I would—I just will. What was that? I will stand by all of the facts that I gave you. Okay. But I get a chance to respond, right? Well, my time's out, it's up to the chair. You can respond. Okay. So on the other things you're not talking about, which I think are very valuable to this, are these global health compacts that we're entering with thirty-two countries, twenty-seven of them in Africa, in which we're basically going to the country and saying, "Okay, you used to give money for clinics, we used to give money for healthcare, we used to give money for maternal care, and we used to have it in a bucket and it was maternal care globally, and then we went out and dished out contracts for people to go into individual countries." Now we're entering into contracts, compacts, agreements with the country, and we're saying to them, "Okay, what are your needs?" And we're doing this through the embassies. "What are your specific needs in this country?" and entering into a compact, not just to provide them aid for these things that they need, but to help them strengthen their national healthcare systems so that long-term they will be self-sustaining. Now, in some countries it may take ten years to get to that point, some it may take less. But for the first time, we are not just having these buckets that then are distributed broadly around the world. It is targeted at the highest needs of those countries based on their own domestic strategies, allowing us to become a value-added to their strategy and to build their capacity. That's something that—that hasn't been talked about. You look at what we've done with OCHA, where we've—we've signed the first humanitarian reset agreement in Geneva, along with our anchor pledge of two billion in support for the eighteen country-based, crisis-level pooled funds. This is going to allow us to respond in a more effective way. With the Global Fund, we've entered into an agreement with the Global Fund. They've put out repeated statements thanking the United States for the role that we are playing with the Global Fund, and we're prepared to do more. If donors match what we are providing, we're prepared to do even more in that regard. The list goes on and on. The point is, this is not about—first of all, this is not about denying or being punitive towards the world. This is about delivering aid, but delivering it in a more effective and concise and consolidated way that actually gets more aid to more people faster. That is the goal, and I think we're well on our way to achieving it. Now, as far as the budget is concerned, you know we operate under an OMB guidance that tells us, "This is how much you have; tell us what you would do if this is what you get." We present this to you. Having served here for a long period of time—I said this before you walked in, so perhaps you missed this point—we always understand that there's going to be a congressional process in which you're going to look at our request and generally ignore it, but in many cases add to it or—or reframe it. And we're prepared to work with you, as we did last year in the passage of an appropriations bill which we would like to see passed, because when you pass appropriations bills, it gives us, you know, the structure that we need in order to carry out these reforms. Mr.—Mr. Secretary, I just—I just will tell you, I appreciate that you have words to explain everything from your point of view. I'm talking from reality on the ground and from what I am seeing and hearing, and I fear deeply that we are losing our place and our value globally. So you and I have a disagreement. Thank you. Senator Moran. Mr. Secretary, so good to see you. Thank you for your...

Anthropic President Amodei on the Future of Claude

Bloomberg Technology(727.0K)·23:28·6.6K views·2026-06-04
Daniela AmodeiPresident & Co-Founder, Anthropic Shirin GhaffaryBloomberg
TL;DRAnthropic President Daniela Amodei highlights the company's rapid scaling—marked by $7.3 billion raised and a projected $850 million annualized revenue run rate—while outlining how strategic, multi-cloud partnerships with Amazon and Google fuel…
capital-intensive frontier...

TL;DR

Anthropic President Daniela Amodei highlights the company's rapid scaling—marked by $7.3 billion raised and a projected $850 million annualized revenue run rate—while outlining how strategic, multi-cloud partnerships with Amazon and Google fuel capital-intensive frontier training without compromising the startup's independent, safety-first mission.

Leadership Dynamics and Scaling Humility

  • Divergent Co-Founder Roles: Daniela Amodei and her brother, CEO Dario Amodei, split duties to balance long-term technical ambition with operational execution.
      • Dario Amodei (CEO): Acts as the technical visionary, directing model scale, capabilities, and future research directions.
      • Daniela Amodei (President): Manages the day-to-day organization, leads the executive team, and drives decisions regarding customers, product strategy, and business applications.
  • Conflict Resolution: Relying on decades of shared history, the co-founders resolve business disagreements by prioritizing curiosity over division. Approximately 80% to 90% of disagreements end in a hybrid "third way" compromise rather than a binary win-lose outcome.
  • Rejecting "Frontrunner" Distractions: Despite rapid commercial success, Amodei emphasizes that chasing metrics or competitor rankings is the wrong focus. The company's primary objective remains building ethical, responsible, and fair AI technologies.

Funding, Capital Intensity, and Tech Giant Partnerships

  • Capital Mobilization: Anthropic has raised $7.3 billion in funding to date and is projecting an annualized revenue run rate of $850 million, highlighting the massive capital requirements of the sector.
  • Strategic Mega-Deals: Anthropic recently secured a $4 billion partnership with Amazon and a prior $2 billion deal with Google.
      • Distribution and Reach: Rather than just acquiring compute resources, these partnerships allow Anthropic to deploy Claude directly within the existing cloud environments (Amazon Bedrock and Google Vertex AI) where enterprise data already resides.
  • Preserving Autonomy: Despite deep financial and infrastructure ties to massive tech partners, Anthropic remains an independent company, maintaining total control over its product roadmap, research directions, and corporate governance.

Enterprise Traction and Multi-Cloud Strategy

  • Operational Resilience via Multi-Cloud: Anthropic runs its model training and inference workloads across both AWS and Google Cloud. This internal multi-cloud setup provides operational redundancy and deep insight into how customers experience these distinct environments.
  • Enterprise Growth Drivers: Scale is heavily driven by large enterprises in the financial services, legal, healthcare, and technology sectors.
      • The 200k Context Advantage: Corporate clients leverage Claude's 200k token context window to process massive, multi-document workloads, such as auditing entire codebases, legal documents, and dozens of financial reports simultaneously.
  • Preventing Vendor Lock-In: Amodei supports a "multi-model, multi-cloud" future where enterprises deploy diverse AI models tailored to specific use cases rather than relying on a single, winner-take-all provider.

Constitutional AI and the Safety-Frontier Alignment

  • Constitutional AI vs. RLHF: Unlike traditional Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)—which can suffer from scalability limits and models trying to superficially please human raters—Anthropic uses Constitutional AI.
      • Principles-Based Training: Models are trained using a written "constitution" (incorporating sources like the UN Declaration of Human Rights). The model is programmed to critique its own outputs against these principles before human-reinforced training is applied.
      • Transparency: This approach provides a clear, inspectable set of values, building greater trust with enterprise clients and regulators.
  • The Frontier Safety Imperative: Anthropic maintains that a company must build state-of-the-art frontier models to truly understand and mitigate advanced AI risks. Safety research cannot be executed effectively in a vacuum or on lagging technology.

Notable Quotes

"I have to say like, I don't know how one CEO just runs a company by themselves. It seems like it's actually much easier to do with two people, particularly when they know each other really well." — Daniela Amodei
"It's a very capital-intensive business to train AI models... my guess is that over time, the sort of core set of companies that are working to advance the frontier are just going to need access to capital. And I think the cloud partnership is very well suited to that." — Daniela Amodei
"We believe that the only way to build safe models is to be at the frontier of building these models, because you have to understand what these models are capable of in order to understand how to make them safe. If you are not at the frontier, you don't actually know what the risks are." — Daniela Amodei
We were chatting not so long ago about when we first met and started covering Anthropic. But for those who aren't familiar, you and your brother, Dario, and a handful of other people left OpenAI to start Anthropic back in 2021. Now you employ thousands of employees, many of them in a reporting chain up to you in your role as President. I'm curious, what is the difference in the leadership style between you and Dario? Well, first of all, thank you so much for having me. It's great to be here today. And, you know, Dario and I, I really think of how we sort of split duties. Dario is this incredible technical visionary. I think, you know, years before AI was this amazing idea that people were excited about around the world, he had this really strong conviction that artificial intelligence is going to be a big deal. This is like the early 2010s. And I think Dario is constantly reminding the company about the scale, the ambition, and sort of where the technology itself is going. I think of my role much more as actually running and managing the day-to-day of the organization. So, I manage the executive team, and we spend time making decisions about customers, about our products, about the research, and how that applies to the people that are using artificial intelligence. And I like to think that the two of us are a great balance. I have to say, like, I don't know how one CEO just runs a company by themselves. It seems like it's actually much easier to do with two people, particularly when they know each other really well. I remember, in one of our conversations earlier, you telling me that you and Dario rarely fight. And I'm curious how you manage. What are some examples of places where maybe you have disagreed and have been able to settle it in a healthy way with regard to the business? Well, as someone who also has a brother, I know you know. I think we've had several decades to kind of practice fighting and still loving each other, right? So, as early as like, "Why did you take my toy? Can I have that back, please?" I think that has served us well in being able to operate well together at Anthropic. I think what I would say is, you know, we do disagree sometimes. But I think how you disagree really matters. And I feel like there is always an incredible mutual respect between us. So, if we look at the same situation and we see something different, I like to think that what our default is is to be curious, right? So, I usually view it as like, "Oh, this is not like an obvious call to me. Why do you see it that way?" Because usually, he'll have a piece of information that I don't have, or he'll have a different perspective. And I would say about 80 to 90% of the time, what ends up happening is we meet in the middle. And so, we'll think like, "Oh, I think this is the right thing to do, you think this is the right thing to do." Actually, there's probably a third way to do this that's the combination of what we both think, which is actually a little bit better. I think that's part of the strength of having both of us—the ability to practice looking at situations and decisions just from different points of view and perspective, and I think it helps us make better decisions. So, two years ago when you were sitting on the Bloomberg Tech stage with Dario, Anthropic was still very much the underdog in the competition against OpenAI and just in the race to AI generally. Now it's in a very different position, no longer the underdog. Claude is a runaway success, particularly among coders, your company is projecting 850 million dollars in annualized run rate revenue, and the company has raised 7.3 billion dollars in funding to date. Do you feel like you're definitively the frontrunner right now? And how does that change your approach? You know, something that I think both Dario and I really hammer at Anthropic is that that is just completely the wrong way to think. I think our view has always been, you know, this is really about how do we show up every day for our customers, how do we stay humble and make sure that we're really focused on the mission? Right, the whole reason we started Anthropic is we want to be able to build and develop this technology in a way that is ethical, that's responsible, that's fair. And I think it's really incumbent upon everybody at the company, but especially leadership, to say, "You know, all of these numbers, they're actually not the point, right? We are really here to do a job. We're here to support the businesses that rely on us every day." AI is increasingly a bigger part of everybody's workflow, their experience at the office, their experience in their personal lives. That's a huge privilege and responsibility. And so, I think if anything, we work really hard every day to say like, "Our job is to be the best version of who we can possibly be as a company every day." That takes work, that takes humility, that takes time, that takes focus and energy. I want to talk about the big news earlier this week that Anthropic has partnered with Amazon on a 4 billion dollar deal. And before that, of course, a 2 billion dollar deal with Google. How do you manage those relationships, especially as you also compete with them in some areas? And do you think that there's a risk of being too dependent on these tech giants? So, yes, we're really proud of, you know, both of those partnerships. And, you know, I think they give us the sort of resources to potentially train these very, very large models. But I think, you know, more importantly, they are really about how we reach the customers that already use these cloud providers. Right, so Amazon and Google, they have massive enterprise footprints. And for us, being able to make Claude available to those customers in the environments where they already have their data, where they're already running their workloads, is just incredibly valuable. And so, I think we've been able to maintain our independence. Right, we are still an independent company, we make our own decisions about our product roadmap, about our research. But we also have these very deep, strategic partnerships with two of the most important tech companies in the world, and I think that's actually been a really, really great balance for us. I mean, if we can just maybe level up a little, what is the calculus for an AI company generally to partner with a tech giant? What are some of the pros and cons here? Because on the one hand, yes, you get the resources and the distribution, but on the other, then you're beholden to their interests, maybe their cloud infrastructure, etc. So, what are some of the pros and cons, just generally, for an AI startup, do you think, to partner with a tech giant in this moment? So, I think, at least speaking for ourselves, and I think, ideally, probably really for the AI industry more broadly, it's a very capital-intensive business to train AI models. I think that's something we've been, you know, very open about from day one, and I don't think that's a surprise in the industry. And I think this kind of concept of like, how do you access the level of capital that you need to train these models, right? It's a really big kind of upfront cost to train the models and to serve inference on them. And I think that is, you know, my guess is that over time, the sort of core set of companies that are working to advance the frontier are just going to need access to capital. And I think the cloud partnership is very well suited to that. So, there are obviously of course tradeoffs, but my sense is there's just a fundamental structure of how training and kind of serving these models for customers works, that it will require that level of access. It's clear that many businesses can't get enough of Claude. But at the same time, we are hearing about some customers who are concerned about the pitfalls of so-called vendor lock-in, of, you know, being too dependent on a single provider. Google's cloud head, Thomas Kurian, recently talked about how they are seeing a lot of interest in multi-cloud strategies. Do you think there's truth to the idea that some businesses are looking to diversify their AI partnerships, and how does Anthropic fit into a multi-cloud world? So, I think, I think kind of two, two things are true at once here. I think, you know, first of all, our approach from day one has been to make Claude available on as many platforms as possible. Right, so we are on Amazon Bedrock, we are on Google Vertex, we have our own API, and we are on other platforms as well. And I think that's because we believe that customers should have choice. Right, they should be able to run these models in the environment that works best for them, whether that's because of security or latency or just because that's where their other cloud workloads are. And I think the second thing that's true is, we do see a lot of customers who want to use multiple models, right? So they might use Claude for some tasks, they might use other models for other tasks. And I think that's a very healthy way for the ecosystem to develop. It means that there isn't just one winner, but that there's a really diverse set of models that are suited to different use cases. And we're really comfortable in that world because we feel like Claude's unique strengths, especially around, you know, reasoning, around long context, around safety, really make it stand out. Does Anthropic have a multi-cloud strategy itself? We do, yeah. So we run on both AWS and Google Cloud. And we use both of those for our training, for our inference. And I think that's been really valuable for us as a company because it gives us that operational resilience, and it also means that we can see, you know, how these different clouds work from the inside, which helps us build better products for our customers who are also running in those environments. So, we're very much a multi-cloud company ourselves. What type of customer is driving the most growth for Claude right now? Is it the large enterprises, the startups, or individual developers? Honestly, it's a mix of all three, but I think, you know, enterprise is really where we are seeing some of the most exciting scale right now. We have companies in financial services, in legal, in healthcare, in tech, who are taking Claude and really integrating it deep into their workflows. And that's because, you know, Claude's long-context window, our 200k context window, allows them to upload entire codebases or, you know, dozens of financial reports or legal documents, and have Claude analyze them all at once. And that's a use case that is just incredibly valuable for large organizations. But at the same time, we're also seeing great traction with startups who are building new applications on top of Claude, and with developers who are using it for coding and other tasks. So, it's really a very broad-based growth. Dario and Anthropic's leadership has been vocal about the need for safety, and you've pioneered this concept of Constitutional AI. Can you tell us a bit about that? How is it different from traditional RLHF, and why do you think it's a better approach? Yeah, so Constitutional AI is really our approach to training models to be helpful, honest, and harmless, using a set of principles, like a constitution, rather than relying solely on human feedback. With traditional RLHF, which is Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, you have humans constantly rating model outputs, which is, first of all, very hard to scale, but also can sometimes lead to models just trying to please the human rather than being truly helpful or honest. With Constitutional AI, we give the model a written set of principles, things like the UN Declaration of Human Rights or, you know, principles about being helpful and objective. And we train the model to critique its own outputs based on those principles, and then we use RL to train the final model. And I think the reason we like this approach is, first of all, it's much more scalable, but more importantly, it's much more transparent. Right, anyone can look at the constitution we used and see exactly what values the model was trained on, which we think is incredibly important for trust. You've also been vocal about the potential risks of AI, and Dario signed the statement on AI risk last year alongside many other leaders. How do you balance that, you know, that dual mission of wanting to build these incredibly powerful tools but also being deeply concerned about the risks they pose? Yeah, I think it's, you know, it is a very difficult balance, but I actually think the two parts of the mission are deeply connected. Right, so we believe that the only way to build safe models is to be at the frontier of building these models, because you have to understand, you know, what these models are capable of in order to understand how to make them safe. If you are not at the frontier, you don't actually know what the risks are. And so, we feel like our role is to be a safety-focused lab that is also at the frontier of capabilities. And we hope that by doing that, we can show the rest of the industry that it's possible to build, you know, very powerful, state-of-the-art models in a way that is responsible and safe. And we also hope that we can help set, you know, standards and benchmarks for safety that the rest of the industry can adopt. So, we really see the two parts as, as reinforcing each other. And what's next for Claude? What can we expect from Anthropic in the coming months? Is there a Claude 3 on the horizon? Well, we're always working on the next generation of models, but I can't say anything specific about, you know, release dates or version names. But I think what you can expect is, we're going to continue to focus on, on those core strengths, so making the models better at reasoning, making them more reliable, making them safer, and also continuing to build out the features that our enterprise customers need. Things like security, administrative controls, and integrations with their existing tools. So, we have a very busy roadmap, and we're really excited about what's coming. Well, Daniela, thank you so much for joining us. It's been great having you. Thank you so much. Great to be here.

Is the Global Economy at a Turning Point? Carlyle CEO Harvey Schwartz Explains

Forbes(2.1M)·14:02·258 views·2026-06-04
Harvey SchwartzCEO, Carlyle Steve ForbesChairman & Editor-in-Chief, Forbes
TL;DRThe global economy is undergoing a historic inflection point as 80 years of post-WWII globalization and geopolitical cooperation reverse in favor of national security, onshoring, and energy diversification.
While geopolitical tail risks remain difficult for markets to...

TL;DR

The global economy is undergoing a historic inflection point as 80 years of post-WWII globalization and geopolitical cooperation reverse in favor of national security, onshoring, and energy diversification. While geopolitical tail risks remain difficult for markets to price and private credit faces structural adjustments, the underlying financial system remains robust and presents vast opportunities for private capital.

The Rewiring of the Global Economic Model

  • Reversal of Multi-Decade Trends: The fundamental trends that defined the global economy for the past 80 years—globalization, post-WWII geopolitical cooperation, and frictionless mobility of capital and labor—are slowing down or reversing, with technology being the sole exception that continues to accelerate.
  • The Shift to Energy Diversification: Global discussions have pivoted from "energy transition" to "energy diversification" because energy security is now treated strictly as an issue of national security.
  • Onshoring and Deglobalization:
      • Supply Chain Resilience: The 1990s focus on "just-in-time" inventory and global supply chain efficiency is being replaced by onshoring and localized economic growth to eliminate international dependency.
      • The Cost of Rebuilding: While geopolitical cooperation can be dismantled quickly, physically unwinding and rebuilding complex global supply chains is a capital-intensive, multi-year process.
  • The Demand for Private Capital: Ballooning government deficits mean public funds cannot support this global transition. The trillions of dollars required for energy diversification and onshoring over the next 10 to 15 years must come from private sources, including banks, private equity, and private credit.

Regional Growth and Market Dynamics

  • Japan's Structural Pivot: After 30 years of stagnation and deflation, Japan is actively executing a multi-pillared growth strategy.
      • Capital Activation: The government is encouraging citizens to move wealth out of bank savings accounts and into investable assets, alongside reforming corporate behaviors and investing in national defense.
      • Investor Sentiment: Sentiment toward Japan has turned highly positive as capital moves freely to chase these structural reforms.
  • Carlyle Portfolio Performance: Carlyle manages approximately $500 billion in assets, with portfolio companies employing 750,000 people globally.
      • Key Indicators: Portfolios show solid EBITDA growth and stable (though slowing) hiring.
      • The AI Spending Squeeze: Heavy investments in IT and AI implementation are currently squeezing out other capital allocation options within businesses.

Pricing Risks: Geopolitics vs. Financial Systems

  • The Difficulty of Pricing Geopolitical Risk: Geopolitical tail risk is nearly impossible for markets to price accurately, leading to stark pricing anomalies.
      • Disproportionate Reactions: The collapse of Silicon Valley Bank moved financial markets roughly 10 times more than Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. This demonstrates that markets over-respond to immediate financial system shocks while struggling to price long-term geopolitical shifts.
  • A Robust Financial System: Unlike the pre-2008 era, the post-crisis regulatory work done over the last decade has left the core global banking and financial systems highly resilient.

The Reality of Private Credit and Systemic Risk

  • An Essential Capital Source: Since the year 2000, the number of public companies globally has decreased by half. Private credit has stepped in to provide the essential capital required for private middle-market companies to scale.
  • Correction of the 2021–2022 Bubble: During 2021 and 2022, a bubble formed in private credit as assets were acquired at highly inflated multiples.
      • While companies with weak business models and poor capital structures will struggle, high-quality businesses with challenged balance sheets are expected to successfully navigate the transition.
  • No Impending Systemic Domino Effect: The private credit market does not pose a systemic risk to the global economy.
      • Lack of Crisis Triggers: A systemic crisis requires a combination of excessive leverage, high liquidity, deep interconnectedness, and panic—variables that do not align in the current private credit landscape.

Notable Quotes

"I think we're actually in the early stages of the most significant inflection point in recent financial history." — Harvey Schwartz
"If we were sitting here at this conference in the '90s, we would all be talking about just-in-time inventory and the need to be globally competitive... You can unwind geopolitical cooperation very quickly, it's much harder to unwind and redo all that supply chain work." — Harvey Schwartz
"The one thing about systemic risk is you need a handful of variables. You need lots of leverage, lots of liquidity, lots of interconnected activity, and you need panic... [Private credit] doesn't really pass the test." — Harvey Schwartz
Please welcome Harvey Schwartz, CEO, Carlyle, and interviewer Steve Forbes, Chairman & Editor-in-Chief, Forbes. Well, thank you very much, Harvey, for joining us today. Well, it's great to be back and congratulations on a fantastic event, you and the Corient team. It's great to be here. So I have to lead off, I have to lead off with a critical question about civilization. NBA Finals, Knicks or the Spurs? So, it's definitively clear, it's another sweep for the Knicks. Although, my girlfriend lives in Texas and her kids are big Spurs fans, so I hope we're, we're not as brutal as we otherwise might be. How's that for an answer? But it's all Knicks, all the way, come on. So, so there's hope for civilization. But, we, we look around the world, you're in a catbird seat, so to speak, in seeing a lot. Quickly give us what you see as the macro trends in the world, but also are markets, bond markets, equity markets mispricing risk, not realizing risk? The reason I ask is we ran into Ray Dalio before we came here. He relates a story back in 1971 when he was young—we were once all young. And, August 15th, Nixon announces on a Sunday night he's blowing up the global monetary system, imposing wage and price controls, peacetime first time, putting on tariffs. Dalio goes to the market Monday morning, figured it's going to be chaos. Instead the markets think it's happy days are here again, the markets boom. So, macro trends, and are the markets pricing in black swans? What are the risks out there? First the trends and then the risks. Well, Steve, thank you. And again, it's great to be back, and it's good to see everybody, lots of familiar faces. The, I think we're actually in the early stages of the most significant inflection point in recent financial history. And if I say that and that sounds a little dramatic, is because I think it is a, a little dramatic, but it doesn't necessarily, necessarily mean that it's negative. And what I mean by that is, we just pick a moment in time, like for example, I was born, a very lucky time, 1964, I'm 62 years old, born, white giant in America. Pretty good, pretty good launching point. And during my lifetime, Steve, the trends that really defined everything economically, for the most part, are slowing or reversing, other than technology, which continues to accelerate. And the bedrock of those trends really was around, whatever language you want to use, Steve, globalization, geopolitical cooperation, post-World War II, and, we're, I'm not going to bore everybody with a history lesson here, you all know the history, but that underlying trend of globalization... Reducing barriers. Reducing barriers to capital. That created basically a more frictionless mobility of capital and labor, some of that not good, some of that quite good, billions of people came out of poverty around the world. But, the Berlin Wall came down in '89, and then in this century, you've had some material events, 9/11, 2008 financial crisis, pandemic, which when you actually now take a step back, interest rates declined, now not, for the most part, stickier, maybe rising, deflation, not, all those trends, and geopolitical cooperation that set the bedrock for that, Steve, that really began, again, in World War II. So you're talking about 80 years of foundation, which then gave rise to economic, an economic model, which right now is being rewired. And if you just look at, for example, all the discussions we have with leaders around the world, around energy. All the discussion, mostly around the world, was around energy going from, conventional energy to energy transition. Now it's all about energy diversification. Why? Because everything about energy is around national security. If we were sitting here at this conference in the '90s, we would all be talking about just-in-time inventory, and the need to be globally competitive through efficiency, supply chain efficiency, which then led to capital efficiency and all forms of efficiency, and labor efficiency. Well, you can unwind geopolitical cooperation very quickly, it's much harder to unwind and redo all that supply chain work and all of that that means in terms of cooperation. So, when I go around the world, the trends that dominate and I think will dominate for the foreseeable future, by that I mean, 5 years, 10 years, and at Carlyle today we manage about $500 billion of assets, we commit capital for 5, 10 years on behalf of our clients across private equity, real estate, private credit, insurance, secondaries, energy platforms, all of it. National security, onshoring, which is really code for local economic growth and lack of international dependency, these are the topics that dominate the conversation. Government deficits are too big, as we all know, so all that capital has to come from somewhere. That capital has to come from banks, firms like ourselves, wealthy people around the world, but the demand for capital to support all of this is enormous and will be enormous for the next, I, I really think for the next 10, 15 years. I also, by the way, as a backdrop, I'm generally very optimistic, and while some of these shifts can be unsettling, I think that we should all feel pretty optimistic about the fact that everyone really at the end is focused on economic growth, although this transition is not without its complexities. Well, this, you hit on what happened after World War II, and despite, all the bad raps that, globalization has had, there's a phenomenal increase in the standard of living, dire poverty fell by 2/3 in that period of time. We all prospered from it, we're in a rough period now. Are we backsliding? Well, I don't know necessarily if we're backsliding, I do think this K-shaped economy, you know... And, and how, how is that reflected in market prices? Just give you a quick example, you've cited this, you look at, Japan, on paper, that should be a disaster, financial institutions loaded with underwater bonds, huge deficits, no growth for a long period of time, yet the stock market is booming, money's coming in, looks like it's on the rise. Europe, no growth in the last, virtually no growth, some exceptions like Poland and others, but Europe has way lagged the United States. So to get capital, you need growth. So, where, where, where is this first of all, are we, are we erecting barriers that are going to stand in the way of growth? How do we get the growth, and, what, what's happening in the markets? Is this like 1971, happy times, not realizing the black swans out there? Well, I'll get to the black swan in a second. I do think that, let's take Japan just as a great example. You know, Japan went through 30 years of no growth. If you were growing up in Japan, and this is very hard for us as Americans to appreciate, you actually expected your rent to decline over time, not go up. And so once that mindset of deflation set in... But, we've been in Japan for 26 years, we're the only private capital firm that's actually had a dedicated team in Japan, I will tell you that when you meet with Japanese officials, and we've been there, again, we've been there for a very long time through all these cycles, the steps they are taking to foster growth, free up, basically, corporate behavior to take corporate actions, encouraging, basically, people to move what they've been trained to actually keep their money in bank savings accounts, into investable activities. They have a very deliberate, multi-pillared strategy of achieving that growth, they're now, for the first time ever, investing in defense, and they'll need to. So, the good thing about capital is that capital really, for all intents and purposes, doesn't have barriers. And it can find places of growth, and so I would say, I was just trading emails with a very large institutional client, last week because I was in Asia, I was in Japan, and I said, "Oh, I'm headed to Japan." And they said, "Oh, everybody loves Japan." So, sentiment can actually is so forward-looking. And I think, when you think about markets today, what I would say is, we look at our portfolios, we're only 2,500 people at Carlyle, but we have 750,000 employees in our portfolio companies around the world. That makes us one of the largest employers in the world. What we see is, solid EBITDA growth, I'd say slowing of hiring but still marginal hiring, huge investment in IT spending as part of this shift on AI, which is squeezing out some other capital allocation, but generally speaking, performance across the board portfolio is quite good. I do think, again, this inflection point doesn't necessarily mean markets are mispriced. I think the hardest thing for all of us to price is geopolitical risk. When Silicon Valley Bank went out of business, the market moved something like 10 times when, versus the day Russia went into the Ukraine. I would argue, in terms of this inflection period that's sort of redefining the future of capital markets and finance and geopolitical cooperation, I think that's a way more significant event. But what we've been taught as investors is that geopolitical tail risk, fortunately, doesn't have that black swan outcome. We have been taught that you can have a financial crisis like 2008. So, the world will overrespond to those types of issues, which is not, relative to the geopolitical event. But I do think the geopolitical event is impossible to price. And so, that's a very, very complex issue, and people have to make an assessment about their risk appetite in that context. Does that mean that, people should, cut down on their leverage, have reserves, just because there's so much unpredictability on the geopolitical side? Yeah, so CEO of Carlyle, we tend to run a very, very conservative financial firm, so I tend to lean conservatively in terms of how we think about our firm. We're a fiduciary, we have our obligations to our clients around the world. I think for people in this room, you know, who are dealing with wealthy people around the world, you hear Adam before, I think it's a lot about what that individual wants in terms of, how much risk appetite do they have. You know, FOMO, not participating is a pretty painful feeling for people, and so, you have to understand, you have to decide like, okay, do I, do I want the pain of missing out or do I want the pain of knowing that I was being quite conservative, and that's a very personal thing. I think when you look at the financial system, it feels quite robust today. And that is really all the work done after 2008. Now, now one of, one of the things that, obviously concerns people is the cry- private credit markets, it's under stress test. There's a tendency to fight or look at the last crisis, is this a 2008 and if not, why not? So, from a credit, well, what's happened here, Steve, and again, we, we don't have that much time left, but I'll say, basically since 2000, we've lost half the public companies in the world. This is not a great thing for capital markets, but a very understandable trend. And what replaced, what, what companies needed to stay private was to generate all that capital, and they needed capital to grow. Again, capital is the elixir that allows companies to execute their plans, hire people, and grow and create economic value. Private credit played an incredibly critical role. What we see here is, a big bubble in private credit in '21, '22, where companies bought at very high multiples. So there are some companies with not great capital structures that aren't great companies, which likely will suffer. And you're going to see some great companies with challenge capital structures actually sail through this. But I think it's a moment in time where the market is saying, you know, software was really the concern 2 months ago, now people are less concerned about software. I think it's just going to be a process where the market observes it. We've been pretty clear in telling people for a long time we don't see as a systemic risk. If it is a systemic risk, the good news is it's getting more coverage, more eyes on it than any other systemic risk I've ever seen, but it doesn't really pass the test. So, so we're not going to have a domino effect that we had in 2008. It, it doesn't feel like it, you know, the one thing about systemic risk is you need a handful of variables. You need lots of leverage, lots of liquidity, lots of interconnected activity, and you need panic. I will say parts of the media are working really hard on getting the panic going, but, you know, it's been studied for a long time, there's a lot of aspects about the market that don't lend itself to systemic risk. But I think it's going to get a lot of focus for a while and it probably should. I think it's, it's a healthy part of the process. So we'll have some knockdowns but not a knockout. And, in terms of, basketball, the world is going to be right, and in terms of, the turmoil or change in the world from the post-World War II period, we'll find a way around it, and we'll find our way through it. At least that's my interpretation of my age and my happiness. Well, I'm generally, again, I'm a pretty optimistic person with a really keen eye on tail risk, I would say that's the way I sort of, live my life. Right. Harvey, thank you very much. Everybody, great to be here. Thanks so much. Good to see everybody.

AIPCon 10 June 2026 | Livestream

Palantir(153.0K)·140:05·13.5K views·2026-06-04
Alex KarpCEO, Palantir
TL;DRTrue business value in the AI era is unlocked not by generic large language models, but by combining LLMs with Palantir’s Ontology and Apollo deployment infrastructure.
This integration allows organizations to digitally encode their unique, proprietary "tradecraft" to...

TL;DR

True business value in the AI era is unlocked not by generic large language models, but by combining LLMs with Palantir’s Ontology and Apollo deployment infrastructure. This integration allows organizations to digitally encode their unique, proprietary "tradecraft" to automate decisions, orchestrate complex physical and digital assets, and protect critical operations at machine speed.

Bridging AI with Operational Reality: The Power of the Ontology

  • The Fallacy of Generic LLMs: Palantir CEO Dr. Alex Karp warned that off-the-shelf public models treat businesses as generic entities, which can lead to rapid token expenditures without delivering actual competitive advantages.
    • Karp emphasized that businesses must own their "means of production" and "alpha-generating data," rather than exporting proprietary intellectual property to public clouds.
  • The "Tradecraft" Advantage: Real value comes from digitally encoding the tribal knowledge, edge cases, and judgment of human domain experts into the software system.
    • The Ontology Concept: Palantir’s ontology serves as the centralized, semantic map of an enterprise, translating fragmented datasets into real-world concepts (e.g., a specific vehicle, a client contract, or a farmer's plot of land).
    • Cost Optimization via Evolve: In scaling these deployments to thousands of users, McCarthy Construction noted that leveraging Palantir's "Evolve" framework led to a 60% drop in LLM token usage, addressing a key operational cost barrier.

Physical Asset and Logistics Orchestration: Hertz, NScale, and McCarthy

  • NScale (AI Factory Infrastructure): NScale builds and operates massive, vertically integrated AI data centers (including 8 GW in West Virginia, 1.2 GW in Texas, and 400 MW in Portugal).
    • Supply Chain OS ("Delivery 360"): NScale utilizes Palantir to track tens of millions of components across hundreds of suppliers.
    • Automated BOM Analysis: The system uses agents to ingest messy, multi-thousand-line Bills of Materials (BOMs), instantly identifying mismatches, proposing alternate suppliers, calculating lead-time impacts, and updating financial projections.
  • Hertz (Fleet Management & Turnaround): Operating a fleet of 500,000 vehicles, Hertz deployed its "Daytona" control tower to 15,000 frontline employees, orchestrating over 4 million monthly operational touchpoints.
    • VIN-Level Automation: When an associate reports a vehicle issue (such as a broken seatbelt), an agent automatically opens a work order, pairs the incoming customer with an alternative vehicle, and suggests optimized transport paths.
    • Financial Impact: Recovering even a single percentage point of fleet utilization translates to tens of millions of dollars in annual revenue.
  • McCarthy (Large-Scale Construction): The 160-year-old commercial contractor utilizes "Pulse"—a job site mission control system—across 150 active projects.
    • Critical Path Mitigation: For complex change requests (such as an air handler unit redesign), Pulse cross-references procurement logs, models schedule impacts, and provides customized scenario options to protect the construction timeline.

Reimagining Customer Experience and Specialized Services: Parts Town and Kirkland & Ellis

  • Parts Town (Specialty Distribution): To tackle parts identification for commercial kitchen and refrigeration breakdowns, Parts Town integrated "PartsIQ" across its support teams and field technicians.
    • Real-Time Live Call Assist: AI agents actively listen to customer support calls, automatically parsing blurry images, partial serial numbers, and legacy manuals to populate customer contexts and identify parts in seconds.
    • Bottom-Line Impact: Reimagining the service lifecycle is projected to deliver over 200 basis points of EBITDA margin improvement across the business.
  • Kirkland & Ellis (Private Equity Fund Formation): Managing hundreds of billions in capital formation annually, the law firm built the "Fund Formation Engine" on Palantir's ontology to centralize decades of legal tradecraft.
    • Agentic Term-Sheet Drafting: Specialized agents collaborate to draft term sheets, extract precedent clauses, and automatically draft side-letters based on negotiated parameters.
    • Obligation Tracking: Post-close, the engine tracks complex, decade-long obligations, automatically generating checklists and draft communications for fund managers looking to make time-sensitive investments.

Securing Infrastructure and National Interests: Accenture and the USDA

  • Accenture (Software Estate Protection): Accenture manages thousands of IT estates globally, orchestrating security for over 700,000 internal employees.
    • Security Forge: Accenture uses this agentic harness alongside Palantir’s ontology to scan codebases, classify vulnerability severity, and push fixes directly into developer environments.
    • Apollo Deployment: Upon confirming a threat, Palantir's Apollo platform initiates automated, blue-green rollbacks to secure versions across thousands of production environments, reducing incident-to-remediation times from days to minutes.
  • U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA Modernization): The USDA overhauled hundreds of fragmented, legacy IT systems that cost nearly $1 billion annually to maintain.
    • One Farmer, One File: Operating on the "Landmark" platform, the USDA consolidated agrarian data into a single, unified source of truth to support secure, digital-first farmer services.
    • Unprecedented Capital Deployment: Utilizing the ontology, the $11 billion Farmer Bridge Assistance program went live instantly, distributing $4.4 billion to row-crop producers in its first five days and breaking all historical records for online USDA enrollment.
    • National Security & Farmland: The USDA's "Admin Command Center" leverages this data fusion to proactively identify foreign investment risks and track ownership of American farmlands.

Notable Quotes

"Enhancing the unique, individual, tribal knowledge that your business has to the point where no one else can compete with that is where all the value is... and our products augment the differentiated specific over the generalisable." — *Dr. Alex Karp, Co-Founder and CEO of Palantir*
"Software without domain expertise is just a platform waiting for someone to wield it. And expertise without the right software can't keep pace with adversaries operating at machine speed." — *Chris Burkhardt, Chief Information Security Officer of Accenture*
"Federal technology providers and their affiliates, their integrators, and their contractors, must stop treating the government as a permanent opportunity for cost-overrun modernization efforts that too often lead to nowhere. Instead, pursue federal business with foundationally AI-enabled platforms, with lean engineering teams, with rapid and aggressive deployment timelines." — *Sam Berry, Chief Information Officer of the USDA*
Growth, plus ontology, plus Foundry, plus Large Language Models. Everything that looks like, "I am the same as my competitor," is not that valuable. You need something that is totally specific. The expertise of our employees is an unrivaled asset. Humans are focused where judgment is required. No fair fights, if I can avoid it. Enhancing the unique, individual, tribal knowledge that your business has to the point where no one else can compete with that is where all the value is. But digitally encoding our best practices and engineering expertise using the AI agents through AIP makes it very hard for people to eviscerate these cultural advantages. And our products augment the differentiated specific over the generalizable, and that's where literally all the value is going to go. Please welcome Co-Founder and Chief Executive Officer of Palantir, Dr. Alex Karp. Well, great to be here. I always find these things very impressive, and Palantir is going through many phases. First, we didn't have videos, we couldn't do videos, and it was dental music. And now it's kind of rocking. Now, I don't know, I don't know what that means, but it's definitely better than waiting for the drill to hit your tooth. I think one of the interesting things about Silicon Valley, or first of all, it is Silicon Valley, it's still Silicon Valley, as much as we would like it to go all over the world, it seems to be very specific and it's where we started and I love to come home. But then the obvious thing is every single company has some kind of secret that they're trying to eviscerate. And in enterprise sales, that's mostly been a sales secret. Like, you think you're buying a widget but it's buying you. You think you're getting the steak dinner but you're paying. And then, so, I was thinking, well, what is our current secret we're hiding? And, I have one. Our sales team—obviously everyone knows we barely have sales, and Samir and his partner run it, and his wife was actually asking, "Does Karp really think I'm an idiot?" And it's like, no, I don't. And we do have sales, but we actually have the biggest and most effective sales force in the world that we never talk about. And that's one of the main reasons we're bringing you here. We're hoping that before, during, or after, you'll go to a large language model company and learn that they don't care about you at all. And, no matter how bad this conference is, we can never sell you like they're going to sell you, which is, "We're going to give you something that makes you feel smart while your business goes out of business, that doesn't actually work unless you have an ontology, Foundry, Apollo, or something useful. We're going to show you we're smarter than you," which they may not be, "and you're going to go home feeling poorer and less safe, and you buy the product, and you're going to pay a lot of tokens, and it's going to be very hard to understand how it helps you, but investors are going to know you're—you're smart." That's our sales secret. Please feel free to go and learn about that. And then, what you will find is there are a myriad of problems that these very, very important models solve, and there are even bigger problems that they create. And what are we in the business of? We're in the business of giving you the ability to solve those problems for yourself and own the means of production. And what are those problems? Well, you can write code cheaper, faster, but what does the codebase look like? Who does it? How do you make your engineers into forward deployed engineers as opposed to people just writing code? How do you deploy that code in a secure, safe, fast way? How do you manage that asset so it's actually an asset? An asset is defined by its being valuable. What is valuable? Something that you own, that you have, that no one else has, that can't be outsourced. How is your data yours? What part of the stack belongs to a public cloud? What part of the stack do you not share, i.e., you own the exact part of the data that creates the alpha that you do not share with your competitor or anyone else? Your problem is exactly the same problem the Ukrainians have, the French have, the Department of War has, which is you have personal, private, very specific data that will and must belong—must belong to you, and things trained on that data have to belong to you, while simultaneously using public sources. How do you manage the code inside that? Who manages it? Who owns that asset? How do you deploy it? How do you define and understand your company? How do you find vulnerabilities in your company? Of course, models are very useful for that, but can they do them at the pace you want? Can they fix the vulnerabilities? Who owns that information? How—how do you quantify it? Does you owning it mean the person down the street owns it? These are the problems that we solved for the intelligence community and the defense community globally, from Ukraine to Israel to America. You can decide which ones you like and revere. We're very proud of our clients. We're very proud that we stick up for America. I'm very proud of the people who hate Palantir and me. Very proud of it. Put a list of them up there, please. You would be proud, most of you, too, to be on our side when you look at the people who don't like us. You may not love it—you'll love us a lot more after you visit the labs and you look at the people who hate us. And I encourage you to do it. But these structural challenges were exactly the structural challenges that we solved at scale, and we're in the business of transferring that to you. Now why do we do that? Well, I mean, we think we're likeable, but we obviously do it because it's a Pareto optimal situation. These problems are real, they've been exposed, we do very well by solving them for you, and we can do them natively, meaning we get to keep our culture, our way of being, our way of working, the way we like to live and work, and you get to keep the same thing, too, at scale. And that's essentially what Palantir is doing. It's the reason why we're doing so well, especially in the US commercial context, and it's the reason why you are going to fly. And the flying part is really interesting because you fly precisely because everything I just said is both true and specific and not accepted, and that's how you do very well, and that's what we're transferring to you. And so, yeah, and we also just, by and large, with exceptions, but very few, really actually like our customers. Which most people don't. Many of you guys, I mean, I don't mean people in this room, but definitely the Valley. And that ends up being a compounding and very important asset. So, with that, welcome. You're going to see a lot of actual things that work. We do very little kind of show but don't—you know, tell but don't show, and you can trade notes here, and it's always a lot of fun, and I'm very glad you guys came. Thank you. Please welcome Chief Digital and Information Officer of NScale, Lauren Hurwitz. Hi, good morning, everyone. So great to be here. What a venue. So every demo you're going to see today will rely on one common resource, and that is a GPU that is running. And at NScale, it is our mission to bring those GPUs online, and we're doing so at an unprecedented scale. We have 8 gigawatts in West Virginia, 1.2 in Texas, 400 megawatts in Portugal, and the list goes on. Every one of those numbers will attach to a cascade of delivery dates, and each one of those dates will require that we build correctly, in parallel, at speed, every single time. We were founded 2 years ago to meet this exact challenge. We design, build, own, and operate AI factories. And we're vertically integrated, which means we control the campus, the building, the power, the cooling, the network infrastructure, and the GPU deployment as a singular system. So, why is this hard? Why did we call Palantir? First, scale. A data center is multiple buildings, a building is several halls, a hall is hundreds of racks, and each rack has roughly thousands of parts. Multiply across seven active sites, and you are tracking tens of millions of components against thousands of purchase orders and a supplier base in the high hundreds. Second, the hardware is moving really fast. Every new chip generation comes with new power and cooling requirements. To address this, NScale is pioneering modular design. This means that our halls are engineered to accept new chips, so when NVIDIA moves from Blackwell to Rubin to whatever comes after Rubin, we can accept the new generation without having to redesign the entire building around it. Finally, you have scarce components with really long lead times. Items like switchgears and transformers have to go into procurement before the final design is locked. So we're placing orders far in advance, knowing that specs will change. And when they do, those revisions cascade through the entire delivery chain. The industry's standard answer to this has been an old ERP, a lot of asset management software, a lot of project management software, and like an army of expeditors with an ungodly amount of spreadsheets. And so, to meet the ambition that we have and our obsession with execution, we knew we had to do something very different. We had to leverage AI to build for AI. And actually, I have to tell you that before I joined NScale, I spent nearly a decade at Palantir, building with some of the most complex organizations on Earth. And it was a hugely informative experience, as you can imagine. And I came to NScale knowing one thing above all else to be true, which is that for agents and humans to work together, to really work together, they need a common ontology. So we started with the outcome and we built backwards from there, using Palantir. We started with our supply chain operating system, which we internally call Delivery 360, and I'm really pumped to show you the first two things that we built. Imagine I'm a solutions architect. My job is to manage everything that goes into building a data center, captured in a bill of materials, what the industry calls a BOM, which is a great name. You can think of it as a highly specific list of ingredients. And that list can change for any number of reasons, usually due to a customer request. And just to bring this to life, a minor tweak in a network design could mean you have to order 4,000 extra cables. So, a revised BOM lands in my inbox. On the left, you can see that the agent is immediately getting to work on trying to understand this new BOM. These BOMs are messy; they include thousands of lines. And it's a really great workflow for an agent because the agent can traverse the ontology, looking at things like SKUs and compatibility checks. It is—it is not a raw text diff. As you can see here, there are a bunch of changes. The agent is putting them into two piles. The first pile is based on everything the agent knows—what can we just automatically accept? And that pile saves me a load of time. The second pile are changes that an engineer like me has to review. And in the example here, you can see there's a mismatch in this top-of-rack switch. So as an engineer, I can understand the implication of moving between this one switch and the other. I'm okay with this change. But I don't necessarily understand the implications of the switch on timelines and budget. The agent is one step ahead of me, looking through the ontology, the purchase orders, and understanding the implications of moving from the old switch to the new one. It surfaces three recommendations for me. First, I can add this new switch to my existing order—same supplier, same delivery date. It's going to cost me half a million dollars. So I ask the agent to do some homework. I tell it to go figure out where I can put this old switch in inventory; maybe I can use it at a different site. So it goes off and does some homework. Meanwhile, I'm looking at my other two options. I can amend my existing purchase order, take out the old switches, and put in the new ones. I don't have to spend any extra money, but my delivery date gets pushed out by 9 days. Third option: cancel the order entirely, put in a new order with a new supplier, but I have to take a risk on the delivery date. It probably won't meet my SLAs. It doesn't feel like a good option. While I'm thinking about this, the agent is doing homework. It comes back and it says, "Actually, I've consulted all the other build schedules and we can send this old switch to a different site." Great. Option one is clearly the best. I'm going with that one. The switch is handled, the order is placed, and I can continue working through this inbox that would have taken me 5 to 10 times more time than it does now. And the real key thing here is that this decision gets codified into the ontology. So that judgment call compiles into every future agent decision making, meaning it gets surfaced to every single engineer in the company. But good orders don't mean anything if they don't arrive on time. And so, our—our second use case was around delivery, and we used the same ontology, the same foundation, and we built this other workflow in a fraction of the time. So, what you see behind me is our delivery dashboard, and it helps understand and mitigate the impact of delayed deliveries. We have 13 shipments in flight, and they're mapped against build schedules in real time, which is very cool. We have a 92% on-time rate. Great. And one shipment at risk. In this case, a power supply module is delayed by 12 days. The agent has already traced the cascade and identified that a customer date will slip. Since the agent already knows the approved vendor list for the site, the performance history of those vendors as it relates to this particular part, as well as current supply chain disruptions and things like weather patterns, it suggests an alternate supplier. There's a cost premium of 42k. It's a trade I'm willing to accept because I know that if we do, I can get this back on track, and I can deliver on time. The agent drafts the supplier communication, and the Gantt has already updated on screen. And the next step here for us is actually to get the—to get the agent to draft the purchase requisition to send to the finance department, which the engineers typically don't like doing. So, that's a—that's a win. So these two workflows in Delivery 360 are what it looks like to build the operational core of your company, AI natively. But the supply chain is just the starting point for us. We're taking the same approach and applying it to workforce planning, site operations, purchasing, and a number of other areas across the business. The thing that I'm really excited about, and the thing that compounds this AI-native approach, is that we're pushing everyone at NScale to be a builder. And so, the compounding effect will come from subject matter experts building on top of the ontology. And actually, last week, one of our corp dev VPs shipped a—a really compelling use case that he calls the Contract Agent. And he's using the ontology in a really creative way to solve a problem that only he knew existed. And that's the paradigm shift. The people who understand the problem space are going to be the people who build the solutions. And the wild thing about doing this at NScale is that we are doing so from the beginning. We're not going to get to it later. We're not going to launch a transformation program. We are doing it from day one. So, we use AI to build for AI. But in the end, it still comes down to your stellar people, just with the ceiling taken off of what they can do. And that is the company we're building, and it's how we're going to bring 10 gigawatts of compute power online, build after build, faster every time. Thank you. Please welcome Senior Director of Process Engineering of Hertz, Matt Naylor. Good morning, everyone. You may recall seeing Hertz at AIPCon 7, where we displayed an application, deployed in our vehicle turnaround process, to deliver the right car, at the right time, for our customers. That app is deployed to more than 15,000 frontline employees and is generating over 4 million operational touchpoints a month. This is part of the foundation for Hertz, in our mission to advance the way the world moves. We orchestrate one of the most dynamic operating environments in the world. Every reservation, every vehicle movement, maintenance event, customer interaction, and frontline decision creates a signal. Individually, these signals are moments. But together, they are intelligence. What makes this process powerful is not just the data that it generates, but it is the orchestration of it. Let's take a look at how we are deploying AIP agents into employee workflows to analyze operational events and optimize change actions. This is what we call Daytona, and our control tower, a system of processes designed to execute operational excellence. Across our fleet, our people, our customer, and our facilities, Hertz has built an operating model to transform real-time data into real-time action. On your right side of the screen, you will see a live feed of operational events that we are capturing and deploying directly to the frontline. Where decisions impact customer experience, asset utilization, cost, speed, and ultimately, return on our assets. Some of these events are reported by the workforce, and others are automated by camera inspections or connected car solutions. This is about more than just dashboards. It's about creating a connected operating system that helps the right decision happen at the right moment. On the left side of the screen, you'll see different workstreams across our operation, where agents are embedded in decision making. Whether that's optimizing fleet availability, accelerating turn times to position cars ahead of demand, or improving labor deployment to proactively solve issues to avoid customer friction before it occurs. In an industry where assets are constantly moving, and customer expectations are constantly rising, orchestration becomes the competitive advantage. Let's take a look at some specific examples and the orchestration engine in action, showing how Hertz operationalizes Foundry's ontology, and brings intelligence directly into the hands of the people running the business. So what I see here is a new event that has been detected for a safety-related issue with one of our vehicles after an inspection by our associate, causing the vehicle to be removed from our rentable fleet. The associate has recorded an audio transcription and a picture, showing us that the seatbelt is broken. Our vehicle agent analyzes the information provided to automatically open a work order. Leveraging the ontology, the agent checks multiple sources of context, such as warranty coverage, or expected time to repair, and the demand to influence the repair decision. Knowing this vehicle is not rentable, the agents collaborate across the workstreams, kicking off our customer assignment agent, to suggest a new pairing for the expected customer that we see is arriving in 2 hours. To orchestrate the optimal outcome, our agents demand a robust ontology, with granularity down to the VIN level, to turn plans into specific recommendations and action. So let's dive a bit deeper into the ontology that's powering this vehicle agent. As a maintenance manager is reviewing some of the details we've just seen, they begin planning for the work that is coming into their shop. And they can see the vehicle's current location, upcoming preventive maintenance schedules, diagnostic information, previous rentals, moves, or camera scans on the vehicle. This enables the team to start thinking about recovering our cost, but also ensuring that work is bundled when it hits our shop. A full reliability visit extends the time between visits into our shop and avoids future downtime. With an end-to-end view of the vehicle state, this allows the humans in the loop to influence change of actions as they are needed. As work is planned, it's time to arrange the delivery of the vehicle. The agent has produced three options. One, a priority move to our internal repair shop. Two, holding the vehicle in its current position for later. Or three, moving this vehicle for vended maintenance. For each option, the agent again references the ontology, searching for additional context on part availability, transportation labor, maintenance capacity, and ensuring delivery to a new future assignment. The selected action has modeled cost, time to execute, and revenue opportunity. In real time, a decision is made on priority, and it is ready for review by our transportation team. The transportation manager is now notified that prioritization of this vehicle is needed. And they are the next action to resolve the issue reported from our associate. Understanding the current location of the drivers and the move scheduled for their workforce, the manager is able to slot this move at the beginning of an employee's shift. Driving efficiency and throughput of their staff, while ultimately reducing vehicle downtime and increasing vehicle availability for the rest of the organization. Historically, operators would optimize decisions for their department goals. But now, they're making decisions that optimize the entire system. The AI-driven orchestration is the enabler, but the cross-functional human execution is what drives the outcome. On the frontline, the move is accepted and the flow of the vehicle is tracked from arrival to maintenance, repair, and continuous orchestration through the subsequent moves and cleans. When the vehicle is stalled on our ready line, the assignment agent fulfills the future reservation. This allows our customer service agents to focus on the experience for our customers, confidently providing a safe and reliable vehicle on time for our customers. So what you've seen here today isn't separate tools. It's one system. Continuously sensing, reasoning, across hundreds of thousands of decisions every day. Let me put it in perspective. The vehicle agent triages, reduces diagnostic and decision time, recovering days of idle time for every vehicle. The transportation agent ensures the highest value actions are taken first, deploying labor for revenue potential ahead of first-come, first-served. The assignment agent controls fleet availability, revenue optimization, and ensuring fulfillment of every rental, creating pull signals that turn assets and employees into revenue generation. All of this is operating in collaboration with our people. This is only possible with LLMs and our choice—our choice of the unique ontology that we are deploying at Hertz. Across half a million vehicles, these hours and days compound. Every percentage point of utilization we recover is worth tens of millions in annual revenue. Even just a fraction of that, driven by smarter decisions, faster actions, and connected systems, can transform our economics. This is what it looks like when AI amplifies operational excellence to deliver a frictionless customer experience. So I want to thank you guys for allowing us to spotlight Hertz and demonstrate how Foundry is driving orchestration of the data into thousands of actions daily across our assets, our people, and our customers in operations. Thank you. Please welcome Vice President, Innovation from McCarthy, Rico Bertucci. All right, hi everybody. I am Rico, VP of Innovation at McCarthy, but also I run our AI program. So, I am so excited to talk to you all about our partnership with Palantir. For those folks who don't know about McCarthy, we're a US-based major construction company. We work across multiple markets in a pretty broad array. But I think the common theme for all of our clients is that they call on us for their largest, most complicated, highest visibility projects. And they do that not because we have like a fancy resume. It's because they believe McCarthy is the right business partner for them. And they believe the teams that we're going to put on the job site are the right business partners for every stakeholder that touches a McCarthy project. And that's a tough challenge. Our teams are super excited. But in the construction industry, what I have seen is that both the pace and the difficulty of the work continues to compound over the last 10 years. We're asked right now to build double the square footage in half the time at twice the complexity. And, oh by the way, we're not quite done with the design yet. And so, there just has never been a need greater right now to be human-powered but AI-backed in the construction industry. And so what I mean by AI-backed, you can see it on the screen. This is the McCarthy ontology. And so we have more than 160 years of construction expertise overlaid on a single data core that is driving our business. We've carved out folks from within the business who are subject-matter experts related to every functional aspect of the McCarthy business. And they have embedded their business logic end-to-end from source system to AI-native tools. So let's show you how this ontology and AI is going to be used on the job in the coming months at McCarthy. So I am super, super excited to introduce what we're calling Pulse. And so Pulse is a job site mission control for each one of our job sites. We have about 150 working right now. What it does is it takes all the complexity, all the noise that you saw in our ontology, and it streamlines that down to the specific things that our job site teams really need to know. So right now we're able to click through and pick up signal from self-perform, trade partner performance, financial performance, staffing and staff planning, equipment maintenance and management, and formal requests for information. The persona that we're going to jump into right now is a senior superintendent on a McCarthy job site. And for context, that senior superintendent has to understand every single aspect of the job at a 10,000-foot level but be ready to dive into the details at a moment's notice. And so typically that senior superintendent, when he gets to the job in the morning, has to swivel chair between about 15 to 20 applications to get up to speed on the job.
