Now, we've taken all of that signal into one place to make sure that that individual, who's got a line out their door every single day with subcontractors that need answers, has the right signal and the right understanding of the job. And if we just stopped there, our teams would be super, super excited. They're asking me right now how quickly can they get their hands on this. But we overlaid AI into a digest on the left-hand side. And that is individually relevant information surfacing risks and opportunities for our senior superintendent. And that's what we're going to click into in a second. So the opportunity that we're looking at, the risk that we are looking at, is an RFI that came in. There was a design change on a job site that identified specs for an air handling unit that needs to be set on a rooftop in a few months, which no longer matches what was spec'd out. Now, it's a few months away and perhaps that could be missed, but Pulse looked across our procurement log and identified that this piece of equipment is a long lead item, is a long lead item. And so it pulled together all appropriate context from the McCarthy ontology so that our superintendent could dive in and understand whether this really is a risk. The other, like one of the coolest parts, is up in the right-hand corner. Pulse compressed down our critical path schedule to specific activities that are most important or that are impacted by this air handler unit. And so as you can see, there's a couple of pieces of the critical path schedule that this will impact, especially structural topping out. And so this absolutely is a risk, and we need action on it. And this is where the Pulse process really kind of pulls away from typical construction. So what we're seeing here is Pulse in plan mode. What that does is capture all the context that we just walked through around this specific issue and risk, and put it into an overview where we can leverage AI to help us scenario plan what potential solutions are. We take advantage of McCarthy's management system, which is more than 2,000 documents of policy, procedure, and lessons learned. And we layer on top custom-built skills with AI for construction to leverage. Now, if we just stopped here, then this would be a generic plan. It would probably be 80% of good, 85% of good. But Pulse knows that it needs to draw from the experience of the superintendent to make sure that we get 100% of good when we put this in front of an owner. So what it does next is it enables our superintendent to team with AI. It starts asking specific questions that are most relevant to this issue. And every one of our projects is bespoke. We make one building, one time, and we pack up and we go home. And so our superintendent on the ground understands the unique specific risks of this project better than anyone else. And most of that information is in his head. It's not even in our ontology. So being able to answer these questions really refines the plan that we can deliver, so that we can have a customized solution that meets the needs of every stakeholder. Now our superintendent has provided that rudder steer. We know specifically what to plan around, and we run through all of the business logic that you've seen already. So the logic's in place, we have steer from our superintendent, and now we know we're going to get a specific actionable plan for the project. At this point, just for context, for our teams, this would take about three weeks or so to get all this pulled together and track everything down. So the ability to come up with not one but two viable solutions is pretty special for us and for our project teams. Right now we've got two, two scenarios that we can work and provide clear communication and choice to our owner and to our design partners around trade-offs for each. In this scenario, we're going to choose to optimize around lost time rather than a nominal fee. And so we're going to choose to protect the schedule. And as we move forward, this is the last pull from the ontology that we're very excited about. So at McCarthy, we absolutely want our senior superintendents on the job sites working with our trades. We don't want them behind a desk writing memos. And so pulling from the ontology and McCarthy's business logic, we already have a plan to communicate to every stakeholder what is most important. And there's a lot of stuff to tie off before this decision is finally made. We have to submit a substitution request to our design partner for approval. We have to notify our owner formally. We need to provide briefing to our subcontractors. We need to work with our internal supply chain and logistics hub to place a hold on the new piece of equipment that is going to meet the specs. All of that happens in an automated fashion and can be actioned out of this one platform so that our superintendent does not have to swivel chair. They can complete their entire job in one sitting, and they can get back out on the job site because that's really where they can provide the most value for us and for our stakeholders. So zooming back out, and I think this is going to be a theme, that was one narrow operations use case, and we believe at McCarthy being able to leverage our ontology in the right way across our enterprise is really the only way that we can get the maximum value out of this. So I'm super excited about a product in our pre-con and design space that is like right on the heels of Pulse in operations. And also at McCarthy, we have a strategy where we are embedding our own engineering teams arm in arm. They are delivering product with our Palantir FDEs. The partnership has been incredible. And I would just say to folks in the room, take advantage of the power of the platform. I'm totally non-technical, and yet it's addictive. I'm pushing PRs on the weekends, and I've made my own ontology and my own products that I think make myself wildly, wildly more productive. And so you know, it's an incredible experience to kind of sit in McCarthy at the end of the day and feel like in three months, I've developed superpowers. But that's the power of this platform. And being able to deliver that to McCarthy partners and our clients is something that we're super excited to do in the next few months. Thank you. AI is incredibly powerful in the quantitative world. It can process more information than we can, it can find patterns faster than we can. It can decompose problems, reason across complexity, and generate new possibilities. But AI does not replace the human being who understands the reality on the ground. This is where tradecraft comes in. When I say tradecraft, I don't mean a job title. I mean the individual, the specific human being who has spent years building judgment—the person who knows the exceptions, the edge cases, the way the work actually gets done. So McCarthy is a 160-year-old plus general contractor. So we operate only in the commercial space, so we don't do residential. But we operate across really all kinds of commercial sectors. So we build everything from hospitals to advanced education to even some stadium work, and then we certainly do some work in renewables, industrial, wastewater, and things like that. So this is one thing that we are really excited about because we have 160 plus years of building knowledge and expertise, and that comes through every day. So freeing our people up to do what they do best, what they went to school for, what they spent years to do, is to be amazing builders, not to be stuck in individual tools. And Palantir is elevating that for us so they can consume all this information and make the right decisions. And that equipment is helping you build better, faster, more efficiently, not trying to replace something. Actually, they get to be craftsmen again. Exactly. I guess, I always love asking customers their perspective on the ontology, because everyone's got a different view of what the ontology is... So across our stack, I mean, we have literally over 200 different applications that we use today, from business development all the way through handoff on a project. And so, you think about the fragmentation of data. We capture a lot of information, but it's in pockets, it's in silos, and that's really difficult for our folks to manage. We truly believe that the ontology layer is that secret sauce. We always have to be really cognizant of, "Hey, we're rolling something out, it can't feel additive." It can't just be a plus one. They need to understand and feel the value immediately. And I think that's what we're going to see with more and more of what we release through Palantir, is they're immediately going to see like, "Oh my gosh, like this helps me in my day-to-day job." We have to think about the adoption side, how that's creating value, but we also have to think about the cost and the return on investment as a company. And so, think about 4,000, 5,000 people banging on this thing, and suddenly token burn is going like this... Yeah, exactly. ...we also have to be smart business leaders as well. And so we're really excited about the work we're seeing with Evolve. We've seen almost a 60% drop in token usage as part of our large language model. So that's just a really, really valuable tool. So thank you for that in terms of Evolve, and your team bringing that mindset in. As you know, starting early can be a little bit difficult when you're conceptualizing which models do we use, but as we start to get to scale, we need to go back and ensure, "Hey, we've architected this thing the right way so that we are minimizing token burn and maximizing value." Yeah, and value maxing, right? I think it comes back to like, you know, "I made it exist first," so maybe I'm using frontier models, get it to work, and then it's like, "How do I make it good, and how do I make it efficient?" Because, you know, if we think about value, we have to also think about the cost to get to that value. We're never really growing, you know, in a massive people business. We are selling software, enabling people. But I think it's curious how you're seeing the perspective in the real world of how does that FDE model affect you? Yeah, well, I would say we love our FDEs. We really appreciate our Palantir team. They come in and help us just think broadly. And also this is relatively new to so many of the clients, right, in terms of how to think about how to apply AI and think differently about some of these tools. And back to kind of the McCarthy way, like we also want to be able to control our own destiny. So, I'm really proud of the work we've done. We've kind of reorganized our overall digital organization, we've thought about how we think about governance so we can still move at speed but yet have the proper controls in place. So, typically our forward deployed engineers help us take a concept to kind of an MVP, and then we take it from MVP to scale, and then we care and feed it in perpetuity. We love the work that we do, the complexity that we do, and to see our McCarthy cranes scattered across our cities brings just a real sense of pride. I'm the first forward-deployed CEO. Oh, what does that mean to you? I think about a CEO, it's—it's often said they do three things. We set the overall strategy for the company, we hire and retain the best people, and we always make sure there's money in the bank. And like, for 16 years, that was my only role at Mixology. I didn't—I didn't come off the sales floor, I never bought a piece of clothing. I led teams, I built systems, and I grew the business. And at the end of 2024, hyperinflation was evaporating our margins. And I had to find a solution, and that solution was Palantir. We had 60 SaaS solutions. Six zero? Six-zero. Everything from Canva to my ERP. And I was trying to solve this very specific problem: workforce scheduling and labor optimization. And my payroll provider was supposed to have it, but they didn't. And we were scheduling on a piece of paper. And I couldn't believe it. There was a Google Doc. I first got to Palantir and went to my first bootcamp, and it was a miserable failure. I was completely embarrassed. I did what I've always done, which is I sent my team. I have a great technical team. And I sent them into the front lines to forward deploy and learn Palantir. I didn't have a choice. I had to charge to the front lines. It was a "burn the boats" moment. And I found myself ontologized, and I had to beg Taylor and the team over there to let me in. I asked, "Hey, where's your CEO track?" They said, "We don't have a CEO or executive track." I said, "What do you have?" "Analyst developer." So I begged them to be a part of the analyst training, and I became their first CEO to go through it. Cool. And then I knew, by maybe the third or fourth class, that I had it. I was introduced to the beta version of AIP FDE. AIP FDE. And this was—it was not in wide circulation, you couldn't download it from the marketplace, but the CSM saw something in me about the way that I was speaking so clearly about my business problem. And he's like, "Let's just talk it into this." Yep. It was the most magical, beautiful thing in the whole entire world. And I began solving problems that I used to pay hundreds of thousands or even millions of dollars for, in an afternoon. And every person I showed it to—investment bankers, private equity guys, people in my network—had a red pill moment. It's like, you saw the world one way before, and then you learn what the ontology is and why it makes the AI work better, and every single thing changes about that. I don't know a lot of the things that you just mentioned. But I know my business, and I know what the margins are supposed to be, and I know that I'm using a tool, and I'm bleeding on the keyboard until the application is telling me the right answers. And when I started seeing positive margin change on labor and workforce optimization, that's how I know it's working. Yeah, yeah. When I see adoption, I know it's working. Well, and what you're talking about is really the tradecraft. Your expertise of all the learned experiences that you have about how to run the business, how it works, what's important, what's not important. And then really, it's not AI replacing that, it's—it's giving you the agency to go accelerate. It's giving you superpowers essentially across your business. Yeah, that is where the ontology has fit in for me, of being able to take all of my business knowledge, all of my tribal knowledge. And it started with just the nuts and bolts of our systems, like our ERP, our accounting data. But very quickly, I found out that I could encode my values. And that was like the most game-changing thing for me. So, you ready for what the forward-deployed CEO actually means? Yeah. Yes. All right, so I told you what the CEO does. The forward-deployed CEO sets the overall company strategy, hires and retains the best people, always makes sure there's money in the bank, and now, they orchestrate the AI workforce at their company. Iron Man is cool. An army of Iron Mans is better. And we have built dozens and dozens of applications, agentic workflows, and I'm showing them that the technology barrier isn't the gap anymore. What I keep coming back to is, you know, the only thing that matters is winning. And winning... Look, look, hold on one second. You see what I got over here? You see—you see my... Yes, exactly, you're wearing it. It says, "Ask yourself constantly, am I winning? If the answer is yes, nothing else matters. Chaos is tolerable, pain is tolerable. The only thing that matters is to win." Surf Air is an air mobility platform, and really, the original thesis was there were over 5,000 airports that exist here in the US, and 90% of the population lives within 20 minutes of those airports. There was no operating system or software to be able to bring that whole world together, and see it in one kind of single, single data brain to really build this operating system for the charter side as well as the scheduled side. So, SurfOS was born out of that. So you've seen, you were pre-AIP as partners, and now you've seen AIP and kind of things progress. How has AIP and the—the integration of LLMs and agents and stuff really transformed, you know, how you're working? So, for example, fuel prices have been, you know, have been going up with the world environment that we're in now, and we quickly stood up AIP. We started running fuel optimization across the airline to see how we could cut costs on fuel optimization, where we were buying, how we were fueling the planes. And that was, you know, stood up in AIP within weeks, where, you know, if we did something, you know, three, four years ago, that would take way more engineers, way more resource, and way more time. We're experts in the product of all things aviation, but having AIP has allowed us to be able to move far quicker. This is the next phase of us with connected aircraft, how are we using AIP and Foundry to get real-time data back into the SurfOS system to go drive more optimization for maintenance, for pilot utilization... You know, the thing I hear differently about a lot of our Palantir partners, especially with what I'm hearing from you, is this connected operating system which is powered by the ontology, right? What are the things that you're building that are being really useful? So SurfOS is like the overarching sort of operating system for air mobility of today and then—and then, you know, future electric planes like we talked about. And when you think about like kind of the modules and what—what are we building, you have, you know, Operator OS, which Operator OS is your end-to-end booking platform to run your operations. So that's, you know, we've built a screen which we call Control Tower, right? And that's where, whether you're the chief pilot, you're the director of operations, you're the head of maintenance, you know, whatever that work group is, we've built it in an OS SDK format because that allows us to really control the UI. And what we've kind of learned over the years is, you know, each group of customers wants to—wants to see certain things and not see other things. So Control Tower, like, you'll look at that screen and that is, you know, your chief pilot, your director of maintenance. That's giving you a whole insight, that's telling you what flights are on time, where, you know, what's going on for the day. It has AIP Chat on the—on the right side giving you, you can query tails and ask it, you know, "What tails are down? What are my problems?" AIP is giving you suggestions. You can go into something we call Pilot IQ, which, you know, you have to make sure the airframes are maintained and ready to fly, then you always need to make sure you have a pilot ready to fly throughout. So it's giving real-time insight back to the ops team, back to the management team, so we know what's going on day-to-day. Then you look at, you know, our Broker OS tool. Find the supply, find the plane, find the pricing, find the right aircraft for that customer's mission. And in that, you'll see, you know, we've built—with thousands of flights we've done, we've built an AIP pricing engine. Then we have a module which kind of plays in both worlds, which plays in the operator side as well as the broker side: it's called Aircraft Intelligence. And that's our real-time availability that's giving us insight tail-by-tail. That's—that's a lot of stuff you built in Foundry and AIP. That's really freaking cool. It sounds like also it's like—it's an optimization problem of: I've got to have the right piece of equipment, the right people, at the right place, the right maintenance, all these things, and so all of those things have to be aligned and coordinated, which I don't think people think about on the surface, but it sounds like it's a highly complex business that, you know, you're the expert in. And I love that you guys have built so much of your expertise into the platform. You know, we've spent a lot of time too on our own airline. You know, we're doing 250 departures a day. You know, we've built a lot of scheduling components too. So, using, you know, Foundry and AIP to actually help build the schedule. And we're seeing results where, with AIP, we're able to—something that would take, you know, it would take us days and a lot of humans, AIP, we're able to do it in, in minutes. I think of Palantir as customer number one for Apollo. We built it for ourselves. We've been working on it for the last 10 years. It's how we ship all of our software. Right, so if you have Foundry and AIP, you're using Apollo. Yeah, it's the infrastructure that allows us to ship our software. It's how you take the code written by an engineer out in Palo Alto, how you get it deployed, whether that's on a cloud stack in something like AWS, or on the back of a Humvee. There's a lot of continuous delivery solutions out there. Most of them, and in most enterprise contexts, the way you ship software is you write some code, you package it up in a container, and then once it's packaged, you go into your staging environment, you click install, and you test it out, maybe it sits there for a day, and then you go to your production environment, you click install in like some console somewhere, and your code then gets deployed into that environment. Apollo is really built around what we call release channels. And a release channel is a declarative definition for an environment that says, "I want this environment to have this behavior. I want it to take the latest version of every piece of software. I want it to be one week behind so that it has the most stable version of every software," or some kind of permutation of those different, you know, strategies for deploying software. Which then allows our customers to pick the right level of risk for what they're trying to do, for which workflows and other things. It allows you to really customize that deployment. It allows the forward deployed engineers to get the latest versions out for the right use cases, but hey, this is a stable version. So it allows me to really customize my stack, my risk profile, and everything through Apollo. To put that kind of a sense of scale on it, one of our development stacks I was looking at yesterday, there's 3,000 products, which is roughly equivalent to a Helm chart in Kubernetes, deployed on that stack. And about once a minute, a new change is pushed to that environment—a new version, a config update. The volume of permutations of 3,000 products with one change per minute is—is... Yeah, it's wild. The scale is crazy. We have, I think, one of the most complicated deployment structures in the world. We have cloud stacks, we have edge stacks, we have, you know, our—our Titan trucks where we're shipping literally onto a physical truck, mobile command outposts, all over the place. We have order—like hundreds of substrates we ship to. Apollo lets you ship to any of those. They can be connected, where you're always pushing out changes if it's an internet-connected system, or an Apollo spoke that's disconnected, you can actually still push out changes to those when they gain connectivity. So you can have disconnected edge nodes, you can have a data center, you can have a node in a factory that's, you know, running a computer vision model and you're using Apollo to ship to that. You can do all sorts of substrates. With vibe coding and things, we have infinitely more code being built probably in the last year than was created ever before. We now have new threat capabilities where people can find vulnerabilities at a new scale and a new pace. And so now, I have both vectors increasing at an alarming rate. So people are like, "How do I manage this?" And that's where Apollo really comes in. If you actually have Apollo and you have the ontology and AIP, this is how we model it and actually secure your infrastructure. With Apollo, when a vulnerability is detected, you're able to recall that, stop the bleeding, and get that thing out of the field. So you're mitigating, and you're able to roll back to mitigate the security concern here while you figure out the remediation path to go fix things and roll out a patch. Exactly. You have an object, like a product. You have an action, like rollback. And then, like any other ontology, you can compound on top of it. Once you define those things, you're able to build workflows on top of it that actually use those objects and actions and operate your business in the way that generates alpha for you. It's this underlying fabric and layer that really makes all the magic happen, which comes into how we work with forward-deployed engineers. Really, Apollo is a key component to forward-deployed engineering work. I talk about the backpropagation of the pain turning into product through forward-deployed engineers, but that is made possible by Apollo. This is where a lot of my thinking happens: out here, moving through the woods, feeling the wind, seeing the trail curve in front of me without knowing exactly where the next bend is. There's something about being in nature that resets how I see problems. Because the real world is not clean, it's not perfect, and it's not structured. It's not just rows and columns, inputs and outputs, dashboards and models. The world's complex, it's messy, it's beautiful, and it's unpredictable. It should allow people to bring more of themselves into the work—more judgment, more creativity, more context, more responsibility, and more agency. Please welcome Chief Information Security Officer from Accenture, Chris Burkhardt. Everyone, welcome back from the coffee break. My name is Chris Burkhardt. I've been with Accenture for a long time, but this is an exciting new phase. We're starting our next chapter of our relationship with Palantir, and I'm going to share a little bit today about that. I'm going to speak about Security Forge and Apollo. To begin with, Accenture's software landscape is complex and sprawling, not just across our applications but across the teams and operations that really underpin it. And we don't just manage our own estate. We manage thousands of estates in partnership with some of the largest commercial and government organizations globally—customers who face increasing velocity and volume of threats enabled by frontier models and the rest of AI. Doing this at our scale has historically required massive amounts of human and software orchestration. Imagine 700,000 Accenture employees stitching together ticketing systems, asset inventories, vulnerabilities and threat feeds, and team rosters, applying tens of thousands of patches and changes daily. Working with Palantir, we've begun our own journey to ontologize our entire estate. We can see our digital world as a connected system, not only the software and infrastructure but the teams responsible for each component, the response actions tied to incidents and vulnerabilities, and the business outcomes those components support. Every asset is linked to its owner, its dependencies, and its remediation path. This ability to leverage the ontology to scale security triage really offers the responsiveness needed for a first line of defense. But it's no longer sufficient just to react. Adversaries are pointing both frontier and open-source models at our attack surface, using them to try to subvert codebases, reason through exploit chains, and weaponize newly disclosed vulnerabilities within hours, rather than weeks. The window between public disclosure and massive exploitation has collapsed, and capabilities that were once the domain of only the most skilled threat actors are now commoditized and running at machine speed. I'm therefore excited to share how we are tackling this new frontier with Palantir. Security Forge is the agentic harness that's enabling us to combine large language models with the ontology, deploying agents against the logic of the enterprise, and identifying and acting on issues across our entire codebase. We begin by building the framework for agents to review the attack surface, with the goal of intercepting the specific offensive techniques employed by our adversaries. Each pipeline chains together multiple specialized agents, each with their own set of instructions, tools, and context to conduct a specialized security review. These pipelines are the basis of the protection layer. Much like data pipelines would pipe context into enterprise applications, these pipelines are taking the codebase as input, applying agentic logic to conduct a deep investigation, and generating remedial actions as output. Once we've built the pipeline, we can then run it across the breadth of the codebase ontology, selecting the model's goals and governance safeguards for the given task. Our security review pipelines, like all workflows on AIP, are model-agnostic. The resilience and protection of the state is not constrained to one model family or at risk of becoming outdated. As adversaries become increasingly capable, this agility is key for us to stay ahead. The pipeline really matches how a human engineer would review the codebase with tooling to identify vulnerabilities. Security Forge harnesses the ontology and the large language models to scale this effort without compromising on quality or speed. As the run continues, we see the full chain of the agent's investigation. Every single log is captured in the ontology. It's necessary for audit and governance, but it also serves as a compounding value asset, feeding best practices and known bad patterns back into our engineering org, so each next line of code is more secure than the last. With the run complete, we're presented with the findings of our agent's investigation. This then immediately triggers the evaluation layer to review the findings, consolidating duplicates and scoring severity. The ontology logs really become key here, with every step of the severity scoring and common weakness enumeration being captured. This compiles in detail how the agent identified the vulnerability, what the business context was that informs this common weakness, and all known occurrences that require remediation. But identification and early warnings are only as valuable as one's ability to act on them. Built on a foundation of human-in-the-loop and AI teaming, Security Forge immediately passes identified vulnerabilities to the humans who own the affected applications for adjudication and review. All the context is available to those users, right down to the underlying logic that the agents used to detect the issue. This enables a seamless and thorough human-in-the-loop review, with any feedback from the user captured in the ontology and made available to future agentic executions. After confirming a finding, the engineer can then move directly into remediation with the ability to copy the command-line tools into the IDE of choice and connect the Security Forge MCP server to their environment. This will pull over the exact findings, the code locations, and the remediations from the ontology right into their coding session, enabling rapid response to the vulnerabilities. But here's the magic: we can also automate this chain. The same tools our engineers would have to enact remediation can also be handed to our swarm of agents to take forward. Automated remediation is available to spin up, resolve downstream fixes, manage dependencies, and handle refactors. This scales not just the identification of the issues, but the deployment of the solutions as well, which has always been one of the hardest parts. Now that we've started the remediation process, we need to ensure the entire fleet is secured ASAP. Enter Apollo. Apollo enables us to manage the complexities of our software estate in a proactive manner. As soon as Security Forge confirms the finding, it sends those vulnerability tags to Apollo, which immediately issues a recall to all the environments where that package version is deployed. This rolls us back to safe versions while ensuring that dependencies on that package in all those environments are still maintained. As soon as remediation is complete and the new version of the impacted entity is pushed, it is picked up by Apollo, rolled out to the fleet autonomously, and all expected guardrails and processes are followed. Blue-green deployment, soaking periods, and anything that you'd normally expect from an operations team, Apollo does. Apollo adheres to your custom policies, shaping itself to your software development lifecycle and not forcing you to design your operations around it. This full loop would typically take humans working across engineering, infrastructure, and support days to complete. It's time that's no longer afforded to the commercial world given our advanced adversaries. We can also harness the same methodology beyond just security remediation. It could be performance improvements, feature requests, or customizations for our apps and for those of our customers. This is empowering Accenture to embrace the same forward-deployed engineering that you've seen other Palantir customers benefit from today. In summary, Palantir brings the software. The ontology turns a sprawling, complex estate into a single connected system. Security Forge loops through threat hunting and finding vulnerabilities at speed and scale, and Apollo deploys and updates the infrastructure to remediate those. Accenture brings the expertise: decades of running mission-critical applications and infrastructure for the largest organizations in the world, tens of thousands of patches and changes daily, and the operational judgment to know what to harden and in what order. Neither alone is sufficient. Software without domain expertise is just a platform waiting for someone to wield it. And expertise without the right software can't keep pace with adversaries operating at machine speed. Together, we've got something that neither of us could do on our own: complete domain assurance. This is not a dashboard explaining your exposure after the fact, but a living system that finds, adjudicates, remediates, deploys, and observes, so the estate hardens faster than it can be attacked. This is what the mission requires. The deep expertise of Accenture and the tooling of Apollo and AIP come together to stay ahead in the fight. Thank you. Please welcome global executive committee member and co-head of global fundraising and fund transactions of Kirkland & Ellis, Erica Berthou. Good morning, everyone. It is totally awesome to be here. I'm a corporate lawyer by trade. No surprise to anyone in this room, technology is transforming how we work. I'm a member of the global executive committee at Kirkland & Ellis, the world's foremost private equity-focused law firm. At Kirkland, we've been sprinting up that curve, integrating AI into almost every aspect of our work. But we had a problem. The off-the-shelf tools available for what my team does have two major weaknesses. First, while they are great at discrete tasks, they do not knit together the complex moving parts of transaction execution. The second weakness, which is much more important, is that they are trained and developed on widely available market knowledge and know-how, which means that they tend to serve a lowest common denominator. That does not work for us. At the absolute beating heart of what we offer our clients is our differentiated expertise. We do more of what we do than any other law firm on Earth. And so we need tools that unlock and leverage our expertise—tools that sit atop and amplify our sprawling web of institutional knowledge, which is our real competitive advantage. And not just that: we need a system with perfect fidelity to client confidences, ethical walls, and data security. I am so excited to share this morning what we've built together with Palantir. So what is it that my team does? You are all well aware of the scale and importance of private capital in the global economy. The fundraising and fund transactions group that I lead at Kirkland & Ellis with another partner handles fundraising and capital formation for virtually all asset classes. We do it at a massive scale, helping raise hundreds of billions a year for well over a thousand fund formation clients. It's an elaborate exercise, one that demands deep technical expertise, rich market knowledge, and sensitivity to a complex set of commercial goals, all while managing the competing needs of multiple stakeholders. That knowledge lives across thousands of documents and other sources, and in the minds of hundreds of accomplished lawyers. We asked ourselves a key question: what if we could take all of this diffuse institutional knowledge and put it at the fingertips of every one of the thousand-plus lawyers supporting our group on every fund? So what did we build? We call it the Fund Formation Engine. Through our work with Palantir, we have built a new operating model for legal services, one that centralizes and compounds the expertise of our most senior lawyers. It embeds that expertise across our workflows and makes it available at a massive scale. Unlike off-the-shelf legal AI tools, we've built our engine from the ground up, leveraging Kirkland's institutional knowledge, tradecraft, and judgment. We partnered with Palantir for its ontological expertise to build the connective tissue across the fundraising lifecycle, linking funds, market technology, obligations, and transaction history into a single operating system. This allows knowledge that historically lived across a vast number of attorneys, documents, and spreadsheets to be structured and applied directly in the flow of execution, improving transparency, speed, consistency, and decision-making. Every step in the fund formation process will be informed by our institutional memory—literally decades of knowledge—without the latency inherent in the old methods for tapping it. Okay, enough talk. Let me show you what this actually looks like. I want to bring the tool to life for you with an example and highlight a few elements of what this engine can do. Meet Vulcan Industrial Partners—that is a fictional name, by the way. It's named for the Roman god of the forge, which is very fitting for a fund focused on industrials. Vulcan has raised two successful funds, and they come to us to launch their third. Their goal is three billion dollars in six months from over 300 investors on fair terms for all parties. Step one is the drafting tool. A lawyer enters a commercial term sheet or a set of instructions, and the engine gets to work. It draws on our model document library, live market data, and decades of know-how. What used to take days for a lawyer to analyze, discuss, and draft now happens in minutes. This works because every step of the drafting process is owned by a specialized agent. One agent captures insights from comments and senior partners' prior art. Another extracts and structures language from model documents. Another agent drafts new provisions, and another folds feedback into each revision. So if we turn toward the screen behind me, we will see the result of the specialized agents working collaboratively. The underlying ontology, together with the specialized agents, is what uniquely enables this outcome. A month into preparation, Vulcan provides a twist. The managing partner wants to expand its geographic mandate to include emerging markets. This is an important commercial change. It sounds straightforward, but it has significant implications for Vulcan's business and needs to be handled carefully. The engine assesses what changes are needed for the governing documents. It pulls market data, surfaces examples, and then reads the current geographic restriction, offering a menu of alternatives. So let's move a little further into the process when we're working with investors to bring them into the fund. Remember, we're dealing with hundreds, maybe thousands of simultaneous inputs. The system organizes every deal point and suggests a solution drawing on market solutions and firm know-how. A Kirkland lawyer applies her judgment to those suggestions, but now she does so with all of that market intelligence, prior art, and senior judgment at her fingertips. Let's look at what's going on at this stage with our new term for Vulcan. As predicted, once investors review this change, we get many questions, comments, and requests. The engine organizes every comment and suggests a solution. Once again, the Kirkland lawyer's judgment is critical, but now she has everything she needs right in front of her immediately. Her speed and expertise are vastly multiplied. This would be impossible without the ontology. It captures the tradecraft of the senior partners, enables the agents to find the needles in the haystack, and suggests responses to the comments. You'll see an illustration of the underlying ontology on the screen behind me. Oftentimes, our work with an investor results in a bespoke bilateral agreement; we call them side letters. Here, another part of the engine takes over, drafting the side letter automatically. The engine ingests every agreed term and generates appropriate language based on a hierarchy of potential solutions. Back to our Vulcan example, let's say we need to provide an investor with a side letter to help them get comfortable with the proposed change. The engine drafts three suggested solutions, and here is what they look like. Okay, let's skip ahead, much further in time. Once a fund is raised, that is not the end; it's where the obligations begin. And they run for a decade or more. Our engine tracks each one. It ingests every commitment and creates a liveable, searchable record of deadlines, notification requirements, investment process points, restrictions, and more. It connects directly to the fund manager's workflow, generating reminders, checklists, and pointing the right people to the right place at the right time. Back to Vulcan one more time. VIP III has just closed, and our new emerging markets flexibility is just one of hundreds of obligations. Eighteen months later, the investment team reaches out. We have a time-sensitive new deal in sub-Saharan Africa. What obligations do we have? Our engine pulls every relevant commitment and generates a checklist, flags the right people, drafts the reminder emails, and compresses timelines to deliver immediate advice. Before I close, I want to share some final thoughts with you. This engine is more than just an efficient way to do the work we've always done. The most important point is that it delivers better outcomes. Every judgment call is now informed by the best underlying knowledge deployed at full strength and speed. And the benefits of the engine compound. Every engagement makes the next one smarter. Importantly, this engine is better not just for our clients, the fund managers, but also for their investors. We will be better equipped to craft solutions to the investors' requests and needs. It makes this key private markets ecosystem more efficient. Simply put, it reduces friction and adds value. Finally, let me humbly submit this about our working relationship with Palantir: we do not see this as a vendor relationship or a one-off endeavor. We think this is a revolutionary change in how our work gets done. Thank you all, and thank you Palantir. This is just the beginning of what we are innovating together. Please welcome Chief Executive Officer of Parts Town, Bill Geary. Morning, everyone. Parts Town's business is very simple to explain, yet uniquely difficult to execute. Every day, we help customers keep mission-critical equipment running. When a commercial kitchen goes down, when a refrigeration unit fails, or when a technician is working against the clock to get the kitchen back up and running, we are on the call. And across all of those situations, one challenge appears again and again for us, and that's parts identification—getting the right part to the right person at the right time. It sounds simple, but in reality, customers often don't have complete information. You can imagine that we get a blurry image, a partial part number, or a partial serial number. For nearly 40 years, our people have helped build expertise solving these specific problems. But as we continue to grow, we began asking a different question: how can every individual at Parts Town harness that expertise, not just those with years of tenure? And what if we could repeatedly compound that expertise, improving our operations and customer service with every single conversation? This isn't about taking our people out of the equation; it's about empowering them to leverage the very best of Parts Town to deliver an unparalleled customer experience. That question has become the foundation of our partnership with Palantir. And we had to begin by meeting our people where they're at, on the front line of customer support and on the phones. Our journey therefore began with PartsIQ, a tool deployed to our customer support teams to solve the core challenge of parts identification. By bringing together equipment information, manuals, service records, images, years of transcripts from customer calls, and historical knowledge, PartsIQ helps teams identify the right part faster and with much greater confidence. While on the phone with the customer, the support agent starts typing any details they receive. Whether it's manufacturer information, an incomplete part number, or a rough description of a part, the ontology starts working for them, scoping down to the relevant model to help the agent drive the conversation with that particular customer. PartsIQ was never designed to replace expertise; it was designed to amplify it. We're intentionally not replacing our people with AI agents; we're delivering our people the tools that they need to deliver the best possible support without losing what makes us uniquely great at Parts Town. PartsIQ traverses the ontology from the identified model to retrieve all relevant parts. At this point, PartsIQ has the context to start proactively guiding the user to that very specific answer. Partner, an agent operating against our ontology, jumps in to ask questions to scope down that very specific search, traversing the ontology context to expedite that resolution. Now, the customer isn't asking questions; they're assisting our agent in doing the work for them. That's a fundamental change for us. With minimal back and forth, customer support has identified the relevant part in seconds with the assistance of our new PartsIQ tool. The system will further steer the user to the most likely part, given its knowledge of how often these parts are deployed in the field and the full context of past support events. This context is all deeply linked in the ontology, and therefore traversable across our agents. With this all tightly integrated with the ontology, successfully identifying the part triggers two very key results. One, the support agent can now directly create a quote for the replacement to forward the workflow and close out the issue with that customer. And equally, if not more importantly, this resolution is captured in the ontology alongside any tribal knowledge that was used to inform that decision. That compounds our field context and further enhances the customer experience for the next caller. So PartsIQ changed the game for our people on the phones, but working with our Palantir forward-deployed engineers, we realized we could inject LLMs plus the ontology further up in the process. What if AIP could sit on the call with our people and proactively support them in delivering the best possible customer experience without losing that unique Parts Town experience? That was the inception of our work across our Unlimited Service Group, our division of 1,800 technicians in the field fixing commercial food equipment. Step one was to further transform the support process with agents working actively to support our people on the phone, backed by the same ontology that made PartsIQ so powerful. Rather than waiting for the support teams to type in queries, AIP is already capturing the conversation and guiding next steps based on our unique SOPs, our CRM data, and captured tribal knowledge. Customers can share photos, they can provide symptomatic pieces, and they can send appliance details—all of this is being actively captured by the AI agent in real time. Keep an eye on the right of the screen: the AI agent isn't just capturing the information, it's actively using it to populate the user's screen live on the call, proactively presenting them with the right customer information based on details shared on the call, and then suggesting next steps to accelerate the path to resolution. Those next steps appear directly in the user's chat window to facilitate a seamless chat with the customer. And of course, this is backed by a rich ontology, with all the relevant links to that identified customer immediately available by searching around that object. In seconds, we've identified the full history with a customer, all of their equipment, all of their warranty information, and more, without the user having to input a single search item, leaving them focused on what they're uniquely positioned to do: work with the customer to solve that problem. This is game-changing technology for us. With the additional details clarified, we dial in on further critical information: warranty details, equipment history, and everything conducive to the optimal support experience for that particular customer. But as we extend further down the decision chain, we realized we can push this initiative even further. Together with Palantir, we began reimagining the service lifecycle from end to end. Starting with a pilot inside one of our largest service brands, we worked side-by-side with the technicians, the dispatchers, and the operational leadership to build a solution from the ground up with our forward-deployed engineers, who have been absolutely fantastic from Palantir. We're able to compound on our existing ontology, extending this to the technicians' operations in the field: work orders, route optimization, and proactive service recommendations. The input is the same—the customer calls with a problem—but now the dispatch engine analyzes a work order in the context of thousands of similar ones that came before it. So two techs across branches, across the country can share the same historical knowledge base; this is a massive unlock for us across our different brands. We can recommend parts and inventory, best approach the job, route that technician with the most relevant experience to the job, and help maximize that fix rate on the very first visit we make. We again have agents working on behalf of our people, but we've massively expanded that scope, traversing all techs, their experiences, specialties, and availability to find the right individual for every work order. Here we see the agent working with the ontology context to identify a tech, prepare the work plan, and finalize the scheduling, completing all back-office work to get that technician to the customer who needs them as soon as possible. But of course, human and AI teaming means our skilled professionals are still in the loop. If they want to make a change to a schedule item, they jump into the same schedule our AI agent was writing earlier. Operators can leverage the best of the agent's work, but still make edits and inject their own unique context. The impact of this work extends far beyond technology. Across both PartsIQ and our ongoing USG use case, the projected value opportunity for our business exceeds 200 basis points of EBITDA margin—that's massive at scale. But the most important outcome is measured in capability, not in dollars. Today, Parts Town is building an intelligence platform that helps our people do what they do best: serve customers, solve problems, and make better decisions faster than ever before. What began as a parts identification challenge has become a broader transformation of how Parts Town fundamentally operates the business. Together with Palantir, we're not simply building tools; we're creating a new operating model where expertise, data, and AI work together. It is one that helps our people move faster, helps our customers succeed, strengthens our partnerships, and positions Parts Town to lead the future of specialty distribution. And we're just getting started. Thank you. Please welcome Chief Information Officer of the United States Department of Agriculture, Sam Berry. Hi, I'm Sam Berry, Chief Information Officer at the most important civilian department in the federal government: the Department of Agriculture. Going all the way back to the creation of our country, the founding fathers were passionate about the criticality of agriculture in national sovereignty, security, and even morality. President George Washington famously said, "It will not be doubted that with reference to either individual or national welfare, agriculture is of primary importance." To this day, agriculture remains a foundational element to our nation's success, both domestically and geopolitically. Look no further than the current conflict in the Middle East and its disruption to the fertilizer supply chain. Countries that are minimally self-sufficient are left in a state of emergency, while the United States is able to adapt and persevere. This self-sufficiency is not by accident, and it requires our constant attention to defend and protect as a national security asset. In that effort, it is important for us to realize that our most consequential battlefield is invisible. It resides in the networks, machinery, and data that power the core infrastructure of our nation, including the agriculture industry. Prior to this administration, the technology behind USDA's most important systems was stuck in a different era. There were hundreds of fragmented legacy systems, none of them designed to talk to each other. Here's how that translates to our farmer-facing systems. What you are looking at is an architecture that costs nearly a billion taxpayer dollars a year to support: thousands of contractors, hundreds of systems, and a cybersecurity challenge, to put it mildly. An environment like this makes it impossible to deliver a positive experience to our citizens. Fragments of farmer and farm records are duplicated and distributed across dozens of systems with little to no communication. USDA previously attempted to transform this, and it resulted in a ten-year modernization project that ran hundreds of millions of dollars over budget. One audit reported to Congress that an estimated fifteen percent of what was promised actually got delivered. The people inside USDA will tell you that that number is actually much closer to five percent. So, hundreds of millions of dollars were spent across multiple administrations with essentially zero progress. That is the estate that our current administration inherited. Thankfully, we have a team with private-sector experience who is willing to focus purely on outcomes to rebuild our systems with speed and precision using modern, state-of-the-art technology. We started at the foundation, because all of our problems were rooted in the fact that data was everywhere. This wasn't just a case of building pipelines; we had to start at the meta-level and define the semantics for our department. For example, what are the different attributes, documents, and historical information that define a farmer? What are the congressionally-required details and programs? Then we did the same exercise for a farm, and then the same exercise for a payment. These are all actually much more complex than you would expect. We realized that we were faced with an impossible task, and that the only way we could deliver on our mandate to reduce cost and improve security would be to take an entirely new approach. That is what led us to Palantir. With Palantir, we centralized USDA's core data assets under a single, unified model of reality. One record, one source of truth for each farmer. That's Palantir's ontology, and at the USDA we call it "one farmer, one file." It's the difference between data that sits in systems and context that actually understands the mission it serves. And this, of course, was key to delivering the latest technology. Everyone wants to implement AI and deliver stronger, more powerful solutions. But we know that pointing an LLM at hundreds of disconnected, ungoverned databases gets you a system that hallucinates, is insecure, and is unauditable. For something as consequential as our nation's agricultural data, that is not just useless—it's dangerous. The ontology has been the key to delivering AI-enabled technology to every farmer in the country. The LLMs will now be able to wield this governed model of reality—real farmers, real acreage, real programs—with permissions and lineage built in. On that foundation, we built Landmark. Landmark is the platform powering "one farmer, one file." It cuts through the red tape farmers face when they need something from USDA. It gives them and our staff digital-first tools they can use from home, reducing the time to complete tasks such as acreage reporting and payment certification, improving our overall ability to support the farmer without complicating the process. Here's the principle behind it: we're bringing this technology directly to the Americans who grow this country's food—people who would otherwise have little or no access to such tools. For a farmer, every hour of daylight matters. They shouldn't have to leave their land to receive critical services. Acreage reporting was a natural first area of focus, as it is something that nearly every farmer interfaces with. It is also the foundational data component to our larger national picture. This enables us to better understand our agricultural position so that we can respond to adverse events, provide intelligent recommendations to farmers on what crops will yield most successfully, and develop tooling which will detect foreign ownership of our farmlands. All of this is done while delivering a much better experience to farmers and saving them hours or days of time. We are currently deploying Gear, the tool that you see behind me, to county offices across the country to support USDA staff in reporting acreage in partnership with farmers. This streamlines the process, allowing users to subdivide land and report and certify acreage in one spot. For the first time ever, farmers will soon be able to report their own acreage independently, or work with county staff to do it on their behalf. So, we started with the operational side of agriculture, meeting farmers where they needed us today. But concurrently, we were able to quickly build on that same ontology to deliver major new initiatives like the Farmer Bridge Assistance program. For the first time ever, farmers could certify their information from the comfort of their home with a single click. One farmer told us that he submitted his application on his phone while boarding an airplane. This was the fastest deployment of capital to farmers in the history of this country. It was an eleven billion dollar program that provided payments to row-crop producers to stay solvent during market volatility. The program offered immediate cash flow relief to secure operations for the planting season while long-term, market-driven policy improvements are implemented. Within sixty-two minutes of opening, the program broke all prior USDA records for online farmer signups. Farmers were able to enroll without visiting a county office. It delivered over 4.4 billion dollars directly to farmers in the program's first five days. This wasn't a case of just building a new front-end portal for farmers to apply. Instead, this utilized all the connectivity of the ontology, enabling us to orchestrate the delivery of this program across multiple stages, the respective teams, and processes. Here we see one of those applications moving through the whole process. Palantir enabled us to connect these stages end-to-end, with human operators and technology working in parallel to ensure critical reviews were completed efficiently and accurately. Some steps are fully automated—the repetitive, mechanical work that used to take days. Others, like the state office review, are intentionally done by a person. The human review isn't a bottleneck we failed to remove; it's a checkpoint that we chose to keep. When you're deciding whether a farmer gets paid, someone is accountable for that decision. And every one of those decisions is logged and auditable. The platform allows us to control automation and apply it where it is needed. The result? Applications don't sit waiting. Actions, reviews, and updates are timely, and our farmers get what they need faster than ever before. Then, of course, this has to scale. Thousands of farmers flow through the process, all orchestrated on Landmark. Auditability is critical here: every action taken against an application is logged and can be audited if needed. We're running this process with better visibility and accuracy than ever before, without having to compromise on speed. So, you've seen how we were able to rapidly solve long-standing challenges for our department and deliver transformational programs to every farmer in the country. Now I want to talk about what's next and how solving these challenges unlocks USDA to start thinking about delivering truly next-generation tooling to every American farmer. Jumping back to acreage reporting, Landmark is already reducing the time it takes for county offices to report acreage and support our most critical programs. Now we're looking at how we can make this even more seamless with AI. Landmark Vision represents the next era, where farmers simply capture an image of their reporting documents and vision models extract all the details from the report. The crop type, plant date, irrigation status, and fields can all be captured using models, making it easier for farmers and staff to input information. Critically, this is built within our existing ontology context. This ensures the models are deployed with the appropriate guardrails and evaluations to ensure incorrect or infeasible information isn't captured. That connected ontology means that as soon as the details are extracted, all downstream actions and workflows are automatically kicked off, minimizing the need for the farmer to do any manual data entry. Workflows like this are positioning USDA for the future, and this is only the start. Let's take a step back and think about the system as a whole. A platform like this only works if the people it serves trust it. How farmers' data is used and protected is of paramount importance to us, and it is a significant part of why we're working with Palantir. Building everything on a single governed model means security isn't a wall we put around hundreds of fragmented systems. Instead, it's built into every aspect of the ontology. The same permission model that keeps our AI workflows evaluated and accurate is what protects the farmer. Every workflow, every integration, and every piece of farmer data is more secure than it has ever been, and every action can be traced. That same approach extends to a much larger national security mandate. The same ontology powers what we call the Admin Command Center: centralized visibility across the department to recognize risk before it reaches the food supply. Tracking who owns and who is buying US agricultural land used to be a slow, manual process. We're digitizing it and giving USDA clear visibility into land ownership so risk is identified quickly and proactively. Here we get a full view of the US agricultural landscape while also having visibility into threats and scenarios that could harm farmers and their ability to provide food. For example, we are able to produce comprehensive reporting on the impact of a potential drought. These signals equip USDA with the data we need to ensure our food supply remains resilient in every scenario. You've seen what is possible with this incredible technology—the pace that we can move when efficiency and outcomes are the focus, how operations can be transformed, and how the entire posture of our nation can shift through AI and proper data orchestration. But this is only the start. We still have a lot of work to do inside of USDA and beyond. Zooming out to the state of our republic, we have an imbalance in what we are capable of doing and what we are actually doing. In many ways, decadence and abundance have led to excessive corruption and waste. We have forty-plus million Americans dependent on state-sponsored food programs. Large corporations are poisoning our food supply in favor of profits. Politicians are pushing our country towards global dependence while allowing foreign adversaries to buy up our land. Intellectualism and technology are distracting our society and removing us from what it truly means to be human—to be one with the land, and to be one with how we nourish our families, communities, and nation. We have work to do. We need builders and innovators to activate and become excited about partnering with the government and serving our country. We need technology companies to operate as if every decision is life or death, leading to motivated, vibrant, hard-working cultures that develop products like what you see coming out of Palantir. Federal technology providers and their affiliates, their integrators, and their contractors must stop treating the government as a permanent opportunity for cost-overrun modernization efforts that too often lead to nowhere. Instead, they must pursue federal business with foundationally AI-enabled platforms, with lean engineering teams, and with rapid and aggressive deployment timelines. Learn from Palantir, and our country will be stronger, more secure, and more capable because of it. As an American, right now is the greatest time to be alive. We have the unique opportunity of being on the cusp of a golden age; all that we have to do is make it happen. We are on the frontier of AI. We have a president, a cabinet, and a general rising political class that is relentlessly pursuing American dominance and independence. We have a younger generation that is hungry, innovative, and ready to build like we've never built before. Data fusion and AI make every single person more capable. USDA is equipping farmers with the advanced technology they deserve, improving the resiliency of their operations while keeping our great nation secure. Thank you. Please welcome Head of Corporate Development of Palantir, Sasha Spivak. Another round of applause for our morning speakers. AIPCon 10 makes it sound like I can say something like looking back on a decade of AIP, when in reality, the first AIPCon was only three years ago. And in fact, it was meant to be our fourth FoundryCon. In that time, it felt internally like we were sprinting to deliver against a vision of an entirely new era: to deliver a new platform, and to deliver against what our customers needed. What we were actually doing was building on 15 years of investment into the Ontology. And today's moment, and in fact, every moment at Palantir, feels somewhat similar. We're able to sprint in the moment because we've been running a marathon for years. And the group of customers that we have the privilege of serving are the same way. Your tradecraft is built over years. It's hard-earned, it is highly specific, and it is an asymmetric advantage. Everything we do is in service of amplifying that tradecraft and amplifying that advantage. So even more than the gratitude that we feel for you sharing your stories, is the gratitude we feel for you doing the work itself. We know what it's like for the work to be hard. We know what it's like for the work to be unpopular. And we're so proud to work hard alongside you and to be unpopular alongside you. Have an incredible rest of the day. Please be painfully honest with each other. Thank you to everyone who's tuned in online, and thank you for creating this community.

WATCH LIVE: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent Testifies Before House Lawmakers

Forbes Breaking News(5.6M)·285:40·29.0K views·2026-06-04
Scott BessentTreasury Secretary
TL;DRTreasury Secretary Scott Bessent testified before the House Ways and Means Committee to defend the administration’s economic agenda, highlighting $325 billion in refunds from the Working Families Tax Cuts.
However, Democratic lawmakers aggressively challenged Bessent...

TL;DR

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent testified before the House Ways and Means Committee to defend the administration’s economic agenda, highlighting $325 billion in refunds from the Working Families Tax Cuts. However, Democratic lawmakers aggressively challenged Bessent over persistent inflation, high gas prices, a critical data security error involving the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), and controversies surrounding President Trump’s tax audit status.

***

The Working Families Tax Cuts & Middle-Class Relief

  • Tax Refund Milestones: Secretary Bessent declared the recent filing season the most successful in IRS history, resulting in approximately $325 billion in total refunds, with the average individual refund increasing by over 11%.
      • Over 62 million tax returns (representing 44% of filers) claimed at least one of the administration's signature provisions, including tax exemptions on tips, overtime, and Social Security, as well as deductibility of auto loan interest.
  • Demographic Reach of Tax Cuts: Committee Chairman Jason Smith and Secretary Bessent emphasized that the legislation primarily benefited lower- and middle-income Americans.
      • Tipped and Hourly Workers: Over 70% of filers receiving a tax cut earned under $100,000. Specifically, 90% of tipped workers utilizing the "no tax on tips" provision earned less than $100,000, and 96% of those utilizing the "no tax on overtime" deduction earned less than $200,000.
      • Pro-Family Initiatives: The Child Tax Credit was permanently increased to $2,200 and indexed for inflation to serve roughly 40 million families.
  • Support for Main Street: Lawmakers discussed the importance of Section 199A, which provides a permanent 20% deduction for pass-through small businesses.
      • 1099-K Threshold: The administration repealed the previous $600 reporting threshold, restoring the standard back to $20,000 and 200 transactions to reduce paperwork burdens on small service businesses.
      • Proposed Expansions: Rep. David Kustoff highlighted his proposed *Small Business Tax Cut Act*, which would increase the Section 199A deduction from 20% to 23%.

Macroeconomic Health, Inflation, and Geopolitics

  • Core Inflation Arguments: Bessent pointed out that core inflation has fallen to 2.8%, down from a peak of 6.6% during the prior administration.
      • Democratic members countered that "Trumpflation" continues to diminish real purchasing power, citing a 40% rise in utility costs, gas prices exceeding $4.00–$4.50 per gallon, and a national savings rate that dropped to 2.6%.
  • The Geopolitical "Blip": Bessent characterized elevated gas and grocery prices as a temporary, short-term spike driven exclusively by the conflict in Iran, asserting that prices would fall once the Strait of Hormuz blockades are resolved.
      • Rep. Judy Chu and Rep. Terri Sewell sharply disputed this, noting that the "war of choice" in Iran has cost American households over $100 billion, with gas and grocery costs remaining high far past Bessent's initial 50-day temporary prediction.
  • GDP Growth Discrepancies: Rep. Sewell pointed out that economic growth in late 2025 slowed to 0.5%, missing the Atlanta Fed's 5.4% GDP projection. Bessent defended the overall record, stating the U.S. has averaged 2.6% GDP growth per quarter since Trump took office.

Trade, Tariffs, and International Tax Policy

  • OECD Pillar 2 Agreement: Bessent highlighted a side-by-side agreement secured by the Treasury on January 5, 2026, which largely exempts U.S.-headquartered multinational enterprises from the OECD's Income Inclusion Rule (IIR) and Under-Taxed Payments Rule (UTPR), protecting the U.S. tax base from foreign overreach.
  • The Tariff Refund Dispute: Rep. Steven Horsford questioned Bessent on a $165 billion dispute regarding broad tariffs that the Supreme Court ruled unlawful.
      • Horsford criticized the Justice Department’s decision to appeal a court order aimed at accelerating tariff refunds to U.S. businesses. Bessent clarified that the Treasury acts strictly as the payment agent and does not control the Justice Department's litigation strategy.
  • Russian Sanctions and Waivers: Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick questioned the Treasury’s 30-day extension of waivers permitting the purchase of Russian seaborne oil.
      • Bessent defended the extension as a measure to stabilize global energy prices requested by vulnerable nations, but emphasized that the administration has enacted unprecedented sanctions on Russian state oil giants Lukoil and Rosneft.

Data Security, Regulatory Oversight, and IRS Investigations

  • DOGE Data Breach: Rep. Suzan DelBene confronted Bessent with a recent Government Accountability Office (GAO) audit of the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), led by Elon Musk.
      • The audit revealed a DOGE employee was mistakenly granted temporary administrative access to a Bureau of the Fiscal Service payment system housing millions of Americans' Social Security numbers and banking information.
      • Bessent accepted responsibility but asserted the access was strictly "read-only" and that data protocols have since been reinforced.
  • Presidential Audit Immunity: Rep. Linda Sanchez and Rep. Brad Schneider scrutinized a legal settlement between the Justice Department and President Trump that reportedly shields the President and his family from standard IRS audits. Bessent declined to comment, citing ongoing litigation.
  • Nonprofit Status Reviews: Rep. Nicole Malliotakis and Chairman Smith targeted foreign-funded nonprofit organizations accused of sowing civil discord or supporting foreign adversaries like China and Iran. The committee has urged the IRS to investigate and potentially revoke the 501(c)(3) tax-exempt status of 11 organizations, including Code Pink.

Future-Focused Financial Policies

  • The Launch of Trump Accounts: Bessent reported that nearly 6 million "Trump Accounts" have been opened to help children build long-term savings through compound growth.
      • 86% of the accounts are linked to families making under $200,000, with 1.4 million children qualifying for a $1,000 seed contribution from the Treasury.
      • Major private philanthropists, including Michael and Susan Dell, have committed $6.25 billion to contribute $250 directly to eligible children in lower-income zip codes.
  • Digital Asset Parity Act: Representatives Max Miller and Steven Horsford advocated for the bipartisan *Digital Asset Parity Act* to provide tax certainty for stablecoins, digital asset lending, and staking rewards. Bessent agreed that establishing clear tax frameworks is vital to maintaining global U.S. dollar dominance.

***

Notable Quotes

"This Tax Day, under President Trump, we celebrated how much more money hardworking Americans kept, not how much the government took." — Scott Bessent, U.S. Treasury Secretary
"Workers and families are being forced to stretch their dollars further every day and the pain so clearly stems from many of these projects... We've watched the price hikes that are coming because of this tariff regime." — Richard Neal, House Ways and Means Committee Ranking Member
"We are creating productive capacity here, and productive capacity is what generates future tax revenue... I call it the slingshot effect." — Scott Bessent, U.S. Treasury Secretary
The committee will come to order. Welcome back, Secretary Bessent, to the best committee in all of Congress, the Ways and Means Committee. Since you last came before this committee, the average American has more money in their pocket from the Working Families Tax Cuts. This filing season, refunds were up more than 11%, and tax refunds totaled nearly $325 billion. A family of four with two kids making $73,000 or less owed zero in federal income taxes. Sixty-two million Americans claimed at least one of President Trump's new tax relief priorities, such as no tax on tips, no tax on overtime, no tax on Social Security, and no tax on auto loan interest. Your department just revealed that the majority of Americans who benefited from these policies earn under $100,000. Nearly 6 million kids now have Trump accounts to save for their future. Nearly 40 million families claimed the Child Tax Credit that was increased to $2,200 and permanently, permanently indexed for inflation so that the credit, the credit never loses value. Americans across the country are experiencing what tax relief means for them. At a hearing, a waiter from Arizona told us how he is using his tax savings from no tax on tips to start law school. A Texas steel worker said he can now afford to start a family from no tax on overtime. A couple in Texas adopted a second child with the help of the Adoption Tax Credit. And in every community, working-class Americans have their own version of the story because President Trump, because President Trump and Republicans in Congress delivered real tax relief. However, every single Democrat voted against this tax relief, hiding behind the false claim that these tax cuts only help billionaires. But we all know the truth. There aren't any billionaires who work for tips. I don't know any billionaires that work for overtime. There aren't any billionaires who rely on Social Security. There aren't any billionaires that need an auto loan to afford a car. Republicans targeted tax relief to the Americans whose paychecks were stretched thin by Democrat inflation that needed help. The Working Families Tax Cuts provided bigger refunds and bigger paychecks and permanent, permanent pro-growth tax policy. And it will make it easier to build, to invest, produce, and hire in America. Our economy is in the middle of a historic, historic construction boom that means new jobs and opportunity for working-class Americans. Factory activity grew every month this year and reached its highest level since May of 2022. Equipment investment rose 17%, and investment in intellectual property rose 11% in the first quarter of this year. In the same period, economic growth was driven by two things: investment and exports. Republican policies are making investing in America a solid bet for companies after decades, decades of America's manufacturing power declining. The President's tax and trade policies work hand-and-glove to fuel the economic investment that creates great-paying jobs. Reciprocal trade agreements have secured $10 trillion in investment commitments in leading industries, opened new markets formerly hostile to US exports, and made our trading relationships fairer for American workers, farmers, manufacturers, and producers. American producers exported just last year a record $3.4 trillion. And in the first three months of this year, first three months of this year, US exports were over $300 billion each month. That's the highest in 250 years of our country's history. America has become less dependent on China, a goal long promised by Washington politicians with no actual progress until President Trump.
