But we invented today's numerals and this enabled a whole area of mathematics, like a child can do mathematics very quickly, and so it enabled these very tangible new ways of thought. And I think this is the opportunity ahead of us, this possibility to expand what we think about and have new tangible things that we think about, and this requires—this requires a very intentional research work and product work in this direction. When I interviewed you early 2023, ChatGPT had just changed everything. You were CTO of OpenAI. A few folks I talked to said you basically ran the place, just saying. When you left to found Thinking Machines, were you running towards something or away from something? I most definitely was running towards something once I figured out what that thing was. But I had an incredible—I mean, I had an incredible experience at OpenAI. I was so incredibly lucky to work with some of the most dedicated and most talented people in the world, and that's—that's incredibly special, and I'm very grateful for that experience. And eventually, I had my own view of, and very strong view of, how I think this technology ought to be developed, and it's very rare to start something from scratch once you have developed such a strong perspective on it. And that's—that's a rare privilege to have, and I think having a company like Thinking Machines gives us an opportunity to focus on where we have the highest conviction and build and orient the entire company around that conviction. Take me back to the board crisis of that year, later that year. You testified under oath that you were worried OpenAI was at catastrophic risk of falling apart. You raised concerns about Sam Altman's leadership. But in that moment, you had to act. Looking back, do you think you got that moment right? It was a very intense time. And it was a very complex situation, and I'm sure many people have been in situations that have felt impossible, where you have to make really hard calls and very quickly. And I think there is a part of this complexity that wasn't—to the world, it appeared that it happened overnight, but to me, it was years of thinking about, you know, the mission, the governance, how you build a team that can build durable, transformational technologies with that responsibility. There was a lot of complexity to the organization that was there for years, and we had thought about a lot. This particular thing that happened, this acute moment, there was a lot of intensity to it and time. And, you know, when the board asked me for feedback, I shared feedback, and I stand by that. And when they made the decision and asked me to step up as interim CEO, I did that. And when I realized that their decision was like potentially catastrophic for the company and things would potentially fall apart, I felt like I had to act very quickly. And even though on the surface it just looked very chaotic, I think at each point in time, I felt very clear about what I had to do because the thing that I cared the most about was the mission, the continuity of the mission, and the people and the team. And this is not like an abstract thing; it's real people that I worked with for many, many years and cared very deeply about. And all of this was about to potentially implode. So it was very clear to me what I had to do to create—to provide continuity, stability, and help bring Sam back, restore that, get the team in place to then deliver GPT-4o and o1. And so that was—I think it was that principle that made it very clear to me what I had to do. In retrospect, I think that I would have paused more on understanding the transition plan. And like of course, with the benefit of hindsight, there wasn't any transition plan, and there wasn't much thought put into transparency, bringing the team along, providing continuity, and in retrospect, I would have paused more on that. Helen Toner testified, former OpenAI board member, "Mira was waiting to see which way the wind would blow, and she didn't realize she was the wind." What do you think would have happened if you didn't do what you did? I think quite likely OpenAI would have imploded. The case got thrown out on a technicality. So the court never decided whether the leaders of OpenAI breached their mission. How much does the character of the people building the most powerful technology in the world, how much does that matter? Whose hand is on the dial? I think the integrity, character, values of the people matter a lot because there are a lot of decisions, micro-decisions you have to make every day and you have to trust your team that they're making the right calls. And I do think there is plenty of focus on that and that's great. Where there is less focus is sort of like the institutional design, decision-making, transparency, and increasing the level of agency that people have to actually decide for themselves. So it doesn't have to be like an authority that says, you know, "this is safe, this is good, stamp of approval," but each person can sort of weigh for themselves what works for them. And I think the way that we're going about building AI systems today is just very concentrated, and it's part of the legacy way because we didn't have real-world data and experience. The only way to advance AI was sort of in a silo and in a vacuum. But I think today it's a different time to build where we can actually learn a lot from the capabilities, tensions, and limitations in the real world and use that data and information to actually steer the direction of research. And this enables people to actually think for themselves. Like often, people will ask me about ChatGPT and how the impact of all of that. And of course, it's magnificent from a technology perspective, but I think the most important aspect of—or impact of—ChatGPT is bringing AI into the public consciousness, and everyone kind of understanding what it is by interacting with it versus being told what AI can do and what the capabilities and limitations are. What do you think this moment demands of a leader? And should we trust the leaders in power right now, should we trust Sam Altman? I think that everyone should be—everyone should have the tools and information to be able to make these decisions for themselves. And ideally like, you know, the structure of governance and decision-making should not hinge on one person; there should be checks and balances. I think it's very important who is working on the systems and, you know, the character of people but I do think that the conversation gets too wrapped up there and not enough thinking more broadly about checks and balances and systems, because even people that are well-intended, they can make mistakes and they can misestimate the—the consequences of making a call. And so morality is not everything; you have to think about actual decision-making structures and transparency and governance, and these are all complicated things. And to get as many people involved in these things as possible, you actually have to share the knowledge, you have to share tools, and this is part of the reason why we've actually taken a more open approach with our lab. So, that was my next question which is, how is everything that you've learned shaping what you're building at Thinking Machines and how you're building, and the culture that you want to create? Yeah, I think I have very high conviction that the way to continue building frontier AI systems is to bring people along and to have humans in the loop. Like actually having humans in the loop doesn't quite describe it because it sounds like a checkpoint where we're signing off something and then you're good to go. It's more like creating systems that are not just like autonomously advancing and leaving civilization behind, but are more like a tandem bike where both people are pedaling, but, you know, when you're going up a hill maybe whoever is stronger is pedaling harder. But both hands are on the—are on the wheel, and that's quite important because that's a different system; it's a system designed for collaboration. And—and that's what we're trying to build with Thinking Machines and I think it's quite differentiated, and I hope that it will increase the level of agency that people have, and also it will help steer the research direction towards creating outputs that are more value-aligned. And so you also get alignment as a result of this approach in addition to usefulness, like actually creating technologies that are useful in the real world. You've hired a lot of top talent. We've reports of nine-figure deals. There've also been some high-profile departures. How would you describe the war for talent? How brutal is it and what should we read into these exits? It's definitely a big part of building a frontier AI lab, having the right people and that's, you know, people that have the competence to do it but also are aligned with your overall mission, conviction, what you're trying to achieve. I wouldn't call it a war because it might mean that the highest bidder wins, and—and I think for the most part, people that are working in this field are very, you know, they genuinely care about advancing the field. Now, it is an absolutely crazy time and a lot of it is unprecedented. It's incredibly intense to build a frontier AI lab from scratch during such competitive times. And so that leads to, you know, a lot of uncertainty, unpredictability, and some of the volatility that we've seen. But yeah, I think when you're trying to compress progress in such a short amount of time, things that happen in other startups, companies over the course of 10 years, five years, both good and bad, are going to happen in the course of a few months and in the course of a year. And I think part of—part of what we're seeing is contextualized in that. Just compressing the amount of progress that's happening, and whenever you have something good, something bad will come with it, and so you have to balance the two. But people leave for different reasons, you know, maybe their passion evolved for what they want to work on. I think the—the high numbers and the compensation numbers, they capture the imagination of people because obviously they're very big, but I do think that, you know, some of the most sought-after people, that's—that's not—that's not the main story. And I think there is a lot that goes on here and it is very intense to build a company from scratch, let alone a frontier AI lab. So that's part of, you know, it's part of the challenges and suffering of building a company and doing anything zero to one. Dario Amodei has predicted mass, you know, white-collar job loss, Sam though recently walked back some of his predictions about a job job apocalypse. Where do you think the industry is being too pessimistic and too optimistic? What is Mira Murati's vision for the future? So, you know, predicting sort of a dystopia or utopia, to me feels—feels very, very simplified because the truth is we actually have a lot of agency in how we build this technology, in the tools that we're building, how we're deploying it, and—and so it's not a predestined outcome. There are certainly those risks; we all understand the potential for greatness that comes with building frontier AI systems, and that's why we're working on them. And I think there has been a lot of talk around the uncertainty, around the downsides, and I agree with a lot of these risks, and perhaps where I might disagree or take a different path is that I think we have a lot of agency. This period of time where both humans and AI systems have their hands on the wheel and we can collaborate is a very important time to get right. And the more that we can reduce that discontinuity that comes from new capabilities and huge change in capabilities, the better. And this is why we took this approach with Thinking Machines and why the company exists. You say the goal is to keep humans in the loop, but is there ever a point where humans don't need to be in the loop? And then what happens? It is—it is possible. It's more about aligning, at that point, it's more about aligning intent and aligning value. But if you—if you sort of reduce progress to removing humans from this loop of development already now, then I see very little future possibilities that we can get this right in the future when AI systems are even more capable. And so I think the more likely path and more durable path to—to advance AI in a way that enables and advances civilization is by keeping humans in the loop for as long as we can and really making sure that this period is actually very meaningful. You've raised a lot of money, there's great expectations. If anything's clear from, you know, the last few months in—in AI news, there are some, you know, it's a cutthroat competition out there. Do you think you have that killer instinct to take on OpenAI and Anthropic and Meta and Google and China and X? Like, it's a competitive world. It is extremely competitive and yeah, we did raise a lot of money. We're—we're proud of that, but that's—that's not any big accomplishment. We didn't set out to break some sort of record. It's really what you do with that, and—and—and we're not a normal company and so we do need a lot of capital in order to build the infrastructure, the foundations of science, and all that's required to actually have a claim at building frontier AI systems that are differentiated. And so I think that part in itself is not unique. And yeah, in terms of killer instinct, I'd say that's not really what motivates me to, you know, like I think there is actually a lot of room. We're building—we're building a technology that has, you know, infinite potential, and I think that it's going to be hard to capture all of that potential. And what motivates me is really capturing part of that potential, creating useful things in the world, bringing the technology into the world in a way that's useful and increases human agency and advances our civilization. I think, you know, there is—there is healthy competition and competition is good. It—it actually like creates better products and better technologies for people, it raises the bar in many ways, and—and, you know, I—I respect a lot of the companies that are doing this. But my—my motivation is not necessarily day-to-day when I wake up in the morning, I am not thinking about how to kill the competitor. Love that. You've said you plan to release a preview later this year. How far along are you? What will we see? With all of these other companies going public, do you think they'll be distracted and you have a little more room to run? We're—we're on our own path. Of course, the competition is fierce and we have to move quickly, but it's important to balance that with, you know, durable long-term progress. I care a lot about the decisions that we make today being good for our long-term success, and that's something that I really try to instill in the team day-to-day. There is a lot of details here with how you build the team, how you build the infrastructure, and really resisting the short-term pressures, and—and in some cases, you do have to kind of embrace the pressure. But yeah, I would say for us, this is just a first step in—in showcasing the interaction models and making it more concrete what it is that we think about and care about. And I expect the next few months to—to show increased capabilities, for us to show increased capabilities on the model side, more products in this direction, and make it more obvious, like really letting the technology and the products speak for themselves. So, if Thinking Machines succeeds beyond your wildest expectations, what exists in five years that doesn't exist today? It is extremely difficult to predict the future. But I'd say that the most important thing would be to create a future where regardless of how—how many of hours of work we do day-to-day or week-to-week, we feel a sense of agency, a sense of dignity, and possibility about the future. And I hope that we'll continue to advance the capabilities that will enable a lot of progress and we'll do it in this way that will keep—will keep us hopeful and also with a sense of possibility that we can drive about the future. Well, here's to less impossibility and more possibility. Thank you. Thank you.

Should You Invest In Digital Credit Backed By Bitcoin?

Anthony Pompliano(645.0K)·52:18·5.4K views·2026-06-04
Matt ColeCEO, Strive Asset Management Anthony PomplianoHost, Anthony Pompliano
TL;DRDigital credit—high-yield preferred equity backed by Bitcoin—is emerging as a critical transition asset to bridge the gap between fiat currency debasement and a hyper-bitcoinized future.
By eliminating maturity and equity conversion risks for issuers while providing...

TL;DR

Digital credit—high-yield preferred equity backed by Bitcoin—is emerging as a critical transition asset to bridge the gap between fiat currency debasement and a hyper-bitcoinized future. By eliminating maturity and equity conversion risks for issuers while providing double-digit yields to investors, instruments like Strive's daily-dividend-paying "Seta" are redefining institutional fixed-income portfolios.

Understanding Digital Credit and Its Value Proposition

  • The Mechanics of Seta: Seta is Strive Asset Management's flagship preferred equity security, currently paying a variable interest rate of 13% (compared to Strategy's competing Stretch product, which pays 11.5%).
      • Carry Trade Strategy: The issuer uses investor capital to buy Bitcoin, betting that Bitcoin's long-term compounded annual growth rate (CAGR)—projected by Strive at roughly 30% over the next decade—will comfortably outpace the financing cost.
  • Key Advantages for Issuers: Digital credit solves structural financing issues for Bitcoin treasury companies.
      • Maturity Risk Mitigation: It provides a perpetual liability, matching the perpetual, non-cash-flow nature of Bitcoin.
      • Eliminating Negative Convexity: Unlike convertible debt, it prevents the liability from converting to equity when Bitcoin's price surges, allowing common equity holders to capture full upside performance.
  • The "Transition Asset" Thesis: As fiat currencies face systemic debt crises, digital credit serves as a vital bridge to a Bitcoin-standard economy.
      • Volatilty Absorption: It allows risk-averse investors to participate in Bitcoin's upside via a stable, yield-bearing instrument, smoothing the transition over the next 20 to 50 years.
      • Fresh Demand Source: The success of these instruments drives secondary demand for Bitcoin, potentially accelerating hyper-bitcoinization.

Dividend Innovation and Risk Mitigation Strategies

  • Daily Dividend Payouts: Strive is positioning Seta as the first listed security in US capital markets history to pay a daily dividend.
      • Compressing Volatility: Transitioning from monthly payouts to a daily, continuous stream eliminates the massive price and volume spikes typically observed right before monthly dividend events, making the asset behave more like a money market fund or savings account.
  • Robust Cash and Asset Reserves: To survive severe market downturns, Strive maintains a zero-debt balance sheet backed by deep reserves.
      • Extended Runway: Strive holds over 12 months of cash in STRC reserves and 6 months of Stretch reserves, recently increasing its Seta dividend reserve from 12 to 18 months despite entering a bear market.
      • Bear Market Modeling: In a severe downside scenario where Bitcoin bottoms at $40,000 and remains flat until late 2027, Strive can pay all dividends purely from cash reserves without selling any Bitcoin. Beyond cash reserves, the firm holds 5 to 7 years of Bitcoin coverage.
  • Unprecedented Transparency: Strive updates its risk disclosures and metrics on its website every 15 seconds.
      • This starkly contrasts with traditional private credit, where institutional investors are accustomed to receiving opaque asset marks only once every three months.

Critiques, Target Audiences, and Corporate Treasury Shifts

  • The "Just Buy Bitcoin" Critique: Conviction-driven, volatility-tolerant investors should buy Bitcoin or amplified Bitcoin directly rather than digital credit.
      • Product-Market Fit: Digital credit is specifically designed for the vast majority of investors who are volatility-averse, require reliable cash flows, or are looking for a productive replacement for the fixed-income portion of a traditional 60/40 portfolio.
  • Lack of Traditional Debt Protections: Because digital credit is preferred equity and not debt, investors take on the maturity risk of the issuer in exchange for high double-digit yields.
  • Evaluating Michael Saylor's 32 BTC Sale: MicroStrategy’s recent sale of 32 Bitcoin was widely criticized as a "U-turn," but it represents a necessary evolution in capital allocation.
      • Optimizing Total Returns: Establishing a willingness to sell enables tax-loss harvesting and dynamic treasury management.
      • Net Buyer Reality: Despite occasional tactical sales, MicroStrategy remains a structural net buyer of Bitcoin on a monthly basis, and dynamic management ultimately reduces the risk of the firm becoming a catastrophic "forced seller."

The Competitive Landscape and the Future of Tokenization

  • Cooperative Capitalism: The rivalry between Strive (Seta/ASST) and Strategy (Stretch/STRC) is a "friendly competition" that actively grows the market.
      • Open-Source Innovation: Strive pioneered cash reserves and cleared daily dividend distributions with Nasdaq and the DTCC, creating templates that Strategy and other future issuers can replicate.
  • Preventing Single Points of Failure: A healthy ecosystem requires a highly diversified landscape of 20 to 50 issuers to prevent federal regulators or market shocks from targeting a single dominant corporate entity.
      • Path to a Digital Credit ETF: Launching a diversified, compliant 1940 Act ETF will require at least 30 distinct digital credit issuers.
  • The Tokenization Frontier: Digital credit is the ideal asset class for securitization and tokenization.
      • Challenging Commercial Banks: If tokenized digital credit is eventually integrated with debit cards and instant payments, consumers can bypass traditional bank deposits entirely, escaping depreciating fiat currencies while earning double-digit yields on their liquid balances.

Notable Quotes

"As a Bitcoiner and a long-time Bitcoiner, I firmly believe that this debt crisis will not get better and we're transitioning to a Bitcoin future. And I think digital credit could be the most important asset in this transition period..." — Matt Cole
"If you move to something like Strive is doing with Seta, where it will be the first, literally the first listed security in US capital markets history... to pay a daily dividend, then that dividend event is not an event that you really want to plan around... It should reduce and compress the volatility." — Matt Cole
"If we want our political system to allow Bitcoin, to allow us to have Bitcoin in self-custody, then having corporations—highly profitable corporations, large corporations—that have a material interest in Bitcoin succeeding is actually one of the best protective moats that self-custody Bitcoiners could have." — Matt Cole
As a Bitcoiner and a long-time Bitcoiner, I firmly believe that this debt crisis will not get better and we're transitioning to a Bitcoin future. And I think digital credit could be the most important asset in this transition period. What's going on, guys? Today we got a great conversation with Matt Cole. Matt is the CEO of Strive Asset Management, and in this conversation, we do a breakdown of digital credit. He explains what it is, how it works, what the risks are, and how they would respond to different situations. We also get into all of the pros and cons of both Strategy and Strive having these instruments in the market, and how institutional investors are responding. I found this conversation fascinating. It helped me better understand digital credit. I hope it helps you as well. Here's my conversation with Matt Cole. All right, Matt. I want to talk about digital credit. This seems to be taking the world by storm, but at the same time is creating massive controversy. Can you just describe: what is the problem that digital credit is solving? Yeah, I've been reflecting on this a lot, and I think the problem that it's solving is actually bigger than I first imagined. So maybe I'll start with what I thought the problem was that it was solving, and kind of what I think the problem is that it's solving now. When we first launched Seta, what I viewed it as was just a preferred equity security. It pays a high interest, it is backed by Bitcoin risk, and as an issuer, I was concerned with a couple of risks on the issuer side—namely, maturity risk, just because Bitcoin is such a long-duration asset, it has no cash flow, and we're trying to underwrite a perpetual bull thesis in Bitcoin. And what I wanted to do is have the longest liability I possibly could have, which is obviously a perpetual liability. Then secondarily, I wanted to remove negative convexity to the maximal degree to the upside. Because we know Bitcoin over time on average goes up and to the right—and I think it's going to go to literally infinity—I would prefer for my liability to not convert to equity when Bitcoin's ripping higher. That can constrain the total return as an issuer. So, I just viewed digital credit as a better source of financing, and I was happy to pay a double-digit interest rate to not have the negative convexity to the downside of maturity risk, and not have negative convexity to the upside of equity conversion. I just thought that was a good trade for us as an issuer, and so I wanted to be all-in on digital credit. And just coming from the fixed income world, I know how yield-starved we are. You've covered a lot, and I agree with this, the concept of the 60/40 portfolio being dead. Coming from a fixed income background and just thinking through that, it's almost like a consensus position in our circles that 60/40 is dead. But what is completely not consensus is: what do you do with the 40? Do you put it in Bitcoin? Do you put it in prediction markets? Do you put it in digital credit? What do you do with it? There are a million ideas. Do you put it in trend-following solutions? I've seen so many different ideas, a lot of interesting ideas, but I thought digital credit could make a play at that. So that was just kind of the simplistic idea to start. Where I think it's going now is something much bigger. This much bigger idea is that right now, fiat currencies are still the primary form of currency. The dollar is still the reserve currency of the world. As a long-time Bitcoiner, I firmly believe that this debt crisis will not get better and we're transitioning to a Bitcoin future. The hardest part about that transition is: how long will it take? No one knows the answer to that. I think Bitcoin continues to go up over the course of time, but does it take 5 years? I don't think so. Does it take 10 years? Does it take 20 years? Does it take 30 years? Does it take 50 years? No one really knows what that transition will look like. But when you look at other emerging countries that have had their currencies debased, what you'll see is that as that starts to happen, more and more citizens look for alternative things to use as currencies. They look to ditch the Argentine peso or whatever currency we're talking about, and I think that's going to happen in the US as well. And I think digital credit could be the most important asset in this transition period that kind of smooths it out and minimizes the volatility. People can see where it goes, but you don't have to make as hard of a prediction of when it happens, and you don't have to ride out as much volatility. What's interesting is that if that thesis plays out, and digital credit is this transition asset—maybe the ultimate transition asset, though I don't think it'll be the only transition asset—then it in and of itself could actually accelerate hyper-Bitcoinization, because you actually have fresh sources of demand coming into digital credit. So I think it plays that role. I don't think it's a forever thing. I think if we move to a hyper-Bitcoinization world, then Bitcoin becomes money, but I think digital credit could become very interesting for several decades. Now, when we think about this digital credit, can you just explain the very simple concept? You all are taking capital from investors, promising them this yield in perpetuity, and then you are buying Bitcoin. How does this work? When you describe it to someone for the first time, what do you say? Yeah, so as an issuer, it's really a carry trade. We have a cost of capital. For Seta, it's 13% right now. It's a variable rate because, as the issuer, we are trying to peg it and minimize volatility around par, which is 100, so it could go up or it could go down. For Stretch, Strategy's products, it's 11.5%. And so what we are making an investment bet on is that the average compounded annual growth rate of Bitcoin into the future will be better than that financing rate. That is what needs to be true for our common equity investors to outperform Bitcoin. Bitcoin is the hurdle rate; that has to be true. That does not have to be true for the preferred equity investor to win and to have a great asset that could pay the interest in perpetuity. The interest rate required for that to be true, for them, is much lower. So for Seta right now, it's in the neighborhood of 6.5% as that break-even interest rate where Strive could pay interest literally forever into the future. Sometimes people hear that and they say, "Oh, Strive is saying that if Bitcoin goes up 6.5%, everybody wins." No, everybody doesn't win. The Seta investor wins because they have a good credit. For the common equity investor, how it would likely play out would be that Bitcoin is going up, call it 6.5% on average, and the common equity probably largely just holds flat. It doesn't participate because all the Bitcoin returns are filtering into the preferred equity instrument. So, it's just a structured finance instrument where you have the more senior preferred and the more junior common. Now, when you think about this actual return of Bitcoin going forward, what do you think that is? Is that 20%, 30%, or 40% year-over-year, let's say for the next decade? Yeah, we have a long-term projection, call it for the next couple of decades, in the neighborhood of 30% a year. Right now, with Bitcoin in a bear market, when Bitcoin goes down, that compounded annual growth rate projection actually goes higher to make up for the bear market. And so right now it would be on the higher end of that range. I think this is a time where you want to take risk. This kind of gets into the theory of amplification for a Bitcoin treasury company. What I mean by that is that we have stated we think somewhere in the neighborhood of 30% to 60% or 70% is, for a clean balance sheet company, the range of amplification that generally makes sense and can be managed. The reason that we go to 60% to 70% actually is to say that Bitcoin going down itself could actually push amplification beyond that in the depths of a bear market. And so we ourselves as an issuer would likely stop around there, when Bitcoin's kind of at a current level right around a 200-week moving average. But I think you really want to go and be aggressive—the concept of being greedy when others are fearful—and you really want to push it to the max exactly at this period of time. On average, though, a 30% CAGR is what I think we should expect. I don't think that that's really that crazy; it tends to be right around where I think it is as well. When you look at the way that you are paying out these dividends, obviously everything was monthly. Now we see Stretch going to twice a month, and you all have gone to daily. Can you talk about the pros and cons of you guys going to daily or even twice a month? Yeah, it really gets into this epiphany I've been having of how big digital credit could be. Digital credit is a preferred equity instrument—it's not debt, it's not a money market, it's not money—but in this transitionary period, to the extent that you can make the dividend a more continuous stream of events, then what it means is that someone that holds it doesn't need to hold it waiting for the next dividend event. Right now, when it's monthly, what you'll see in the behavior of digital credit is the price of Stretch or Seta moving up to par right around the dividend event, and you'll see the volume spike, which I think is the most interesting tell. Sometimes, if you're just looking at price, you actually miss the liquidity, which I think is the most important driver of what's kind of happening behind the scenes here. And you see a massive liquidity spike because everybody wants that dividend event, and then post-dividend event, it drops on average by about the price of the dividend, but some months more and some months less. That economically makes sense; it's not anything that's crazy. But if you move to something like Strive is doing with Seta, where it will be the first listed security in US capital markets history to pay a daily dividend, then that dividend event is not an event that you really want to plan around. It's just a continuous stream. You either like Seta or you don't like Seta. And if you like it, you just hold it. You're not trying to time a dividend event, which should reduce and compress the volatility. And if you reduce and compress the volatility, then it can be used more like a money market fund, or like a savings account type of instrument. Now, when you think about what can go wrong here, the first thing that my mind goes to is: if you have to pay this in perpetuity, do you always have to pay a dividend? Could you pause the dividend? It sounds like you could lower the rate. What are some of the things that you could do to maybe mitigate risk over a very long period of time? Because a couple of years sounds great, but what about 20 or 30 years from now? Yeah, so what are the rights of the issuer? We could pause dividends, and we actually would have a fiduciary obligation as a board to pause dividends if paying a dividend would put Strive into bankruptcy risk. The next natural question is, well, what does the balance sheet look like? What would that scenario look like where something like that would be in the interest of the issuer of Strive? Seta is our flagship product, and if we were to do something that impaired the confidence in Seta, that would permanently impair the entire strategy that we're trying to execute. Obviously, as an issuer, we're going to do everything we can to not do that. But it's a fair question to ask: what does your balance sheet look like? Strive has no debt. We have 18 months of cash and marketable securities—so cash in STRC and reserves—we have over 12 months of cash, and about 6 months of Stretch reserves. What that means is that when you start to look at risk, you ask: what does downside risk look like? If you were to assume the 2022-2023 bear market played out as an example, what that would mean in Bitcoin terms today would be about a $40,000 Bitcoin bottom, and for Bitcoin to not move off of the 200-week moving average to the upside until very late 2027. That is not an impossible scenario to imagine. It is more bearish than my base case scenario—I'm pretty bullish on Bitcoin for the back half of this year—but it's not an insane scenario. If that scenario were to play out, Strive could literally do nothing and just use our cash dividend reserves, and not have to sell a single Bitcoin through that entire bear market. And then, if you were to say, well, what if Bitcoin has something worse than 2022-2023 that is longer in duration and down longer? Well, then we still have, depending on where you think the Bitcoin price is, five to seven years of Bitcoin coverage at $40,000 in and of itself. So once you start dipping into the Bitcoin, you really have to start thinking about a scenario where it's a substantially longer and worse bear market than 2022-2023 in duration and downside before we even have to touch our Bitcoin. And so it becomes very hard—not impossible, but very hard—to see a scenario where we have to actually pause the dividends. You basically have to underwrite Bitcoin failing for that to be true. When an investor buys Seta, how much of the dollar that they give you goes into Bitcoin versus going into the dollar reserve? It's a dynamic question. We're not just issuing Seta, we're also issuing the common equity, ASST. And we don't have a strict mandate to preserve the dividend reserve, so actually looking at the history of our actions is more interesting than anything I could say. Don't trust, verify. When we IPO'd Seta in November, we started with a 12-month dividend reserve. Bitcoin was over $100,000 a coin when we IPO'd Seta. We've since gone into a bear market, and so it would be natural to say, "Well, I would expect that in a Bitcoin bear market, that might be when an issuer would start to use the dividend reserve." That's probably what it's for. But what Strive has actually done is we've increased the dividend reserve from 12 months to 18 months. We were under no obligation to do so. So not only have we maintained it, we've increased it as Seta has been increasing in size. The reason is that we are actually seeing the opportunity for Seta to be bigger than we expected, and we want to do everything as an issuer to preserve confidence in this instrument to be able to withstand downside scenarios. And so, right now we feel that it's pretty appropriate to try to maintain an 18-month dividend reserve. I think it would be very possible, like if Bitcoin went down to $40,000, that we would use it. The reserve is a reserve, and if capital markets and liquidity conditions completely dried up, it should be used when needed—otherwise, what's the point of even having it? So I think it's possible we could dip into it. It's possible with Seta issuance that we often will put 100% of the money raised into Bitcoin. It's also possible that we might reserve an 18-month or a 12-month dividend reserve if we think that's the most appropriate action. But what's been actually happening is that both the common equity ASST and Seta have been firing. So it's really a dynamic question, but what is the first principle? What is the goal? The goal is to maintain confidence and maintain Seta as a low-volatility, high-yielding instrument. What that means is that if we have to reserve cash to do so, we will, but our goal is to stack Bitcoin. What do you think is the single best critique of these digital credit instruments? I think there are two different sides of the best critique. One is: why don't you just own Bitcoin? Why don't you just own amplified Bitcoin? As a Bitcoiner, I've been very open that I do not own digital credit. I own Bitcoin, and I own amplified Bitcoin exposure, because I'm so confident in the direction that this goes. That is what I view as appropriate for me and what I think is going to maximize the returns for me and my family over time, as I can ride out the volatility. I think this bear market is a bear market for ants; it doesn't even phase me. The reality is that while there are a lot of Bitcoiners that have that mindset, that's kind of like a sicko mindset. Most people are volatility-adverse. They have different needs in their life; they need cash flows, and they have a lower duration tolerance. What I saw as the problem with Bitcoin, just based on anecdotes of people I know, is that they tend to buy Bitcoin at the tops of the bull market. They don't actually have conviction, they ride off of other people's conviction and don't do the homework, and then they sell in the depths of a bear market because they weren't prepared for the volatility. And so I think digital credit provides a great product for them. But if you have the conviction and the ability, then going further out the risk spectrum is appropriate. I just think that the product-market fit for digital credit is insanely massive. On the other side, the risk-adverse side, people say, "Well, it's not debt, you don't have the credit protections." And then they'll list 50 different risks that are disclosed in the disclosures, which is fine. I think what's fair is that every individual should look at the risks of an investment, assign an expected value or probability to those, and then use those to make a risk determination. So if you think that Bitcoin's going to fail, or if you think that people and companies can't be trusted, then you will likely assign a higher expected value to those risks than maybe I would, or maybe a lot of other people would. And if you assign a higher value, then maybe you think the risk-reward of these instruments is not favorable. I think that there's really one true risk to these instruments, and the reason I say that is, as an issuer, there was one key risk that we were trying to eliminate: maturity risk. And so, if the issuer is trying to eliminate maturity risk, then that means that the investor of Seta takes that risk, and then they get compensated for that risk with the return. If that's the key risk, then you have to put an expected value on that, which we kind of went through a little bit already when we were talking about downside scenarios. You have to put a probability that you think that scenario plays out, and then evaluate what you think that means. But I think if you're willing to view Bitcoin as an asset that will likely succeed, and you need income, then I think these are a very attractive risk-reward security. Let's talk about Strategy and Michael Saylor selling Bitcoin. Obviously, they haven't done that in a number of years. They sold, now infamously, 32 Bitcoin. There are a lot of folks who are looking at recent price action of Bitcoin and saying Bitcoin went down a lot because Michael Saylor has given up. I think the Wall Street Journal called it a U-turn. It seems that there is a lot of speculation as to why this happened and what the implications are. What's your read? I think it was a necessary evolution for Strategy. When we launched Seta, in our investor calls and the IPO process, we explicitly said that if we need to sell Bitcoin, we will sell Bitcoin. It wasn't off the table, and the institutional investors really appreciated that from Strive. In call it 90% of instances, Strive has learned from Strategy. They've been the pioneers in the space. In a couple of instances, I think that we've done something that has proven to be valuable, and one of them was introducing a cash reserve when we IPO'd Seta—and now Stretch has a cash reserve also, which I think better reduces the risk profile. The second was being willing to sell Bitcoin. Saylor had been out there very publicly with a lot of messaging: "Never sell your Bitcoin, we will never sell." And so I think he just had to message that, "I'm willing to do this." That willingness to do this was always going to come with the critics saying, "Oh, this is the tip of the iceberg. First it's 32, next it's going to be 3,200." But your goal should be maximizing total returns. Bitcoin is your hurdle rate, but ultimately the goal is to maximize the total returns for your common equity shareholders. To not have selling Bitcoin on the table when it might be advantageous is not the optimal way to do that. Selling 32 Bitcoin clearly shocked the market. I think just the fact that a 32 Bitcoin sell would shock the market shows me that he had to do this to get to the point where they can operate maximizing total returns into the future. With Bitcoin down, there's potential that, just from a tax-loss harvesting perspective, more Bitcoin sales might make sense. But what's most important there is that I think he's going to be a net buyer of Bitcoin effectively every single month into the future. So, if he did a tax-loss harvest transaction and sold, whatever, 50,000 Bitcoin, and then the next day or even the next hour he buys back 50,000 Bitcoin, I think that shareholders should be thankful for that, because it actually helps them put themselves in a better long-term position. I don't think you, nor I, believe that Michael Saylor has given up on Bitcoin or has changed his views on Bitcoin, but do you think that there could be negative ramifications in terms of the story now being a little bit more nuanced? When you just say, "Hey, we will never sell Bitcoin," it is simple and anyone can understand that. The second that you start to talk about, "Hey, we are intelligent capital allocators. Sometimes we're buyers, sometimes we're sellers, it kind of depends on the market," the story changes a little bit. Obviously, that doesn't mean it's not a smart decision, but how do you look at story versus what is the actual right decision as a capital allocator, whether for you or for Saylor? I think the actions ultimately will speak the loudest. The story is kind of like how we talk about whether you have to have a dividend reserve: what have you actually done? I think the story becomes a little bit scary to some when you first see that first Bitcoin sell. But if over the course of the next year Strategy is a net Bitcoin buyer every single month, then I think that story and that concern quickly ages poorly. You're on X, I'm on X, and the amount of big accounts that are predicting right now that this is the start of continual Bitcoin sales from Michael Saylor is just part of the narrative and the story for now. But I think that story will quickly evolve over time. That evolution actually reduces the tail risk that I think people have always been concerned about—namely, that Strategy becomes a forced seller of a million Bitcoin. If you're willing to manage it on the fly, then that risk becomes substantially less. And I think ultimately the risk of Strategy failing reduces substantially, even though I think maybe the consensus is that it's increased right now, and ultimately that I think will be good for the price of Bitcoin. Today's episode is brought to you by Simple Mining. Bitcoin mining has a reputation for being complicated, risky, and hard to evaluate as a real investment. If you're considering mining in 2026, what actually matters isn't headline profitability. It's uptime, repairs, and whether the operation is run like a real business. That's why I've been using Simple Mining. They're based in Cedar Falls, Iowa, and they run a white-glove hosting operation where you own your miners. You choose your own pool, and you have Bitcoin sent directly to your wallet. They were featured on the Inc. 5000 list as the fastest-growing company in Iowa, with over 40,000 machines under management. What stands out to me is execution. They have the number one rated ASIC repair center, and for the first 12 months, repairs are included. If mining margins get tight, you can pause with no penalties. And if you want to resize or upgrade your fleet, there's a marketplace to resell equipment instead of being stuck. To help people think through whether mining actually makes sense right now, they've put together a short resource called the 2026 Bitcoin Mining Blueprint. It walks through the five mistakes investors make when allocating to mining, and they also explain how to avoid them before deploying capital. If it sounds interesting to you, you can get it for free at simplemining.io/pomp. Go check it out today and see if you should get into the mining game. Today's episode is brought to you by Bitget. Bitget is the world's first Universal Exchange, serving over 125 million users with access to over 2M+ crypto tokens, and TradFi markets such as 100+ tokenized stocks, ETFs, commodities, FX, and precious metals like gold and silver. They officially opened their TradFi trading suite on January 5th, 2026, and already hit the milestone of $4 Billion of daily volume just days later. Users can now trade 79 instruments across global macro assets, such as Forex, precious metals, indices, and commodities directly with USDT, all within their existing Bitget account. This experience is designed to feel familiar to crypto-native traders, while opening the door to macro-driven strategies without the need to switch platforms. Users can also enjoy high liquidity and minimal slippage, while trading these assets with up to 500x leverage. The Bitget ecosystem is committed to helping users trade smarter with its AI agent to execute trade orders as a co-pilot. Bitget is driving crypto adoption through strategic partnerships with LaLiga and MotoGP. Aligned with its global impact strategy, Bitget has joined hands with UNICEF to support blockchain education for 1.1 million people by 2027. Bitget currently leads in the tokenized TradFi market, providing the industry's lowest fees and highest liquidity across 150 regions worldwide. Check them out at bitget.com today. Now, when you look at the actual price action of Bitcoin, obviously we have Saylor selling 32 Bitcoin. There seems to be two narratives around Bitcoin in relationship to geopolitics and the conflict in Iran, etc. There's also a deluge of information on a daily basis in the headlines of Wall Street institutions, sovereign wealth funds, etc., all adopting, using, and becoming much more sympathetic to Bitcoin. It seems like all of the news is fairly positive, but the price keeps going down, and then you see the Nasdaq is going up. How do you rectify the news with the price action, especially in the context of all of these other assets that seem to be going up and to the right while Bitcoin is not? I remember back to previous bear markets that I've lived through, where I was looking at scary headlines and asking myself: has my fundamental view of Bitcoin changed? Both in the 2018 bear market and the 2022-2023 bear market, my foundational belief in the fundamentals of Bitcoin actually increased, even though the price action did not behave. That wasn't surprising to me, but it was just interesting. And I think right now in this bear market, I believe the same to be true. I think we're at the early stages of broad institutional adoption, one of those reasons still being the Bitcoin ETFs. When you look at the growth trajectory of ETFs in general, it is a 3 to 5-year maturity process, and we've already seen them be the most successful launch in financial products history. And it's only in the first couple of years. Track records are being developed, issuers like Morgan Stanley are getting their Bitcoin ETFs out there, and different allocators in the space are making them investable in their investment policy statements. These things structurally take time, and so I think we've only seen the beginnings of ETF and Bitcoin adoption. On the digital credit side, it has only existed for almost a year at this point. So if you think about that in its track record building process, from an institutional perspective, it still has a couple of years to go. And I would argue it's building a great track record. The number one question that you would get as an issuer of digital credit last year is: how will it behave in a bear market? And what we've seen is that Bitcoin's gone down north of 50% from its all-time high, and you have digital credit instruments still trading right around par. As we're recording this, they're just a couple of points below par. But importantly, the total return for both Seta and Stretch, since they've been issued, is positive still, because they're paying a high yield. That yield has been more than even the prices that they've dropped right now. And so, that correlation to Bitcoin is very low, because it had a positive return when Bitcoin went down 50%. And so, it's proving the thesis of having substantially less volatility than Bitcoin, which I think eases the biggest institutional fear. On the retail side, you have people that want it to stay at 100 every single day, and that's what we're trying to do. I think these things will evolve to have less volatility over time. But on the institutional side, anything with a double-digit yield is just attractive. And they're prepared for substantial, north of high-yield volatility. If you pull up a chart of the HYG high-yield ETF and look at how volatile that thing is—and that has a yield to worst of 6.5% or so—and you double that with digital credit while having less volatility, then I think it's just going to make a lot of sense for institutions to adopt. But we're in the early stages of that happening. If that happens in a couple of years, and ETFs get a 3-year track record, then you could see mass institutional adoption continue to scale in 2027, 2028, and 2029. Now, when you think about this playing out, obviously this digital credit has become attractive to somebody. Who is out there buying this? Is this a cash replacement in their portfolio? Is this a fixed income replacement? How are people thinking about their portfolio construction when putting this in? Where is it taking capital from, and how do you see that evolving over time? I think it's going to evolve over time in a major way. Where I think we are right now, the biggest people that I see allocating to it are allocating more from fixed income sleeves—that 40%—and putting it into digital credit. We've seen a lot of examples of real estate investors actually selling some real estate property and buying digital credit because they get more yield with less work. Real estate investors generally understand the problem with fiat currency debasement; they're hard asset investors, and so we've seen a lot of examples of that. As Seta and digital credit generally start to pay dividends more frequently, I think tokenization will be a further innovation on top of that. You're going to see a lot of tokenized securities in the future. I think there's no better security to tokenize than digital credit. As you tokenize digital credit, I think it will move ultimately to a place where dividend frequency can continue to increase. Right now Seta pays dividends on business days, but in the future, there's no reason that can't increase further, or that you won't see banks, banking products, and financialization products built on top of digital credit. A lot of brokerage accounts already today offer checking-type features, so this is not something that's new. But if you combine tokenization, instantaneous dividend payments, and the ability to have financial products like debit cards or credit cards tied directly to the digital credit instrument, I think in that future, it'll start to be used as an alternative to currency for more people. I think that'll make some people on the internet lose their minds about the risks of that, but I think that's ultimately where it goes and it makes sense. One of my core beliefs around tokenization, securitization, and the fiat currency debt crisis is that as consumers look to move out of the dollar, the financial ecosystem will allow securities in general to become alternatives to dollars. You don't necessarily need to hold dollars if you have tokenized things with a card attached to them that you could sell. I think digital credit is probably the best version of that, but we're still in the early stages of that adoption cycle. As I'm looking online at various news coverage of this, I think that people are still wrapping their heads around this idea of Bitcoin-backed credit. Are there other Bitcoin-backed types of instruments or assets that you all are looking at, or do you think that it is less about diversifying from an asset standpoint and more about focusing on just the Bitcoin-backed credit? I think the opportunity set for Bitcoin-backed credit—digital credit, or whatever you want to call all these different things built on top of Bitcoin—is close to infinite. It's part of the reason why Strategy has done this in a major way, why we do this, and why a lot of other players in the ecosystem do this. Strive in and of itself will not be able to offer all of these products. We have Seta, and there's a possibility that maybe one more product comes, but there's also a possibility that it doesn't because the demand for Seta is just so high. We can't be everything to everyone. And we just can't utilize our balance sheet to provide a million different products. That would create too complex of a risk structure within Strive. But other players that have Bitcoin on their balance sheet will make plays into this Bitcoin-backed credit ecosystem and will provide real products with real demand and a differentiated risk-return profile than Seta. I can think of a bunch of different examples of this. The international examples are so easy and so obvious, but I think there are also a lot of examples in American capital markets. The international version would be Seta but paid in the pound, the yen, or the euro. If this truly is the best bridge between the current fiat-based system and the Bitcoin future, then different people are going to want to have different types of exposures to that risk profile, and it just makes sense that there should be multiple products out there. So, I think Seta gets really big, but also, if digital credit were to speed up hyper-Bitcoinization, then you could start to see that really making banks mad. Think about Jamie Dimon losing his mind about stablecoins paying interest, and the fight between Jamie Dimon and Brian Armstrong. If stablecoins paid interest, you might see a run on the banks. And so that creates a systemic risk for the banks. I think that for the US capital markets, it would be best if banks are the Blockbuster of today—where that transition happens orderly and doesn't happen in an overnight, chaotic fashion. Nobody wants that. But the problem is not the banks; the problem is the money, the dollar. Ultimately, I think you can't constrain through regulation where this will go, and digital credit will play a role in that. So as an issuer, if you see that as a risk—if you see Jamie Dimon screaming at Strive or Michael Saylor in the future—then you want a few different things. One, you want to educate everybody in DC. But two, you also want a diversified ecosystem of issuers. You don't want there to be just one head to attack; you want there to be a lot of different products out there providing different risk-return profiles. That actually increases the chance of success, which is just kind of a unique thing to this ecosystem and to Bitcoin. It's why I firmly believe this is a friendly type of competition rather than a cutthroat one, because we grow the pie together. Even Strategy is getting a little bit of heat online right now because of their support for Strive while their stock is down. But what I think is true is that Michael Saylor and Phong Le are preparing themselves to run the largest corporation in the world, and they see this from a longer-term perspective. That is why they want to see growth and success across the ecosystem with Strive, as well as with several other players. Now, this is part of the uncomfortable conversation in the industry. I know you pretty well, and I know Michael pretty well. I think both of you are highly competitive, but you are also both very polite gentlemen, so there is this friendly competition. At the same time, to an outsider who doesn't understand some of the interpersonal dynamics and the belief that you guys are actually benefiting from having each other, they might say, "Hey, wait a second. They're going to a twice-a-month dividend, you go to daily. They come out with an 11.5% interest rate, and you go to 13%." That seems pretty competitive. What are the downsides to having multiple players? I do believe there is a benefit to more players because it grows the pie and normalizes this in the eyes of institutional investors, showing them it is a repeatable, scalable strategy. But there have to be downsides. Do you think Strive is taking away some of the capital flows from the Stretch product, or vice versa? Possibly in the short term, or vice versa. You're a big believer in capitalism, obviously, and so am I. Just a little bit. Just a little bit—I think you're a fan. And as we know, the great thing about capitalism is that competition pushes innovation. It pushes people and companies to be the best versions of themselves, which ultimately grows the pie to be substantially larger. If you were to have a system with only one issuer, it would likely lead to lackadaisical innovation. You don't need to innovate because there's no competition, so you could just sit and provide an inferior product as the only option. I think that is how it would play out. Think about some of the things Strive has innovated on, like providing a cash reserve. That might seem like a silly thing, but it improved the product, and Strategy followed once they saw how the market reacted to that. The same goes for the willingness to sell Bitcoin and pushing the frequency of dividend payments. Strategy had made some initial statements that Nasdaq rules only allowed you to pay a certain frequency of dividends. We actually thought that was true, too; it wasn't like we thought they were wrong. We actually were pushing every angle to see if that wasn't the case. And that is the great thing about capitalism—we found a way within the system to get Nasdaq and DTCC to say yes, which is now open source. Strategy in the future can copy that or innovate on this, and that innovation from different minds makes these products substantially better and grows the pie. In the meantime, we might do something cool and stack a bunch of Bitcoin, and they'll do something cool. Their scale is so massive compared to ours; even when we stack a lot of Bitcoin, for them it's effectively nothing, but for us, it is meaningful. Ultimately, I think this provides a better system. In the short term, that can cause flows to go more in one direction than another. But ultimately, as a Bitcoin balance sheet company, the most important thing is making Bitcoin win. If Bitcoin wins, every company with a substantial Bitcoin balance sheet wins. If it doesn't, then we all lose. Innovation that makes these products better and drives demand ultimately benefits the most important thing, which is our balance sheets. Now, when you think about Strategy, they obviously own a lot of Bitcoin—something like 840,000 or more Bitcoin. You all have also gotten into the double-digit thousands. Is there too much Bitcoin that could be held by public corporations? If it was 100% of the Bitcoin, sure. But when you think about money, whether you're talking about dollars or gold, the majority of those assets are typically held by institutions anyway. I think that's just the natural arc of these things. What I think is great about Bitcoin is that it always preserves that ability to opt out and keep your freedom money in self-custody. That's something we fundamentally believe everybody should do. But what's interesting about Bitcoin, because it's so scarce, is that the amount you need to hold in self-custody to have that insurance is not very much. One of the stats that I like to talk about is from 2017. My wife and I gave basically everyone in our family 0.05 Bitcoin on a Ledger with a note attached. And what that note said was, "With 0.05 Bitcoin, even if 100% of the Bitcoin was held in America, you will have more Bitcoin than the average American can ever hold." Yep. No. And so, is that too much? No, I don't think so; I think it is appropriate. I actually think the idea that corporations own Bitcoin is very valuable for protecting the ecosystem from a regulatory risk standpoint. Once public corporations own Bitcoin, they become part of the political structure of the US. They fund lobbyists and political campaigns. We are already seeing this happen with PACs funded by several crypto companies that are supporting friendly candidates, who then regulate in a friendly way. That is just the reality of how our political system works. If we want our political system to allow Bitcoin and allow us to have it in self-custody, then having large, highly profitable corporations with a material interest in Bitcoin succeeding is actually one of the best protective moats that self-custody Bitcoiners could have. So is there too much? It depends on your perspective. But from the perspective of protecting my freedom money, I think they can't own enough. I want them to own as much as possible because then they will spend as much money as possible in Washington protecting my right to hold my own Bitcoin. It is a fascinating nuance. Where can we send people to learn more about you or find you on X? Yeah, so on X, my handle is @MattCole_Strive. Strive's website, Strive.com, is where you can find Seta (ticker: S-E-T-A), which is the preferred equity, and ASST, which is the common equity. For Strategy's products, Strategy.com is where you can find Stretch and STRC. Both companies have different ideas—some similar, some different—and we're competitive, but ultimately we want each other to succeed. We want Bitcoin to win, and so we want institutions and investors to adopt digital credit because we think it is going to help Bitcoin win, and we also think it is the best financial asset for people who need cash flow. Awesome. Matt, thank you so much for joining me today. I really appreciate it, and we'll have to do this again in the future. Thanks, Pomp. Really enjoyed it. Thank you. In their eyes, I don't think it's anything to the level of concerning. Even if he doubled it, it would be very large, but I don't think it would be something that overtakes or takes down the network. It's always interesting to me because, as a corporation, Michael Saylor doesn't own 100% of the company. There are shareholders who have a claim on the assets and the cash flows, so it's a little weird because they are pooled together under the name Strategy or Strive. But at the same time, you control the company to some degree in the sense of making decisions as the steward for those shareholders. I guess the other part of this is: can there be too many digital credit instruments? Is there a point where you say, "Okay, two is better than one, three is better than two, but 25 of them would be a net negative because of fractured energy, capital, and mental focus?" There probably is a level there, but it's going to be such a high level that it's not even a concern of mine. Frankly, I would love to see that happen. Think about financial services: how many banks or insurance companies are there? If you were to ask the average American what the difference is between JPMorgan and Bank of America, I don't think they could give you an answer. They would just say they are two really large banks. So even just having two of the same thing is a good thing. Arguably, when you have too few, you get systemic risk tied to single institutions. We've seen what happens in the banking industry, and I don't think that is a good thing for the ecosystem. Having 20, 30, 50, or 100 different issuers would be a great thing. It starts to look more like a capitalist society where there aren't single points of failure. If JPMorgan ever fell, you know 100% that the government would bail them out, but in a true capitalist society, you wouldn't want that to be true. Part of the reason the banks got so big was because of post-GFC regulation that made it very hard to compete with them, leading to the emergence of these mega-banks. I think it would be healthier for Bitcoin, Strive, and Strategy for that not to be true. You think about like if Strategy held all the Bitcoin, in several different ways that would increase risk even to Strategy shareholders. It kind of creates a single point of failure for the US government to potentially go after. And so, a thriving ecosystem is better. The last thing I'll say on this is that just even looking at ETFs, if you wanted a digital credit ETF, you need probably at least 30 issuers of digital credit to have a diversified, compliant 1940 Act product. And so when you only have two, you're kind of in this land where it might be really good for Strive or for Strategy on an individual basis, but for the ecosystem, it's not great. And then you come back to, well, our ecosystem is built on a foundation of a Bitcoin balance sheet. And so a thriving Bitcoin ecosystem is going to be the best thing for our balance sheet over the long term. When you talk to your team internally, how do you describe what the opportunity is in front of you guys? It was this evolution that we talked about. So, the first version being the best form of financing for us. We thought it was a superior form of financing that reduced risk and allowed us to take on a higher level of amplification while controlling risk. And now it's really taking this kind of, I would say, stewardship position. If we believe, and we do believe, that digital credit will play this transitory role between today's fiat world and tomorrow's Bitcoin future, that's a really big role to play as a firm. And we need to be excellent stewards of that through Sata, through helping evolve the growth of digital credit, and through working even with Strategy in an official capacity going out there and telling the world, trying to be the most transparent companies that have ever existed. I mean, you can go to our website and the risk of Sata and the risk of ASST refreshes every 15 seconds. Outside of the Bitcoin ecosystem, I'm not sure of another ecosystem where you can refresh the risk of a security every 15 seconds. The institutional world lives in the private credit land where you get a mark every 3 months, and in the meantime, it's complete opaqueness. And so ultimately, it's being transparent, it's being out there, it's openly discussing the risk and the opportunity set, and then actively working to improve these products and actually making the things we say true, which I think that any great entrepreneur and investor can do through actions. It has just been true historically that the true innovators in the world, the people that we all look up to like an Elon Musk, they go out there and they say crazy things. They actually believe these crazy things. I think a lot of people think they're just BS-ing people and that they're grifters or whatever. But what I found in the innovator ecosystem—and I would put you in this category too, and I don't say that just because I'm on this program, because I actually believe it—you say crazy things, but I know you believe them. And then you go out there and you take risks and you try to make those things happen. That's what we're trying to do as well. And, you know, ultimately, in these things, there's risk. There's risk that the innovator doesn't succeed, but ultimately, I think that's what makes our capitalist society great: you put capital in the hands of people that have major visions, and then they go out there and they try to make it happen. That's what we're trying to do here. You know, it's always funny. Sometimes you say things and you know it's going to sound crazy, or it's going to be received as crazy. Other times you say things that seem like common sense and then everyone else thinks they're crazy, and so you sometimes get surprised by their reaction. Matt, thank you for taking the time to do this. I find it fascinating, the entire digital credit space. I think that both Michael Saylor, you, both of your companies, and your teams have really been pioneers here and continue to push this forward. And it feels like something that people may not quite understand yet, but is very rapidly becoming normalized and obviously entering into the portfolios of very smart capital allocators. Where can we send people to find out more about Strive or find you online to be able to follow along? The best place is just following us both on X. So, myself @collmacro, and Strive @Strive. And then our website at strive.com. You can refresh that risk every 15 seconds and see what Sata looks like. Amazing. All right. Well, thank you for doing this. We'll do it again in the future. Awesome. Thanks.

World Labs' Fei-Fei Li on Creating Large World Models

Bloomberg Live(185.0K)·18:22·153 views·2026-06-04
Fei-Fei LiCo-Founder & CEO, World Labs Emily ChangBloomberg
TL;DRWorld Labs Co-Founder and CEO Fei-Fei Li is betting on "spatial intelligence" over traditional Large Language Models (LLMs), developing simulator-class Large World Models (LWMs) to bridge the gap between digital text and physical-world AI action.
Beyond Words: Large...

TL;DR

World Labs Co-Founder and CEO Fei-Fei Li is betting on "spatial intelligence" over traditional Large Language Models (LLMs), developing simulator-class Large World Models (LWMs) to bridge the gap between digital text and physical-world AI action.

The Case for Spatial Intelligence and Large World Models

  • Beyond Words: Large Language Models (LLMs) cannot execute physical tasks like putting out fires or cooking. Human and animal intelligence evolved from perceiving, navigating, and interacting with the physical, 3D world.
  • The Functional Taxonomy of World Models: To clear up industry confusion, Li categorizes world models into three distinct types:
      • Renderers: Models that generate beautiful pixels on a screen (e.g., video generation). They prioritize visual appeal for human eyeballs over strict adherence to physics, dynamics, or geometry.
      • Planners: Action-oriented models used primarily in robotics that take world states as inputs and output the next logical physical step.
      • Simulators: The lynchpin of spatial intelligence and the core focus of World Labs. These models simulate 3D/4D information, semantic data, and physical laws, and can serve as the baseline for both renderers and planners.
  • Target Audience: Unlike ChatGPT's viral consumer-facing rollout, the "ChatGPT moment" for world models will likely target professional creators, industrial designers, developers, and researchers first before trickling down to consumer design tools.

Robotics and the Humanoid Reality Gap

  • Underfunding the Robotics Revolution: While humanoid robotics startups have secured $6 billion in funding, Li argues this is "too small" compared to the massive capital historical precedents like self-driving cars and LLMs required.
  • Closing the Hype Gap: Bridging the gap between humanoid promises and actual physical capability (like unloading dishwashers or retrieving packages) depends heavily on unlocking advanced simulation layers and spatial-physical intelligence.
  • Industrial and Virtual Applications: Beyond physical humanoid robots, spatial intelligence will power virtual world design, gaming, interior and industrial design, and digital twin inspections.

Cutting Through the "AI Hate Wave" and Safety Theater

  • The Rise of Public Anxiety: The current "AI hate wave" and public backlash are fueled by a polarized media landscape split between doomerism and utopianism. This void of thoughtful, moderate public discourse breeds anxiety and a perceived loss of human agency.
  • Real-World Safety vs. Safety Theater: Li advocates for scientifically grounded, responsible technology design over "safety theater" and heavy-handed regulation.
      • Healthcare Integration: Real-world, responsible AI integration is already happening quietly in hospitals, where doctors use AI for clinical charting and radiologists use it to assist in reading MRI and CT scans.
  • Educational Transformation: Because AI can now outperform average humans on standardized K-16 tests, the education system must pivot away from traditional rote-learning evaluations. Instead, educators must teach students how to use AI tools to augment their own human agency.

Rejecting "AGI" in Favor of the Scientific Quest

  • The Problem with AGI: Li avoids using the term "AGI" (Artificial General Intelligence). Instead, she focuses on the classic scientific quest defined by AI's founding fathers: creating machines that can think and act to improve human lives.
  • World Labs' Immediate Milestone: By the end of the year, World Labs plans to ship a spatial intelligence model designed to inspire entirely new, previously impossible product opportunities.

Notable Quotes

"Can words put out fires? Can words cook an omelet? ... Much of life, human life, human work life, human private life has a lot to do with perceiving, understanding, reasoning, interaction with the world including imaginary worlds of creativity." — Fei-Fei Li
"Robotics is going to be one of the most important revolution in human industrialization. Six billion dollars is too small." — Fei-Fei Li
"Right now, the oxygen, the air is all sucked into the polarized extreme of doomerism or total utopian. And when hype takes all the oxygen in the room, that void brews the kind of anxiety... because that's where real people live." — Fei-Fei Li
Everyone is focused on LLMs, ChatGPT, Claude, Large Language Models, but you have raised a billion dollars to build something different: Large World Models. Make the case for us. What is the bet you are making that others aren't? Right, so, this is my co-founded startup, World Labs, and we are all-in on spatial intelligence. The means to spatial intelligence is building a large world model. So, what is the case for us? The case for us is a 500-million-year story that animal intelligence starts with seeing and moving in a physical world. That evolution began with us as animals knowing what the world is, knowing, knowing who we are, knowing how to move around it, and interacting with it. Much of life—human life, human work life, human private life—has a lot to do with perceiving, understanding, reasoning, and interaction with the world, including the imaginary world of creativity, of, of productivity, as virtual worlds. So, unlocking that capability in machines, unlocking the capability of generating 3D, 4D worlds, unlocking the capability of reasoning within any world, unlocking the capability of teaching agents or robots, or or assisting humans to interact with the world, is what spatial intelligence is about, and that's what we are focusing on. So what can world models do ultimately that LLMs will never be able to? Can words put out fires? Can words cook an omelet? I think there's so much, right? So, we, for example, creativity—people design, whether we're designing interior space, we're designing machines, we're designing, we're designing homes, we're designing stories, so much of that is beyond words. We also use agents, whether we use agents in virtual worlds, whether it's for entertainment like gaming, or for more serious industrial, industrial applications, whether it's digital twin design, or or inspection, or or, no, or what kind of, no, many kinds of optimization tasks. Or we build robots to help us do a lot of things from putting down fires to helping healthcare scenarios to manufacturing. All these are downstream applications of unlocking spatial intelligence and building world models. So what's the, what do you think the ChatGPT moment for world models will be? Like how will we know this has arrived? Yeah, that's a great question, Emily, because chat is such a consumer behavior, that ChatGPT moment tends to be used to describe a viral, public consumer moment of getting so close to what AI can do.
