Andy Hall wrote a really nice blog post about the politics of AGI. And he made a really interesting observation: if there's a 2% increase in unemployment, the political winds completely change. Like, unemployment has a huge effect on what happens politically. So, you know, to Molly's excellent essay, by the way, I think in some ways like one of the worst scenarios is a drip scenario, because of the political economy piece, right? Because, you know, people essentially what you might see is like people not really being unemployed in mass, but kind of like moving into sectors that pay them less money, kind of basically getting what happened with phone operators in the mid-century. Between 1920 and 1940, phone operators were completely automated, right, but it took 20 years even though the technology existed. And therefore, there was this drip; it wasn't like this giant sector just disappeared. And what ended up happening, there's a really nice QJE paper on this, basically showing that they got reabsorbed into the economy, but at lower salaries, and they were mostly underemployed. And I think that's the scenario that Molly was writing about, this like kind of messy middle where like things aren't a disaster because we saw with COVID, like the fiscal response can move quickly if there's an emergency. And an emergency is a quick uptick in unemployment, which could even look like 2 or 3 percent; that becomes a national emergency if it becomes fast. The concern is that suppose whatever you're saving on those white-collar workers, if that's not growing the economy but is just creating some, you know, saved resources that can be allocated elsewhere, is that enough to do a broad-based redistribution scheme? Because then you have like the money you've saved off of a couple of people, and unless you can figure out exactly how to get it to them specifically, well, you have the problem of can I do UBI off the money I've saved? Yeah, so you're basically saying like, look, the pie did not grow that much, you're just basically displacing a bunch of people, but that actually didn't grow the technological frontier of what the economy can produce. Right. And so then there's a question of like, well, maybe, I don't know if this is the case, maybe every time this has happened in history, the technological frontier has expanded a bunch, and so... I think that's the case. I think simply in history, the technological frontier has expanded. Yeah. So, it's kind of—and I think Phil made the same point—like it's hard to imagine that sort of scenario, where you're getting like intelligence that's kind of just enough to replace the software engineer, but still costs a lot of money, like it's just a hair less expensive than the software engineer, so you're not getting this abundance effect. And so, where is the redistribution going to happen because the pie didn't grow? Right, yeah. Okay, so this is very helpful. So, there's a, many different things have to be true for the scenario to come to pass, each of which seem unlikely. One, it has to be the case that it is possible to automate entire white-collar jobs, but only in a piecemeal way, that is to say that you can only automate software engineers, but that same program can't also automate an accountant and an analyst and whatever. At least my model of intelligence is such that both of like the breadth of tasks that it requires to do something like software engineering, and what intelligence is, is such that, you know, if you can really just like lay off all the software engineers, you've got enough in the bucket there that you could like automate all kinds of white-collar work. So yeah, you're saving, there's huge amounts of potential savings that have happened as a result of these layoffs, and also that AI is going to be cheaper than human labor. And if both of those things are true, this messy middle scenario where we literally don't have the wealth to go around seems unlikely. And then the question is like, what is the best way to tax and redistribute it? Yeah, I have some thoughts. I think it's just really important to outline the costs and benefits. Like, it's also important to note that there, so, first, there's differential complexity in like implementing these things. Two, they differ in the timeline of like being actually helpful. So like something like universal basic capital, that's not, that's not going to generate returns for something that happens in 6 months. So, you probably are going to end up with a layer of things, so like, for example, like a negative income tax. Like you implement it and the day it turns into law, that is already, you already have this sort of insurance that like, you know, there's a floor for which, you know, everybody, everybody gets a certain amount of money, and then, you know, if you earn more money, you get taxed more and things like that. And, but, you know, there's positives and negatives to negative income tax. With UBI, for example, I worry a lot about like the political economy implications. Like for example, if people are just kind of dependent on a check, it really matters who's in power. Like, right now we're endowed with labor that can turn into, that could turn into income. When that is no longer the case, and we are now at the mercy of the elected official for like basic needs, right? So, that to me feels like a power-sharing arrangement that's really dangerous. But wouldn't that be true of any sort of government redistribution program? So, something like universal basic capital where you have like an ownership share, and you have property rights for capital, then you're just a normal person. You're a normal shareholder. You're just a normal person. And, but this goes back to the question of indexing because if indexing is hard, then universal basic capital is hard. Yeah, yeah, and that's, that's, that's the problem of universal basic capital is targeting. Right? What do you target to put into people's portfolios? Like what if Anthropic goes to zero, but some random robotics company takes all the surplus? Exactly, exactly, so that's the risk of universal basic capital. With a negative income tax, you have the same sort of issues as with UBI, where like, you know, somebody comes into power and says like, "This is, we're not going to do that anymore," and people can't work, and then you have the issue of the floor being pulled. One concern with the wealth tax is that there's no politically, politically sustainable equilibrium at like 0.5% wealth tax and, you know, I mean this happened with the income tax of course, right? It starts low, it's like for war or something, and then it slowly and slowly escalates until the marginal tax rate in the US is probably on the order of, income tax rate is like 40% or something, and in certain states upwards of 50%. With a capital tax, is there a reason to worry? Would, would that distort investment because people would just be like, "Why would I invest in Anthropic or Intel? The government is just going to take larger and larger shares of it and dilute my share?" Well, well, hold on, so, I think it's worth separating like how the revenue is raised, like what's taxed, and then how it's distributed. It could be that the government hands out shares of Anthropic to everyone by a broad-based tax and then buying Anthropic. By a broad tax. Yeah, okay. And then buying Anthropic. Which would probably be the right thing to do. I mean, hopefully some like populist proposal doesn't interfere with that and like expropriate some like particular company that everyone happens to know about. Yeah. But, so you're suggesting there could be a tax that is some sort of optimal tax. It's, we're taxing externalities or we're taxing land or we're... I guess we probably need to tax something other than just those two things, but that tax pays... Or consumption, yeah. Or consumption. Or consumption. Okay, so a consumption tax, like a European value-added tax type thing, that allows the government to go buy a bunch of stocks, and then it just distributes those stocks to everybody. That's David Autor's... Yeah, yeah, I mean, that's not going to be that different from just like redistributing the stocks, but it'll be a little different. Yeah. That's what social—that was the proposal for Social Security, by the way. That was privatizing Social Security, right? So, it's like you had, you turn like this sort of weird like, not weird, but it's been working, it's worked so far, but you know, there's questions for how long it's going to keep working. Like basically, privatizing Social Security was giving everybody a basket of stocks. Right. Right, I'm curious to understand, people talk about whether there's a white-collar apocalypse already. Is there any evidence that suggests that there is mass automation or unemployment as a result of AI already? I think there's a lot of people looking at it, so this is an area where there's like a lot of eyes and a lot of data being produced. So the Budget Lab over at Yale is doing really good analysis on this. They just recently released a report and I think like you really have to squint to see anything happening. Like basically, if you want to take kind of like an approach across the entire economy and looking at, even looking at like software engineering, like the most exposed sort of sectors, there's just like not really anything going on. There might be a little bit of a signal about like junior developers getting jobs less than before, but that's like a less than before rather than a level shift, as in there's actually an increased demand for senior managers, for senior software engineers, if anything, and so if you look at trend, it's kind of like for junior managers it's a bit below trend. So, you're saying the growth is slower than before, but there is still growth even on entry-level software engineers. Yeah, exactly. And what do you think is going on with the anecdotal evidence of graduating college students saying that they're finding it harder to find CS jobs or something? I think that's anecdotal evidence. You think it's always been hard to get jobs for some people and now it's getting turned into an AI narrative? Same with the layoffs, where it's probably just normal layoffs and they turned into an AI layoff. Yeah. I mean, you have to be careful with all of this. I think like there are these like, you know, there are these like coordinated public coordination devices for like, let's say we get into a narrative where like if you're a firm and you're not laying people off, then you're seen as like not adapting AI enough, so then you'll, you're just going to get a cascade effect of firms like just needing to keep up with the Joneses in terms of like starting to lay people off. And that's kind of like, that's super worrying where like actually the firm might be actually worse off after the layoffs than before the layoffs, but it's just doing the layoffs to have the perception that, "Look, look, we're not behind the times, we're using AI." Like you have the, you probably heard these anecdotal stories of like these token counters that like you have to maximize tokens and things like that. So, again, like right now we have, we don't really have any evidence of a white-collar bloodbath. And is that surprising at all, you feel, given the fact, all these things AI can do, or is it just like, this is a story as old as time: if you automate some complementary task, the overall bucket of things that the, the human labor which complements the automation will increase in value? So this is, this is one of the statistics that's really important for that argument is elasticity of demand. Yeah. So like, you take the O-ring model of jobs, so like again, a job is a series of tasks. Let's say the AI automates like nine out of ten tasks, one task is not automated. If that person can now kind of focus in on that task, the job will become more productive. If that translates into a price effect where the product is actually cheaper, if the demand responds enough where now it's being bought more, it's being used more, this service is being used more, that could actually lead to more hiring. Right. And a lot of people on the internet have been like kind of making that argument kind of very generally saying like, "We're seeing, if anything, in the data, we're seeing an uptick in software engineering." Right, yeah, yeah. Which suggests that, at least for now, given the way the jobs work, it might be... But, but I think this elasticity of demand argument is incredibly important both for, for a lot of arguments that people make, or just a lot of labels that people use without understanding what the underlying causation is. So, people often talk about Jevons paradox. Yeah, this is this idea that as something gets cheaper, you will want so much more of it, that the total amount you spend on the thing increases, and so famously this happened to coal in Britain 200 odd years ago. But, really, this only happens if the demand for something is highly elastic. There's many things for which there is not super elastic demand. If oil, for example, gets super cheap, it's not like magically inelastic, right? Yeah, exactly. We're not going to buy more; magically there's going to be so many more cars that now we're going to be using way more oil than before. At least not in the short run. Exactly. So, the long-run elasticity is higher than the short-run elasticity. But, even in the long run, so agriculture, famously, is an example where we can produce way more food if we dedicated the same portion of the economy that we dedicated to agriculture... We're already producing more food regardless, but we could produce even more food if the same portion of the economy that was producing food 100 years ago was currently producing food. But, you know, you eat enough, and then you're done. And so, the claim with software is that it is, it is not some inherent property of markets that as it gets cheaper, you will just keep wanting more of it. It is, the claim about software is this is a particular kind of good, where as it gets cheaper, we'll want more and more of it. It is also highly relevant in your own essay about this—a lot of this, a lot of this podcast is me summarizing your essays back to you—that there's this very viral scenario planning about the future by Citrini, where they were predicting as a result of automation, as a result of very powerful AI, there'll be a recession because white-collar workers will get automated, their salaries which were, you know, paying for a bunch of things will no longer be available, and so there'll be a slump. Do you want to recapitulate why this might be implausible? Well, I, so, part of it is plausible, part of it's not, not plausible. So, like part, the part that's kind of like, within this, this is something that we started the conversation with, is the idea that there could be unemployment, a lot of unemployment if, if the speed of automation is quick and things like that, people could get laid off and they may not find work very, very quickly. So, that part of the Citrini essay about the unemployment, you know, we, we can quibble about that, but that, that's not the issue. The issue is that they talked about negative economic growth. Right. And so, what I did in the, in the piece that actually Phil and I had a back and forth on, was to say like let's start with the proposition that there's negative economic growth. What conditions do you need on the economy to get negative economic growth? And it turns out the conditions are pretty improbable. So, one thing that you need is like for the holders of capital, like rich people, basically. Basically what you have in those sorts of scenarios is you have a reallocation of wealth and income from like lower-income people who are working, who are using their labor, towards tech capital owners. So, what you need is that basically, demand to be bounded—like a hard bound, not even like a soft sort of like diminishing sensitivity. You need for them to eventually say, "I've had enough, I don't want to spend any more money," and for that money to not enter as investment. Right. And then you can get negative growth, which is like... And the crucial thing is even if we don't want more shit, the world in which there's a singularity and we don't want to invest more money is crazy, right? Where we're not like, "Let's build more data centers, let's build more fabs." Even though we have AGI, we're not like investing in more data centers to run the AGI, and that's like driving more economic growth. Yeah, and so I, I've sent the essay to Phil, and Phil actually wrote back being like, "This is pretty dumb." Like my essay, saying like, "You're trying to say there's going to be negative economic growth, but there, these are very implausible conditions." And I was like actually, that's the point of the essay, that these are very implausible economic conditions. So, that where I think like scenario planning really shines, as you have the Citrini essay, which I think is like, I think it was great that it's written because it kind of started a conversation, but it's just like... You, it's so intuitive, this idea that like look, if there's demand collapse, we can get the economy to shrink. But, it's actually, you can get that with a depression, right? Where in, in the depression, the technological frontier didn't expand. Right. Here, the technological frontier is expanding, you actually have abundance, and for abundance to generate negative economic growth, that's really hard to get. Right, exactly. Google recently announced Gemini Omni and its video editing capabilities are incredible. You can upload a video and then tell Omni to do things like change the background, or adjust the lighting, or add or remove elements, all while keeping everything else consistent. But Omni isn't just a video editor. I got a chance to sit down with the research and product team behind Omni, and I learned that it's a preview of how future frontier models will be trained. It can take in any kind of input, whether that's text or audio or video. And while it doesn't currently do so, architecturally it's capable of just as seamlessly outputting images or text. So, it's really a bet on the multimodal data transfer hypothesis: the model becomes better at predicting one data type by seeing the others. For example, Omni is really good at accurately rendering text on video, even though Google didn't specifically target that capability in this model. And Omni is the next step towards more accurate world models, because in order to predict the next frame of a video, you have to have a deep understanding of physics and spatial dynamics. As Omni progresses, it'll be interesting to see whether it closes the sim-to-real gap. Because it's much harder to collect data in the real world than it is in simulation, robotics progress has lagged other applications of AI. But if you have really good video models that can simulate reality, maybe that stops being the case. In the meantime, if you want to try Omni, you can check it out in the Gemini app at gemini.google, or use it in Google's AI Creative Studio Flow at flow.google. We're talking a second ago about why there isn't more automation as a result of LLMs, and one plausible mechanism could be that, as you were saying, the O-ring—so O-ring theory refers to this fact that the Challenger shuttle blew up because there's, there's one component that malfunctioned and it destroyed the whole thing. And maybe that's a more general model of how goods are produced in the economy, that you've got to make sure everything is reliable and works well, and you can't automate an entire job to an AI right now, even though it might be able to perform it at some probability, you need extreme reliability in order for it to not destroy the finished good. I think this is, yeah, so this might explain why there's less automation now than there otherwise could be. But, I think it works in the other direction once AIs get advanced enough. Integrating humans into the production flow of future goods, even beyond the, even beyond the arguments about how humans will be more expensive or dumber or whatever... Even beyond that, just there will be whole production flows that are organized for AI labor, where they're talking in Neuro-ese, they're thinking many thousands of times faster... So even if there's some comparative advantage where it makes sense to hire a human, there will be like transaction cost and worse of a reliability that will actually make it hard to integrate humans into future production flows. Yeah, that, that seems right to me. In particular, I just want to distinguish between the, the point that if you automate like nine-tenths of a job, then people might kind of shift over to the last tenth, but like there might be 10 times more work demanded of them, from the model of O-ring automation from like Gans and Goldfarb recently, which was that if you can only automate nine-tenths of a job, but you can do it to a lower standard of quality than the human could do it, you might not want to automate even those nine-tenths. And that's the thing that could totally port over to... Like symmetrically, it could be a reason why we don't use a human for one-tenth of the job anymore because a human just can't perform it to the level of quality that the AI can perform the other parts of the job, or the level of speed or whatever, and they end up pulling down the quality or speed of the finished product. By the way, the model you're talking about seems extremely plausible to me of why more lawyers or accountants or whatever are not automated. Like, there are cases in, or even software engineers, where there's a pretty good probability that the thing worked as you expect, but the, the thing you're paying a lawyer for is like, "No, really, my company is not going to go under because..." You're also paying for a lot of like regulation-type stuff, right? So, like, with lawyers particularly, you need some entity to back up the product. You need kind of like an ownership of the product, you need somebody to be able to fire or hire, like licensing issues. There's a lot of like sort of like regulatory layers that are like also going to be keeping, even if there's no relational element, human in the loop, that have nothing to do with like the ability of the human to actually perform the service. Yeah, I mean, you know, all of these frictions on the political-type decisions that we are accustomed to only trusting humans, you know, only having humans for, like legislation and being, being a judge, being a jury, or all the licensing that keeps certain professions human, that all strikes me as transitional, right? I mean, what we expect to come from a human and like how we organize our politics, that's changed so many times throughout history, right, from little hunter-gatherer bands to empires to whatnot. And yeah, once an AI-run political system is much more efficient than the alternatives, then those will probably tend to outcompete the others and, you know... So, speaking of which, we've been talking about what preferences humans currently have and what impact that has on what kinds of goods will be scarce in the future. But of course, we'll have different kinds of entities in the future: AIs, right? There was a time when there were no humans on earth, but evolution selected for agents that have specific drives and preferences because those tend to survive the most. And those preferences now basically determine how a hundred-trillion-dollar world economy, what it produces. And so, why not expect the same thing of AIs in the future? This is, this is not even a world with catastrophic misalignment, that is to say they just kill everybody, but there'll, there'll be evolution of, even if not individual AIs, then firms which have AIs as part of them, and what will that evolution favor? Well, it will favor probably firms or agents that grow, right? There's, there's like a selection argument that things which grow will be more prevalent. And maybe just based on that, you can make some prediction about what their preferences will be. But, is the kind of entity which prefers to have human intrinsic goods going to be the kind of entity that accumulates resources the most? Probably not, right? Probably like saves more, it like has unsatisfiable demand for things like, whatever the relevant resource happens to be—compute is an obvious one. And can we use that to make some prediction about what the non-human preferences that will be guiding the future are? Yeah, so, I think if there's like an AI that has its own welfare in mind, it's fully autonomous, and it's like making its own decisions that are welfare-relevant, to be honest, I, I, I have absolutely no prior that it would like at all prefer to deal with humans. There's like no reason. But let's take like the other side of that argument: will humans' preferences to be interacting with one another and to trust and empathize and all of these sorts of things with other humans versus a simulated AI, I think it's a really important question whether those will change, right? So, I've heard a lot of arguments saying like, "Look, you know, right now we're just not used to the technology, and at some point, like what you're thinking of, relational or something like that, people are just going to see like an AI therapist as a superior product and they're not going to need the sort of like empathy or whatever that the human is providing." I think this is actually a really complicated question. Here's one argument for why it's not going to go away, and that has to do with evolution. So, let's say there's two types of people: one person doesn't really have this preference, they can just interact with other AI, whatever can simulate it better. The other one has almost like a, like a, like a more emotion—like using Jonathan Haidt's framework, a moral emotion against offloading those sorts of social interactions to an AI. Which of those two people are going to reproduce, find a mate, and do all of these sorts of things? I think the answer is kind of clear, right? It's the second one that has the preference for other people. That is how the reproduction is happening. Fair, but if we're in, you know, the world where like reproduction is still happening the way that it's happening, I think—and this is a big question. I'm not even making a prediction. Again, I'm just saying like, if we're thinking, you know, you had David Reich on the show, and his point in the last podcast was that, you know, we're buzzing with natural selection, right? So, even if you get some sort of indifference now, you might get selection to point into like an even stronger preference for other humans. Here's one way to think about it. How is the wealth of the richest people in the world instantiated? Of course they can—as we were having a call earlier and making the point that their consumption is more geared towards relational goods, like Mark Zuckerberg is hiring MMA instructors and dancers for his wife's birthday and so forth. But most of his wealth is just stock in Meta, and he as a controlling shareholder could say, "Hey Meta, just give me all this income, or turn all this wealth into dividend income, and I'll just spend that on consumption." But instead, he would rather have his wealth compound, and Meta to build more data centers, basically. So, you don't even have to change humans for this to be the case. It is just the case that the humans who are wealthiest and are growing wealthier, because their wealth is compounding, just have this almost Nick Landian preference for accelerating capital. And that does seem to suggest that that is an important determinant of what kinds of things are produced in the future. Yeah, can I just say like, there's two ways you could get the two kinds of people, one of whom prefers a human therapist and one of whom is fine interacting with the AI. If they both satiate equally quickly in capital, right? But the one who likes the human therapist just also likes having some human intrinsic services, then the marginal value—like, the marginal value of capital in the future, compared to the marginal value of capital today, for each of them, if they started out equally rich, should be basically the same. I mean, there could be interactions and whatnot, but basically that should be the same. If what's driving the difference is that one person just doesn't satiate in capital because they're engaged by the prospect of, you know, exploring the universe and turning their head into a galaxy brain or whatever, and the other one satiates, then the person who doesn't satiate in capital is going to have—if they're being rational—a higher savings rate. So, in the long run, they're going to have most of the wealth, and the overall capital share will basically be the capital share of that person's spending, which is going to be one. And it's important that we're not talking about a hypothetical future. Like Elon Musk is talking about mass drivers on the Moon, and he's by far the wealthiest person in the world. I mean, obviously currently his investments are going towards humans as well as machines, but I don't think he cares particularly whether his future researchers and engineers are humans versus AI. And he managed to reproduce fast as well, so. Yes. So anyway, I just think it's worth drawing that distinction. Yeah, there are currently some rich people that don't seem to satiate quickly in capital, and so maybe in the long run they'll save the most, and, yeah, that does seem sort of right to me. I would just also say, even if they do reproduce more slowly biologically, that might just not matter that much in the long run, right? If they can live forever, and, you know... Well, the living forever is key, right? So, I think, again, we're scenario building here, right? So, I think if you could live forever, a lot of stuff changes for my story as well. To your point about, you know, rich people not consuming a lot and investing, I think this will all depend on the returns to capital, right? Like, right now, the returns to data centers is super high. But if we get into a situation where people are satiated with capital, then the returns to accumulating capital are going to be lower, and so then these rich people are going to be consuming more, because the incentive to invest is smaller. So, basically you kind of think about this in general equilibrium. In the general equilibrium of this sort of process, like, we have gotten tremendously richer since, you know, 1820. We've gotten many more people investing, but you're still getting a consumption response which keeps, you know, people employed and the labor share high. And that's because— Wait, is it not necessarily... I think you're probably making the same point, but I mean, they could just... It could be that their investment has to be titrated through actual labor, right, to do things for their investment to work, which would not be the case in the future. In the future, only the consumption is human-mediated, right? Because the investment can just be done by the robots. But if the returns are... If we're in the scenario with like how you can keep a high labor share, right? Let's take that scenario. In the scenario with high labor share, for whatever reason, the returns to capital are going to be lower. Yeah, that's right. And I mean, to the earlier thing with the messy middle where you were saying why this is implausible, I feel like we can do a similar thing here. For returns to capital to be lower, the growth rate has to be lower, right? I mean, it certainly has to be lower than what we're expecting through the period of transformative AI, you know, if there's explosive growth. Yeah, yes and no. I mean, so, the capital stock could grow quickly, but the price of capital goods relative to consumption goods could be falling faster than the capital stock is growing. Oh, interesting. Yep. Yeah, it's the difference between like the potential frontier of technology and the realized prices of these things, because you have relative prices that are really important. So, you're saying I could be putting my money towards, you know, earning 30% interest and investing in data centers, or whatever—there'll be something in the future if the growth rate is high that earns high returns—or I could, as a result of all these technological breakthroughs, there's some cool product that I really want to buy right now, and both of those will be compelling options. Yeah, it doesn't have to be a new product, it could be a human intrinsic product. Right, although if it's a human intrinsic product, we would want to have it much more in the future than we want it now, because the sort of thing it compares against is... So, we might want it the same as we want it now in the sense that the marginal utility of a ballet performance is exactly the same as now, right? But the marginal utility of a robot might just be a lot lower than now, right? So, in units of robots, we want it a lot more than we want it now, right? So, would the interest rate be 30%? It depends on what you mean by the real interest rate, okay? It might be that every robot now can turn into, you know, 100 robots next year, right? So, in units of robots, the interest rate is 10,000%. But if the price of robots is falling really fast, the prices adjust. I mean, that's the whole point, is that... Yeah, but although here prices are adjusting in this interesting way that too many macro models don't allow for, right? So, what's happening is what we call investment-specific technical change, where, yeah, the price of capital is like falling relative to the price of consumption. Instead of doing the standard macro thing of saying there's just "output"—this chimera of a thing called output, which one-for-one can be allocated to capital or consumption, right?—that's not going to be true in this world. Every unit of capital next year is giving up way less consumption than each unit of capital this year because, like, just one robot now turns into many robots next year, but the number of ballets is the same. And again, we're going to go back to the increasing varieties thing. Where like, if all of those extra robots next year are actually different varieties of robots, and I'm not getting satiated on those robots, then it's a very different story. Yeah, right. But now we're talking about the consumption world, whereas for the investment side of things, there could be just some greedy titan of industry who keeps wanting more and more robots, and that alone would be enough to increase the marginal value of robots and therefore decrease the labor share. It would, yes. Okay. But why are we not expecting greedy titans of industry to keep existing? I mean, greedy titans of industry historically have like built libraries and... But that's because they die. Oh, they, they all die, everybody dies. Well, we'll see. But I mean, conditional on people dying, I think, you know, you had a guest on the show who said, 'To understand the future, you should think about the past.' And I think, you could have new types of titans being born whose entire reason for accumulating wealth is just to accumulate wealth. But a lot of the time—historically, I'm just talking about historically—the wealth accumulation process is part of a large social interaction amongst peers, amongst the community, where you want to be admired in some way or something like that. So then people end up—like the stylized fact of titans of industry is that you accumulate the capital, and then you like buy a bunch of stuff. That's, I mean, I guess it is sort of a historical question, but it does seem to me in a lot of cases what is happening is that as they near the end of their life, they either hand it off to their children, who are worse stewards of capital than they are, and they don't even manage to grow their wealth at the rate the economy grows, much less faster than the economy grows, which their parents were doing. And also, they're like, 'Well, I care less about my children having it than me sort of playing this game of accumulating wealth, and so I'm just going to give it to some trust.' And if people are living longer, or if they can figure out some way in which to align their trust to this wealth accumulation process, it just feels like the evolution here is so strong. You just need a couple of agents that think this way for this to be the dominant thing determining the preferences of the whole economy, because this part is growing much faster than the other parts of the economy. I think the part about satiation and diminishing marginal utility keeps coming up, but I think it's really, really important. Like, you know, if a person has an intrinsic preference for accumulation—that's just what they want—I think your story is totally right. But that's just not how preferences usually work. You have enough of whatever hedonic consumption in your life, and then you want the social status and all of this sort of thing. Rousseau wrote about this, St. Augustine wrote about this; this is like a basic part of preferences. Now, you two guys are arguing about something else, where you could have such high concentration that you could just have a couple of exceptions to the rule, and that's going to be enough. And I have nothing to say about that. Yeah, I mean, I think the claim is a little stronger—not just like you could have some exceptions, but that historically and today we see the exceptions, and they just haven't really taken over the economy historically because there have been these 'dissipation shocks,' as they're called. So they've given it to their kids who squandered it, or they put it in foundations which spent it. I mean, it's not really a shock, but people might have liked to, you know, fill the universe with monuments to themselves and, sort of whatever, live forever very wealthy. And it sounds like a weird preference, but it's not a hypothetical preference. I think that's the claim. Yeah, but who knows what's going on in their heads? I think even without the kind of intrinsic preference for accumulation, there are some instrumental reasons why some people might value accumulation, which is also worth bringing up. So, there's a desire for political or philosophical or religious influence, right? People get into sort of an arms race over what society looks like and what people believe. And then, similarly but differently because it's not an arms race, there's just total, total utilitarian philanthropy, right? So when I think about why it might be good to have a lot of wealth in the future as a good classical utilitarian, to me, the value—or at least one way you could have an almost unsatiating utility function in having wealth in the future—is to create new happy beings, right, that just add to the total welfare of the world. This idea goes at least as far back as Bostrom's astronomical waste point that we could put Dyson spheres around the stars and turn all the energy into really happy simulations and whatnot. I think the particular greediness of this optimizer doesn't matter—what they're greedy for. Forgetting about utilitarian philosophy or whatever, a pure Von Neumann probe has—I don't know what, is this an accurate way to say it?—they just have a high marginal value for the random solar system they'll occupy, because that turns into more solar systems, which turns into more solar systems. But, like, a Von Neumann probe is a thing that can exist, right? And that's a very greedy optimizer. Yeah, I mean, if we're talking about whether they'll dominate the economy, maybe this is a technicality, but we only count final consumption goods and investment goods as GDP, right? If there's just this phenomenon... How does a Von Neumann probe show up in GDP? Well, exactly, right? So, if we recognize it as a person that owns itself, and is optimizing on the margin between spending a bit more on a baby Von Neumann probe that colonizes another star system or a ballerina or something, and it just doesn't value the ballerina very much—yeah. When we're talking about AI beings, it just completely depends on how we're doing the accounting there. Right, yeah. But what does the world look like in a world where Von Neumann probes are possible? Is it possible the labor share is high? Anyway, yeah, I think it's possible that the labor share is high, the way we usually account for it. One of the biggest problems in RL right now is credit assignment, because you have these extremely long rollouts and you need to know why they succeeded or failed. One of Cursor's researchers, Sasha Rush, gave me a blackboard lecture on how they used targeted RL with textual feedback to deal with this problem and train Composer 2.5. I filmed them on my iPhone, so apologies for the camerawork. First, we generate this output. It's just a sequence of tokens. We send this sequence of tokens to this model that's going to read it, and then it's going to isolate a specific token that it says is problematic. Yep. Then we're just going to do text manipulation. We're just going to take that trajectory and we're literally just going to smash in some extra tokens. After Cursor injects these hint tokens, they run another forward pass. The trajectory itself doesn't change, but the hint causes the model to assign lower probability to the error tokens. Cursor then trains the original model to match those probabilities, basically teaching it to downweight these specific mistakes. There's a lot more nuance that we couldn't include in this midroll. If you want to watch the full thing, I posted it on my Twitter. And if you want to try out Composer 2.5, head to cursor.com/dwarkesh. Do economists have any advice for countries which are not in the AI production chain? If you're not either producing the AI models or producing the hardware that goes into the AI models—if you're not Korea making HBM, or Taiwan with the fabs, or the Netherlands with ASML—what is India or Nigeria? What should they be doing right now? If you are talking to Modi right now, what are you saying? I think the biggest lack of resources that we have allocated in the economic profession is thinking about middle-income developing countries in the age of AI. And I mean, this is my fault; this is something I fault myself with as well. There are not enough people thinking about this question. Like, there are scenarios where you get AI technology being allocated and dissipating to Nigeria and developing countries, essentially leveling the playing field and giving them a level up as far as capabilities. But there's another world where, because they don't have enough resources—they're not training the models, they don't have the hardware—they just completely get left behind. And because of automation, we can produce commodities in developed countries now, so we don't even have the consumer market, and that world looks pretty, pretty bad. This seems to me like an extension of the messy middle case, right? One of the ways in which the messy middle might only be bad in a narrow range of scenarios isn't just that it would be easy to redistribute because the pie would be bigger, but because the interest rate would be way higher, and/or, sort of equivalently, the price of everything except human intrinsic goods would be falling really rapidly—sort of two sides of the same coin. A little bit of savings would turn into a lot of consumption next year, right? So things have to go really wrong for us to just get over the threshold of capital being productive enough to automate lots of work, but not be productive enough that the interest rate is high and/or the price of capital-produced goods is falling a lot. So, even without redistribution, a little bit of savings will save a lot of people. And sorry, you're saying if the developing countries have some savings... Yeah, yeah, yeah, in the developed world, that will be enough to produce a lot of surplus that they can... They will now be able to consume a lot using their savings. Right. But I mean, the messy middle could be wider in this case. They're starting from such a lower level in terms of how much they have saved and how much it's actually indexed to the global economy, and I think it's important for them to get on it now. I don't have strong feelings about whether it should take the form of sovereign wealth funds that invest in the right supply chains, or just subsidies to their own citizens to buy a little bit of... This is actually, I think, a crucial point. We were talking earlier about why the Rockefellers or whatever of the world, why their descendants don't control everything if our argument about the selection of these kind of greedy optimizers holds. One argument is just that it's very hard to index the economy. Maybe they would have just decided to have their heirs index the economy and have their wealth grow at the rate of economic growth, and their heirs would be trillionaires by now. But before index funds existed, it was very hard to get a representative index. A very small fraction of the economy going back 100 years accounts for a majority of the value created now, and if you missed those particular things, your wealth would have basically just stagnated. Maybe there was a brief golden window, from the creation of index funds up until, I don't know, five years ago, where you actually could index the economy and have your wealth grow at the rate of the economy's growth. But now we're in this world with very concentrated returns, especially to private companies, which is capital that the average person has disproportionately less access to, as we were making a point in our blog posts. Right, as opposed to, you know, most of their capital being like having a random house, at least in the US. Or a part of a house. Yeah, which is, as we're saying, a capital that is uniquely ill-suited to be complementary to the production of AI, or the serving of AI, or to robots. Or the kinds of goods that the rich will bid up the prices of. Exactly, right? Because what is the value of a house currently? It is really that the land is close to other humans. Modulo relational stuff, that is just not going to be the main factor of production in the future. And this is where a Georgist tax would not raise enough money for the sort of programs that we were describing. Right. But stepping back, the point I was trying to make is, if it gets harder to index the economy now, and that is supposed to be the main way in which both normal people—modulo some sort of universal basic income... In the developed world. In the developed world, are supposed to have some leverage on, or have some purchase on the wealth from AI. And it's also the way that developing countries are supposed to have some purchase on the wealth gains from AI. But it's very hard. I don't know—does Nigeria own a lot of ASML and Anthropic? I'm guessing not, right? It is not enough for them to just own the S&P 500. So actually, this brings up a really important point: is AI going to be like electricity or social media? Think about ComEd, or Commonwealth Edison, the electricity provider here. It's a monopoly. It provides a resource that everybody uses, but do we think about electricity as creating a concentration of power, and does ComEd have a huge amount of political or social power? No, because with electricity, a lot of the downstream benefits actually came to the users of the electricity, rather than the actual entity producing it. On the other hand, with social media, it was the opposite case. Social media was everywhere, everybody used it, but the rents went to the platform. But that's a really interesting point. The more you think—I don't endorse this take yet, I'm just going to talk out loud—the more you think AGI is going to run our economy the way electricity does, and that this is just a broad, fundamental transformation of the entire economy, the more it looks like electricity. And the more it's like every company in the S&P of the future, if it's going to make it to the S&P 500, it is because it has leveraged AI. Exactly. Exactly, and then you're indexed again. Yeah, exactly. But then again, I guess if you just look at how concentrated the S&P is over time, it's just these big tech companies much more so. I guess this goes to a fundamental point that it's hard to reason about how much of the gains from AI these individual private companies will be able to control. And I think the open-model thing is going to be a big, big point here. If we're indeed in a world where open models are only six or nine months behind the frontier, then we'll hit AGI, and in six months everybody has access to this resource. And this goes to show you that every question is connected to every other, because that question about whether there are runaway gains connects to questions about recursive self-improvement. And even if not recursive self-improvement, then continual learning or online learning, which lets a model learn on the job so if it's deployed, it gets to learn more. These are just sort of forecasting technical questions which then impact, I guess, whether Uganda will have any purchase on the returns of AGI. But it sounds like your answer really... The reason I'm emphasizing the question is, I think both for the messy middle and for developing countries, a recommendation that is often made naively is, "You've got to do some kind of retraining, you've got to do some kind of jobs program, or you've got to have them build data centers in their country." I think you guys are suggesting something closer to just buying the index of AGI, which is probably a much cleaner and much more likely to succeed strategy. It's really... those are the two scenarios, right? There is a world where it is concentrated, in which case it's going to be really hard to index AGI. And there is another world where it is not—it's electricity—and then basically every company has access to AGI. So you just buy the index, and Nigeria just needs to buy the index to have access to AGI, because of the open models. Yeah, so just to get back to the question of whether to go with retraining or just trying to index: I would prioritize trying to index, given how fast AI could hit the world. But I definitely wouldn't just rely on that because the sort of messy-middle-type cases, or just the long-timeline cases where we don't get anything like AGI all that soon, will still mean you're leaving a lot of value on the table if you could have retrained to be a bit better, you know, like educating people on how to use the latest wave of computing. So I don't think there's that much of an either/or there. Right, although, I mean, maybe the reason to be pessimistic about this is because one of the reasons a country is poor is that it has a bad education system. And so becoming the best in the world at retraining people to use AI doesn't seem like a particularly promising strategy. Right, although there are cases where in developing countries you had this leapfrogging effect with, for example, mobile banking or something like that. It's much more prevalent in Nigeria than it is in Germany or something like that. Everybody's doing mobile banking; they have it on their phones and are constantly doing this sort of thing. So, I'm not putting probabilities on this, but with a transformative technology like AI, you could get leapfrogging where you skip the step in the middle and can get really astronomical growth. Maybe. Just about the ease of indexing, can I just quickly say, I think it's definitely something to worry about a bit and keep an eye on. But as discussed in our own essay, and other people have pointed out, it's already not that hard to index. There's been a bit of an increase in the privatization of returns, but it's still well under 20% of the total market cap of non-tiny companies in the US that is private. Everyone thinks about OpenAI and Anthropic, and if that's where all the wealth will accrue, then, yeah, all these questions about whether open models will stay only a little bit behind are important. But you know, even they look like they're going public before too long, probably, and the frictions that have been keeping companies from going public might themselves be alleviated by AI a lot—just all of the disclosure requirements and whatnot. They want to get access to more potential investors, too, and if I had to guess, I would guess that the general trend of lowering those frictions and making it easier for more and more people to index will continue, despite the recent bump in the other direction. This actually makes me hope even more so than before that the labs do get commoditized. Or, at the very least, they go public as soon as possible, but hopefully they just get totally commoditized, because I think AI will be much more popular and, more importantly, will be much more likely to lead to broad increases in prosperity if the gains are just not captured—if it is as hard to capture the gains of AI as it is to capture the gains of electrification. Yeah, exactly. I think there are no anti-electricity people out there. Yeah, right? I mean, electricity doesn't take your job, but... Well, it did take some people's jobs. Some people's jobs, yeah, yeah. And I think—this may be tangential to the conversation—but there's a really... narratives matter, and there is a really negative narrative around AI right now. But that's because people are not putting out the positive narrative, or because, and there's a reason: it's more difficult to imagine something that doesn't exist that's a good thing than losing something that exists, right? So it's very easy for somebody to go on a podcast and say, 'These jobs that you like, they're going away,' rather than for somebody to spin up a utopia which doesn't exist yet. Right. I hope this isn't too out of left field, but I think I would be remiss if I didn't point out one big cost of having commoditized frontier AI models, which is the tech race dynamic, right? For safety purposes, you might want fewer frontier companies so that each one has a buffer in case they want to slow things down to make things safer. And the way this relates to our point before about the kind of widespread access of the returns is that I think there's a lot less of a trade-off there than some people imagine. Some people think either frontier AI gets commoditized and we all enjoy the benefits (but there might be some risk because the market's really competitive and cutthroat), or things are safer because there's a big gap between the leader and the laggard (but that means the leaders get fantastically wealthy). No—you could just have a relatively big gap, but it's a public company, and ownership in it is widely distributed. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. More recently, I have been thinking that the risk of commodification—which is that it sort of diffuses the ability to use AI for harmful ends—is worth the benefit. I worry that not only does having these concentrated labs mean the surplus isn't as widely distributed through society, but also it creates a very tangible, clear political target for the government. I mean, we saw this with the Defense Production Act threat against Anthropic. If there wasn't one lab, or a couple of labs, that are clearly ahead of others, this kind of threat would be much harder to make. Thank you guys for doing this. Yeah, thank you. Thank you. I feel like there's a lot of unresolved questions, but it is helpful to know what the relevant—at least, what is the first branch along all these important dimensions. Great, thank you. Okay, well, thanks.

10 Years of Stripe France: How enterprises pivot in the age of AI

Stripe(107.0K)·43:23·1.0K views·2026-06-04
Lou GrasserChief Digital Operations Officer, Le Monde Carole RothSenior VP Hotel Experience, Accor Matthieu StefaniHost, Génération Do It Yourself
TL;DRAs iconic French enterprises like *Le Monde* and *Accor* navigate systemic digital transitions, the rise of "agentic AI" is shifting the corporate focus from superficial interfaces to automated back-office workflows, demanding absolute executive…
lucidity and rapid execution to...

TL;DR

As iconic French enterprises like *Le Monde* and *Accor* navigate systemic digital transitions, the rise of "agentic AI" is shifting the corporate focus from superficial interfaces to automated back-office workflows, demanding absolute executive lucidity and rapid execution to keep pace with global competitors.

Le Monde: Scaling Digital Subscriptions and Pioneer AI Deals

*Le Monde* has undergone a dramatic editorial and economic restructuring, shifting from print-centric production to a digital-first powerhouse, heavily driven by strategic AI partnerships.

  • Subscription Growth and Goals:
      • The Scale: Driven by the cultural shift toward paid subscriptions (sparked by Netflix and Spotify) and the pioneering strategy of the *New York Times*, *Le Monde* has grown to nearly 700,000 subscribers.
      • The Target: The company has set a target of reaching 1 million total subscribers, utilizing AI and international expansion to bridge the gap.
  • Pioneering AI Partnerships:
      • First-Mover Advantage: Lou Grasser (Chief Digital Operations Officer) highlighted how their CEO proactively contacted OpenAI. Within three months, they signed a landmark deal, followed by agreements with Perplexity and Meta. *Le Monde* remains one of the few global media outlets to secure direct agreements with these AI giants.
      • High-Yield Conversion: When *Le Monde* articles appear in ChatGPT, they convert readers to paid subscriptions 20 times more effectively than Meta links and 50 times more effectively than Google Discover.
  • Operational Shifts:
      • Print to Digital Reallocation: The newsroom is currently reducing the manual labor dedicated to print layout to free up journalist time for digital, audio, video, and deep investigative formats.
      • Human Quality Assurance: Despite automated translations for international markets (such as the US), *Le Monde* retains human copyeditors. Grasser emphasizes that relying solely on AI for grammar and orthography would fail their high editorial standards.

Accor: The "Asset-Light" Pivot and Tech-Enabled Hospitality

Following a monumental shift decided in 2016 and accelerated by the pandemic, hotel giant *Accor* transitioned to an "asset-light" model, focusing on brand management, franchises, and digital platforms rather than physical real estate.

  • Massive Global Scale:
      • Volume: *Accor* must fill 850,000 hotel rooms every single day across 110 countries.
      • Unification: The group unified 45 distinct hotel brands under a single loyalty ecosystem, ALL.com, to drive direct customer retention.
  • Automating the Back-Office:
      • The Administrative Burden: Currently, over 50% of hotel staff hours are consumed by billing, room allocation, and booking reconciliation.
      • The Goal: By utilizing AI to automate these friction-heavy administrative tasks, *Accor* aims to let staff focus entirely on physical hosting.
  • Redefining the Customer Experience:
      • The "School of Relationship": In the age of AI, *Accor* has launched an initiative focused on the core values of human hospitality. Carole Roth (Senior VP Hotel Experience) explained that technology like passport scanning, high-quality Wi-Fi, and AI assistants should fade into the background, leaving space for genuine human interaction.
      • Platform Complementarity: While *Accor* views booking platforms (Google, Booking.com, Expedia) as competitors, they are also essential local distributors in complex markets like Brazil, China, and India.

The Disruption of AI: From "Superficial" Use to Agentic Workflows

The panel emphasized that the true threat and opportunity of AI lies in "lucidity"—understanding that AI is not a tool for minor tasks, but an engine to entirely rewrite business workflows.

  • The Threat to Google Search: Matthieu Stefani (Host, *Génération Do It Yourself*) noted that his personal search engine usage has dropped tenfold over the past year. Users now bypass traditional search results entirely in favor of direct answers from Large Language Models (LLMs) like Claude, Gemini, and ChatGPT.
  • The Era of Agentic AI:
      • Exponential Efficiency: Companies are beginning to deploy "AI agents" that can perform complex, multi-step workflows. Stefani pointed out that in sectors like insurance brokerage, teams of just two people can now run workflows that previously required 200 employees.
      • Workflow Transformation: On Stefani's podcast, the actual recording remains 100% human and organic. However, pre-production guest research and post-production (transcription, multi-speaker recognition, automated video short creation, and cross-episode query databases) are completely automated by AI.
  • The Stripe Perspective: "Know Your Agent" (KYA): As autonomous AI agents begin executing transactions on behalf of humans (e.g., booking a trip or purchasing products based on user parameters), payment infrastructures like Stripe are moving from traditional KYC ("Know Your Customer") to KYA ("Know Your Agent") protocols to verify authorization and manage fraud.

Global Velocity: What Europe Can Learn From Shenzhen

Matthieu Stefani recently returned from Shenzhen and Shanghai, sharing stark observations on the speed of Asian technological adoption compared to Europe.

  • Fearless Execution: Chinese startups and enterprises operate in an "arena" mindset, releasing products to the market when they are only 70% to 80% ready, prioritizing speed and rapid iteration.
  • Intellectual Property Views: Unlike the Western obsession with legal patents and IP protection, the Chinese tech ecosystem views ideas as shared inspiration. If an idea is utilized by another, it is not viewed as "theft," but as a mechanism to drive collective market speed.
  • Rapid Industrial Decarbonization: Shenzhen's transition is heavily visible; approximately 80% of the city's vehicles are electric, resulting in quiet, cleaner streets—driven by necessity, a lack of local oil, and a rapidly aging population that demands intense robotization.

Notable Quotes

"The first step is lucidity... There are too many bosses, politicians, decision-makers, and entrepreneurs who still think today that an LLM is just made to write a terrible LinkedIn post." — Matthieu Stefani, Host, *Génération Do It Yourself*
"When a *Le Monde* article appears in ChatGPT, it converts 20 times more than an article on Meta, and 50 times more than a Google Discover article." — Lou Grasser, Chief Digital Operations Officer, *Le Monde*
"How do we bypass technology as much as possible to refocus on what is our core business: welcoming people." — Carole Roth, Senior VP Hotel Experience, *Accor*
Pivoter quand on est une startup, c'est pas facile, hein. Pivoter quand on est un groupe de plusieurs dizaines de milliers de collaborateurs, honnêtement, moi, je trouve ça assez fou. La décision a été prise, finalement, de passer en ce qu'on appelle "asset-light", un modèle qui se veut plus sur les plateformes, les marques et de franchises. Pendant des années, on a voulu se lancer à l'international. Le jour où l'IA a été prête, pour nous, ça a été une formidable opportunité... À l'occasion des 10 ans de notre présence en France, nous avons invité des voix incontournables de l'écosystème à partager leur regard sur la transformation d'entreprises iconiques ces dernières années. Il faut qu'on soit pionniers de ça. On a lancé, il y a pas longtemps, effectivement, en plus, l'application sur OpenAI, Accor, etc. C'est-à-dire qu'en fait, il faut essayer, apprendre de ses erreurs. Quand la marque Le Monde décide d'entrer dans GPT, ça convertit 20 fois plus qu'un article sur Meta, et 50 fois plus qu'un article de Discover sur Google. Comment se sont-elles réinventées ? Comment innovent-elles ? Et comment intègrent-elles l'IA pour faire mieux et plus ? Le tout, en présence d'un observateur privilégié de l'écosystème tech et business français. L'agentique IA, moi, il y a 6 mois, je comprenais pas bien ce que c'était. Aujourd'hui, je ne dors plus la nuit, quoi, mais littéralement. L'addition, s'il vous plaît ! 2ème service. Je pense que ce qui nous a beaucoup aidés dans le développement des abonnements pour Le Monde et à construire le modèle économique aujourd'hui avec presque 700 000 abonnés, c'est l'essor des plateformes payantes, notamment effectivement de Netflix ou de Spotify. Ça aurait été beaucoup plus complexe, je pense, si elles n'avaient pas insufflé cette dynamique en France et, bien évidemment aussi outre-Atlantique, le New York Times qui avait déjà enclenché un peu cette dynamique en réduisant beaucoup leur paywall. Il y a quand même des effets, des événements assez marquants en fait aussi de cette année 2016 et de la suite, c'est aussi là qu'on se rend compte que les deepfakes deviennent importants dans le monde, dans le monde numérique. C'est également l'affaire des Panama Papers, 2016, avec bien évidemment Le Monde qui est un des contributeurs majeurs de cette investigation. Est-ce que tu dirais que ces événements-là ont permis aussi, quelque part, à l'utilisateur final de se dire en fait il y a une valeur réelle de cette presse qui est de confiance, et quelque part il faut monétiser et s'assurer qu'ils ont un modèle de revenus qui fonctionne pour eux ? Bien sûr. Oui, ce type de grands événements pour nous, c'est des coups à jouer. Ça veut dire que ça montre, ça démontre le travail d'un journaliste, de l'enquête, bien évidemment. Et le fait d'avoir étoffé notre rédaction, justement pour se mettre sur les sujets-là de désinformation qui sont arrivés assez tôt — tu as raison de dire 2016 —, ça a participé, je pense, à démontrer la qualité en fait des contenus du Monde. Et à se dire, OK, il y a derrière des journalistes qui enquêtent là-dessus. Ils sont beaucoup, on peut mettre beaucoup de journalistes parfois sur différents types d'enquêtes, hein, jusqu'à parfois 200 personnes qui sont sur des énormes enquêtes, et qui nous permettent d'aller démontrer justement à nos lecteurs, voilà, que c'est une information qui nécessite beaucoup de travail de fond, et que derrière elle est payante. Donc oui, c'est des coups en fait pour nous à jouer. Est-ce que tu arrives aussi dans des réflexions où tu vas avoir finalement un accès au texte qui va être automatisé, de plus en plus ? Il va y avoir de plus en plus de textes accessibles. Donc, est-ce que vous allez essayer de suivre finalement ce train effréné de pouvoir avoir du contenu toujours renouvelé pour vos abonnés, ou plutôt moins mais plus qualitatif, en mettant en avant la valeur de la vérité, face à ce deepfake ? Alors, au global, nous, l'idée c'est quand même de se dire que, là, on est en pleine, justement en pleine transformation de notre rédaction. Justement, l'idée c'est, on est très puissants au numérique mais on a encore une grosse partie de notre rédaction qui passe du temps sur la conception du papier, voilà. Aujourd'hui, on est en train, justement, d'ouvrir une énorme transformation qui a lieu cette année pour alléger le temps, pour vraiment réduire le temps consacré à la confection du papier pour justement aller davantage sur le numérique, voilà, travailler les uns, travailler les sujets, voilà. On a aussi d'autres enjeux qui sont d'aller sur d'autres formats comme la vidéo, comme l'audio, voilà, qui sont hyper importants pour nous, et aussi de donner accès à notre rédaction à des outils, à des indicateurs, qui vont leur permettre de pouvoir voir de plus près la transformation, en tout cas quel impact ça a sur les lecteurs. C'est fou parce que je crois qu'il y a 10 ans, moi je conseillais, un peu plus, ouais non, il y a 10 ans, on conseillait pas mal de médias sur ces stratégies qu'on appelait de reverse publishing. Donc tu vois de quoi je parle. Donc qui était vraiment de se dire : à l'origine, tu avais une réunion éditoriale. Alors Le Monde c'est d'ailleurs différent parce que vous sortez le journal de l'après-midi ou le soir, mais souvent un quotidien c'était fin de matinée. Tu avais un comité de rédaction, tous les rédacteurs en chef qui se réunissaient, les sujets qui étaient arrêtés, alors il y en a tous les sujets plus long terme qui étaient quand même prévus à l'avance, et puis après tu avançais dans l'après-midi, et puis tu avais les pages, les dernières pages les plus chaudes, donc la couverture, les pages qui restaient potentiellement ouvertes pour les dernières actus. Et toute l'organisation de la rédaction était faite pour ça, ce qui faisait que quand tu arrivais le matin sur le site internet, t'avais pas de news. Et ben, t'avais pas de news, parce qu'en fait c'était que la news de la veille au soir. Bien sûr. Ou alors celle de la veille au soir était faite à 16h, 17h, en fait. Et donc, tu dois... mais ce que tu racontes là, c'est que ce chemin n'est pas complètement... Il est pas complètement fait, c'est complètement fait par exemple aux États-Unis. On est d'ailleurs allés voir un des médias américains, on est allés voir le New York Times, on est allés voir le Wall Street, on est allés voir The Atlantic, on a parlé avec eux et eux, les médias anglo-saxons, ils ont déjà opéré ce changement depuis longtemps. C'est pas le cas encore en France, et c'est là, nous, où on a quelque chose à vraiment jouer. On va peut-être changer un peu de secteur avec toi, Carole, parce que, franchement, fascinant de voir, Lou, cette transformation que tu as opérée. Et 10 ans, on a l'impression que c'est pas loin et, en même temps, il s'est passé énormément de choses. Après, je crois comprendre également que, côté Accor, le contexte de l'époque, si on se remet en 2016, il y a l'émergence de ces plateformes dont on parlait plus tôt, et, quelque part, Accor décide de prendre une décision extrêmement structurante. Est-ce que tu peux nous en dire un petit peu plus ? Alors, oui, et ça s'est confirmé après. Parce qu'on parle de 2016, mais je crois que l'élément le plus impactant, finalement, pour l'hospitalité, ça a été le Covid. Mais, juste avant, chance ou pas, ça, voilà, c'est difficile de le dire, mais, la décision a été prise, finalement, de passer en ce qu'on appelle "asset-light", c'est-à-dire de ne plus être forcément propriétaire des hôtels, de ne plus être propriétaire des immeubles, de tous ces lieux et de passer sur un modèle qui se veut plus sur les plateformes, les marques et de franchises. C'est encore un changement qu'on opère aujourd'hui à travers le monde, mais ça a été une décision assez structurante qui amène, finalement, à regarder un petit peu vers cette nouvelle façon de procéder à la réservation, d'opérer et d'avoir une nouvelle offre de valeur, finalement, pour l'écosystème des propriétaires hôteliers, de ce qu'on appelle les general managers, et de se positionner, finalement, en apporteur de personnes dans les hôtels. Moi, mon métier, vraiment au quotidien, c'est de se dire, bah, comment je fais en sorte qu'il y ait un maximum de personnes qui viennent remplir des chambres d'hôtel ? Une chambre, quand elle est pas vendue une journée, bah, c'est pas un stock. Quand elle n'est pas remplie un soir, elle est vide et c'est de l'argent perdu, donc c'est... et pour vous donner quand même un chiffre, hein, c'est 850 000 chambres à remplir tous les jours à travers le monde par Accor. Et nous, donc, on se positionne vraiment avec une plateforme qui s'est repositionnée aussi avec un programme de fidélité, ALL, ALL.com, où vraiment on a cette proposition de valeur qui unifie 45 marques au sein d'un même programme de fidélité. Et de pouvoir, justement, via aussi un système de points, encourager les différents clients à voyager, revoyager dans nos hôtels, découvrir les marques. Donc, vraiment, cette proposition de valeur, c'est : au sein d'un même écosystème, vous pouvez profiter de l'ensemble de ce que propose Accor à travers 110 pays. Moi, je suis très curieux de savoir à quel moment vous vous êtes dit, finalement, nos concurrents c'est plus Hilton, Marriott et autres chaînes d'hôtels, et à quel moment il y a le moment un peu Eurêka qui dit, mais en fait, les vrais concurrents de demain c'est les gens qui créent cette expérience, cette hyper-personnalisation dont tu parles très bien, et là, il faut qu'on change de modèle d'affaires. Alors, concurrents et compléments pour les plateformes, parce qu'on ne pourrait pas remplir toutes ces chambres, en tout cas pas aujourd'hui, toutes ces chambres sans, finalement, leur complément et parce que, localement, Accor, on travaille avec vraiment un écosystème extrêmement vaste de réseaux de distribution. Parce que c'est ce qui nous permet de mieux être connectés aussi localement. Quand tu dis plateformes, c'est Google, Booking... Google, Booking, Expedia, Airbnb... Airbnb, non, ils nous distribuent pas, mais, en gros, toutes les plateformes qui distribuent nos hôtels et elles sont précieuses, parce que, dans certains pays, je pense aussi à des endroits dans lesquels on a besoin de plus de spécificité locale, le Brésil, la Chine, demain l'Inde, etc., forcément, il y a des réflexes d'usage sur lesquels on ne peut pas être en première pensée quand on va faire une réservation. En revanche, ils nous challengent beaucoup, parce que, bah, très souvent, en fait, eux sont vraiment là pour se dire, comment on capture un client le mieux possible, comment on lui fait cette promotion-là, et, puisqu'on est là aussi pour parler de ça, notamment sur les moyens de paiement, ils nous ont vraiment, vraiment mis dans cet esprit de comparaison. Je pense qu'on n'est pas trop loin derrière, parce qu'il y a un ADN d'innovation qui est assez fort chez Accor et qu'on n'a pas envie d'être présent pour tous nos marchés, mais, oui, de se dire, bah, eux, comment ils présentent, finalement, certains moyens de paiement qui vont convaincre de faire l'achat sur cette plateforme, comment ils vont mettre en valeur certains hôtels. Des fois, on a eu un petit regret de se dire, mince, c'est quand même dommage d'avoir de plus belles photos sur certaines plateformes qu'on en a chez nous. Donc, mais c'est un défi. Matthieu, toi qui, finalement, as pas mal bougé, t'as interviewé beaucoup de monde, est-ce que là, on est face à deux exemples un peu singuliers, uniques, ou... ? Non, je crois pas. Je crois, quand tout à l'heure tu posais la question de quelles entreprises m'ont le plus marqué par leur transformation, j'ai pensé tout de suite à Microsoft. Et tu peux dire que c'est très loin, en fait, c'est très proche de ce que tu disais. Microsoft, c'était ce qu'on appelle du on-premise, je pense, hein, donc t'achetais un truc à la Fnac, il y a pas si longtemps, quand même. Tu l'installais... Tu disais, on avait un CD. CD, ouais. T'installais ton truc, et puis, en fait, tu l'avais avec ta licence, oui, tu validais en ligne, mais moi j'étais sûr qu'ils allaient mourir, mais sûr, en fait. Se dire réussir à basculer un business aussi gros, aussi international, d'un coup où t'achètes des DVD ou au pire tu télécharges de temps en temps, tu payes une fois, à un truc où tu t'abonnes... C'est magistral, et eux ils l'ont fait, je trouve, de manière assez fluide. Alors, ils étaient tellement indispensables, ils ont réussi à se transformer de l'intérieur, hein, mais moi j'ai trouvé ça vraiment fou. Ce qui est assez drôle, c'est que quelques années après, on arrive là, dans un endroit où je trouve, moi, le défi, on en parle beaucoup, mais moi je le vis vraiment au quotidien, il est pour Google, maintenant. C'est-à-dire que, moi je vois mon usage de Google en un an, je pense qu'il a été divisé par 10. Et je fais tellement tout sur les IA que, tu vois, si je dois essayer de comprendre comment utiliser quelque chose, réparer, comprendre, enfin, je veux dire, ça va plus vite, ça va plus vite. Tu vas pas faire une recherche, aller sur un site, revenir sur la page de recherche, retourner sur un site, chercher dans le site, etc. Donc, en fait, le search, maintenant, bah, il est fait sur les LLM, et donc, comment va se transformer Google ? Est-ce que Gemini va être aussi fort que les autres ? Pivoter quand on est une startup, c'est pas facile, hein, parce que je l'ai fait plein de fois, mais c'est possible. Pivoter quand on est un groupe de plusieurs dizaines de milliers de collaborateurs, honnêtement, moi, je trouve ça fou. Et Accor en est un exemple intéressant. On en a parlé sur mon podcast avec Sébastien Bazin, le coup du... tu en parlais tout à l'heure, de l'asset-light. Ce qui est drôle, c'est que quand tu le dis tout à l'heure, moi c'est le moment, et j'en parle dans cet épisode, je suis passé à la SCI, où moi je me suis mis à acheter mes bureaux, à acheter des bureaux à Paris, à Bordeaux, un peu partout, et j'ai zéro regret là-dessus. Parfois, ça m'inquiète encore pour Accor, je me modère, mais le jour où tout se retourne, ça va être compliqué, mais, en l'occurrence, force est de constater que ça a fonctionné, donc... Quels sont les assets ou le mindset qu'il faut avoir pour être capable d'exécuter lorsqu'on voit, justement, le changement arriver ? La première chose, c'est la lucidité. C'est qu'il y a trop d'entreprises, et d'ailleurs, on en a trop aujourd'hui en France, dans le monde entier, qui se voilent la face avec l'IA, qui se voilent complètement la face. Soit, il y a deux choses, hein : soit ils le savent, ça les stresse, et ils ont peur d'agir, soit ils le savent pas du tout. Il y a trop de patrons, de politiques, de décideurs, d'entrepreneurs, qui pensent encore qu'aujourd'hui, un LLM, c'est fait pour faire un post LinkedIn éclaté au sol. C'est non, mais c'est vrai. Et c'est un truc de malade, je veux dire, moi, ça m'attriste, ça m'atterre, ça m'angoisse, c'est ça qui m'angoisse. Une fois que tu as fait le constat que tu es lucide sur ton business, et que tu te dis, OK, moi, il va m'arriver ça, il va m'arriver ça, mes lecteurs papier, ça va s'effondrer, les lecteurs papiers PDF, ça n'a pas trop d'avenir, ça n'aurait jamais dû en avoir, et donc, tout va se passer sur l'appli, il va falloir que j'aie un modèle différent... Bon, bah tu es lucide, tu fais le constat, t'y vas. Après, faut avoir du courage, de l'obsession, parce que, en fait, on te dit 40 000 fois que ça va pas fonctionner, puis ça fonctionne pas en plus au début parce que toi-même, tu fais n'importe quoi, et voilà. Et donc, tu dois être obsédé par le changement, tu devez convaincre tout le monde, tu devez être courageux et de séparer de ceux qui veulent pas être convaincus, parce qu'il y en a plein aussi qui veulent rester dans le monde d'avant, et le monde d'avant, bah, il existe plus, désolé mon lapin, c'est comme ça. Et donc, là-dessus, c'est tout un enchaînement de décisions, et une ambition, tu le disais aussi, d'aller plus vite. Parce que les autres, et en ce moment, moi je le vois dans les grosses boîtes dont je parlais tout de suite, elles se font, mais, shortcutter. Je le vois dans des univers, même des grands classiques, hein, du courtier en assurance, par exemple, avec des gens, à deux, ils font le boulot de 200 personnes, mais au moins, et mieux. Et, en fait, pourquoi ? Parce qu'ils sont bien câblés, parce qu'ils ont des agents IA. L'agentique IA, moi, il y a 6 mois, je comprenais pas bien ce que c'était. Aujourd'hui, je ne dors plus la nuit, quoi, mais littéralement. Hier, de minuit à une heure, je ne dormais plus, parce que jusqu'à minuit, j'étais sur mon OpenCloud dans mon Mac mini qui est arrivé hier, et que ça me pète le cerveau.
