Et ça, il faut... enfin, ça va tout changer. Et donc, il y a un moment, cette lucidité, faut être curieux, il faut l'avoir, et arrêter de se dire que tout ça, c'est juste un petit effet de bord. Et donc, là-dessus, c'est une lucidité, puis derrière, l'expérience, l'icourage, l'obsession, et puis en avant. C'est, pour le coup, là, la même réflexion qu'a eue Lou Dreyfus, qui est notre patron, sur l'IA. Tout ça pour être capable de tout contextualiser, de créer des productions, des shorts automatiques pour les réseaux sociaux, de créer des recherches dans tous les épisodes passés sur toutes les fois, toutes les occurrences où on a parlé de ça, etc., etc. D'être capable de créer un mashup de tous les conseils sur les paiements de tous nos entrepreneurs, de tous les plus beaux fails de Génération Do It Yourself, et d'aller les chercher, etc., etc. Et donc tout ça n'a plus rien à voir. Ce qui est très différent pour Le Monde, ça, là, on est sur des structures qui n'ont rien à voir. Et, par exemple, nous, l'IA, ça nous a servi à faire une expansion de marché. Pendant des années, on a voulu se lancer à l'international, le jour où l'IA a été prête, pour nous, ça a été une formidable opportunité de se dire, alors, sans qu'elle soit générative, mais là, c'est qu'elle puisse, en fait, traduire, tout simplement. Et qu'on puisse se dire, bah, elle nous permet, en fait, de traduire un socle de contenus qui est important, c'est plus de 100 contenus par jour, avec toujours, ce qui est hyper important et ce que disait Matthieu aussi, une relecture derrière, c'est-à-dire, qu'il y ait la caution de nos journalistes, donc, relecture, réécriture si besoin. Et, aujourd'hui, on voit que, bah, il y a des marchés qui sont particulièrement gros, mais, intéressés, comme les États-Unis. Tu veux te dire que je t'en veux, c'est juste que c'est un secteur que je connais mieux, l'hôtellerie... Mais, question qui tue quand même, j'ai besoin de savoir, avez-vous encore des correcteurs d'orthographe humains ? Oui, on a encore une équipe, bien sûr. Ils sont très importants. OK. C'est la réponse politique. Mais, oui, il y a. Ils sont très syndiqués, c'est juste ça. Non, mais c'est vrai, faut le laisser, hein, ça, absolument. En fait, pour Le Monde, c'est... aujourd'hui, moi je pense que aujourd'hui, bien sûr que l'IA va apporter beaucoup de solutions là-dessus, tu as encore d'énormes oublis, même quand tu corriges toi-même tes propres textes, l'IA, sur la partie orthographique, et pourtant, elle passe à côté de... Les LLM sont moins forts que tes correcteurs d'orthographe humains ? Non, je dis pas ça, je dis que nous on a une exigence tellement forte sur l'orthographe aujourd'hui, que c'est aujourd'hui une garantie pour nous d'avoir un rendu, qui est de très grande qualité. Se fier uniquement sur l'IA, ce serait un énorme échec là-dessus. Peut-être que dans 10 ans, ce sera la même chose, hein, je n'en sais rien. Mais, il y a un point crucial quand même, c'est que, on parle justement d'automatisation, on est censés libérer plus de temps, faire moins de choses, et on voit que c'est tout le contraire. On a de plus en plus de sollicitations puisqu'on va aller sur plus de marchés, on va pouvoir faire encore plus de choses. On va aller dans quelle direction, faire moins ou faire plus ? Faire mieux, si possible, mais c'est vrai que... c'est vrai que moi, je suis dans cette phase où, comme je dis, je dors plus la nuit, je bosse plus que jamais, parce qu'en fait, j'onboarde mes IA et je découvre des choses, et puis ça se trouve dans 6 mois, il faudra onboarder une nouvelle IA, parce qu'il y a 3 mois, c'était Gemini, après c'était ChatGPT, maintenant, tout sur Claude, et donc, c'est toujours... et puis ça change, puis ça va vite, et puis, on doit axer, désaxer les business, etc. mais quelle chance on a de vivre ça, quoi. Moi, je peux donner un cas un peu concret, mais, par exemple, demain, ça va aussi avec la formation des personnes qui arrivent. Bon, alors déjà en plus, toutes les personnes qui arrivent et qui sont plus jeunes, elles sont quand même très très formées à tout ça, et j'aime bien quand même le mot de : on a des agents, on a des assistances. On ne sait pas ce qu'est le monde de demain, mais en tout cas aujourd'hui, pour les quelques années à venir, on est là pour être aidés, accompagnés. Et, je vais prendre le cas d'une nouvelle personne qui est formée dans un hôtel, même un hôtel de luxe, etc. Je préfère 10 000 fois que, finalement, sa gouvernante, qui est là depuis 5, 10, 15, 20, 30 ans parfois dans les hôtels, puisse lui donner tout ce qu'elle sait de l'héritage du lieu. Et, des lieux qui sont magnifiques, voilà, qui vraiment, sont un lieu de patrimoine. Je préfère 10 000 fois cela à accueillir ce nouvel arrivant dans les équipes en lui montrant derrière un outil un petit peu fastidieux : "Alors, pour réserver plusieurs chambres, tu vas cliquer là, machin." Demain, il y a un assistant, en fait, qui, même, fera ça, va l'aider, répond à ses questions, mais, surtout, ça sera automatique. C'est quand même, en termes de partage d'expérience pour la personne, de dire, mais tu comprends, quand on accueille un VIP, en fait on fait comme ça, tu as telle et telle chose, et cette chambre-là, elle a telle particularité. Je trouve ça, en fait, 10 000 fois plus riche, parce que, et vraiment chez nous, c'est ça, c'est de se dire, comment on fait fi un maximum de la technologie pour se reconcentrer sur ce qui est, nous, notre cœur de métier, accueillir quelqu'un. Et, justement, en fait, alors, en plus, c'est Maud Bailly qui vient de lancer ça, l'école de la relation. À l'ère de l'IA, elle vient de lancer l'école de la relation. C'est de se dire, en fait, c'est quoi les fondamentaux, quand on accueille quelqu'un et quand on fait du tourisme et de l'hospitalité. On voit tous, en fait, je pense, une accélération incroyable dans nos entreprises, enfin, des transformations spectaculaires, que ce soit Le Monde, que ce soit Accor, également dans ton domaine, Matthieu, des transformations, et j'ai l'impression qu'il y a encore beaucoup beaucoup beaucoup de choses à venir. Nous chez Stripe, on le vit également au quotidien, que ce soit la création, finalement, des fondations, la plateforme qui permet, bah, le commerce agentique de demain de pouvoir se faire, aux paiements personnalisés, partout dans le monde. Et nos collaborateurs le vivent au quotidien avec une vitesse d'exécution qui est parfois, effrénée. Vous sécurisez des transactions entre agents ? Entre... exactement, mais on onboard, on onboarde les agents sur nos plateformes pour qu'ils puissent, permettre la transaction et s'assurer également de faire la gestion de toute la fraude. Donc, finalement, aujourd'hui, t'as plus simplement "Know Your Customer", KYC, mais t'as "Know Your Agent"... Puisque tu as besoin, finalement, de lui donner une procuration pour qu'il puisse exécuter, jusqu'au paiement, en ton nom. Et ça, dans... finalement, dans une définition d'un contrat smart qui lui permet de dire, voilà, je peux acheter là, je peux pas acheter là, je peux aller dépenser 250 € pour ta paire de baskets ou pas que tu auras retrouvée sur ton LLM ou que ton fils aura décidé d'acheter pour toi. Donc, oui, c'est... comment on crée cette infrastructure qui permet de de faire ça. L'addition. Ce que j'aimerais voir, c'est, finalement, pour vous, si vous vous projetez dans les 5-10 prochaines années, qu'est-ce qu'on peut souhaiter au groupe Le Monde ? Qu'est-ce qu'on peut souhaiter à Accor ? Qu'est-ce qu'on peut souhaiter à Génération Do It Yourself ? Et à notre écosystème, finalement, bah, qui nous permette de de tirer parti le mieux possible de toutes de toutes ces évolutions, de toutes ces opportunités dont on a pu échanger lors de ce déjeuner ? Bon, on en a reparlé, on en a parlé tout à l'heure, mais je pense qu'au Monde, si on a, si on a deux supports distincts, c'est qu'il y aura, moi je pense que dans 10 ou 15 ans, il y aura encore du papier. Mais ça sera, comme on dit, beaucoup plus minoritaire, sur des populations, effectivement, plus âgées, mais qui permettent de conserver aussi un un lien avec le papier, donc, ce qu'on disait tout à l'heure, plutôt démocratique. Nous, on s'était fixé un chiffre d'un d'un million d'abonnés, tout confondu. Bien évidemment, l'IA va nous y aider, voilà. Après, il y a aussi le fait de pouvoir aller à l'étranger, le fait de de continuer, on n'en a pas beaucoup parlé, mais le fait de continuer à enrichir nos contenus. On a aujourd'hui une rédaction qui a doublé en 10 ans. C'est le fait aussi de proposer beaucoup de contenus et très différents, qui fait que les gens, en fait, aujourd'hui s'abonnent. Après, faudra peut-être réfléchir à de de nouvelles verticales, de nouveaux services, voilà, ce qu'on voit beaucoup outre-Atlantique, qui feront que demain, on ira encore plus loin. Mais moi j'ai confiance, en tout cas, dans Le Monde là-dessus pour proposer quelque chose de très authentique, et produit par une rédaction. Je dirais que c'est en deux points. Pour nous, c'est vraiment de se dire, comment on accompagne, finalement, de des propriétaires hôteliers, des équipes, parce que c'est quand même vraiment notre premier client, bah, qui font ce choix de continuer d'ouvrir des établissements pour faire tourner, bah, le tourisme. Le voyage, on est quand même dans des lieux où on réunit des personnes, des restaurants, etc. Donc, ces lieux où, finalement, les personnes se rencontrent individuellement, en business, en loisir, etc. Donc, comment, finalement, on leur donne tous les moyens pour pouvoir gérer ça au mieux, avec toutes les contraintes qu'on connaît aujourd'hui, d'écologie, de reconnaissance des personnes, etc., et en donnant la meilleure expérience sur le terrain possible ? Et, en étant, bah, justement, c'est le deuxième point, très en amont, c'est-à-dire, pour le client, lui donner, finalement, toutes les possibilités, mentionné le plus simplement possible, de, "je veux aller faire un voyage là, et j'ai envie de soleil de telle date à telle date". Eh bien, en fait, finalement, comment Accor on est là pour recueillir ce besoin dès le début, et les amener, finalement, bah, dans tous nos hôtels Accor, pour les amener à vivre cette expérience le mieux possible, et la plus, parce qu'on n'a pas fini d'arriver tout tout tout au bout, personnalisée possible. En deux mots, c'est moins d'outils. En fait, on s'est autoconvaincus que notre job, c'était potentiellement des interfaces, mais aussi du Word, du Excel, du PowerPoint, des mails. Les gens passent leurs journées, quand on y réfléchit, on passe nos journées à faire des mails. Se dire, mais c'est quand même fou. Il y a il y a 20 ans, 30 ans, 40 ans, les gens, ils faisaient pas des fax ou des courriers au bureau toute la journée, ils produisaient vraiment du vrai travail. Et donc, on s'est perdus, moi je pense qu'on s'est perdus. Et, et moi, j'aimerais que, ce que je crois, à 5 ou 10 ans, c'est qu'on revienne à plus d'humains, en fait. Moi, plus d'organiques dans mes contenus. C'est le cas, j'y travaille à fond. Moins de factures, moins de toutes ces choses-là, ces bullshit jobs, qu'on fait tous, partiellement, ou ou entièrement. Plus de relations, plus de journalistes, plus de choses comme ça. Pour moi, c'est vraiment, je je crois, je veux croire en un monde où on va revenir sur quelque chose de réel, et où l'IA va automatiser toutes ces choses qui ne servent à rien. Et ça, moi j'y crois assez fort, en tout cas, c'est là où j'ai envie d'aller. Et on arrive à la fin à la fin de ce déjeuner. Merci, c'était très... Merci, c'était délicieux. Merci pour votre partage d'expérience et vos retours sur le monde qui qui nous attend.

Announcing NVIDIA RTX Spark | GTC Taipei 2026 Keynote by CEO Jensen Huang

NVIDIA(2.2M)·17:07·45.2K views·2026-06-04
Jensen HuangCEO, NVIDIA
TL;DRNVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang announced the NVIDIA RTX Spark, a revolutionary superchip built in partnership with MediaTek and Microsoft, designed to completely reinvent the Windows PC for the era of local, autonomous AI agents.
The "N1X" Architecture: Developed in close...

TL;DR

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang announced the NVIDIA RTX Spark, a revolutionary superchip built in partnership with MediaTek and Microsoft, designed to completely reinvent the Windows PC for the era of local, autonomous AI agents.

The Core Technology: NVIDIA RTX Spark Superchip

  • The "N1X" Architecture: Developed in close partnership with MediaTek, the RTX Spark integrates NVIDIA’s full software stack, making it 100% CUDA-compatible and capable of running all existing Windows and NVIDIA applications.
  • Hardware Specifications:
      • GPU: Blackwell RTX GPU featuring 6,144 CUDA cores and delivering 1 petaflop of AI performance.
      • CPU: A custom 20-core Grace CPU.
      • Interconnect & Memory: Fused by NVLink, boasting 128 gigabytes of unified memory.
      • Manufacturing: Built on TSMC's 3-nanometer process, packing 70 billion transistors.

Reinventing the OS and Software for AI Agents

  • The "Agentic" Runtime: Jensen Huang described the future of the PC as a shift from traditional applications to autonomous agents. Under this paradigm, Large Language Models (LLMs) act as the "modern DirectX," handling computer vision, prompts, sound, and video generation.
  • Local, Sandboxed Workflows: Agents run natively in secure sandboxes, executing complex workflows by bridging local tools with cloud-based models.
      • *Example:* A demo showcased a local agent using an OpenShell sandbox to orchestrate Rhino and Blender, automatically correcting its own design mistakes and utilizing the Flux 1 model to render photorealistic architectural mockups.
  • Adobe Partnership: Adobe has re-engineered the core architectures of Photoshop and Premiere for RTX Spark.
      • Performance: The updated suite is twice as fast as previous versions.
      • Integration: Includes Model Context Protocol (MCP) server support, allowing the software to interact natively with agents running on the laptop.

The New PC Lineup: Laptops, Desktops, and Workstations

NVIDIA and its partners are launching three distinct product families covering desktop, laptop, and workstation form factors:

  • RTX Spark Laptops & Desktops: Showcased partners like MSI. Designed for 24/7, meter-free, local agent operation. Users can run local models—such as NVIDIA's NeMoTron-3 Ultra or Meta's Llama—without cloud subscription costs or latency, connected to smart home and office security systems.
  • DGX Station for Windows: A powerhouse workstation designed for developers training and deploying large language models locally.
      • Compute Power: Delivers 20 petaflops of performance.
      • Memory Bandwidth: Features 768 gigabytes of memory and 8 terabytes per second of memory bandwidth, allowing developers to run trillion-parameter models directly at their desks.

The Strategic Vision: From PCs to Home Supercomputers

  • The "Smartphone" Pivot: Huang compared the current shift in personal computing to the transition from mobile feature phones to smartphones. Ten years from now, the PC will no longer be a device where users simply click and type.
  • The Household AI Supercomputer: NVIDIA envisions a future where homes feature dedicated AI supercomputers running continuous assistant agents.
  • Industry Alignment: 100% of the world's PC ecosystem has aligned to support and manufacture systems for the RTX Spark platform.

Notable Quotes

"Microsoft and NVIDIA over the last three years... took this long to completely reinvent how the PC is going to work so that we could be ready for this moment." — Jensen Huang, NVIDIA CEO
"The new operating system is, of course, the old operating system plus large language models. Large language models, in a lot of ways, is the modern version of DirectX." — Jensen Huang, NVIDIA CEO
"There is no question this reinvention of the computer is as big of a deal as the reinvention of the phone into what we now know as the smartphone." — Jensen Huang, NVIDIA CEO
My relationship with you started here. And many of you, many of you, many of my friends and partners here in Taiwan, your companies started here. This is, in a lot of ways, the beginning of the modern computer industry. It has been 40 years now, and NVIDIA is 33 years old. The PC industry was already starting to get to Windows 1 and Windows 2, and Apple 1 and Apple 2, and by the time that we came along, Windows 3.1 was the PC. And as you know, Windows 95 made the PC personal. It took the PC from enterprises and companies, and made it into a consumer electronics device. Everybody should have one, and everybody does. This is the beginning. This computing platform did several things incredibly smart. Windows was not just disaggregated; as you know, Windows was properly abstracted. It was architected just right. It had systems BIOSes, open chipsets, the operating system with drivers that could be connected and installed at runtime, and an abstraction layer with a multimedia API that opened up the PC to what we all know today. Each one of these elements was essential in making the PC so popular. Forty years later, Microsoft and NVIDIA are going to reinvent the PC. This is going to be the new PC. Tomorrow night—I think it's tomorrow night our time—I'm going to be with Satya, and we're going to talk a lot more about the work that we're doing together. It took Microsoft and NVIDIA the last three years to completely reinvent how the PC is going to work so that we could be ready for this moment. As I mentioned earlier, that compute pattern called the agent is going to run in AI clouds. It's going to run inside enterprises, and it is also going to run on your PC. What's going to happen to that PC when it has an autonomous agent? This is an agent that's helping you and understands you. You could talk to it, it could look at you, and you could ask it to read files or help you do some research. It could do a lot more that I'll show you. But the new operating system is, of course, the old operating system plus large language models. Large language models, in a lot of ways, are the modern version of DirectX. It has, of course, input and output, understands prompts, understands computer vision, can generate video, and can generate sounds. It is the modern extension, the intelligence extension of the PC, of a computer. On top of that, the application, as I mentioned before, is going to be replaced by an agentic runtime, and that is the modern application and agent. Let's now take a look at what it can do. It started with a spark. An idea. To reimagine the PC for the first time in 40 years for the age of AI. What becomes of our personal computer in a world of agents? We will have agents running natively, connected to models, either locally or in the cloud. It will be our personal AI, sandboxed for security, running continuously and getting work done. The chips and the OS must evolve. Introducing NVIDIA RTX Spark. This is everything we've learned over 33 years distilled into one chip: a Blackwell RTX GPU with 6144 CUDA cores, delivering 1 petaflop of AI performance, and a custom 20-core Grace CPU built in partnership with MediaTek. Fused by NVLink, it features 128 gigabytes of unified memory, built on TSMC's 3-nanometer process with 70 billion transistors. And in close collaboration with Microsoft, we have a Windows platform for agents. We're reinventing the personal computer for creating, for gaming, and for agents. This is the dawn of a new personal computing revolution, and it starts with NVIDIA RTX Spark. Here it is. Of course, I've got to show you the most beautiful part, which is video games. It's also the closest to our hearts. This is Forza. This is 007, by the way—the new 007 game. I'm looking forward to playing it. I look a little bit like him. Ladies and gentlemen, NVIDIA's RTX Spark laptops. Thank you. I have too many things in my pocket. 太多東西了 (Too many things). Okay, all right. This is the most amazing chip the world's ever built. This is the N1X that we built in partnership with MediaTek. I think I saw Rick earlier. This is N1X; it's a beautiful chip. And this is a chip that, frankly, would take 33 years to build. And the reason for that is because 100% of NVIDIA's software stack runs here. If you want to run digital biology, no problem. If you want to do seismic processing, no problem. If you want astrophysics, no problem. Everything associated with CUDA—all the physics, all the biology, all the genomics, all the AI, and all the computer graphics—runs with no problem. It runs every single application NVIDIA has ever created, and every single application that Windows has ever run. Microsoft and NVIDIA meticulously optimized everything so that this computer literally runs everything the world has ever created, plus it now runs agents. It is an incredible computer, and I'm so proud of it. Now, I want you to keep that in mind for the next video I'm going to show you. Just imagine everything here is going to run on your PC. That computer could have a local NeMoTron-3 Ultra model or NeMoTron-3 Super model, or it could have a Claude Code or CodeX or some other model in the cloud or on the network, and it's going to work and do something amazing. Let's play it. Every house starts as an idea. Getting from idea to design takes a myriad of tools, expertise, and a lot of time. Now, an agent running locally on RTX Spark can help me design a house using the tools on my laptop, with an OpenShell sandbox running the Hermes harness connected to Claude Sonnet in the cloud. I select the site, share my concept sketches, and share a mood board of styles to inspire my design. And the prompt is a text description of the requirements and the design intent. My agent goes to work. Using the tools on my laptop, it opens Rhino and starts modeling the site, shaping the terrain, setbacks, and the building envelope. Then, it proposes building forms optimized for cost, comfort, and quality. With the form defined, my agent generates the entire interior layout. Walls, circulation, and rooms begin to take shape. I can jump in whenever I want to adjust or change something. Doors, windows, and structural elements are placed automatically. My agent even detects its own mistakes and fixes them. When I approve, the agent exports the model from Rhino into Blender. Materials and object properties transfer with the design context intact. I fine-tune the materials to get the look just right, and then I pick the shots. Blender renders the house, and my agent, using generative AI with the Flux 1 model, makes them photo-real with multiple viewpoints and lighting conditions. What was once a complex workflow is now guided and simplified by my agent working with me on RTX Spark—design at the speed of imagination. This is the PC in the world of agents! The developers are so excited about this. It is an incredible computer, with all of the acceleration and all the software capabilities associated with it, working with every developer to make it incredible for all of you. The next one is Adobe. It's an incredible tool suite, of course, used by tens of millions of people around the world. They have re-engineered the architecture at the core of Adobe Photoshop and Premiere, and they will release it for RTX Spark. It is twice as fast—it is already fast, but now it's going to be twice as fast—and it is also designed to be agent-friendly. With its MCP server, it can now interact with agents on your laptop. The number of customers and partners who are so excited to bring RTX Spark to the market is just incredible. You know, this is the first complete lineup of PC reinvention in 40 years, and I'm just so happy that all of you and the ecosystem around the world have joined us. This is basically everybody; everybody will support RTX Spark and will be building incredibly smart, powerful, and beautiful laptops with all of us. Thank you very much. But that's not all. That's not all. RTX Spark is a reinvention of the laptop, but in fact, Microsoft and NVIDIA are reinventing all of the PC, and today we're announcing a whole new line. We have three revolutionary Windows machines covering desktop, laptop, and workstations—all 100% Windows compatible, 100% CUDA, and 100% NVIDIA AI Tensor Core. Everything that runs, everything that you see that runs on NVIDIA in all these different platforms around the world, runs here. This is the first completely re-engineered, reinvented line of PCs that has happened in 40 years. Now, what's really amazing is this. This is the RTX Spark laptop. And this is the desktop, which is from MSI. Joseph, this one's yours. Look how beautiful it is. This agent could run 24/7, meter-free, and you could download your agent and raise your llama in here. This is your Claude; it is running all the time with no meter anxiety, and it is sitting here connected to your whole house. It is connected to your laptop, connected to your display, all the cameras, your dryer, your water cooler, your water heater, your security system—everything, whatever you want, is all connected to this, and this becomes your personal AI, your personal AI agent. And it gets smarter and smarter and smarter over time, because today we have NeMoTron-3 Ultra, tomorrow we have NeMoTron-4, then NeMoTron-5, NeMoTron-6, and we will just keep getting smarter and smarter. Meanwhile, this is sitting at home, helping you do things. If you want to book travel, no problem. And if you want an incredible system, this is a DGX Station for Windows. It is compatible with Windows, runs everything in Windows, and it has 768 gigabytes of memory, so you could run a trillion-parameter model. This is unbelievable: 20 petaflops, 8 terabytes per second of memory bandwidth, and this sits by your desk. Basically, if you are a developer of large language models or a developer of agents, having this sit by your desk gives you all the compute you need, and then when you deploy it, you put it into the cloud. Now, there is something that if you look at this and think about it, you realize something is happening here. Remember, 15 or 20 years ago, we used to have an idea called a phone. Today, we have an idea called a PC. Today, when you think about your phone, the one thing you don't do with it is make phone calls. You do just about everything else, and so that phone means something very different to you than a phone of the past. I am certain what's going to happen here is that the PC 10 years from now and the PC that you think about today—a tool where you launch applications, click, and type—are going to be completely different. Here's my theory: I can totally imagine, just as every house today has a home theater, or many houses have home theaters, big TVs, lawnmowers, or dishwashers, that someday there will actually be an AI supercomputer in your house. It will be running all of your agents and all of your assistants, and they'll be doing all kinds of things for you all the time. You will have to have it in your house, just like you have a home theater, stereos, or game consoles in your house. You would have assistant AI agent computers running in your home. These, in time, will become a lot more like R2-D2 or C-3PO to you than they feel like a PC. There is no question this reinvention of the computer is as big of a deal as the reinvention of the phone into what we now know as the smartphone. And so this is the beginning of that journey. This is the beginning of a new line, and we have a roadmap for this. This is a brand-new product family for us. For every single generation of architecture, we will have a desktop, a laptop, and a workstation. The thing that I am just incredibly pleased and incredibly honored by is that 100% of the world's PC industry has joined us to reinvent the PC. This is a new line, a new beginning. Thank you.

'Will You Commit To Following The Law?': Chris Coons Presses Rubio About 'Scaring' NATO Allies

Forbes Breaking News(5.6M)·10:17·7.5K views·2026-06-04
Chris CoonsUS Senator (D-DE) Marco RubioSecretary of State
TL;DRDuring a Senate Appropriations Committee hearing, Sen.
Chris Coons pressed Secretary of State Marco Rubio on the administration's compliance with statutory U.S. troop minimums in Europe, while seeking updates on the transition timeline in Venezuela, global health...

TL;DR

During a Senate Appropriations Committee hearing, Sen. Chris Coons pressed Secretary of State Marco Rubio on the administration's compliance with statutory U.S. troop minimums in Europe, while seeking updates on the transition timeline in Venezuela, global health compacts, and backlogged Afghan Special Immigrant Visas (SIVs). Rubio defended the administration's strategic redeployment of global military forces and outlined a three-phase transition plan for Venezuela, while committing to resolve delayed national security board appointments.

***

NATO Commitments and European Troop Posture

  • Statutory Troop Minimums: Sen. Coons questioned whether the administration would comply with a provision in the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) prohibiting U.S. troop levels in Europe from dropping below 76,000.
      • Coons highlighted abrupt troop movements, including the withdrawal of 5,000 troops from Germany, the temporary cancellation of a 4,000-troop rotational brigade to Poland, and a lack of replacement plans for 1,000 troops rotating out of Lithuania.
  • Allied Anxiety vs. Global Balancing: Coons reported that Baltic and European parliamentarians expressed deep anxiety and felt "stunned" by unexpected U.S. troop departures.
      • Rubio's Response: Rubio emphasized that troop posture is a Department of Defense responsibility but defended the actions, stating the German troop changes simply returned forces to 2022 baselines.
      • Global Resource Limits: Rubio argued the Pentagon must constantly re-evaluate and balance limited military resources across high-demand regions, including the Indo-Pacific (South Korea/Japan), Africa, and the Middle East.

Venezuelan Democratic Transition Strategy

  • Three-Phase Plan: In response to Coons' inquiries regarding concrete timelines for Venezuelan elections and the release of 400 political prisoners, Rubio outlined a phased transition following the capture of Nicolás Maduro five months prior.
      • *Phase 1: Stabilization:* Aimed at preventing civil war and mass migration, which Rubio stated has been largely achieved.
      • *Phase 2: Recovery:* Building the necessary institutions to support free elections, such as establishing an independent judiciary and Electoral Council.
      • *Phase 3: Democratic Elections:* Allowing independent media to operate, restoring political parties, and facilitating travel (via direct flights) for political organizing.
  • Prioritizing People Over Resources: Coons urged the administration to remain committed to democratic transition rather than "putting extraction and oil and gold and emeralds first and the people of Venezuela second."

Global Health Compacts and SIV Backlogs

  • Kenyan Ebola Facility Controversy: Coons raised concerns about local alarm in Kenya regarding an Ebola facility associated with one of the 32 U.S. global health compacts.
      • Clarification of Protocol: Rubio clarified that the facility is strictly for *observing* potentially exposed Americans. If any American tests positive, they will immediately be evacuated from Kenya to the U.S. or Europe for treatment, ensuring Kenya's domestic healthcare system is not burdened.
  • SIV Backlogs in Pakistan and Qatar: Coons urged Rubio to collaborate with the Secretary of Homeland Security to use Executive Order authority to expedite case-by-case admissions for thousands of cleared Afghan SIV applicants currently stranded in Pakistan, Qatar, and Afghanistan.

Board Vacancies and Counterterrorism Funding

  • US Foundation for National Security Delay: Coons criticized a 1.5-year delay in appointing board members to the US Foundation for National Security and Counterterrorism, noting that private-sector donors are starting to lose confidence.
      • Commitment to Expedite: Rubio acknowledged receiving bipartisan pressure on this issue and pledged to work with the White House to fast-track the vetting and appointment of the board nominees.

***

Notable Quotes

"Can we stop scaring our NATO allies and sustain a regular troop presence in compliance with law?" — Sen. Chris Coons
"The Pentagon and the Department of War have to constantly take extraordinary, but still limited resources and allocate them around the world... None of [these alignments] is being done as a shock or surprise to our allies in NATO." — Secretary of State Marco Rubio
"I just hope that we will move past this current interim period and towards democracy and free and fair elections... and not leave the unfortunate image of putting extraction and oil and gold and emeralds first and the people of Venezuela second." — Sen. Chris Coons
Thank you, Chairman Graham, and thank you, Secretary. I'm just going to continue this. One of many things that Senator Graham, Senator Boozman, Senator Moran, and I have worked on over many years is the US Foundation for National Security and Counterterrorism, or to put it simply, "good for animals, bad for terrorists." That's Lindsey's description of it. A year and a half ago in your confirmation hearing, I asked if we could count on you to be helpful in swiftly implementing it, and you did sign the incorporation. But we've been waiting for a year and a half for the board. Can we move this quickly so you can stop being asked questions in all four? Yes. No, we want to, and we will. I'll go back to personnel because they vet every nominee for every position, and I think there was an early list that somehow fell off. There was an early list, and then there was a second list, and we have been waiting for months since then. There is significant private sector support for this initiative that is waiting and beginning to lose confidence because of just how long it's taking. This is a very small piece of everything I want to address with you, but it'd be great. No, I get the message. I've heard it consistently now for four committee hearings from Republicans, and everybody that works for me that's here is hearing the same thing, so I got it. We gotta get this thing moved forward, and I'm gonna do everything in, you know, obviously in my role at the White House to make sure that we can expedite these appointments. Thank you, and I just want to associate myself with Senator Collins' comments about RUT-F and the bipartisan comments about GAVI and the urgency of releasing those funds, if I can be helpful in any way in facilitating resolving that. I know that's an HHS issue, but at the end of the day, it's in your hands. I'd love to talk to you briefly about NATO and troop posture. I asked you yesterday about the importance of Article 5 and NATO being one of our most central security commitments. There have been some really striking changes. I met with a whole series of parliamentarians from a number of Eastern and Northern European countries yesterday. On May 1, President Trump announced the abrupt withdrawal of 5,000 troops from Germany. On May 14, he announced the cancellation of a rotational brigade of 4,000 to Poland. On May 19, the president then reportedly reversed that decision about the Poland deployment, but we don't have any further details. And I just learned yesterday about there being no plan to replace the thousand troops in Lithuania who just finished one of those heel-to-toe deployments. As you may know, four Americans were killed in Lithuania in a training accident. I was at Dover Air Force Base, along with senior leaders in the administration and a bipartisan group of members of Congress, to receive their remains and was really stunned. The people of Lithuania formed a human chain from where they left to the airport, miles and miles, to celebrate their service. We have great and close allies in NATO, but because of some comments that have been made by the president and others about Greenland and Denmark, and because of some of these troop rotations, they really lack confidence in us. As you know, there's a provision in last year's NDAA that prohibits the administration from lowering the number of troops below 76,000, and with these latest troop realignments we're getting dangerously close to that. Will you commit to following the law and sustaining US troop levels in Europe at a minimum of the statutorily required? And do you understand how comments about Spain not pulling its weight really don't meet the reality that the overwhelming majority of our NATO allies—largely because of bipartisan and sustained pressure on them—have significantly stepped up, as you just said a few minutes ago? They're funding all of the weaponry going into Ukraine now. Can we stop scaring our NATO allies and sustain a regular troop presence in compliance with law? Okay, so a couple of points. You know that on the issue of troop deployments and numbers around the world, that is not a Department of State function. That is a Department of War. Yes, but I'm the leading Democrat on defense approps and I got nothing out of Secretary Hegseth. Okay, so but—so but—so but I can speak to a few of these in my other role, and I get to see some of this. I'm not here to testify as national security advisor; I was told by the lawyers to say that. All right. So, but I can speak to it because I'm aware of it. A couple of points. On some of these rotations that have occurred, like, for example, the one in Germany, I know it got a lot of headlines, but frankly the Germans themselves were not that exercised about it. They are aware that all it did is take us back to 2022 numbers. So they knew this was not a surprise to them. This was ongoing and it was planned. What was a surprise is that it hit the media before they had been informed of it formally, but they were aware of that up front. In the case of Poland, as you rightly pointed out, there was this short-term rotational withdrawal, but the president wanted to make it clear that our relations with Poland are very solid and therefore made the announcement the following day as I was arriving at the foreign ministers' meeting of NATO. What I will tell you, and I can say—but I can't speak with specificity because it's not our department—is we are constantly re-evaluating our force posture all over the world. Just as NATO wants us to have X number of troops in Europe, the Indo-Pacific wants to make sure we're not going to leave South Korea and we're not going to leave Japan. And our partners in Africa now, for example, in Nigeria and other places, want to know that we're committed to the counterterrorism mission. And our allies in the Middle East want to know that now we're not going to leave them because some base got hit by a drone. And in the Western Hemisphere, we have growing cooperation and a desire. So the Pentagon and the Department of War have to constantly take extraordinary, but still limited resources and allocate them around the world. I can't speak in detail to those alignments. What I can tell you is that everything we are doing—none of it is being done as a shock or surprise to our allies in NATO. They are informed every step of the way. Let me, I have several other questions to get to. I'll simply share with you that a defense minister and several senior parliamentarians from several NATO allies said to me yesterday they were stunned and unaware of the impending departure of a unit in Lithuania without a follow-on unit, and of the decisions and announcements about Poland. Let's just agree that NATO's critical and we're going to work harder to make sure they understand. I also want to associate myself with the comments about pressure on Russia, finally getting to "yes" on this sanctions bill. And this is me and Secretary Hegseth delivering the interceptors that Zelenskyy is begging us for would put that pressure on. Let me ask you two more things about Venezuela and about the global health compacts and the SIVs, and I'll be quick. I was encouraged to hear you say that we need to make progress towards elections in Venezuela. I couldn't agree more. I recently joined a group, a bipartisan group of senators, in meeting with Maria Machado. Can you give us any sense of a concrete timeline or plan for when some of the 400 political prisoners will be released, for when there will be reforms to the Supreme Tribunal of Justice, the Electoral Council, and when we can expect elections? Well, all of those are topics we discussed with Venezuela every single day. As you know, and I outlined this when I appeared before the Foreign Relations Committee back in February after that operation, it's a three-phase process. Stabilization—we didn't want to see civil war, we didn't want to see mass migration—I think that's largely been achieved, with a little bit more work to be done there. Recovery: and part of that recovery is building the institutions that make it possible to have an election. It's not just the Electoral Council, which is critical, but it's not just that. You have to have independent media that can operate, and while we've seen signs that that's happening, that needs to be sustained. Political parties and individuals involved in them need to be able at some point either to return, or those who are already in the country have to be able to organize. They have to be able to open offices, field candidates, and start to do all the work that it takes to create the conditions for an election. That's critically important as well, and that one we're following. It's one of the reasons why these direct flights into Venezuela were so important, because now people can go and interact directly even if they're not yet living full-time in Venezuela. But we have more work to do. We raise this—I just remind everybody this only, Maduro was grabbed five months ago, not five years ago, so we've come a long way in five months. Yep. I just, to close on this, Mr. Secretary, I know you are passionate about democracy. I just hope that we will move past this current interim period and towards democracy and free and fair elections, and achieve the possibilities of Venezuela, and not leave the unfortunate image of putting extraction and oil and gold and emeralds first and the people of Venezuela second. And I'd love to work with you on that. Two other points quickly. The 32 global health compacts that you referenced, we have no implementation plans, and seeing the details of how they're being implemented would be very helpful. As you know, I've long been close to the Kenyan government and the Kenyan people. We're asking them to take on Ebola treatment, which is getting a very rough reception. There is also real tension in some of the data details around these health impacts. And there is evidence, there's data from State, that there's less treatment, less testing, and less prevention overall, and I need to better understand the role of the CDC and how these health compacts are going to actually achieve your laudable goal of more aid to more people faster. Can we work on these details? Yeah, of course. But on the implementation, remember, you reach a compact and now the implementation is part of the compact you have to work on in collaboration with the host government. So as those plans become available, as those plans become finalized, you'll have insight into those, and you'll be able to see that. In the case, just to correct one point, we're not actually asking Kenya to set up treatment for Americans. I think the one that's been very controversial is a misunderstanding. There is a facility that the Kenyans are allowing us to open; if there are any Americans that are exposed, potentially exposed, they will be transferred to this facility for observation. If they test positive at any time while in that facility, we will remove them from Kenya and send them to the nearest treatment facility either in Europe, or in the home, or in the United States to be treated for Ebola. But what's been controversial is no one wants to see that people are coming into their town. There's never been a case of Ebola in Kenya. They don't have the domestic capacity to deal with it, and there is alarm that I would love to work with you to address. I'll follow up with you about your offer yesterday to work on SIVs. We have thousands still stuck in Pakistan who were cleared, who have chief of mission clearance, and you have the ability under the EO, in partnership with the Homeland Security Secretary, to allow those who were vetted to come to the United States on a case-by-case basis. On a case-by-case basis, and I urge you to use that to get folks out of Afghanistan and Pakistan, as well as Qatar, to safe countries including the United States. Thank you, Mr. Secretary. Thank you.

Build 2026 Podcast: Satya Nadella, Sarah Guo and Elad Gil from No Priors, and Swyx from Latent Space

Microsoft(2.2M)·41:05·3.5K views·2026-06-04
Satya NadellaCEO, Microsoft Sarah GuoCo-host, No Priors Elad GilCo-host, No Priors
TL;DRAI value is shifting from raw foundation models to an ecosystem-driven "harness" layer where enterprises use private evaluations and custom agentic workflows to hill-climb performance.
To capture compounding value, organizations must transition from manual execution to...

TL;DR

AI value is shifting from raw foundation models to an ecosystem-driven "harness" layer where enterprises use private evaluations and custom agentic workflows to hill-climb performance. To capture compounding value, organizations must transition from manual execution to "meta-work"—building autonomous systems that capture institutional tacit knowledge directly onto the corporate balance sheet.

## The Platform Shift: From Foundation Models to "Harnesses"

  • Defining the Platform: True platforms must create more economic value *above* the platform layer than they capture within it. Microsoft's primary objective is to enable all companies—from AI-native startups to legacy enterprises—to operate at the cognitive frontier with their own data and "frontier intelligence."
  • The Rise of the Harness: The core engineering challenge has shifted from model training to building the "harness"—the integrated loop linking models, context, and developer tools.
      • Open and Multi-Model Harnessing: Microsoft encourages open harnesses (such as supporting Meta's Llama alongside proprietary models) to give developers architectural flexibility.
      • The Land O'Lakes Playbook: A key design pattern involves using a frontier model (like GPT-4) to collect process traces, and then utilizing a smaller reasoning model (like Phi-3) to execute the task at a higher performance-to-cost ratio.
  • Private Evals as Definitive IP: Standard public benchmarks are easily maxed out and do not reflect real-world business utility. A company's private evaluation suite is its most critical intellectual property.
      • Model Portability: If an enterprise can swap Model A for Model B using their private evals and continue to "hill climb" performance, they retain strategic control. If they cannot swap models, they are locked into a vendor.
      • Clean Lineage Models: Microsoft's MAI models focus heavily on pre-training data quality and rigorous ablations to avoid the common pitfall of open-weight models that benchmark well but fail in production.

## Unbundling SaaS and the "Balance Sheet" AI Agent

  • Deconstructing the SaaS Stack: Traditional SaaS applications bundle a data model, business logic, and UI. AI is unbundling this vertical stack. While UI will change completely, foundational data schemas (like a general ledger) and business logic (like Power BI semantic models) remain highly valuable databases for feeding agents.
  • M365 and Work IQ as an Enterprise Database: Microsoft 365 is transitioning from a suite of captive applications into an accessible enterprise database.
      • Cross-Domain Workflows: Through Work IQ, an agent can analyze Microsoft Teams design meeting transcripts from the prior week and automatically generate a plan to modify a codebase inside a GitHub repository.
  • Putting Tacit Knowledge on the Balance Sheet: Human capital and institutional experience have historically been impossible to list as physical assets. By capturing the traces of long-running agentic workflows, companies can build "veteran agents" that preserve tacit knowledge. This operational capability will eventually be recognized as balance sheet value.
  • Business Model Adaptations: While per-user subscriptions will persist due to corporate budget predictability, consumption-based pricing is growing to accommodate high-intensity, background agent workloads. GitHub, for instance, adjusted its pricing models to account for the heavy token usage of autonomous agent sessions compared to simple interactive autocomplete.

## Meta-Work, Generalist Leverage, and the Future of Engineering

  • The Pivot to Meta-Work: High organizational ambition in the AI era requires shifting human roles from performing a task to building the agentic system that executes it.
      • Azure's "Miles" System: To handle massive scaling—building more Azure capacity in 15 months than in the prior 15 years—the Azure networking team built an agentic system named *Miles* to manage physical wide-area network (WAN) fiber operations. The team successfully scaled operations by requesting "tokens over headcount."
  • Consolidation of Engineering Roles: Engineering is consolidating around high-leverage disciplines.
      • Full-Stack Builders: Teams at LinkedIn are merging product management, design, and front-end engineering into unified "Full-Stack Builder" roles.
      • Specialized Infrastructure: Demand is rising for distributed systems engineers to build reward systems (RLHF/RLAIF) for specialized applications, such as Microsoft Excel's underlying logic.
  • Satya's Chief of Staff Agent: Highlighting the democratization of software building, Satya Nadella built a personal "Chief of Staff" Autopilot. He utilized Work IQ to create a long-running Foundry agent, stored its memory in Rayfin (backend-as-a-service), and successfully published the functional application to Microsoft Teams.

## Hyper-Scale Infrastructure and Earning "Community Permission"

  • Redefining the Hyperscaler: The sheer scale of data center buildouts is unprecedented, fundamentally shifting the capital requirements and physical footprint of technology providers.
  • Earning Community Permission: Tech companies cannot rely on "trust us" narratives regarding AI's future benefits. They must deliver immediate, tangible value to the local communities hosting data centers.
      • Resource and Infrastructure Co-Investment: Data centers must actively improve the local energy grid, replenish local water supplies through closed-loop systems, and expand the local tax base via construction and long-term operational jobs.
  • A New Pedagogy for Education: AI's ability to lower the friction of information retrieval demands a redesign of educational credentials and continuous learning. Nadella suggests that the next generation of breakout startups could include entirely new universities built around AI-driven training and economic opportunity placement.

## Notable Quotes

"A platform is defined by, fundamentally, its ability to create more value above the platform versus what's captured in the platform." — Satya Nadella
"Every company having private evals may be the biggest IP... If you have an eval that's private, you're using a model A, can you switch it to model B and climb up? If you can, then you're in control. If you can't, you're not in control." — Satya Nadella
"Our job is not to do Azure networking, our job is to build the agentic system that does Azure networking... They basically took their work and made it meta." — Satya Nadella
"The world is going to be way skeptical of tech and tech companies that say, 'Trust us, we've got it. The future is going to be glorious.' You kind of have to deliver tangible benefits." — Satya Nadella
Please welcome Swyx, Sarah Guo, Elad Gil, and Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Microsoft, Satya Nadella. Hello, Swyx. I'm so excited to be here. Welcome to a crossover episode of No Priors and Latent Space with Satya Nadella. Congratulations on an amazing Build. No, thank you so much and it's great to be with both of you. I listen to both of you or both the podcasts all the time. It's great to be on it. Thank you so much. Thank you so much. So, you've been talking about these amazing announcements from across the Microsoft estate all morning for, I think, three hours. What is the, what's the most important reflection or takeaway you have? I, I'd say there are, perhaps the, the biggest one for me is, let's sort of conceptualize this more as an ecosystem play as opposed to a single model or even a single platform, right? I mean, you know, whatever, at least for me having grown up at Microsoft, having seen whatever four major platform shifts, I sort of fall into that camp where a platform is defined by, fundamentally, its ability to create more value above the platform versus what's captured in the platform. And so if you view what's happening right now, I think this morning's keynote was: how can any company, whether it's an AI native company or a traditional enterprise company, participate as a first-class participant, where they can point to AI they create, right? It's not that they don't use other people's AI—of course they will—but to me, what's the path, what's the recipe, how do I do it, what does the stack look like, what does the tooling look like, what is valuable, how do you do that. That's it. That's sort of our job to do. Yeah, ecosystem strategy is very complicated, right, because you end up building certain components, partnering for certain components, and supporting them. You just announced this big suite of models. Like, tell us a little bit about the training strategy for Microsoft. Yeah, so, so the thing that we wanted to do with the MAI models was to build—and as Mustafa talked about—first of all, a great lineage, right, starting with pre-training, with very good data quality, doing all the ablations. This is making sure, because in, in some sense, it's becoming even harder to build a clean lineage model, yes, because there's so much stuff out there that you truly need to ablate out to be able to have a fantastic pre-trained model. In fact, that's one of the challenges of a lot of the open weight models is, they look great on one benchmark or two, but they're not great in practice. So, that's why, in fact, even in our FDEs are, are, are pretty gone really excited about these MAI models because how the heck can a small Phi-3 model hill climb? And it goes back a little bit to what I think is ultimately the key thing to do, which is try to pursue finding that cognitive core. So to me, starting with a clean lineage, then creating that ability for companies to be able to use this, right—not just as a generalist, but to create their own specialist by building this hill climbing scaffold around it, right? So, it's not just the model, but you have a hill climb scaffold around it. Then, you will start building your RLE. You will start collecting the traces. Most importantly, you will have private evals, because we know all the evals out there are good, interesting, but they're not really, at this point, because they all can be maxed. And so the point is each company will have its own private eval. And so that end-to-end platform story around our models is sort of what I think is interesting. And then the one other thing, Sarah, since you brought that up is, I do feel there's a new frontier, like people talk about the frontier, and are you operating at the frontier. Interestingly enough, if you add a little temporality to it, you can use, let's say, in, in, in fact, the, the Land O'Lakes demo we showed was pretty cool. We used, whatever, GPT-4, right? Then you collected a bunch of traces, and then you took a Phi-3 reasoning model and achieved higher. So that is another aspect of what it means to appear, I mean, operate at the frontier. Yeah, I, I think, I, first of all, have to congratulate you on basically building a frontier neo lab inside of Microsoft in two years. I'm wondering, you know, you have all this AI strategy that you're rolling out. Well, I'm wondering what do you know now that you wish you would tell yourself two years ago, or two to three years ago—three years for the Genson partnership, two years for MAI? Yeah, I mean, I think the, the thing when, that I reflect quite a bit, right, which is sort of obviously I got into all this when I got excited by the, the scaling laws paper and, you know, when, you know, even the OpenAI partnership came about when those folks said, "Hey, we're going to really throw a lot of compute at transformers," and they've held, right? The thing that I always look back and say, "Wow, these things do have capability that they're climbing up." We, I mean, this, you know, this crude way of saying it is, intelligence log of compute kind of works. Now, what I think we underestimated perhaps is the real-world complexity of deploying these so that they actually deliver the value in the real world, right? So the outcomes as measured by any benchmark is interesting, important, but the true eval is when people out there are able to do unique things that they only can value, and it's very measurable, right? That I wish we had sort of even, like, had more in our consciousness, right, which is as an industry, because right now, I think when people say, "Wow, I don't want a token max," it's an artifact of us not having thought of ourselves as an industry that we are using tokens to create value every step of the way. So, I think that's kind of what I wish we had gotten there, but I'm glad we are here. What are some of the use cases that you've seen that have created the most value for your customers? Because I know that people talk a lot about code, and I think it's pretty clear that that's something that's having very large-scale impact. Are there other areas that you find in common that your customers are really benefiting from? Yeah, I think, yeah, to your point, obviously coding is now caught, but it's interesting, by the way, Elad, is to even talk about the coding, right, which is coding has worked so well, that we now have to rebuild the IDE, right? I mean, it's kind of nuts to see what we've launched, is like, oh my god, I have these 100 agent sessions, the cognitive load it transfers back to me as a human is so excessive that now I need a new UI. Oh, by the way, like, the, the chat as the only artifact is also impossible, so that's why we need a canvas. So, it's kind of interesting for all the things about where is software needed or where is UI needed. You kind of need that even for code, right, in a fully agentic world. But that said, one of the things that we are starting to see, we started seeing with Copilot, but even some of the work we showed with auto—Autopilot, right, on what you see with Copilot, is a good one because if you sort of think about a lot of human capital is doing the glue work, right? If you now can augment that with tokens/agents that are long-running, durable, right, then your ability to scale even what is still judgment and glue work gets amplified like coding does. So you can, like, I'm positive that six months from now we'll all be saying, "Oh, wow, like, all through the, the night, there was a bunch of stuff that all these Autopilots that I have working on my behalf with my delegated authority, so to speak, right, I can sort of given even my identity, did a bunch of work. Then, of course, I'll need my new IDE to say, what did you do, like, did I do this work and so on." So, I think that's where compressing of workflows, completing of tasks, that's where I think a lot of the value gets created. I think you raise a really interesting point which is, there's the actual agent that's doing the code and then there's a harness around it, and that's the environment, that's the context, that's everything you're setting up as a developer around actually a coding agent. What is the harness for the enterprise? Is there an equivalent concept for broader productivity work or how do you think about that concept sort of... That's right, so, so in some sense, you kind of want the harness to define the models, the, the data, and the tools, so that you have a loop across those three. And so, what we're trying to first of all make sure is each of our products that we build, right, whether it's GitHub Copilot or the Security Copilot, the stuff we showed with mDash, or even the discovery for science, it doesn't matter. All of them are multi-model harnesses, with tools access so that you can do this progressive, disclosure of tools even so that they're token efficient, and then you're feeding it with very rich context, because that's sort of the other hard lesson we've learned in the last two years is, oh my god, the amount of work you need to do to prep the context layer, such that your plan can execute in the most efficient way is where the magic is. So we have, in our case, we have the GitHub harness, which essentially we're using across all our products. It's available in Foundry. And we're open, like, you can use your Llama harness, whatever, or you can use the, you know, any open harness or any harness of yours and train with your tools and multiple models and your context. And so that's the pitch, because right now, a lot of dialogue is, "Hey, if I train the harness plus tools and the model together, you get evals." And what we are proving out is—and the best example of that is what we did with mDash, right, because when it launched, it found bugs or vulnerabilities that were not found by Mythos. And so there is existence proof, I would claim, that you can have a multi-model harness, that can, in fact, be more, performant in the real world. The premise behind the, training at the independent frontier labs is really, you know, we're going to have these models and we'll have an API business, we'll support enterprises and startups, but a first-party product, be it productivity or code or search, drives the majority of revenue. That's a different value equation than you're describing, I think, with the Microsoft ecosystem. If, if that's the case, tell me if it's the case, because obviously you have first-party products and you have enablement products, what is the role of the develop—like, what is going to be hard and the set of skills and the value capture the developer has in that world? Yeah, so I think that there's always going to be the case that someone who is super successful in as a platform builder can also have first-party products. It was true with Windows, it was true, with, the, the SaaS side and the cloud side as well with us and others and so on. But the thing that is is it should not be a limiter to other people achieving that same success, right? That I think is the core difference, which is the, the network effects this time around around intelligence are such because they learn from data, and not really lots of data, it's just the few samples that you have to see to understand what's novel about something. So, that's why the game becomes how to protect. So, that's why I would say every company, having private evals may be the biggest IP, right? I think about it, like, what's that private eval that you can then use even a frontier model to hill climb on and not leak the traces may be one of the biggest drivers, of IP. Like, so, in other words, another test is: you have an eval that's private, you're using a, a, a model A, can you switch it to model B and, you know, climb up? If you can, then you're in control. If you can't, you're not in control. And that's where even the harness decision becomes super important, right? So, therefore, having an open harness, letting all models come in, having your evals, your context, your tools help you hill climb, I think is the skills that an AI native startup needs, a SaaS company needs, or every enterprise needs. Yeah, I think in a very real way, you are, Microsoft historically has been an operating systems company and then become a cloud company. Maybe, like, the third act is that you're a harness or evals company, whatever, whatever the sort of conglomerate of concepts that you want to put together. I, I think like enabling every company to have like frontier intelligence or, what, I forget the exact term that you used, is the is the mission, right? Like, that that is that is the platform promise that you build with us, you will get your intelligence, for your data. That's it. That to me, that is the like, if there was one tagline, for this entire developer conference is, can everybody operate at the frontier with their frontier intelligence, right? To me, that is so important because otherwise it I don't know how you achieve stable equilibrium, right, which is how do I then go and say, "Oh, my company is going to have a terminal value because I now know how to continuously compound on top of what's a platform that gets better," right? So when like Windows obviously came out, Adobe built, Autodesk built, or even like take what Genson said, "We built DX and he built, you know, CUDA on top of it," right? I mean, I always say to Genson, "God, I got the short end of that, right? I wish we had recognized it." But nevertheless, but that idea that you can build a platform layer that someone else can then extend out, and build their own intelligence layer, in this case, I think is everything, right? Without it, why have a developer conference? I can just come and have you all sort of just worship at the altar of one model. That's not a developer conference. Backstage, we we had a discussion about what is IP or what is the value in the company. It used to be the length of human experience in the company, and now it's this other thing which is the evals, the experience in sort of applying agents to the company. Can you, I just want you to like flesh that out a bit more because I think it's really insightful. Yeah, it's a great way to frame it, right, because at the end of the day, every company is going to have both the human capital that is still going to be super valuable, because humans, and their ability to find the gaps that exist at all times is going to be the way we all will create value, right? I mean, so I'm definitely in the camp that this is going to be about expressing new forms of human agency and ambition, even as token capital goes up, right? So, let's say a any corporation has lots of tokens and a lot of human capital. The question is, how do you compound the two? So if you have a like, if you take in Teams, I have a bunch of agents doing work and a bunch of humans doing work and the traces between those, that is really important context of how that enterprise is creating value. Then, that goes back to train not a generalist model, but to train the the company veteran agent, right? That is super valuable. Again, right, which is when a company says, "It should, in fact, go onto the balance sheet," is how I think about it, right? That's so In fact, there may be like human capital was never possible to go put on a balance sheet, because you then know how to capture the tacit knowledge, whereas now I think you can with the agents that have learned through the through time, through all the traces. So that's what at least we think will happen. And then the SEC is going to have to have accounting standards for token, expertise. You're talking about the equilibrium state and a stable equilibrium where companies have this compounding value and can see terminal value for themselves. Another challenge to, you know, the considered equilibrium of okay, there are applications and workflows that are sort of common to a vertical or horizontal, and this was like the generation of SaaS companies and, you know, Microsoft has lots of SaaS properties as well, and then there are things that are very specific to every enterprise that they're differentiated against. I'm sure you have heard much and participated in much of the debate about the end of software because all these workflows are are cheap to generate now. Do you think the equilibrium looks different between what agents get built in enterprises versus in their vendors in the future? Yeah, so I think what's happening there is see we we had a particular way we captured I'd say workflow in apps, right, because we built a data model, right, we schematized some part of some business process. We then built a bunch of business logic, and then we put a bunch of UI on top of it, right? So, that's kind of what every SaaS company... And then a little configuration. For like 20 years, that was it. Right, that and that was it. So, interestingly enough, now you kind of get to re-litigate that vertical stacking, right? So, I still think, for example, that data model that you build underneath every SaaS application is super good, right? It's like, why reinvent it? Like, I, my general ledger better be a general ledger. I don't need new schema creation, in fact, that entity relationship, is actually pretty good, robust thing that I want to feed. And you want it to be stable. That's right. Then, same thing with business logic. Right, if you look at, we have this product called Power BI, right? It was like dashboards galore people created. The beauty underneath that dashboard is a very rich semantic model, right? Someone took the pain to create a dashboard and do all the measures. And you want that, that's business logic, right? I want that to be available to me. So, I think the challenge of the SaaS business model is we packaged one way, we now have to learn how to unbundle these things and re-bundle in new ways and discover new business models, right? I mean, if you look at what's happening today with Microsoft 365 is a great example. Right, we have this thing called Work IQ. In fact, like, what we're realizing is, oh my god, like, you know, if I look In fact, there's a historical parallel to it, right? We sold first Exchange and SharePoint, and, you know, before Teams, we had a thing called Link Server and what have you. And we thought, "Oh, that's all going to move to the cloud," but little did we realize that oh, the number of people who will use servers in the cloud is 10x, 100x, right, because people are not buying servers, they were just buying a subscription. Yeah. The same thing is now happening with M365 because with Work IQ, we've exposed what is perhaps the most important database in a company that never got used as a database because it was only captive to our apps, right? It was oh, email operated on it, Teams operated on it, Word, Excel, PowerPoint, SharePoint. But now, like, this is one of the coolest things I get to do with Work IQ. I go to a GitHub repo, and I say, "Hey, I attended a bunch of design meetings last week related to this repo. Can you capture all that and tell me what changes I should make?" I mean, think about that, right? It literally can go look at all those transcripts, come back with a plan to change your codebase. Right, previously, you could never have thought of using M365 for something like that. So, the value creation opportunity now in the agent world is, in fact, 10x more. But it does require us to have, for example, there's going to be usage around M365, right, which is going to be more than even the the end user, so and we have to even re-architect. Like, in fact, like, what I use to serve an inbox or a mailbox cannot be used to serve an agent. And so that's sort of what we're doing. I don't believe in like permanent business models for any of these domains, but in the near term, do you have a prediction between, you know, outcomes-based pricing, token-based pricing, enterprise bundles? Yeah, the way I think about this is always we've had, like, let's even take the per user pricing. Mhm. The per user pricing is really an artifact of some one creating a budget, needing certainty, right, because it's the most important thing like somebody wants a budget, they need a per user, and and per user is just a set of entitlements to usage, right? That's kind of what it is. And so the way is, the first bundling will be, take some usage, bundle it into per user stacks and, you know, then sell subscriptions. So, subscriptions, I think, are going to be there, per user is going to be there. Then, the next big thing will be consumption. So, people will say, "I want consumption." And it's also possible that people will say, "I don't even want to pay for any of the subscriptions of the consumption, it's outcome." But remember, most people love outcomes until they have an outcome, because once you have an outcome, it's like giving away royalty, right? I mean, like, I've talked to customers who love, you know, outcome-based pricing, and I say, "I'm all in," until they, "Oh my god, like, what are you talking about? You're sharing in my outcome? No, no, no. I want you to go back to per user pricing, and I want you to consumption price," right? So, I think that debate will go on, and all all of these business models have a particular time and a place, versus one to rule them all. And if anything, if you're a SaaS vendor or you're a platform vendor, having that flexibility, and quite frankly, we faced this with GitHub, right? We just recently announced a per user pricing on GitHub because little, you know, GitHub Copilot was constructed at a per user level before we understood even, the intensity of usage of agents, right? It was an interactive way for a developer to use code complete, maybe tasks. It was not like, "Oh, I launch 10,000, you know, agents that are going on all day," right? So, that is what the adjustment is about. So, now that we really want, there will always be a per user, but they will have to be a consumption meter. How do you think about the durability of SaaS more generally? One thing I've observed is in a lot of enterprises internally there will be teams that almost have agent euphoria. They're so excited about the explosion of things they can build, but they're trying to rebuild a lot of applications or going to their SaaS vendors and saying, "We're not going to work with you anymore, we're considering an internal project." And it seems like in six to nine months, maybe some of those people will come back and say, "Actually, we, we can't rebuild everything." How do you think about what's durable in this world and what isn't? I think we have to go through one full budget cycle on this to really see the, the sort of the emergence of the equilibrium, because at the end of the day, there's marginal cost to even generating the app, right? In fact, there can be even a simple way to say it, like, if you should always acquire something if the marginal cost of building and maintaining, something on your own is higher, right? That should be like, it's a quantifiable, right, a quantifiable thing. And and the maintenance part is important, right? Even like you got to remember, like, "Hey, you know, all the security stuff that now AI will find, you better fix them, too, fast." Of course, there's a coding agent to help you with, but then that burns tokens, right? So, whose responsibility is it? It's kind of like a a cycle that you've got to think through. And I think we've gone through the excitement that I can generate a lot of software. I think the next thing would be, what software do I really want to generate, what software do I want to use from others, how do I compose these two into some agentic workflow that I have agency over, right? Because I think there'll be very little tolerance for any body who's inflexible, at the vendor level, but at the same time, I think that anyone who is got that flexibility shows up, delivers the value, will be back at again, right, with selling software, but with just different business models, in fact. Speaking about building software, one of my favorite moments from I think a previous Build, maybe one or two years ago, was you had a pic—they had a pic—there was a section of you building your own software. I'm curious if you're building anything now. Yeah, so I think the, you know, first of all, let's face it, right? Building software has made it possible for even the incompetence of a CEO of a company like ours, you can build. So, thank God. But that said, I I I do feel that, you know, something like, GitHub Copilot to me and especially the new sessions app or the new app, has just made it so much more possible for you to have agency over artifacts that you felt you couldn't touch before, right? So, for me as a CEO, even to go to a codebase, to be able to learn about it, like, I remember joining Microsoft long back, you know, first and then you say, "Man, everyone had to go in and look at, you know, whatever Cutler's malloc or what have you to learn how to do good C, C++ code." So now that ability to be more full stack up and down is so good. But that doesn't mean every one of us should be doing the same thing. The question is, how do you then have the ability to inspect things, learn things, see things, I think is just so much more. And so to me, what I'm building a lot of is these long-running Foundry agents. Right, so, there's Autopilots. So, the easiest thing is to me, I think I just built one, even last week where the idea was, "Hey, can I have an agent that is continuously monitoring essentially my own Chief of Staff Autopilot, right?" We're going to have that obviously in Scout, that's what, we showed. But it was so easy and trivial to build. I took Work IQ, I said, "Take Work IQ, go, and build a Foundry long-running agent, store all the memory in, using Rayfin, right? Basically, my backend as a service." And lo and behold, it built it, and not only built it, I could say, "Publish to Teams," and it published the damn thing to Teams. So, the ability, to have a, you know, some end-to-end project like this complete, it's just pretty miraculous. How do you think, that impacts the different types of engineering roles that exist in the future? Because right now, I think there's, you know, a dozen different types of engineers that you can be, from QA, front end, etc. You know, there's a big swath. I've heard some people argue that in four or five years we'll basically end up with four engineering roles. It'll be people who are managing agents, it'll be forward deployed engineers or FDEs, it'll be security engineers, and then people working on large-scale infrastructure for a small number of services, and then everything else is collapsed into the agentic world. Do you think that's a correct view of the world? Yeah, I mean, I think, I think we'll have to experiment our way through it. But what you said is what there are some very at-scale things. At LinkedIn, they did structurally change, and, you know, basically built up a new discipline called Full Stack Builder, right? So, they went and said, "Hey, let's bring, people from design and product management, front-end engineering, all put them together," but also have an edge, right? It's not like the design person still doesn't have the design edge or the front-end person doesn't have the front-end edge. But you kind of give yourself bigger scope in role, so that you're not confined to one role. And then equally, infrastructure has become very critical, right?
