So, in other words, like, I mean, RLEs, I mean, one thing we've realized is, even for the Excel team, for example, building the RLE in which a reward can be learned, is actually one of the hardest sort of infrastructure problems. And so you kind of need even new talent, right? Distributed systems people even in what was considered an end-user app team, because it's a different skill set. So, yes, infrastructure, science is the other one obviously. So I think we'll see how these evolve, right? Where is the real, I mean, always the world will have a bunch of specialists. You know, I think the generalist role is going to be the most exciting, right, because the leverage of a generalist, is where we are going to see the maximum returns, right? When you when you said, "Hey, coding, I'm now a generalist," like, what I've basically translated knowledge work, right, which I did when I created a Word document or a spreadsheet or even, and now I can build an app, right? It's in the same sentence, right? That idea that, oh wow, my generalist skills have gotten higher leverage, I think is what we are going to see across the board. Music to the ears of CEOs and VCs that are like, a little dangerous, and a lot of... Golden age for idea people. Idea people. Yeah. With a lot of agency. If you take that idea of personal agency and you just zoom it out to the organizational context, my partner Mike Vernal, who, actually started his career at Microsoft, just wrote an essay where one of the big takeaways is it's an age where you can be much more ambitious, and you need to be given the pace of the environment and how quickly actually users and companies are open to adopting new technologies. How do you think about—I feel silly asking this of somebody running a, you know, trillion dollar-plus company already—but how do you think about how Microsoft can be more ambitious now? It's a great question. I think, I think the, the thing in these type of transitions is to have a conceptual model of how work can change to go after outcomes that you could hardly imagine previously, right? In fact, Kevin Scott has this nice line, right, which is, when you can make the impossible like, when you're making hard things easier, that sort of one point of leverage. But true ambition is about making the impossible possible. So, now, the thing that is missing a little bit in all of our organizations is, what is that new conceptual model of what can we build? What was impossible, and what can we build? And I'll give you one example of this, right, which is, I take great inspiration from sort of the people who are managing the Azure network. And they came to this, this is from even last year. You know, we were scaling, you saw the I talked about sort of how we built in the last 15 months more Azure capacity than we built in the first 15 years. I mean, it's crazy. Right, it's pretty wild. And it's the same team. So, they saw that and they said, "Well, this just ain't going to work if we don't reconceptualize our work." So, they built, essentially they said, "Our job is not to do Azure networking, our job is to build the agentic system that does Azure networking," right? These are the folks managing the 500-plus fiber operators managing the WAN, right, all over. And fiber operations ultimately is a physical operation, things get cut, things get, you know, have to be repaired, you know, we have fancy words called DevOps and so on. Basically, emails are coming in and you've got to go respond to them, take care of it. So, they built this agentic system, they even have a character for it, it's called Miles, and it sort of does all this stuff. Right, they started sort of screaming for more tokens and so on. And so they were saying, "Look, we don't need headcount, we need tokens in order to be able to manage our operation." That reconceptualization of what their work is, right, they basically took their work and made it meta, right? That meta work is now their new work. Right, in the '80s, if somebody had come to us and said, "4 billion people are going to get up in the morning and start typing," my model would have been, "We need 4 billion typists?" But we're not doing typing, we're doing knowledge work. So, that to me, I think is it, right, which is whether it's Microsoft or whether it's any organization, is to give ourselves permission to do new types of meta cognition, meta work using these new tools to change the outputs that matter, and then really make the impossible possible. So, completing that dot, or the the connection tissue across those, I think is where a lot of the enterprise value will get created. Can we talk about data centers? Yeah, please ask. Oh, okay. Well, this leads nicely into the data center build up. I always think I'm just impressed at the sheer scale of the build out from Microsoft, but also everyone else. That this is redefining what it means to be a hyperscaler. And I just feel like that that is had unprecedented scale on finances, on the way you run the company, but also the communities that are, are impacted. Just talk a bit more about what you're seeing on the ground, like, when you visit your... Yeah, I think there are two aspects of it. Obviously, the the build out is extraordinary. You know, nothing like this has happened and it's great to be one of the participants in it. But you brought up the other part, right? I think at this point, it's clear that unless we as an industry are very principled about ensuring that the benefits of all the stuff we're talking about are felt in real ways, at the community level, right, because this is not just a a campaign. Right, it has to be real, where people are saying, "Look, this is not changing the prices on energy for me. In fact, if anything, it's bringing down prices because long term, there's going to be a better grid, there is going to be more energy." Water consumption is, in fact, not sort of, in fact, water is being replenished, right? You got to really, you know, educate folks on truly what's happening, the the closed-loop systems we're building. We have to invest in the training, the jobs, the tax base. In fact, the least talked about stuff is the amount of jobs that get created during construction, after construction, what's the tax base that's there in the community. And so, all this has to be real. And and if that is the case, then we will have permission. If it is not, we won't have permission. It's as simple as that, right, which is we I think we have to take it as an industry pretty seriously. I think it's good for communities to be skeptical, ask the hard question, for us to do the hard work, earn that. But at the end of the day, if the if we can really be the produce, like, I always felt like in human history, if you use a lot of energy, but also create a lot of value for society, the story has been fantastic. If you don't do that, it's not been that great. And this time around, I'm a firm believer that ultimately, if you do have a token economy that drives productivity, that drives economic growth, that drives broad-spread, you know, participation, better health outcomes, then, I think, we will be in a great place, and that's at least what we all have to be focused on. What do you most think about currently, or what have you most updated your personal models on regarding societal impact of AI? You're saying what's the, the... What have you updated most on in terms of societal impact? Yeah, I think the the most critical thing is the first question we even started with, which is we need to tell the story and make it real, that everybody has a real shot to participate as a first-class participant in this new economy. Right, that's kind of, I think, in the next 12 months, 18 months, we'll need a way for people to say, "Oh, wow, I get it." Right? There's going to be tremendous capability, tremendous amount of infrastructure, but I can see what's going to happen, whether it's the benefits like health outcomes, or my ability to create a startup, or my ability to run my local sort of store, more efficiently. It's just happening, and I see that, benefit myself. Right, that to me, you know, earning that permission in a path-dependent way, we can't wait. See, the one thing, Elad, that I've now learned is, I think the world is going to be way skeptical of tech and tech companies that say, "Trust us, we've got it. The future is going to be glorious." You kind of have to deliver tangible benefits, and quite frankly, politicians winning elections, because they've advocated for that. That will be, at least, my adjustment, because without it, thinking that somehow because it's too important this time around, it's too much of the economy for it not to be the case. So, one very simple framework I have for, you know, what is going to be the broad benefit of AI, beyond the communities just working in technology, are are sort of wealth creation, that's going to happen in a ton of different companies, startups and large companies. Then, you have healthcare. You had amazing demos today. There are companies like, oh, with evidence, I think that is happening. Education seems like another one that's an obvious good, where we haven't seen as much impact as I'd expect. Do you have a hypothesis on why that might be, or if it'll come? Yeah, I mean, I think this is where again, how we think about education, how, recently I met with the founders of Alpha School, and learned a lot about what they were going and going about. And it was fascinating to listen, to how to even rethink what does education really look like, because I think it's actually very important, and I'm not saying anything traditionally done is less important, right? I was even looking at the, it's fascinating to see, I I I forget the which Stanford class it was, the the agent guidelines for CS something, because you still need people to learn, like, it was an interesting AI class that they were making sure people were learning how to apply softmax appropriately versus saying, "Hey, fix my training run." So, I think learning concepts is important, it's going to be critical. But the way we create the incentives, what are the credentials, how we value those credentials, what is the employment opportunity for those credentials, so I think that there's a complete change that has to happen, given the way to get to information, way to educate yourself, way to continuously keep yourself updated has changed so much. So, I think, interestingly enough, may be the next big startup and success story could be someone who builds a new university, or a new, pedagogy even of how to get someone to go through a curriculum and find economic opportunity, that's highly valued. Well, that has felt, perhaps impossible for a long time, but it's a great note to end on, and something that might be possible. It is now possible. Yeah. Thank you, Satya. Thank you so much. Thank you, I appreciate it. Thank you, all.

Inside Bank of America: Brian Moynihan on Leadership, AI and Global Growth

Forbes(2.1M)·18:29·349 views·2026-06-04
Maneet AhujaEditor-At-Large, Forbes & Iconoclast Founder Brian MoynihanChair of the Board & CEO, Bank of America
TL;DRBank of America CEO Brian Moynihan highlights the resilience of the American consumer and small businesses despite K-shaped economic pressures, while detailing the bank's disciplined approach to AI implementation and global sports investments.
Program Philosophy: The...

TL;DR

Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan highlights the resilience of the American consumer and small businesses despite K-shaped economic pressures, while detailing the bank's disciplined approach to AI implementation and global sports investments.

Global Sports and Community Engagement ("Sports with Us")

  • Program Philosophy: The "Sports with Us" program aims to drive broad athletic participation, support local charities, and provide youth opportunities rather than simply sponsoring one-off events.
  • Golf Initiatives: BofA's golf program is projected to cross 250,000 youth participants this year. It partners with local clubs to let kids play municipal courses for $5 across 3,000 locations nationwide.
      • The Rory McIlroy Impact: After appearing in a BofA promotional campaign, professional golfer Rory McIlroy returned his appearance fee to purchase an additional 80,000 rounds of golf for participating youth.
  • FIFA World Cup Partnership: BofA has signed on as the first-ever bank in FIFA’s global sponsor tier for the upcoming World Cup, leveraging its deep roots in major US host cities (e.g., Boston since 1784, San Francisco for 130 years).
      • Support for Veterans: BofA announced a $2 million donation to Vet Tix to provide free World Cup tickets to veterans and first responders.

Economic Outlook and Consumer Spending Trends

  • Resilient Consumer Spending: BofA internal data shows that consumer spending grew approximately 5% year-over-year (representing about $400 billion in transaction volume).
      • Employment Stability: Low unemployment claims (sitting around 200,000) and steady continuing claims (1.8 million) remain the primary drivers of sustained consumer spending.
  • K-Shaped Divergence: While aggregate spending is healthy, BofA research indicates a clear divide:
      • Lower-Income Bracket: Spending and wage growth are rising but at a slower rate than the middle- and upper-income brackets. Lower-income consumers are actively shifting their spending to big-box and secondhand retailers.
      • Upper-Income Bracket: Discretionary spending remains highly robust, and Boomers/seniors are spending at faster rates as they enter a wealth "de-accumulation" phase.
  • Growth Forecasts: BofA research projects US GDP growth at 2% to 2.1% for the current period and global growth at roughly 3%.
  • Key Economic Headwinds: Affordability issues—specifically rising housing costs, insurance premiums, and fluctuating energy prices due to geopolitical conflicts—remain critical points of concern.

Small Business Health and Credit Capacity

  • Market Position: As the nation's largest small business lender, BofA services 12 million small businesses, holding $200 billion in deposits and maintaining $50 billion to $70 billion in loans.
  • Credit Availability: Small businesses exhibit strong balance sheets. Credit line utilization rates remain low, meaning they have ample borrowing capacity when they choose to expand.
  • Top Business Concerns: Small business owners are highly focused on:
      • Labor Shortages: Particularly acute within the construction and trade sectors.
      • Tariffs & Policies: Uncertainty regarding tariffs, margins, and immigration policies are causing business owners to remain cautious with long-term capital deployment.

Strategic Technology Spending and Artificial Intelligence

  • Technology Budget Allocation: BofA spends $13 billion annually on technology. Of this, over $4 billion is allocated to new technology initiatives, including $250 million dedicated strictly to AI deployments.
  • "Erica" Virtual Assistant: Developed as a predictive "small language model" to accurately translate conversational language into precise financial data, Erica now has 20 million active users completing 200 million interactions per quarter.
  • The Human-in-the-Loop Standard: Moynihan stresses that for any bank function requiring actual financial or ethical judgment, humans must remain central to the process to maintain trust and 100% data accuracy.
  • Operational Efficiency: Implementing AI across internal IT "break-fix" systems successfully reduced employee support call volumes by 50%.

Leadership Reflections on Past Crises

  • The Post-2008 Financial Crisis: Moynihan identified the years immediately following the 2008 financial crisis as the most challenging period of his career.
      • The Scale of the Crisis: BofA was servicing one-in-five mortgages in America and subsequently managing one-in-five foreclosures.
      • Organizational Overhaul: The bank had 50,000 employees dedicated to loan modifications and forebearances, ultimately shifting its mortgage business model away from aggressive market share toward a highly controlled, risk-averse framework.

Notable Quotes

"The American consumer is a wonderful thing. Now there are affordability issues, they're serious... But from a general population and general activity, it's really strong." — Brian Moynihan
"For anything that takes real judgment, a human has to be in the loop because the answer has to be perfect if you're in our business of trust." — Brian Moynihan
"That was a tough time because you know we had to go to Congress and testify, we had states—all the states—after us, we had to settle lawsuits... but the team did a great job. We just kept focused on our purpose, kept saying 'we're here to help our customers, we're here to help the teammates, we're here to help communities.'" — Brian Moynihan
Please welcome Brian Moynihan, Chair of the Board and CEO, Bank of America, and interviewer Maneet Ahuja, Editor-At-Large, Forbes and Founder, Iconoclast. Thanks, guys. So the fun continues, and good afternoon. It's great to be back here, and I'm especially excited for this next conversation with one of the most influential leaders of the last decade, one of the longest-serving CEOs among top Wall Street banks: Brian Moynihan, Bank of America Chair of the Board and CEO. Thank you so much for joining us. It's good to be here, and thank you. So, Brian, we just heard before lunch from Sir David Beckham, who was announced last November as an ambassador for Bank of America's Global Sports Program, Sports with Us. You've framed this as a long-term commitment to the sports ecosystem and more than just one-off events. Why don't you talk us through Sports with Us and the global impact you've already achieved and expect to have? Yeah, so our Sports with Us program is built across multiple different types of sports. The common purpose of all of them is participation—broad participation, helping charities thrive, helping kids get involved, and providing opportunities. If you think about the two marathons we sponsor—the Chicago Marathon, we actually don't sponsor, we run that race; it's our race. For the Boston Marathon, what we're trying to do there is get more and more participation by people running, but also support the charities, so we double the charities there. The campaign is the My Marathon campaign. I'm not going to run a two-hour marathon, and most of the people in this crowd are not going to run a two-hour marathon. But what we can do is raise money for causes we care about, so we built that. And then with the golf platform, we have the various parts of Augusta National. We ended up going from the Latin America, Asian Amateur, and Women's Amateur—helping them when they built that tournament—and now with the Masters. But it was Golf with Us, getting these kids to play golf. So this year, we'll cross 250,000 kids who can play, who join a golf club, and for that, they can play any muni for $5 at 3,000 courses around the country. We're trying to get more and more kids playing golf. Then, with Tom Watson, we did a thing to get people who are retired and others who have the time to go play with these kids who are a little bit older and mentor them, and so that's the golf part. In the soccer part, as the World Cup came and I talked to Gianni, it was about how we have got to build this program, sort of Soccer with Us. The thought there is thinking about it in all its aspects, so it's the team sponsorship, it's the tournament sponsorship, the USA teams, and all their different things. But we also do clinics in schools, putting soccer in schools, leaving fields behind, and opening up the ability for people to see this. We also worked with the Street Kids World Cup, which is street kids from all over the world playing in another World Cup, and we helped sponsor that. So, it's all about just bringing participation in. But we also do it in the arts. This Museums with Us program, which sounds familiar like Golf with Us, is about getting people who are Bank of America customers into all 300 museums around the United States for free. Again, it is to get participation, get access, and provide opportunity for people who otherwise would not have it. But it also lifts the brand, and that's the business purpose. Yeah, so this really translates to helping communities and upward mobility. Talk to us a little bit about some of the impact you've already seen since you initiated this. We spoke upstairs with Sir David Beckham about some of the impact that you've already seen since you started the program, and you also had an interesting experience with Rory McIlroy, right? Yeah, so this is one of those things that we did, and we didn't realize it would have such presence. For the decision to do Golf with Us, the first year of the Masters sponsorship—going back to the Rory conversation—the second year, the team came with the idea of having three kids, who are Golf with Us participants, recreate three iconic shots: Jack Nicklaus's putt, Bubba Watson's shot from the trees, and then Rory's win. We didn't know he was going to win it again, but as part of that, when we had to pay appearance fees since it's a likeness play, Rory saw the commercial. He was so moved that he gave his fee back to buy another 80,000 rounds of golf for these kids. It was just a tremendous gesture by Rory, and then he won the tournament again, which was not even in the cards. So, we've already, we've already tried to think of how that plays out. But it's all about creating opportunity. When we think about these kids coming into a museum, or kids picking up a golf club on a course and doing a tee with a First Tee clinic and two professional athletes—not golfers, but just general athletes—and seeing their reaction to it, and getting that community wound up about it, it's just great stuff. You're just trying to give kids the opportunity to play a sport, a lifetime sport in the case of running and golf, but also to do it in a professional manner with good coaching and good capabilities. In the end of the day, we just keep trying to figure out ways we can help communities build. It's a community-lifting effort and an individual in that community-lifting effort. And so your commitment to soccer, though, extends well beyond Sports with Us. Your FIFA sponsorship makes Bank of America the first-ever bank in FIFA's global sponsor tier. Walk us through the decision, and why that $100 million bet now? Well, when in 2017 or 2018 it was decided it was going to be in the US, I had a friend of mine come and say to me, "You guys ought to be involved in this." We had never thought about it. So, I started to think about it, really off the back of these other programs. If FIFA was coming to the United States, when I talked to Gianni at the outset and when we commenced, I said, "We will bring you out to all these communities. We are already in all these communities, and we have been for a long time." In Kansas City, we've probably been there 150 years. In Massachusetts, we've been there since 1784. In San Francisco, we've been there for 130 years, and in Charlotte for 150 years. We've been in these communities, we know them, we can bring you into them, and we can provide support for that local community—the clinics, the fields, and everything—and we can make it available to everybody. We've had 20,000 tickets given away. Today we just announced that we gave $2 million to give free tickets to veterans and first responders to the games through Vet Tix, which is a vet-owned enterprise. We did that so veterans could go for free. FIFA made the tickets available, we bought them, and we put them into that platform and gave them to that platform. So, we're just trying to think of ways to help in all those communities. My pitch to Gianni was, "If you're going to come to America, you want to be with a brand that knows how to operate granularly in all those local communities, and we can help you lift this." Even figuring out where to think about games, demographics, population, and stuff, it's been a fun experience with them. So, to your point and the point that you made to Gianni, you serve 70 million customers globally and also many small business clients and customers across the US. You've often said that the consumer has been remarkably resilient. Could that resilience turn into exhaustion? You have a lot of Bank of America research tracking consumer behavior. Are Americans spending from strength or from necessity right now? So, if you think about it, we have Bank of America research, Candace Browning and the team. It's a top research firm, and we publish a lot of this data through the institute, which we decided to do about six or seven years ago to make it available. This is sort of real-time data out of our customer set, not identified to customer A or B, but just general data. If you look at that data from May of '26 versus May of '25, it was about $400 billion of money moving in the economy, growing at 5% over last year. If you look within the nuances of that data, you look at boomers and seniors—they're spending at a faster clip because they're in a de-accumulation mode, as the industry refers to it. If you look at lower, middle, and higher-income people, they are shuffling what they're spending on across all of them. There is a little bit more shuffling in the lower-income third, the lower third of incomes, and they're moving stuff around. They're going to a big-box retailer versus a niche retailer, or they might be going to a secondhand store to buy some stuff. But at the end of the day, they're still spending more money. While gas spending is up a lot—25% to 30%—its overall impact is in the single-digit percentage to that number or less, and the rest of it is just general spending. So, it's good news. The reason why people are doing this is because they're employed and work for great companies, like those represented here in the audience, and unemployment has been benign. New claims are sitting there at 200,000 and change, and haven't gone up a lot. Continuing claims are at 1.8 million, which has not gone up a lot. If you compare that to pre-pandemic, when the population of workers was smaller years ago, it's been pretty strong. What happens next, we'll see, but right now they've been extremely resilient. By the way, this was true last year and the year before. Everybody said the consumer was going to quit spending right after COVID, after the payments during COVID, after the checks. They were always going to quit spending, but the American consumer is a wonderful thing. Now, there are affordability issues, and they are serious. That's where we've got to make sure that policymakers are saying, "How can I create more housing to bring the supply up and the cost down?" Those types of issues are real. The war has to resolve so gas prices get to a natural balance, and those are real issues. But from a general population and general activity standpoint, it's really strong. When you go to small and medium-sized businesses, they're in very good shape credit-wise. They have access to credit, good cash flows, and they're making money. They want everything to be resolved because they want to be able to do their business plans without worrying about tariffs and immigration policy and all that stuff, but at the end of the day, they're just going about their business. So, if I'm hearing you correctly, it sounds like what you're seeing and what your data is showing us is that the consumer is resilient and that you're cautiously optimistic. Yeah, our team has the US growing at 2% to 2.1% for '26, and a little faster in '27. They have the world growing at about 3%, which is aligned with a lot of people. But the interesting thing is, last year at this time, that dropped to about 1.5%, then it went up to 2.8%, and back down to 2%. So, what's causing that? Those are the external factors of the war, and gas prices slowing down spending on other stuff. Then you have this big phenomenon that many of your panels have talked about—AI spending, which is driving a whole different element, and deregulation, which is benefiting businesses too. So, you put that whole soup together at 2%, which, if you think about it for the last 15 or 20 years, is actually a pretty solid growth number. So, another important area of the economy is small business, and they're the heartbeat of the American economy, right? And you guys are the largest small business lender in the country. How are small businesses performing right now? What sectors are you seeing, from all the data that your research center has, that are proving resilient? So, if you look at it, we're the largest small business lender, and we do surveys that we publish all the time about them. We have 12 million small businesses total, and we've got $200 billion in deposits from them, plus $50 to $70 billion in loans, so you see a lot of activity. If you look at what they've been telling us as they go through time, they still have plenty of availability on lines of credit. Their draw rates are not high, so they have the capacity to borrow if they want. If you look at their payroll payments, you can see them rising. But if you look at them and ask them what they're worried about, if they're in construction or certain other industries, they're worried about finding workers. If they're in an import industry, they're worried about tariffs affecting their margins and whether they can pass those costs through competitively. They all worry about AI, and some are adopting things like that. That's where the great companies here can provide those services to them. But they've got the benefit of tax structures, and demand is pretty good. So, they're looking at all this and saying, "Gee, as long as this war gets resolved, I think I can see my path out." They're pretty solidly optimistic right now, but again, external factors affect them because they don't have big staffs to go analyze things. They have to sit there and make a judgment with their own money about what they're going to do tomorrow based on what they read. And if what you're reading is that energy prices are up and inflation is higher, there is a side of caution as well. So, some of the latest Bank of America research out there right now suggests that we're in a K-shaped economy, with strong spending at the top with the higher earners and asset owners, and weaker spending with the wage earners. Does the banking system see that divide more clearly than Washington does? Well, I think everybody sees it, and that's what's going on in some of the election discussions, polls, and things like that. This is where you always have to be careful, because I'm giving you aggregate numbers, which represent the whole economic activity. When you look at it by the income thirds that you're referencing, the lower third is growing, but at a slower rate than the middle and upper thirds. The wages of the lower third are growing, but at a slower rate than the middle and upper thirds. That's interesting because, up until the last six months or so, the lower third of earning households was actually growing faster. The benefit of the tax changes went to the lower and middle tiers, and that's helping offset some of this. The question is, what happens on the other side? So, there's an element of the K-shape here. But if you look at the concerns from about 12 months ago, it was about insurance affordability rising fast, and housing. Insurance has leveled off. Gas was giving them a benefit last year; now it's hurting them. If you look at housing cost payments, you can see them kind of mitigating, but still, more supply would be helpful. So, there are differences. The K-economy that our team—Liz and the group—talks about is real. But when you think about it from an aggregate economic impact standpoint, you've got to be careful. What drives the economy is the discretionary part of the spending package, and that is concentrated more heavily in the middle and upper thirds, which are growing faster. Shifting gears now, I know we only have a couple of minutes left, but let's talk about the impact of AI. Bank of America, I think you've deployed over $4 billion already into technology infrastructure, including AI. You have an AI assistant named Erica who services all parts of the Bank of America business. Are you already seeing that measurable impact inside of the bank and with your clients? And I'm also curious if Erica is well-received by your customers. Well, Erica has been out there since 2018. Why did we create Erica? We tried to put Google Search in our mobile app, and when you asked it a question like "what's my balance," it might give you a picture of a scale or details about a yoga class. So we said, "We've got to take the language that people speak and the language that the bank speaks, put them together, and make sure we're accurate." We went and said, "Let's develop a predictive model that can do that." Today, you would call that a small language model. That was Erica, developed with Stanford and others, and we did it. Now, 20 million people use it, and they use it a lot—200 million times a quarter. Our digital interactions overall make up 99% of our interactions. It's just that Erica is much more versatile; there are 700 things you can do with it where it will get you the right answer. This is a lesson learned about all of this: it touches 110 systems at once. That's incredible. You could be driving down—not Sixth Avenue, but driving 60 miles an hour down an avenue—and the infrastructure to get that inquiry to us and back has to be instantaneous, or you're mad at us. So you start to think about how the data has to be perfect. You're asking about one check transaction out of 20 that month in one of your accounts, and you have five accounts, and we have to be exactly accurate. It takes work. Now, looking at AI overall, we spend $4 billion plus on technology initiatives; we spend $13 billion on all technology. Of that, we spend about a quarter-billion dollars this year just on new deployments of AI. We have 200,000 people who all have access to AI on daily platforms. We've got hundreds of initiatives going, with about 190 to 200 projects that are currently implemented. This includes the coding-type work you hear your colleagues talk about. Erica, as you know, is already being used by literally 20 million customers every day. We took that same technology and put it on our commercial side, and 300,000 to 400,000 customers use it to do different things. We also applied it to internal break-fix issues in a tech area, and 50% of the support calls went away. So, you can see this is having the impacts we want. But you have to remember that for anything that takes real judgment, a human has to be in the loop. The answer has to be perfect if you're in our business of trust. That's what we were able to do with Erica. We were able to keep it constrained enough that it could give a right answer to 200 questions, and now up to 700 questions—or "intents" as we call them. Someday we'll replace it with one of these more free-form, agentic-type things, but right now it works, and that's a different approach than what a lot of other people have. Well, that's a positive note about AI. We just have 30 seconds left, and I want to ask you one leadership question. As one of the longest-tenured CEOs on Wall Street, you've led through the financial crisis, COVID, regional bank failures, inflation, and now AI disruption, very successfully. There've been some good times, too. There've been some good times, too, yeah. Well, you're actually quite funny yourself, just like Sir David, where he had the whole audience cracking up. I would say you're pretty funny yourself. He's probably got a little more than I do. Yeah. What's been the hardest period to lead through—today's challenges relative to the recent past? I look at our company coming out of the financial crisis, when we had one in five mortgages in America, and therefore we had one in five foreclosures. There's nothing good that can come out of a foreclosure. We did forbearances, redos, and modifications—all of that. We had 50,000 people working on that. It was not a great customer experience, and that's when we decided to change the mortgage business to be much more controlled, as opposed to going for market share. But that was a tough time because we had to go to Congress and testify, we had all the states after us, and we had to settle lawsuits. We had $20 billion of charges in a single quarter, and we still made money that year. It's a powerful engine, but that was tough to lead through because it just seemed like every day, somebody had a different idea of what we'd done right or wrong, irrespective of what the facts were. But the team did a great job. We just kept focused on our purpose, keeping the mindset of, "We're here to help our customers, we're here to help our teammates, and we're here to help our communities." If we do that, we'll make a lot of money for the shareholders, and that's what we did. So, you led with the same principles, but it was an interesting time. Well, Brian Moynihan, Bank of America Chairman and CEO, thank you so much for joining us at Iconoclast. Thank you.

FULL HEARING: Dem Lawmakers Show Absolutely No Mercy To Scott Bessent In House Ways & Means Cmte

Forbes Breaking News(5.6M)·267:20·5.3K views·2026-06-04
Scott BessentTreasury Secretary
TL;DRTreasury Secretary Scott Bessent defended the administration's economic policies before the House Ways and Means Committee, showcasing middle-class tax relief and manufacturing growth.
However, he faced intense scrutiny from Democrats over a controversial IRS audit...

TL;DR

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent defended the administration's economic policies before the House Ways and Means Committee, showcasing middle-class tax relief and manufacturing growth. However, he faced intense scrutiny from Democrats over a controversial IRS audit immunity settlement for Donald Trump, high cost-of-living inflation, and a GAO report exposing taxpayer data security breaches within the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE).

***

The Debate Over Inflation and the "Golden Age" Economy

  • Republican Economic Outlook: Chairman Jason Smith and Secretary Scott Bessent argued that the administration’s tax cuts and deregulatory agenda have triggered a manufacturing renaissance and resilient economic growth.
      • Key Metrics: Bessent highlighted an average quarterly GDP growth rate of 2.6% during the administration's term and noted that core inflation fell to 2.8% (down from a high of 6.6% in 2022 under the previous administration).
      • Job Growth: The administration reported a net gain of 313,000 private sector jobs and 13,000 manufacturing jobs over a recent two-month period.
  • Democratic Cost-of-Living Concerns: Ranking Member Richard Neal and other Democratic members countered that working families are being "crushed" by high costs, citing record-low consumer sentiment since the index’s inception in 1952.
      • Price Surges: Democrats highlighted severe price hikes: gasoline is up approximately 50% (exceeding $4.50/gallon in places like Pennsylvania and Illinois), health insurance costs are up 26%, electricity is up 8.5%, and basic groceries have surged (beef is up 15% and tomatoes are up 40%).
  • The Geopolitical Premium: Bessent attributed the current spike in utility and fuel prices to the conflict with Iran, calling it a temporary national security necessity to prevent a rogue nuclear regime. Democrats rejected this characterization, labeling the conflict a "war of choice" that has cost American households over $100 billion.

Implementation of the Working Families Tax Cuts

  • Bigger Tax Refunds: According to Treasury data, the tax filing season delivered $325 billion in total refunds, with the average individual refund increasing by more than 11%.
  • Targeted Middle-Class Provisions:
      • No Tax on Tips & Overtime: Roughly 62 million filers (44% of returns) claimed at least one of these provisions. Over 70% of those who benefited earned less than $100,000 annually.
      • Overtime Relief: Hourly workers were allowed to deduct up to $12,500 ($25,000 for married couples). Bessent reported that 96% of overtime claimants earned under $200,000.
  • The Expansion of "Trump Accounts": Bessent reported that nearly 6 million "Trump accounts" have been opened to give children a stake in the stock market, with 1.4 million children eligible for a $1,000 seed contribution from the Treasury.
      • Philanthropic Influx: The program has attracted private sector support, including a $6.25 billion commitment from Susan and Michael Dell, which excludes the wealthiest 20% of zip codes to provide $250 per account directly to low-income children.
  • Main Street Permanence: The tax package permanently established the Section 199A small business deduction and 100% immediate expensing for equipment and manufacturing structures, boosting capital expenditure by 17% in late 2025.

Clashes Over Trump's IRS Audit Immunity and the "Slush Fund"

  • The Audit Immunity Settlement: Representatives Lloyd Doggett, Linda Sánchez, and Brad Schneider pressed Bessent on a controversial DOJ-IRS settlement that allegedly grants Donald Trump and his family complete immunity from tax audits for returns filed before May 19, 2026.
      • Conflict of Interest: Democrats pointed out that the settlement was negotiated and signed by acting Attorney General Todd Blanche, who previously served as Trump's personal criminal defense attorney.
  • Bessent's Refusal to Testify: Bessent repeatedly refused to answer questions regarding the settlement's details, the scope of the audit immunity, or the roles played by IRS officials, citing ongoing litigation.
  • Termination of the Anti-Weaponization Fund: Under intense questioning, Bessent confirmed that acting Attorney General Blanche has declared the controversial anti-weaponization fund (which Democrats termed a "slush fund") is "dead" and that the government will not move forward with it.

DOGE Data Security Failures and Oversight

  • Taxpayer Data Vulnerability: Representative Suzan DelBene questioned Bessent on a recent Government Accountability Office (GAO) report revealing that on January 31, 2025, Bessent personally granted Elon Musk’s Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) access to critical Treasury payment systems.
      • Unauthorized Access: The GAO audit confirmed that a DOGE staffer was inadvertently granted temporary authorization to create, modify, and delete data within the payment database, exposing Americans' Social Security numbers and bank routing details.
  • Unencrypted Data Transmission: The GAO report also found that a DOGE staffer transmitted an unencrypted file containing sensitive USAID payment information and names to external personnel.
  • Treasury's Response: Bessent defended the department, noting the access was intended to be "read-only" and that "inadvertent" errors occurred. He asserted that the Treasury has since tightened its database access protocols.

Trade, Tariffs, and International Tax Sovereignty

  • OECD Pillar 2 Victory: Bessent highlighted a major January 5, 2026, side-by-side agreement that largely exempts US-headquartered multinational enterprises from the OECD's under-taxed payments rules, keeping tax revenues within the US Treasury.
  • Trade Deficit Improvement: The administration reported that the US trade deficit for goods fell by $370 billion over a 12-month period ending March 2026, driven in part by a 32% drop in the trade deficit with China.
  • Delayed Tariff Refunds: Representative Steven Horsford accused the administration of "slow-walking" $165 billion in tariff refunds that were ruled unlawful by the Supreme Court. Democrats criticized the Justice Department for actively appealing court orders meant to expedite these refunds to US businesses and consumers.

***

Notable Quotes

"This tax day under President Trump, we celebrated how much more money hardworking Americans kept, not how much the government took." — Scott Bessent, U.S. Secretary of the Treasury
"Workers and families are being forced to stretch their dollars further every day... Between the tariffs and the Iran war, prices are rising faster than they have in years." — Rep. Richard Neal, Ranking Member (D-MA)
"Truly incredible that a President of the United States could come in and sue the people of the United States for 10 billion dollars and then walk off with an immunity deal that no other American has ever had nor could they receive." — Rep. Lloyd Doggett (D-TX)
"We are creating productive capacity here, and productive capacity is what generates future tax revenues... I call it the slingshot effect." — Scott Bessent, U.S. Secretary of the Treasury
Welcome back, Secretary Bessent, to the best committee in all of Congress, the Ways and Means Committee. Since you last came before this committee, the average American has more money in their pocket from the working families tax cuts. This filing season, refunds were up more than 11 percent, and tax refunds totaled nearly 325 billion dollars. A family of four with two kids making 73,000 dollars or less owed zero in federal income taxes. 62 million Americans claimed at least one of President Trump's new tax relief priorities, such as no tax on tips, no tax on overtime, no tax on Social Security, and no tax on auto loan interest. Your department just revealed that the majority of Americans who benefited from these policies earn under 100,000 dollars. Nearly 6 million kids now have Trump accounts to save for their future. Nearly 40 million families claimed the child tax credit that was increased to 2,200 dollars and permanently, permanently indexed for inflation so that the credit, the credit never loses value. Americans across the country are experiencing what tax relief means for them. At a hearing, a waiter from Arizona told us how he is using his tax savings from no tax on tips to start law school. A Texas steelworker said he can now afford to start a family from no tax on overtime. A couple in Texas adopted a second child with the help of the adoption tax credit. In every community, working-class Americans have their own version of the story because President Trump, because President Trump and Republicans in Congress delivered real tax relief. However, every single Democrat voted against this tax relief, hiding behind the false claim that these tax cuts only help billionaires. But we all know the truth. There aren't any billionaires who work for tips. I don't know any billionaires that work for overtime. There aren't any billionaires who rely on Social Security. There aren't any billionaires that need an auto loan to afford a car. Republicans targeted tax relief to the Americans whose paychecks were stretched thin by Democrat inflation and needed help. The working families tax cuts provided bigger refunds and bigger paychecks and permanent, permanent pro-growth tax policy. And it will make it, it easier to build, to invest, produce, and hire in America. Our economy is in the middle of a historic, historic construction boom that means new jobs and opportunities for working-class Americans. Factory activity grew every month this year and reached its highest level since May of 2022. Equipment investment rose 17 percent and investment in intellectual property rose 11 percent in the first quarter of this year. In the same period, economic growth was driven by two things: investment and exports. Republican policies are making investing in America a solid bet for companies after decades, decades of America's manufacturing power declining. The President's tax and trade policies work hand in glove to fuel the economic investment that creates great-paying jobs. Reciprocal trade agreements have secured 10 trillion in investment commitments in leading industries, opened new markets formerly hostile to US exports, and made our trading relationships fairer for American workers, farmers, manufacturers, and producers. American producers exported just last year a record 3.4 trillion, trillion dollars. And in the first three months of this year, US exports were over 300 billion each month. That's the highest in 250 years of our country's history. America is becoming less dependent on China, a goal long promised by Washington politicians with no actual progress until President Trump. Our trade deficit with China fell 32 percent last year to the lowest level since 2004. The Chinese recently agreed to purchase at least 17 billion dollars annually in US agriculture products through 2028 alongside more Boeing airplanes. President Trump and Republicans in Congress repealed the de minimis privilege that mostly benefited Chinese companies shipping products, undercutting American producers and manufacturers. Our economic security is a cornerstone of our national security and therefore must not be abused by bad actors. Yet this committee's investigation into US-based non-profits revealed many are funded or operate under the influence of foreign countries like China. And they sow division and chaos in our communities that weakens America and strengthens foreign adversaries. Tax-exempt status is a privilege, not a right. We have called on the IRS to investigate and, if necessary, revoke the tax-exempt status of 11 different organizations who are the focus of our investigation. It is critical that the IRS take a close look at tax-exempt organizations with documented histories of activity that destabilizes society and supports terrorism. Looking ahead, our tax policies must continue to make our economy the most competitive for innovative sectors of the future, including digital assets and the over 60 million Americans who own cryptocurrency. America needs clear, modern tax rules to ensure we remain the crypto capital of the world. Next week, we will be holding a legislative hearing on policies that offer solutions to pressing issues surrounding digital asset taxation. Mr. Secretary, I know you are a strong advocate for ensuring Main Street shares in the prosperity of our economy. I look forward to hearing how Treasury is implementing the working families tax cuts so that Americans who power our economy—workers, small businesses, farmers, manufacturers, producers, and innovators—have a brighter future. I now recognize the gentleman from Massachusetts, the ranking member. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Mr. Secretary, welcome as always. The chairman made the point that this is the best committee in Congress; that's likely to be one of the few things we agree upon this morning, as we all know. And you hold, as I pointed out in the past, one of the most important jobs in the world as a successor to Alexander Hamilton. We hope that there'll be some explanations this morning about the failures of many of the economic promises that were made by the Trump administration. 70 percent of the American people disagree with the economic direction that the country has taken. Last time you visited with us, Americans were being socked with price hikes because of the tariffs where you and I met in front of the Supreme Court that day when the Supreme Court took and heard the oral arguments because of the big, ugly bill. We fought on our side every step of the way, knowing and predicting what was to come: higher costs, millions kicked off of healthcare, children going hungry, all in service to the wealthiest amongst us. Despite this, a rosy picture was painted about the Trump economy and what it might deliver. It's safe to say that almost on every front, those predictions from the administration have been wrong. Workers and families are being forced to stretch their dollars further every day, and the pain so clearly stems from many of these projects, including what was supposed to be a cessation of endless wars. We've watched the price hikes that are coming because of this tariff regime. During testimony last year, you suggested that the Trump policies would lift the working and middle class and reinvigorate American manufacturing. It was stated that take-home pay and wages would increase. You relied upon consumer confidence data to tout the strength of the economy. You pointed out that inflation was improving and that costs, including food, energy, and shelter, would be coming down. And now we have a chance to reflect upon what actually happened, including the slow-walking of the tariff refunds that are due. Families were forced to shell out 1,700 dollars more last year to cover many of the costs that were estimated to be rosy here not that long ago. Between the tariffs and the Iran war, prices are rising faster than they have in years. Last month, the inflation rate ate up paychecks. I appreciated the chairman's position on pointing out that there has been a routine of 3 percent increases, but when you're paying more for everything else, that quickly goes away. The price of a tank of gasoline in many places has gone up by almost 50 percent. That's a staggering amount. And despite the bullish assessment in January that economic growth would hit 5.4 percent in the fourth quarter, that growth came in at approximately one-half of a percent, not even a full percentage point. That's not spin; those are facts. The administration continues to argue that down is up and to convince people that their pain should be subjugated to patriotic causes. Whether celebrating higher credit card delinquencies on households, they're spending more every day because of the economic policies of the administration. As if the 1,700 dollars to cover the tariffs wasn't enough, estimates are now showing that the war in Iran has already cost households another 750 dollars. And while an extra 200 dollars in gas costs might not be a lot for some folks in the administration, it sure is for a lot of working families. I'm sure we're going to hear that there are many attempts to save the economy by touting the refunds. Those refunds were eaten up almost overnight because of increases in gasoline prices. Tax refunds fell short of the promises by 65 percent and have been completely wiped out, as I noted, by price hikes. The President's attention remains everywhere but on what's most important. As he said, he's not thinking about people's financial situations; he tends to be focused on more vanity projects, including a ballroom in an arch and painting the reflecting pool. We are now ready for a cage fight on the south lawn and perhaps a 250-dollar bill with his photograph on it. We look at what has happened recently with a slush fund that was created, and our Republican colleagues continue to go wobbly on their defense, perhaps not understanding how this is playing with the American people. I know the administration has been busy trying this week to explain all of it, telling members of Congress that the slush fund is dead—apparently not consistent with what the President offered but 24 hours ago. Let's be real. The 250-dollar bill maybe might be needed because of the price hikes that are coming to the American people. Americans have made this very clear. 77 percent say the policies have increased the cost of living, and more than half say it's worse than they can previously remember. Consumer sentiment is currently at a record low. The savings rate for Americans has dropped to 2.6 percent, cut in half in just a year. When we look at this economy, we see failure upon failure. The policies that have been proposed have really not worked for average Americans. Families are getting crushed, and we would suggest today that a new course of action needs to be taken. Republicans have made it clear that they don't want to put a stop to the chaos. But Democrats are unwavering in our fight for American families to lower costs and to end the harm that is being done. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Thank you, Ranking Member. Today's sole witness is United States Secretary of the Treasury Scott Bessent. The committee has received your written testimony and it will be part of the formal hearing record. Secretary Bessent, you have five minutes to deliver your remarks and you may begin when you're ready. Chairman Smith, Ranking Member Neal, and members of the committee, thank you for convening today's hearing. I'm grateful for this opportunity to discuss President Trump's 2027 budget, which builds on this administration's progress in unleashing a new era of economic expansion. I last appeared before this committee just a few weeks before the House passed the working families tax cuts. So, on the heels of the most successful filing season in IRS history, I'd be remiss if I did not begin by thanking the committee for helping to deliver this once-in-a-generation bill to the President's desk. This tax day under President Trump, we celebrated how much more money hardworking Americans kept, not how much the government took. Over 62 million tax returns claimed at least one of President Trump's signature new tax cuts: no tax on tips, no tax on overtime, deductibility of American car interest loans, and an enhanced deduction for low and middle-income seniors. All told, the average refund increased by over 11 percent with total refunds increasing by 18 percent. But as important as what the legislation achieves, it is what it prevented. If opponents of the working families tax cuts had had their way, our economy would have absorbed the largest, the largest tax hike in its history—over 5 trillion dollars. 90 percent of American taxpayers would have seen their standard deduction slashed, while 40 million families would have seen their child tax credit halved. Instead, this committee held the line, and the American people had a better tax day because of it. Notably, President Trump's pro-growth policies don't stop at putting more money back in the pockets of working and middle-class families. They extend to placing the American dream within closer reach of their children. For context, nearly 40 percent of Americans have no exposure to US equities, no stake in the companies they helped to build. Trump accounts represent a profound reimagining of that arrangement. They'll ensure that every American child can benefit from private ownership and compound growth. That every American child, in short, is born a shareholder. To date, nearly 6 million Trump accounts have been opened, with 1.4 million eligible for the 1,000-dollar seed contribution from Treasury. Of course, as these tax cuts deliver relief for working-class Americans, President Trump's economic agenda is bolstered by two other distinct but reinforcing levers: trade and deregulation. Let me briefly address both of these in turn. First, the President is undeterred in his determination to open markets for US goods and services while rebuilding US manufacturing capacity. Over the 12 months ending March 2026, the trade deficit for goods declined by 370 billion dollars compared to the same timeframe ending March 2025. The economy has added 313,000 net new private sector jobs and 13,000 manufacturing jobs in the past two months. Firm capital expenditures rose at an annual rate of over 17 percent in the final quarter, and companies are investing trillions to build and expand here at home. American industry is winning again to the benefit of American workers. A whole-of-government approach, meanwhile, is coupling our manufacturing revival with a great regulatory reset. Properly calibrated regulation is essential for economic growth, capital formation, employment, and higher wages. So at the outset of this administration, President Trump set the ambitious benchmark of slashing 10 existing regulations before issuing a new one. In 2025, we shattered that goal with a ratio of 129 to 1. And as a result, deregulatory actions generated more savings last year than in those of the prior Trump administration combined. Separately, any one of these initiatives on trade, tax cuts, and deregulation would be substantial. Taken together, they are transformative. Before President Trump took office, our trading partners exploited America's markets, our regulatory state smothered businesses, and our tax code was poised to punish workers and job creators. Today, his policies are driving lower taxes, bigger paychecks, and broader prosperity. So, I thank this committee for its partnership in this critical work, and I look forward to building on our strides through the President's budget for the year ahead. Thank you. Thank you. We'll now proceed to the question and answer session. Mr. Secretary, yesterday in the Senate you received a lot of questions from the Democrats over the anti-weaponization fund, which acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said on the record is dead. You also told the Senate Finance Committee over and over and over and over, a few more times again, that you cannot discuss the settlement or the case due to ongoing litigation. Can you please set the record straight right at the top of this hearing so members do not need to use their limited time asking you questions you cannot answer? What is the status of the fund, the settlement agreement, and what part did IRS CEO Mr. Pizzanano play in both? Thank you for this question, Mr. Chairman. I said yesterday that Einstein's definition of insanity is asking the same question over and over again and expecting a different answer. Let me be clear: as we've done throughout this matter, the Department of the Treasury is following the direction of the Department of Justice. Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche testified on Tuesday that the government will not be moving forward with the fund. We intend to comply with that direction and Attorney General Blanche's direction. I do not have any details into what that means at this time, but I will note that even with acting Attorney General Blanche's statement Tuesday, this matter is still subject to ongoing litigation, so I'm unable to comment further on it. That means I'm also not able to discuss any part of the settlement agreement. As with all department litigation, the Department of the Treasury and the IRS are represented by the Justice Department, so any additional questions about the settlement or the fund should be directed to acting Attorney General Todd Blanche. To your point about Mr. Pizzanano, while I cannot speak on his behalf, I will note that the settlement agreement calls for the United States to issue an apology to the plaintiffs for, among other things, leaking their tax returns. Mr. Chairman, President Trump is a great American who has endured over 10 years of harassment and weaponization from all levels of government. No American should be targeted for political reasons. Thank you, Secretary, for starting out with that information. This tax filing season was the most historic because of the tax relief delivered to working-class families through the working families tax cuts. How much total tax relief did Americans get just this tax season? Approximately 325 billion, Mr. Chairman. And how much did the average individual tax refund increase? The average refund increased by more than 11 percent, but that does not reflect the dollar value. As the ranking member liked to say, we over-promised and under-delivered. That is incorrect because so many people owed taxes last year that the dollar increase was actually much larger, going from negative to positive. As I travel the country, I have so many taxpayers who thank me because they had to pay last year and they got a refund this year. So how many taxpayers claimed at least one of President Trump's priorities of no tax on tips, no tax on overtime, no tax on Social Security, or the auto loan interest? Approximately 62 million filers, or about 44 percent, Mr. Chairman. And so, what percent of tax filers who received a tax cut in filing season earned less than 100,000 dollars? Just over 70 percent, sir. 70 percent. So what percent of waiters and other tipped workers who received a tax cut thanks to the no tax on tips earned less than 100,000 dollars? 90 percent. 90. That's helping people that need it the most. Funny, Democrats always claim, always claim, that the working families tax cuts were for billionaires. Mr. Secretary, I'm sure you know a lot of billionaires. Do you know any billionaire that was able to use the no tax on tips deduction? I have plenty of billionaires I give tips to, but not monetary tips, sir. Do you know of any billionaire that was able to use the no tax on overtime deduction? No, sir. Absolutely not. But you'll hear all day today that this tax cut was for the billionaires. But the evidence proves differently and so I greatly appreciate that. I'm sure we will hear about inflation and the economy today, too. And so at this point, at this point during President Biden's term, what was the core inflation rate? 6.2 percent, which led us to the worst inflation in 49 years, sir. Absolutely. And what was the highest under Biden? What was the highest? 6.6 percent, September of '22. And so what is core inflation this very second? 2.8 percent. 2.8 percent. But at this time during the Biden administration it was 6.2? Yes, sir. Wow. You wouldn't think that was the case based on the rhetoric coming from the other side, but that's what we're going to get. The working families tax cuts renewed and revamped the opportunity zone programs to ensure that rural communities get their share of the benefits. The law provides incentives that will drive an estimated 100 billion in new investments into rural communities, helping small businesses and working families in underserved, often overlooked areas of the country. How is the Trump administration implementing this improved opportunity zone program to ensure, ensure rural communities that I represent are in fact benefiting in the way Congress intended, and how is Treasury utilizing the new transparency requirements within the program to reinforce this effort to target those underserved communities? Mr. Chairman, as someone who grew up in a rural community, I'm very excited by the prospect of these opportunity zones being available there. I think it is new and important seeing the success, and I'm also excited by the permanence. And I would also point out that many members from the other side of the aisle have called Treasury concerned about losing opportunity zones in their districts or states in the case of senators, and I pointed out to them that they voted against them, but we will work with them on these opportunity zones. Congress wanted to reach all levels and we are working on transparency requirements which are critical because to make sure that the investment is reaching the rural communities, small businesses, and local projects. Thank you. On January 5th, 2026, through the hard work of both you and your staff, the Treasury Department secured a side-by-side agreement that largely exempts US-headquartered multinational enterprises from the OECD's income inclusion rule and under-taxed payments rule. This is a major, major win for US tax sovereignty. How confident are you that this agreement will be respected and fully, fully implemented by OECD members, particularly in the European Union? I believe that we have the tools necessary that if our sovereignty is not respected to counteract any failures to enact this. And I believe this is important because with this the money comes into the US Treasury rather than foreign treasuries, Mr. Chairman. What I would say, Secretary, you can count on us as working hand in glove as allies because Congress can also move legislation to make sure that they honor this, and we're willing to do that if they do not honor your commitment. And Mr. Chairman, I will point out that my friend Senator Wyden complimented the side-by-side when he was back in Oregon recently. That's great to hear. What monitoring has Treasury put in place to ensure that US companies are not still subjected to the discriminatory treatment? We frequently meet with the companies and we have active engagement with the OECD on this matter. Thank you. I now recognize the ranking member. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. A reminder to members of the committee, they get to decide how to allocate their five minutes as long as they're within the framework of the questions that they wish to ask. So Mr. Secretary, were you or any employee of Treasury informed of the settlement that the Attorney General entered into with the plaintiff of the fund? Again, Congressman, there's ongoing litigation and as I said, I'm unable to answer. Fine. So I think we can agree upon this: there were 400,000 taxpayers that had their tax information leaked when Charles Littlejohn released that information. We all agree no citizen should have their taxpayer information leaked. That's a clear violation. But if we follow the logic of what has been proposed, then the suggestion would be that these 400,000 people would be never subject to any sort of IRS oversight? Again, sir, I'm unable to comment on that. Okay. So let me go back to part of the statistical data that's been gathered. With Barnstable, Massachusetts, which is a central hub on Cape Cod for commercial and transportation activity, they rely upon tourist dollars. It's a big deal. Over the last year there's been a 54 percent decrease in Canadian tourists. We're slow-walking the tariff refunds. You and I were there, as I reminded the audience today, when the Supreme Court heard those arguments and the suggestion was that the tax refunds were going to proceed expeditiously. That is not happening. There have been arguments that somehow they've been slow-walked on purpose because they did cost 166 billion dollars, which would come from revenue that previously had been allocated. In Las Vegas, those who fly into the Harry Reid Airport—4.9 million people flew in last year, that's down by 12 percent. The positions that we have taken on the tariffs, the positions that we have taken on trade, have really been inconsistent with previous presidents and previous administrations. We understand how tariffs might be used to penalize an adversary, but the application of these tariffs are not working for the American family because of the price increases that are being experienced, particularly in the agricultural states where we can see where much of the political turmoil is now playing out. Ground beef is up by 20 percent; as I noted earlier, gasoline is up 51 percent; airfares have spiked by 20 percent. And everywhere that we turn, there's a price hike because of the economic policies that the President has embraced. Inflation has hit 3.8 percent. We've watched how now perhaps we're going to change the independence of the Federal Reserve Board for the purpose of complying with some political suggestions. Let me suggest here that we think that the independence of the Federal Reserve Board, and in fact the independence of the former chairman of that board, helped to guide us through a very rocky period of time. We watched how that service was denigrated time and again despite the fact that it was an honest, patriotic effort that was made by that chairman to get us through again a very, very difficult period of time. But if we don't like the outcome with the consumer price index, we get rid of the forecaster. If we don't like the result of what the Federal Reserve Board has said, then we threaten and intimidate the chairman of the Federal Reserve Board. The argument has been since your last visit that lowering cost was something that we all embraced, but there's a fundamental acknowledgment that it is not happening. I cited a previous statistic that I think remains very compelling this morning: 77 percent of the American people disagree right now with the economic positions that are being embraced by the administration. With that, I yield back my time. Thank you. Mr. Buchanan. Thank you for being here today, Mr. Secretary. Passing the working family tax cuts was a major win for all Americans. In addition to the record tax refunds that millions of Americans have received, I'm especially excited about the law as it pertains to small business. 100 percent bonus depreciation for business investment will boost the economic growth and manufacturing by encouraging small businesses to invest in America. Someone that's been in business for a lot of years, I can tell you the reality is very much the case. In addition, the permanent 199A small business deduction will make it easier for small businesses to create jobs and reinvest in their corporations or operations. More than anything else, I'm excited about the fact that we made a lot of this permanent. It'll make a huge difference going forward and it has already. Working families tax cuts are ensuring that American businesses can thrive and grow in the economy. I had the good fortune of being chairman of the Florida Chamber. We had 130,000 businesses, and these tax things, I can tell you, make a big difference. And the guy who's Mark, who's CEO, made that reference to me many times. Mr. Secretary, the small businesses in my district tell me 100 percent bonus depreciation is so important that that decision is key to their buying equipment and other things. What are your thoughts on that, or your experience on the bonus depreciation part? Congressman, I believe that the bonus depreciation, the full deductibility of equipment, is one of the greatest driving forces we are seeing in our manufacturing and, in fact, our Main Street revival. And I think equally important is the permanence. The Republican Congress made this permanent, so there is great certainty. And the 20 percent pass-through rate is also made permanent, giving small business permanence. Can you touch on 199A? That's a big thing for a lot of people in the district. It levels the playing field with the corporations where they got cut from 35 to 21, or whatever, or 20. But this will give that 20 percent—it will make a big difference. But I wanted to get your thoughts and what you're seeing. Yes, sir. It gives the average small business about 7,000 dollars more per year, and the small business deduction cuts taxes for 8 million small business owners by 4,600. And again, what is important here is the certainty and the permanence. It allows small businesses to hire, to invest, and plan for the future. And then let me just touch on—in my district, being in Florida, we have a lot of obviously seniors. One of the, I think, fifth most seniors in the country in terms of that. And they're excited about this cut. I think it's 200,000 seniors that I have in my district, but the number is a huge number across the country. Where is that at today, and I think more importantly to me, or one of the big things, is making sure we're talking to a lot of our seniors because most of them are taking advantage, but my sense of it is not all of them are taking advantage. What are we doing to communicate with the seniors that haven't taken advantage of the opportunity? Congressman, I was just down at The Villages in Florida, the largest retirement community not just in the United States, but in the world. And I cannot tell you the enthusiastic reception that them being able to keep most of their Social Security has made. And these are not the well-to-do people maybe at The Villages, but in general. When we saw the senior deduction, 60 percent of the filers made less than 100,000 dollars, and 96 percent of the filers, 96 percent, make less than 200,000. Yeah, for many years, I'd go to these hearings or different meetings that we would be involved in, many times with senior organizations, and they always complained or thought about when we are going to address that we've paid taxes once on Social Security and we've got to pay it again. So that's a big deal. But I just want to make sure we're doing everything we can to communicate with them so they can take advantage of it. Most have, but there's many that haven't. But thanks for your leadership and all you've done for the country. Thank you, sir, and Treasury is working with the Social Security Administration to make sure everyone is taking advantage of this. With that, I yield back. Thank you. Mr. Doggett. During your tenure as Treasury Secretary, has the IRS recommended that any taxpayer be granted immunity from an audit for their taxes? Again, one portion of that is under litigation, so I'm not able to— Yes, sir. It isn't a question of being not able; it's being not wanting to. But my question is about— As I said, sir, there is litigation. Reserving my time. My question to you again is: in the ordinary practice of the Treasury Department, has there ever been a recommendation that a taxpayer be granted immunity from an audit of their taxes? I'm sorry, what was that? Same question. Has the IRS recommended that any taxpayer be granted immunity from an audit of their taxes? Again, I'm unable to answer that. You can answer it; you refuse to answer. Can you tell the American people, in the history of the Treasury Department, has there ever been a taxpayer who's been recommended to have immunity from an audit of their taxes? Has there ever been a taxpayer who had his tax returns released, his family, and every employee? Thank you. I'll take that as a no. Let me ask you, sir, since you say you're relying—maintained my time. And since you refuse to answer, let me ask you: since you say you're following the advice of the Attorney General, the former criminal defense attorney for Mr. Trump—as you know, the Attorney General cannot compromise a tax dispute unless it's referred to him from the IRS for prosecution or defense—have you ever been involved in any way in a decision to refer a tax dispute to the Attorney General? Again, I cannot comment on that. You can't comment. Your argument here that you can't comment in this case makes as much sense as President Trump saying that he cannot— There is attorney-client privilege. I understand you want to talk over me, but you're not answering the question because you may have defined insanity by referring to Einstein, but your appearance here today and yesterday in the Senate defines deception. And that's what you're doing to the American people. Isn't it correct, sir, that the Financial Management Service within the Treasury Department must give approval before any payment can be made from the judgment fund? Sir, we administer the payments. Yes, sir. You give approval to it. No, sir, we administer them. You make a sign-off on them being— We administer them just like if you tell your bank to wire money. The bank does not say, "Do not pay, you shouldn't pay your Visa bill." Did Brian Morrissey, who had been only confirmed by the Senate a few months earlier as Treasury General Counsel, do a good job for you? Can you answer that just with a yes or no? Yes. Yes, he did. And as you know, he abruptly resigned rather than be associated with a seedy Trump slush fund deal and didn't sign off on anything. Can you pledge to the committee— Sir, there is— I'm asking you a question, sir, if you'd listen to it. Can you pledge to the committee that you will assure that no retaliation occurs against the career IRS employees who wrote the 25-page memo asking the Department of Justice to halt action on this outrageous audit immunity deal? Sir, we follow the law. Okay, well, will you follow the law in assuring the committee that you won't retaliate against those courageous career employees? We always follow the law. What's involved here, sir, is the most corrupt deal in American history.
